topics.title: Introduction to Computational Geometry: Basics of Computational Geometry - Introduction and Applications of Computational Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computational Geometry: Basics of Computational Geometry - Introduction and Applications of Computational Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Computational Geometry** is a branch of computer science devoted to the study of **algorithms** and **data structures** that can be stated in terms of **geometry**. It focuses on the design and analysis of efficient algorithms for solving geometric problems on **discrete** geometric objects such as **points**, **line segments**, **polygons**, and **polyhedra**.

The discipline was formally founded in **1975** by **Michael Ian Shamos** through his seminal Ph.D. dissertation at Yale University, and later co-developed with **Franco P. Preparata** in their landmark 1985 textbook *"Computational Geometry: An Introduction"*. The field sits at the intersection of:

- **Combinatorial Geometry** (mathematics)
- **Algorithm Design & Analysis** (theoretical computer science)
- **Discrete Mathematics** (foundation of data structures)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Plain English Analogy — "The Carpenter's Blueprint"**
> Imagine you are a **carpenter** planning the layout of a **new kitchen**. You have a physical space (a 2D floor plan) and you need to:
> 1. Figure out where to place the **stove, sink, and fridge** so that pipes and wires run the **shortest distance**.
> 2. Ensure no two appliances **collide** (overlap).
> 3. Find the **shortest walking path** between any two points in the kitchen.
> 4. Determine the **best viewpoint** to capture the entire kitchen in a single photograph.

You could do this by hand-measuring with a ruler, but that would take **days**. **Computational Geometry** is the science of writing a **computer program** that solves all of these problems **in milliseconds** — by treating every point, line, and shape as a **mathematical object** and applying a clever algorithm.

### 1.3 Core Distinguishing Characteristics

> [!NOTE] **Why Computational Geometry is NOT Just "Geometry on Computers"**
> 1. **Discrete, not Continuous** — Deals with **finite** sets of points/lines, not the infinite curves of classical geometry.
> 2. **Lower Bound Aware** — Always asks: *"What is the absolute minimum number of operations needed?"* using $\Omega(\cdot)$ notation.
> 3. **Output-Sensitive** — Algorithm efficiency often depends on the **size of the output**, not just the input.
> 4. **Degeneracy Handling** — Real geometric data has **collinear points**, **overlapping segments**, etc., which algorithms must robustly handle.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of the Field

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Big Three Sub-Areas (Heard in Every KTU Exam)**
>
> | # | Sub-Area | Core Question Answered | KTU Relevance |
> |---|----------|------------------------|---------------|
> | 1 | **Combinatorial Geometry** | What is the **maximum** number of geometric objects that can be produced? | Proves **upper/lower bounds** |
> | 2 | **Data Structures** | How to **store** geometric data for fast queries? | Range trees, segment trees |
> | 3 | **Algorithm Design Paradigms** | What general **techniques** solve geo-problems? | Sweep-line, divide & conquer, randomization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — How Computational Geometry "Thinks"

The standard operational pipeline when tackling a computational geometry problem is:

- **Step 1 — Abstraction:** Convert the real-world problem into a **geometric model** (e.g., a city map becomes a graph of points and edges).
- **Step 2 — Formalization:** Express the problem mathematically as a set of **inputs** (point set $P$), **outputs** (a structure $S$), and **constraints** (e.g., "no two edges cross").
- **Step 3 — Algorithm Design:** Choose a **paradigm** — typically one of the four core ones:
  - **Sweep Line** (also called *plane sweep*): Move a line across the plane, processing events in order.
  - **Divide and Conquer:** Split the input, recurse, merge.
  - **Incremental Construction:** Build the output one object at a time.
  - **Randomization:** Use random sampling to obtain expected $O(n \log n)$ performance.
- **Step 4 — Complexity Analysis:** Determine the **time** $T(n)$ and **space** $S(n)$ complexity using asymptotic notation.
- **Step 5 — Robustness Verification:** Test against **degenerate inputs** (3 collinear points, repeated points, etc.).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **All Critical Formulas for Module 1 (KTU Board Exam Reference)**

**Table 2.1 — Asymptotic Complexity Notation**

| Notation | Formal Definition | Verbal Meaning |
|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| $O(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0 : 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$ | $f$ grows **no faster than** $g$ (upper bound) |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | $\exists\, c, n_0 : 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$ | $f$ grows **no slower than** $g$ (lower bound) |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | $O(g(n))$ **and** $\Omega(g(n))$ both hold | $f$ grows **at the same rate as** $g$ (tight bound) |

**Table 2.2 — Fundamental Geometric Formulas Used in This Module**

| Formula | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| Euclidean Distance (2D) | $d(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Robot path length, GIS measurement |
| Area of Triangle (Shoelace) | $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)\vert$ | Polygon area, surface computation |
| Cross Product (2D) | $p \times q = x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1$ | Orientation test, area calculation |
| Dot Product (2D) | $p \cdot q = x_1 x_2 + y_1 y_2$ | Angle test, projection |
| Orientation Test | $\sigma(p, q, r) = \text{sign}((q-p) \times (r-p))$ | Convex hull, line-side detection |
| Convex Hull Size Bound | $3 \le \vert CH(P)\vert \le n$ (for $n$ points) | Tight bound for hull output |

**Table 2.3 — Standard Complexity Results (To Memorize)**

| Operation | Time Complexity | Algorithm |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Convex Hull (2D) | $O(n \log n)$ | Graham's Scan / Merge Hull |
| Convex Hull Lower Bound | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | Algebraic decision tree argument |
| Closest Pair of Points | $O(n \log n)$ | Divide & Conquer |
| Line Segment Intersection | $O((n + k) \log n)$ | Bentley-Ottmann Sweep |
| Point Location (Planar) | $O(\log n)$ query, $O(n)$ space | Kirkpatrick's Method |
| Halfplane Intersection | $O(n \log n)$ | Sort + Deque Sweep |

### 2.3 The Foundational Lower-Bound Theorem (Why $O(n \log n)$ is the "Speed Limit")

A cornerstone result proven by Shamos (1975) is:

$$\text{Any convex hull algorithm using only comparisons of coordinates requires } \Omega(n \log n) \text{ comparisons.}$$

This is proven by a **reduction** to the **Element Uniqueness** problem: given $n$ real numbers $x_1, \ldots, x_n$, decide whether any two are equal. This problem is known to require $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons in the algebraic decision tree model.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Where Computational Geometry is Deployed (Industry Map)**
> - **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Solid modeling, NURBS surfaces in mechanical engineering.
> - **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Map overlay, shortest path on road networks (used by Google Maps).
> - **Robotics:** Motion planning, collision detection, visibility graphs.
> - **Computer Graphics:** Hidden surface removal, ray tracing, mesh generation.
> - **VLSI Design:** Chip layout, wire routing, design-rule checking.
> - **Medical Imaging:** 3D reconstruction from CT/MRI slices.
> - **Machine Learning:** K-Nearest Neighbors, support vector machines, k-d trees for fast lookup.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure alignment, molecular surface computation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof of the $O(n \log n)$ Lower Bound for Sorting via Convex Hull

We will **exhaustively derive** why computing the convex hull of $n$ points in 2D requires $\Omega(n \log n)$ time. This derivation is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**.

> [!NOTE] **Setup of the Reduction Argument**

**Step 1 — Identify the hard subproblem.**

We will reduce the **Element Uniqueness (EU)** problem to the **Convex Hull (CH)** problem.

Given an instance of EU: a set of $n$ real numbers $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$.

We transform each $x_i$ into a 2D point:

$$p_i = (x_i,\; x_i^2)$$

which lies on the **parabola** $y = x^2$.

**Step 2 — Show that the upper hull reveals sorting.**

The **upper convex hull** of the point set $\{p_1, \ldots, p_n\}$, when ordered along the $x$-axis, lists the points in **sorted order by $x$-coordinate**.

Therefore, reading the upper hull from left to right gives us a sorted list of the $x_i$'s.

**Step 3 — From sorted list to element uniqueness check.**

Once we have the sorted list, we can determine if all $x_i$ are distinct by simply checking that every consecutive pair is different — a linear pass $O(n)$.

**Step 4 — Combine complexities.**

If the convex hull could be computed in $T(n)$ time, then sorting could be done in:

$$T(n) + O(n)$$

Since sorting is known to require $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons in the algebraic decision tree model, we conclude:

$$T(n) + O(n) \ge \Omega(n \log n)$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)}$$

This proves that **no comparison-based convex hull algorithm** can beat $O(n \log n)$ in the worst case.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Orientation Test (3-Point Turn Test)

The **orientation test** is the most fundamental primitive in computational geometry. It determines whether three points $p, q, r$ make a **left turn**, **right turn**, or are **collinear**.

> [!NOTE] **Exhaustive Derivation of the Cross-Product Sign Test**

**Step 1 — Define the cross product of two 2D vectors.**

For vectors $\vec{a} = (a_x, a_y)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_x, b_y)$:

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = a_x b_y - a_y b_x$$

This scalar is positive if $\vec{b}$ is to the **left** of $\vec{a}$, negative if to the **right**, and zero if parallel.

**Step 2 — Express orientation in terms of the cross product.**

Given three points $p = (p_x, p_y)$, $q = (q_x, q_y)$, $r = (r_x, r_y)$, form two vectors:

$$\vec{PQ} = (q_x - p_x,\; q_y - p_y)$$

$$\vec{PR} = (r_x - p_x,\; r_y - p_y)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cross product.**

$$\sigma(p, q, r) = \vec{PQ} \times \vec{PR}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma(p, q, r) = (q_x - p_x)(r_y - p_y) - (q_y - p_y)(r_x - p_x)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\sigma(p, q, r) \begin{cases}
> 0 & \text{if } r \text{ is to the LEFT of directed line } p \rightarrow q \quad \text{(Counter-Clockwise turn)} \\
= 0 & \text{if } p, q, r \text{ are COLLINEAR} \\
< 0 & \text{if } r \text{ is to the RIGHT of directed line } p \rightarrow q \quad \text{(Clockwise turn)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Computational cost.**

The orientation test requires exactly:

- **3 subtractions** (for the vector components)
- **2 multiplications** (cross product terms)
- **1 subtraction** (final difference)

So the total cost is **$O(1)$** — a constant per triple.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Paradigms to Problems

> [!NOTE] **Reference Matrix: Algorithm Paradigm $\leftrightarrow$ Classical Problem**

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Sample Problems Solved | Complexity Achieved |
|----------|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Sweep Line (Plane Sweep)** | Move a vertical line $\ell: x = c$ from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$, handling **events** at each $x$-coordinate | Segment intersection, closest pair, area union | $O((n+k)\log n)$ typical |
| **Divide and Conquer** | Recursively split point set into two halves, solve each half, then **merge** the two solutions | Closest pair, merge hull, Voronoi diagram | $O(n \log n)$ typical |
| **Incremental Construction** | Insert points one at a time, maintaining the output structure dynamically | Convex hull (randomized), Delaunay (Bowyer-Watson) | $O(n \log n)$ expected |
| **Randomized (Expected $O(n)$)** | Random shuffle input, exploit geometric **expected depth** properties | Convex hull, linear programming in low dimensions | $O(n)$ expected |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — The Convex Hull Size Bound

**Claim:** For a set $P$ of $n \ge 3$ points in general position (no three collinear), the convex hull $CH(P)$ has between $3$ and $n$ vertices.

**Proof (Upper Bound):** Every point of $P$ is either **on the hull** or **strictly inside** the hull. The hull is a convex polygon whose vertices are a subset of $P$. Therefore:

$$|\text{Vertices of } CH(P)| \le |P| = n$$

**Proof (Lower Bound):** The hull of any non-collinear set of $\ge 3$ points must be a polygon with **at least 3 vertices** (a triangle is the minimal convex polygon):

$$|\text{Vertices of } CH(P)| \ge 3$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{3 \le |\text{Vertices of } CH(P)| \le n}$$

---

### 3.5 Cost Model Justification

> [!NOTE] **Why we measure by "Number of Operations," not "Wall-Clock Time"**

In computational geometry, the standard **algebraic decision tree model** charges **one unit** for each:
- Arithmetic operation $(+,-,\times,/)$
- Comparison $(<, >, =)$
- Coordinate evaluation

This abstracts away hardware specifics. A single orientation test costs **$O(1)$** in this model.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Architecture of Computational Geometry

> [!NOTE] **Reading Guide:** This is a **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow** showing how a geometric problem is processed from real-world observation down to a CPU-level instruction trace.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Real World Problem]:::root --> B1[Geometric Abstraction Layer]:::layer
    B1 --> C1[Point Set P]:::data
    B1 --> C2[Line Segment Set S]:::data
    B1 --> C3[Polygon Poly]:::data
    C1 --> D1[Problem Formalizer]:::module
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Convex Hull Problem]:::problem
    D1 --> E2[Closest Pair Problem]:::problem
    D1 --> E3[Intersection Problem]:::problem
    D1 --> E4[Voronoi Diagram Problem]:::problem
    E1 --> F1[Paradigm Selector]:::module
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Sweep Line]:::algo
    F1 --> G2[Divide Conquer]:::algo
    F1 --> G3[Incremental]:::algo
    F1 --> G4[Randomized]:::algo
    G1 --> H1[Big O Time Analysis]:::analysis
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Asymptotic Bound n log n]:::result
    H1 --> I2[Lower Bound Omega n log n]:::result
    I1 --> J1[Final Algorithmic Solution]:::output
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Engineering Application]:::output
    K1 --> L1[CAD Systems]:::field
    K1 --> L2[Robotics Motion Planning]:::field
    K1 --> L3[GIS Map Engines]:::field
    K1 --> L4[Computer Graphics Rendering]:::field
```

> [!NOTE] **Mermaid Tree Diagram** showing the four classical subdivisions of the field, each with its signature problem.

```mermaid

    root1 --> ds[Geometric Data Structures]:::branch

    comb --> prob1[Convex Hull]:::prob
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define *Computational Geometry*. List **any four** of its real-world application areas.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Computational Geometry is the branch of computer science that deals with the design, analysis, and implementation of algorithms for solving geometric problems involving discrete geometric objects such as points, lines, polygons, and polyhedra.
>
> **Four applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — solid modeling and NURBS surface representation.
> 2. **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)** — map overlay and shortest-path queries.
> 3. **Robotics** — motion planning, collision avoidance, and visibility graph construction.
> 4. **Computer Graphics** — hidden surface removal, ray tracing, and mesh generation.

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** State the **lower bound** for any comparison-based convex hull algorithm. Briefly explain the **reduction technique** used to prove it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Lower bound (1 mark):** $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons in the worst case.
>
> **Reduction (2 marks):** Reduce **Element Uniqueness (EU)** to **Convex Hull (CH)**. Map each scalar $x_i$ to point $p_i = (x_i, x_i^2)$ on the parabola. The upper convex hull, read left-to-right, is the sorted list. Since EU requires $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons, so does CH.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **origin and historical development** of Computational Geometry, including the contributions of **Shamos** and **Preparata**. List the **four major algorithm design paradigms** used in the field. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **orientation test** using the **cross product** of two vectors. Derive the formula and state the **three cases** (left turn, right turn, collinear). Show that the test runs in $O(1)$ time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Historical Development (7 Marks)**

> **Era of Classical Geometry (Before 1970):** Geometry was studied as a continuous, deductive branch of mathematics following the Euclidean tradition. Algorithms were rarely considered.
>
> **Birth of the Field — 1975:** **Michael Ian Shamos**, in his Ph.D. dissertation at **Yale University**, coined the term *Computational Geometry* and presented a unified study of geometric algorithms. His work showed that many geometric problems can be solved efficiently by careful algorithm design.
>
> **Formalization — 1985:** **Shamos and Preparata** published the landmark textbook *"Computational Geometry: An Introduction"*, which remains the canonical reference. It formalized **convex hulls**, **Voronoi diagrams**, **sweep-line techniques**, and **divide-and-conquer** strategies for geometric problems.
>
> **Modern Era (1990s–Present):** Randomization, output-sensitive algorithms, and applications to GIS, VLSI, and graphics expanded the field.

**Four Major Paradigms (4 marks — 1 each):**

| # | Paradigm | Key Idea |
|---|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Plane Sweep** | Move a sweep line and process events in sorted order |
| 2 | **Divide and Conquer** | Split, recurse, merge the two halves |
| 3 | **Incremental Construction** | Add points one at a time, update structure |
| 4 | **Randomized Algorithms** | Random shuffle input, expected-case $O(n \log n)$ |

**(b) Orientation Test (7 Marks)**

Given three points $p = (p_x, p_y)$, $q = (q_x, q_y)$, $r = (r_x, r_y)$, define:

$$\vec{PQ} = (q_x - p_x,\; q_y - p_y)$$

$$\vec{PR} = (r_x - p_x,\; r_y - p_y)$$

The **2D cross product** is:

$$\sigma(p, q, r) = \vec{PQ} \times \vec{PR} = (q_x - p_x)(r_y - p_y) - (q_y - p_y)(r_x - p_x)$$

**Three cases:**

$$
\sigma(p,q,r) \begin{cases} > 0 & \Rightarrow r \text{ is LEFT of } p \rightarrow q \text{ (CCW turn)} \\ = 0 & \Rightarrow p, q, r \text{ are COLLINEAR} \\ < 0 & \Rightarrow r \text{ is RIGHT of } p \rightarrow q \text{ (CW turn)} \end{cases}
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["P (origin of vectors)"]:::pt
    b["Q (end of first vector)"]:::pt
    c["R above the line (LEFT turn, sigma greater than zero)"]:::pt
    d["R below the line (RIGHT turn, sigma less than zero)"]:::pt
    e["R on the line (COLLINEAR, sigma equals zero)"]:::pt

    a --- b
    a -. "counter clockwise" .-> c
    a -. "clockwise" .-> d
    a -. "collinear" .-> e

    classDef pt fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px
```

**Complexity:** Exactly **3 subtractions + 2 multiplications + 1 subtraction = $O(1)$** operations.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** Explain the **sweep-line paradigm** in detail. Describe an algorithm to compute the **union of axis-aligned rectangles** using this technique, with a clear statement of the **event-point structure** and the **data structure** used to maintain the active set. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **four algorithm design paradigms** (sweep line, divide & conquer, incremental, randomized) used in Computational Geometry, with **one example problem solved by each** and its **time complexity**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Sweep-Line Paradigm — Rectangle Union (7 Marks)**

> **Concept:** A vertical line $\ell$ sweeps from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$. At every $x$-coordinate where a rectangle **starts** or **ends**, an **event** occurs.

**Event Types:**

| Event | Trigger | Action |
|-------|---------|--------|
| **INSERT** | Left edge of a rectangle at $x = x_L$ | Insert its $y$-interval $[y_B, y_T]$ into active set |
| **DELETE** | Right edge of a rectangle at $x = x_R$ | Remove its $y$-interval from active set |

**Data Structure:** An **interval tree** or **segment tree** stores the currently active $y$-intervals and can report the **total length of union** of these intervals in $O(\log n)$ per event.

**Area Calculation:**

$$A = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} (x_{i+1} - x_i) \cdot L_i$$

where $L_i$ is the total length of the union of active $y$-intervals between event $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$.

**Total Complexity:** $O((n + k) \log n)$ where $k$ is the number of events.

**(b) Paradigm Comparison (7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE] **Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.5 each row)**

| Paradigm | Example Problem | Complexity | Strength | Weakness |
|----------|----------------|------------|----------|----------|
| **Sweep Line** | Segment intersection (Bentley–Ottmann) | $O((n+k)\log n)$ | Natural for time-ordered events | Requires sorting upfront |
| **Divide & Conquer** | Closest pair of points | $O(n \log n)$ | Predictable worst case | Merge step is often complex |
| **Incremental** | Convex hull (randomized) | $O(n \log n)$ expected | Simple to implement | Worst case can be $O(n^2)$ |
| **Randomized** | Linear programming in low dim | $O(n)$ expected | Blazing fast in practice | Probabilistic guarantee only |

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1 (2 marks lost):** Students write the convex hull formula using the **determinant form** but forget the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for the triangle area. Always remember: $\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert \text{determinant}\vert$.
> - **Pitfall 2 (1 mark lost):** Confusing the sign convention of the cross product. **Memorize:** $> 0$ = **left turn (CCW)**, $< 0$ = **right turn (CW)**.
> - **Pitfall 3 (3 marks lost):** Writing $\Omega$ (omega) when asked for $O$ (big-O), or vice versa. **$O$** = upper bound, **$\Omega$** = lower bound, **$\Theta$** = both.
> - **Pitfall 4 (2 marks lost):** Failing to mention **degenerate cases** (collinear points, repeated points). KTU examiners *always* award extra marks for robustness discussion.
> - **Pitfall 5 (1 mark lost):** Confusing the contributions of **Shamos (1975)** vs. **Preparata (1985)**. Shamos *founded* the field; Preparata *co-authored* the canonical textbook.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Your Wall!)**

- [x] **Computational Geometry = Algorithms for discrete geometric problems** (Shamos, 1975; Preparata, 1985).
- [x] **Formal definition:** Study of algorithms/data structures expressible in geometric terms.
- [x] **Big-O** = upper bound, **Big-Omega** = lower bound, **Big-Theta** = tight bound.
- [x] **Orientation test** $\sigma(p,q,r) = (q_x - p_x)(r_y - p_y) - (q_y - p_y)(r_x - p_x)$.
- [x] **Sign of $\sigma$:** $\text{positive} =$ left turn, $\text{zero} =$ collinear, $\text{negative} =$ right turn.
- [x] **Convex hull lower bound:** $\Omega(n \log n)$ via reduction from **Element Uniqueness**.
- [x] **Convex hull upper bound:** $O(n \log n)$ via Graham's Scan / Merge Hull.
- [x] **Hull size bound:** $3 \le |CH(P)| \le n$ for $n$ points in general position.
- [x] **Four paradigms:** Sweep Line, Divide & Conquer, Incremental, Randomized.
- [x] **Sweep Line complexity:** typically $O((n+k)\log n)$.
- [x] **Closest pair:** $O(n \log n)$ via divide & conquer.
- [x] **Applications to memorize:** CAD, GIS, Robotics, Computer Graphics, VLSI, Medical Imaging, ML (k-d trees).
- [x] **The field assumes the algebraic decision tree model** for complexity.
- [x] **Euclidean distance:** $d(p,q) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$.
- [x] **Shoelace formula** for triangle area: $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2-y_3) + x_2(y_3-y_1) + x_3(y_1-y_2)\vert$.
- [x] **Always handle degeneracies** (collinear triples) — KTU loves this.
- [x] **Distinguish discrete vs. continuous geometry** — this is a frequent 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Geometric Objects ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the discipline of **Computational Geometry**, a **Geometric Object** is defined as any mathematical entity occupying a specific position, orientation, and dimension within a Euclidean or non-Euclidean space, which can be represented, manipulated, and analyzed using algorithmic procedures. Geometric objects form the foundational primitives upon which every spatial algorithm, geometric data structure, and computational model is built. Geometric objects are classified based on their intrinsic dimensionality and spatial properties: - **0-Dimensional (0D) Objects:** Points — the most primitive, dimensionless entities defined by a coordinate tuple $(x, y, z, \ldots)$. - **1-Dimensional (1D) Objects:** Lines and line segments — extend along a single axis of direction. - **2-Dimensional (2D) Objects:** Planes, polygons, circles, and discs — occupy an area. - **3-Dimensional (3D) Objects:** Polyhedra, spheres, cones, and general surfaces — occupy volume. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every geometric object in PECST418 must be understood simultaneously in *two* ways — (a) its **mathematical/continuous form** (for analysis) and (b) its **discrete/computational form** (for algorithm design). Failing to grasp both perspectives is a common reason for losing marks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of geometric objects as the **alphabet of spatial language** — just as the English alphabet has 26 letters that combine to form every word, geometric objects (points, lines, polygons) are the atomic letters that combine to form every geometric algorithm, model, and data structure. **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are an architect drafting a building plan. You begin with a pencil **point** on paper, draw a straight **line** to define a wall, connect lines to form a closed **polygon** (a room), and stack polygons to represent a complete floor plan. Each higher-level object is composed of lower-level geometric primitives. This is exactly the philosophy of computational geometry — build complex spatial algorithms from simple geometric objects. > [!NOTE] > **Why are geometric objects important?** > They are the building blocks of: > - Computer-Aided Design (CAD) systems > - Geographic Information Systems (GIS) > - Computer Graphics and Gaming > - Robotics and path planning > - Medical imaging and 3D reconstruction ### 1.3 The 0D Object: The Point A **point** in $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an ordered tuple of $n$ real numbers: $$P = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$$ For 2D space: $P = (x, y)$ and for 3D space: $P = (x, y, z)$. A point has **no size, no area, and no volume** — it is a pure positional entity. **Degrees of freedom:** A point in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has exactly $n$ degrees of freedom (DOF), meaning $n$ independent scalar values are required to fully specify it. ### 1.4 The 1D Object: The Line Segment A **line segment** $\overline{PQ}$ is the finite portion of a straight line bounded by two distinct endpoints $P$ and $Q$. It is parameterized as: $$\overline{PQ}(t) = P + t(Q - P), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$ When $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$, the equation represents the **infinite line** through $P$ and $Q$. A line is a **1-dimensional affine subspace** of $\mathbb{R}^n$. ### 1.5 The 2D Object: The Polygon A **polygon** is a closed planar figure bounded by a finite chain of straight line segments forming a single closed loop. Formally, a polygon is a sequence of vertices: $$\text{Polygon} = \{V_0, V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_{n-1}\}$$ where each consecutive pair $(V_i, V_{(i+1) \bmod n})$ forms an edge, and the edges form a closed boundary. The polygon is **simple** if no edges cross, and **convex** if all interior angles are $\leq 180°$. ### 1.6 The Curved 2D Object: The Circle A **circle** in 2D is the locus of all points at a fixed distance $r$ (the radius) from a central point $C = (h, k)$: $$(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$$ A circle has **2 degrees of freedom** for its center $(h, k)$ plus **1** for its radius, totaling **3 DOF** in 2D. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** Always identify the dimension, degrees of freedom, and representation (implicit vs. parametric) of any geometric object before writing an algorithm. This is the first step KTU examiners check. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Representations of Geometric Objects Every geometric object can be represented in **three equivalent ways**, and KTU exam questions frequently test the ability to convert between them. **Representation 1 — Implicit Form:** $f(x, y) = 0$ - The object is described as the zero set of a function. - Example: line $ax + by + c = 0$; circle $x^2 + y^2 - r^2 = 0$. **Representation 2 — Parametric Form:** $P(t) = P_0 + t \cdot \vec{d}$ - The object is traced as a function of a parameter $t$. - Example: line $P(t) = (x_0, y_0) + t(a, b)$. **Representation 3 — Explicit Form:** $y = mx + c$ - Solves for one variable as a function of another. - Restricted to functions and breaks down for vertical lines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why are multiple representations needed?** > - **Implicit form** is best for testing point-in-object membership (substitute and check sign). > - **Parametric form** is best for ray-casting, intersections, and animation. > - **Explicit form** is best for plotting but is mathematically limited. ### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis | Geometric Object | Space | Number of DOF | |---|---|---| | Point $P$ | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | | Point $P$ | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 3 | | Line (infinite) | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 2 | | Line Segment $\overline{PQ}$ | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 4 | | Circle | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 3 | | Triangle | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 6 | | Polygon ($n$ vertices) | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | $2n$ | | Plane | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 3 | | Sphere | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | 4 | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---| | Distance between two points $P, Q$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $d(P,Q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_i - p_i)^2}$ | Unitless if coordinates are unitless | | Midpoint of segment $\overline{PQ}$ | $M = \left(\frac{p_1+q_1}{2}, \frac{p_2+q_2}{2}, \ldots\right)$ | Vector average | | Slope of line through $P, Q$ | $m = \frac{q_y - p_y}{q_x - p_x}$ | Undefined if $q_x = p_x$ | | Line equation (two-point form) | $(y - p_y)(q_x - p_x) = (x - p_x)(q_y - p_y)$ | Implicit form | | Line equation (point-slope form) | $y - p_y = m(x - p_x)$ | Explicit form | | Standard form of line | $ax + by + c = 0$ where $a^2 + b^2 \neq 0$ | Implicit form | | Distance from point to line | $d = \frac{\vert ax_0 + by_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Perpendicular distance | | Area of triangle (vertices) | $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert$ | Absolute value (unsigned) | | Area using cross product | $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{PQ} \times \vec{PR} \vert$ | 2D cross product gives scalar | | Polygon area (Shoelace formula) | $A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Indices modulo $n$ | | Circle equation | $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$ | Center $(h,k)$, radius $r$ | | Sphere equation | $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 + (z-l)^2 = r^2$ | Center $(h,k,l)$, radius $r$ | | Vector dot product | $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = \sum u_i v_i$ | Scalar output | | Vector cross product (3D) | $\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = (u_2v_3 - u_3v_2, u_3v_1 - u_1v_3, u_1v_2 - u_2v_1)$ | Vector output | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In all formula cells above, the absolute value bars have been written as `\vert` and `\mid` rather than the keyboard `|` to avoid corrupting the markdown table structure. Always do this in your own KTU notes. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Geometric objects underpin nearly every modern engineering discipline: - **Civil Engineering:** Polygons model land parcels and building footprints; points model survey benchmarks. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Spheres and planes model bearing surfaces; cylinders model shafts and pistons. - **Computer Graphics:** Triangles are the universal rendering primitive — every 3D model is reduced to a triangle mesh. - **Robotics:** Configuration space obstacles are polygons and polyhedra derived from primitive geometric objects. - **GIS and Cartography:** Polygons represent administrative regions, land-use zones, and water bodies. - **VLSI Design:** Geometric objects define the polygons of integrated-circuit masks. ### 2.5 Key Properties Every Geometric Object Has Every geometric object in computational geometry is characterized by the following **five properties**: 1. **Dimensionality (intrinsic dimension):** Number of independent directions in which the object extends. 2. **Degrees of Freedom:** Minimum number of real parameters needed to uniquely specify the object. 3. **Bounding Extent:** The smallest enclosing region (bounding box, bounding sphere). 4. **Orientation:** The direction the object "faces" (relevant for lines, planes, and directed segments). 5. **Interior / Boundary / Exterior Decomposition:** Every object partitions space into these three disjoint sets. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The interior/boundary/exterior decomposition is what allows point-in-polygon tests, ray-casting, and the Jordan Curve Theorem to be applied algorithmically. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Distance Between Two Points (Euclidean Metric) **Problem:** Given two points $P = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ and $Q = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, derive the formula for the straight-line (Euclidean) distance between them. **Step 1 — Conceptualize the difference vector.** The displacement from $P$ to $Q$ is a vector $\vec{d}$ whose components are the per-coordinate differences: $$\vec{d} = Q - P = (q_1 - p_1, q_2 - p_2, \ldots, q_n - p_n)$$ **Step 2 — Recognize that the length of a vector is the square root of the sum of squares of its components.** By definition of the Euclidean norm (also called the $\ell_2$-norm), the length of a vector $\vec{v} = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n)$ is: $$\Vert \vec{v} \Vert = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the definition to the difference vector.** Substituting the components of $\vec{d}$ into the norm definition: $$\Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{(q_1 - p_1)^2 + (q_2 - p_2)^2 + \cdots + (q_n - p_n)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Recognize this as the Euclidean distance.** The distance between two points is the length of the displacement vector between them: $$d(P, Q) = \Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_i - p_i)^2}$$ **Why this works:** Squaring the differences removes the sign information, summing accumulates the total "spread" of the displacement across all dimensions, and the square root converts the squared-length back to a linear scale (the same units as the input coordinates). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Area of a Triangle Using the Cross Product **Problem:** Given three non-collinear points $P = (p_x, p_y)$, $Q = (q_x, q_y)$, $R = (r_x, r_y)$, derive the area of triangle $\triangle PQR$ using the 2D cross product. **Step 1 — Translate the vertices so $P$ becomes the origin.** This is a standard trick that does not change the area (translation preserves area). Define: $$\vec{u} = Q - P = (q_x - p_x, q_y - p_y)$$ $$\vec{v} = R - P = (r_x - p_x, r_y - p_y)$$ **Step 2 — Recall that the 2D cross product (a scalar pseudo-vector) is:** For $\vec{u} = (u_x, u_y)$ and $\vec{v} = (v_x, v_y)$: $$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = u_x v_y - u_y v_x$$ This scalar equals the signed area of the parallelogram spanned by $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$. **Step 3 — Compute the cross product for the specific vectors.** $$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = (q_x - p_x)(r_y - p_y) - (q_y - p_y)(r_x - p_x)$$ **Step 4 — Expand and simplify.** $$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = q_x r_y - q_x p_y - p_x r_y + p_x p_y - q_y r_x + q_y p_x + p_y r_x - p_y p_x$$ The terms $p_x p_y$ and $-p_y p_x$ cancel: $$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = q_x r_y - q_x p_y - p_x r_y - q_y r_x + q_y p_x + p_y r_x$$ **Step 5 — Regroup to obtain the standard form.** $$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = p_x(q_y - r_y) + q_x(r_y - p_y) + r_x(p_y - q_y)$$ **Step 6 — Take absolute value and halve.** The area of the triangle is half the area of the parallelogram spanned by $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$: $$A_{\triangle PQR} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{u} \times \vec{v} \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert p_x(q_y - r_y) + q_x(r_y - p_y) + r_x(p_y - q_y) \vert$$ **Equivalent symmetric form (most used in KTU exams):** $$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert$$ **Why this works geometrically:** The cross product's magnitude equals the base of the parallelogram times its height. Halving it gives the area of the triangle inscribed within. The absolute value handles both clockwise and counter-clockwise vertex orderings. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: The Shoelace Formula for Polygon Area **Problem:** Derive the formula for the area of a simple polygon with $n$ vertices $V_0, V_1, \ldots, V_{n-1}$ where $V_n = V_0$. **Step 1 — Triangulate the polygon from a fixed vertex.** Choose a reference vertex $V_0$ and decompose the polygon into $(n-2)$ triangles: $$\triangle V_0 V_1 V_2, \quad \triangle V_0 V_2 V_3, \quad \ldots, \quad \triangle V_0 V_{n-2} V_{n-1}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the triangle area formula to each triangle.** $$A_i = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_0(y_{i+1} - y_{i+2}) + x_{i+1}(y_{i+2} - y_0) + x_{i+2}(y_0 - y_{i+1}) \vert$$ **Step 3 — Sum all triangle areas (with consistent sign convention).** Drop the absolute values and let signed areas sum, so that the final absolute value gives the total area: $$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$$ where the index $i+1$ is taken modulo $n$ (i.e., vertex $V_n$ is the same as $V_0$). **Step 4 — Validate for a square with vertices $(0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)$.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum} &= (0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot 0) + (1 \cdot 1 - 1 \cdot 0) + (1 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 1) + (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 1) \\ &= 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 2 \vert = 1$ square unit, which is correct. **Why this works:** The expression $\sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)$ is a discrete version of Green's Theorem $\oint (x\,dy - y\,dx) = 2A$. Each term $x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i$ represents twice the signed area of the triangle $\{(0,0), V_i, V_{i+1}\}$ — the shoelace "laces up" the polygon by summing these signed triangles. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Distance from a Point to a Line **Problem:** Derive the perpendicular distance from a point $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$ to the line $ax + by + c = 0$. **Step 1 — Identify two points on the line.** Choose any two points $P_1$ and $P_2$ on the line. Without loss of generality, take $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ such that the line direction is $\vec{d} = (b, -a)$ (perpendicular to the normal $\vec{n} = (a, b)$). **Step 2 — Set up the area-of-triangle formulation.** The distance from $P_0$ to the line equals twice the area of $\triangle P_0 P_1 P_2$ divided by the length of the base $\overline{P_1 P_2}$: $$d = \frac{2 \cdot A_{\triangle}}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the triangle area (using the determinant form) and direction magnitude.** $$A_{\triangle} = \frac{1}{2} \vert (x_1 - x_0)(y_2 - y_0) - (x_2 - x_0)(y_1 - y_0) \vert$$ $$\Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify using the constraint that $P_1, P_2$ lie on $ax + by + c = 0$.** Substituting and using the line equation repeatedly to eliminate $x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2$, the expression collapses to: $$d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$$ **Why this works:** The denominator $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$ normalizes the line's normal vector to unit length, so the numerator $\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert$ directly measures how far the point is along that unit normal direction. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Geometric Objects The following block diagram maps the taxonomy of geometric objects covered in this topic and the relationships between them. ```mermaid graph TD A[GEOMETRIC OBJECTS] --> B[0D PRIMITIVES] A --> C[1D PRIMITIVES] A --> D[2D PRIMITIVES] A --> E[3D PRIMITIVES] B --> B1[Point in Rn] B --> B2[Vector] C --> C1[Line Infinite] C --> C2[Line Segment PQ] C --> C3[Ray from P through Q] C --> C4[Polyline Chain] D --> D1[Plane Region] D --> D2[Polygon n vertices] D --> D3[Circle and Disc] D --> D4[Convex Hull] E --> E1[Plane in R3] E --> E2[Polyhedron] E --> E3[Sphere and Ball] E --> E4[Cylinder and Cone] D2 --> D2a[Triangle 3] D2 --> D2b[Quadrilateral 4] D2 --> D2c[General n gon] D2 --> D2d[Convex vs Non Convex] D2 --> D2e[Simple vs Self Intersecting] E2 --> E2a[Tetrahedron] E2 --> E2b[Cuboid] E2 --> E2c[General Mesh] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style E fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style D2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000 style E2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Geometric Representation The diagram below maps the three equivalent representations of a geometric object and the conversions between them — this is one of the most exam-relevant flow-charts in Module 1. ```mermaid graph LR IM[IMPLICIT FORM
f x y z equals 0] -->|substitute point| TST[Membership Test] IM -->|algebraic rearrange| EX[EXPLICIT FORM
y equals f of x] IM -->|introduce parameter t| PA[PARAMETRIC FORM
P of t equals P0 plus t times d] EX -->|introduce parameter t| PA PA -->|eliminate t| EX EX -->|cross multiply| IM PA -->|evaluate at t equals 0 1| SEG[LINE SEGMENT] PA -->|extend t to infinity| RAY[RAY OR LINE] PA -->|loop over vertex list| POL[POLYGON] TST -->|classify| OUT[POINT STATUS
INSIDE
OUTSIDE
ON BOUNDARY] SEG -->|concatenate| POL RAY -->|intersect with object| HIT[INTERSECTION POINT] style IM fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style EX fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style PA fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style OUT fill:#bf8f00,color:#000000 style HIT fill:#bf8f00,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Properties of a Geometric Object ```mermaid graph TD OBJ[ANY GEOMETRIC OBJECT] --> P1[Intrinsic Dimension] OBJ --> P2[Degrees of Freedom DOF] OBJ --> P3[Bounding Volume] OBJ --> P4[Interior Boundary Exterior] OBJ --> P5[Orientation and Normal] P1 --> P1A[0D Point] P1 --> P1B[1D Line] P1 --> P1C[2D Surface] P1 --> P1D[3D Solid] P2 --> P2A[Count of independent scalar parameters] P3 --> P3A[Axis Aligned Bounding Box AABB] P3 --> P3B[Bounding Sphere] P3 --> P3C[Convex Hull] P4 --> P4A[Point in Polygon Test] P4 --> P4B[Jordan Curve Theorem] P5 --> P5A[Unit Normal Vector] P5 --> P5B[Direction Cosines] style OBJ fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style P1 fill:#9dc3e6 style P2 fill:#9dc3e6 style P3 fill:#9dc3e6 style P4 fill:#9dc3e6 style P5 fill:#9dc3e6 ``` ### 4.4 Coordinate System Reference Block ```mermaid graph LR CS[COORDINATE SYSTEMS] --> CS1[Cartesian x y z] CS --> CS2[Polar r theta] CS --> CS3[Cylindrical r theta z] CS --> CS4[Spherical rho theta phi] CS1 -->|convert via
x equals r cos theta| CS2 CS2 -->|convert via
r equals sqrt x2 plus y2| CS1 CS1 -->|extend with z| CS3 CS1 -->|radial distance| CS4 style CS fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style CS1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style CS2 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style CS3 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style CS4 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PECST418]` Define a **geometric object**. List the four fundamental categories of geometric objects based on their intrinsic dimensionality, giving one example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A geometric object is a mathematical entity occupying a specific position, orientation, and dimension in Euclidean space, representable and manipulable by computational algorithms. The four fundamental categories based on intrinsic dimensionality are: 1. **0-Dimensional:** Point — a dimensionless location specified by coordinates $(x, y)$. 2. **1-Dimensional:** Line segment — extends along a single direction, bounded by two endpoints. 3. **2-Dimensional:** Polygon — a closed planar region bounded by line segments. 4. **3-Dimensional:** Polyhedron — a closed solid bounded by polygonal faces (e.g., a cube). > [!NOTE] > **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four categories with examples. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Explain the three mathematical representations of a geometric object (with reference to a 2D line) and state one advantage of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Given a 2D line, the three representations are: 1. **Implicit Form:** $ax + by + c = 0$ *Advantage:* Allows direct point-membership testing — substitute the point and check the sign of the expression. 2. **Parametric Form:** $P(t) = (x_0, y_0) + t(a, b)$ *Advantage:* Naturally handles ray-casting, intersection, and finite segments (by restricting $t$). 3. **Explicit Form:** $y = mx + c$ *Advantage:* Easy to plot and visualize; simplest for hand calculation. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's tip:** A common mistake is stating the explicit form is "best for vertical lines." It is in fact the *worst* for vertical lines, because the slope is undefined. Mention this caveat to earn the third mark. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a)** Derive the formula for the Euclidean distance between two points $P(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ and $Q(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Show every step clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 3)$, $B(7, 4)$, and $C(5, 10)$. Compute its area using the cross-product method. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *Step 1:* The displacement vector from $P$ to $Q$ is: $$\vec{d} = Q - P = (q_1 - p_1, q_2 - p_2, \ldots, q_n - p_n)$$ *Step 2:* The Euclidean norm (length) of any vector $\vec{v} = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n)$ is defined as: $$\Vert \vec{v} \Vert = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}$$ *Step 3:* Applying this to $\vec{d}$: $$\Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{(q_1 - p_1)^2 + (q_2 - p_2)^2 + \cdots + (q_n - p_n)^2}$$ *Step 4:* The distance between two points equals the magnitude of their displacement vector: $$d(P, Q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_i - p_i)^2}$$ *Step 5 — Validity for $n = 2$ (the Pythagorean Theorem):* For $P = (p_1, p_2)$ and $Q = (q_1, q_2)$: $$d = \sqrt{(q_1 - p_1)^2 + (q_2 - p_2)^2}$$ This is precisely the Pythagorean theorem applied to a right triangle with legs $\vert q_1 - p_1 \vert$ and $\vert q_2 - p_2 \vert$. *Step 6 — Unit analysis:* If coordinates are in metres, distance is in metres — the formula preserves the unit of the input. --- **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** Given $A(2, 3)$, $B(7, 4)$, $C(5, 10)$. *Step 1:* Compute the edge vectors from $A$: $$\vec{AB} = B - A = (7 - 2, 4 - 3) = (5, 1)$$ $$\vec{AC} = C - A = (5 - 2, 10 - 3) = (3, 7)$$ *Step 2:* Compute the 2D cross product: $$\vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} = (5)(7) - (1)(3) = 35 - 3 = 32$$ *Step 3:* Take the absolute value and halve: $$A_{\triangle ABC} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 32 \vert = 16 \text{ square units}$$ *Step 4 — Verification using the determinant form:* $$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_A(y_B - y_C) + x_B(y_C - y_A) + x_C(y_A - y_B) \vert$$ $$= \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(4 - 10) + 7(10 - 3) + 5(3 - 4) \vert$$ $$= \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(-6) + 7(7) + 5(-1) \vert$$ $$= \frac{1}{2} \vert -12 + 49 - 5 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert 32 \vert = 16 \text{ square units} \quad \checkmark$$ --- **Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** State and explain the **Shoelace formula** for computing the area of a simple polygon. List the conditions under which it is valid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Shoelace formula, compute the area of the polygon with vertices (in order) $P_1(0, 0)$, $P_2(4, 0)$, $P_3(5, 3)$, $P_4(3, 5)$, $P_5(0, 4)$. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Theory (7 marks):** The **Shoelace formula** (also called the Surveyor's formula) computes the area of a simple polygon whose vertices are listed in order (either clockwise or counter-clockwise). For a polygon with $n$ vertices $V_0, V_1, \ldots, V_{n-1}$ where $V_n \equiv V_0$: $$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$$ **Conditions of validity:** 1. The polygon must be **simple** (no self-intersections). 2. The vertices must be listed in a **consistent cyclic order** (all clockwise or all counter-clockwise). 3. The formula works for both convex and non-convex simple polygons. 4. For self-intersecting polygons, the formula gives the **signed area** of the enclosed regions, not the true geometric area. **Derivation sketch:** The sum $\sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)$ is a discrete form of the line integral $\oint x\,dy$, which by Green's Theorem equals twice the signed enclosed area. --- **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** Vertices in order: $V_0 = (0, 0)$, $V_1 = (4, 0)$, $V_2 = (5, 3)$, $V_3 = (3, 5)$, $V_4 = (0, 4)$, and $V_5 \equiv V_0 = (0, 0)$. *Step 1:* Tabulate the products: | $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i y_{i+1}$ | $x_{i+1} y_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | $0 \cdot 0 = 0$ | $4 \cdot 0 = 0$ | | 1 | 4 | 0 | $4 \cdot 3 = 12$ | $5 \cdot 0 = 0$ | | 2 | 5 | 3 | $5 \cdot 5 = 25$ | $3 \cdot 3 = 9$ | | 3 | 3 | 5 | $3 \cdot 4 = 12$ | $0 \cdot 5 = 0$ | | 4 | 0 | 4 | $0 \cdot 0 = 0$ | $0 \cdot 4 = 0$ | *Step 2:* Sum the two columns: $$\sum x_i y_{i+1} = 0 + 12 + 25 + 12 + 0 = 49$$ $$\sum x_{i+1} y_i = 0 + 0 + 9 + 0 + 0 = 9$$ *Step 3:* Subtract and take absolute value, then halve: $$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 49 - 9 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 40 = 20 \text{ square units}$$ *Step 4 — Verification (visual reasoning):* The polygon is an irregular pentagon roughly fitting in a $5 \times 5$ bounding box. The bounding box has area $25$; the pentagon is slightly smaller, so $20$ is reasonable. $\checkmark$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** — KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** if a negative signed area is reported as the final answer. > 2. **Not closing the vertex list** — When applying the Shoelace formula, the last vertex must wrap to the first. Students forget this and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up rows and columns in the determinant form** — Write the formula on the answer sheet before substituting. Skipping the formula statement costs 1 mark even if the numerical answer is correct. > 4. **Ignoring units** — Always state "square units" in area problems. Examiners treat missing units as a minor but consistent deduction. > 5. **Cross-product sign confusion** — In 2D, the cross product is a *scalar*, not a vector. Writing it as a vector loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist:** - A **point** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an $n$-tuple of real numbers; it is the **0-dimensional** primitive. - A **line** has 2 DOF in 2D (slope + intercept, or two points); a **line segment** has 4 DOF (two endpoints). - A **circle** in 2D has **3 DOF**: $(h, k, r)$. A **sphere** in 3D has **4 DOF**: $(h, k, l, r)$. - A **polygon** with $n$ vertices has **$2n$ DOF** in 2D. - The three equivalent representations of a geometric object are: **Implicit, Explicit, Parametric** — know the conversion between all three. - The Euclidean distance formula is $d = \sqrt{\sum (q_i - p_i)^2}$, derived from the vector norm. - The triangle area via cross product is $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{u} \times \vec{v} \vert$, where $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ are two edges from a common vertex. - The Shoelace formula is $A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$, valid for **simple** polygons with vertices in cyclic order. - The point-to-line distance is $d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ for line $ax + by + c = 0$. - Every geometric object partitions space into **interior, boundary, and exterior** — this is the basis for point-in-object testing. - The **bounding volume** (AABB, sphere, or convex hull) is always an over-approximation of the object and is used for fast collision and culling tests. - The intrinsic dimension of an object is the number of independent directions in which it extends; this is **not** the same as the dimension of the embedding space. - The **degrees of freedom** count is the minimum number of scalar values needed to fully specify the object. - The **Shoelace formula derivation** is a direct application of Green's Theorem in the plane. - The **2D cross product** is a scalar; the **3D cross product** is a vector — this distinction is frequently tested. - Vertical lines cannot be written in explicit form $y = mx + c$; use the implicit or parametric form instead. - For a polygon area, **always wrap the index** $V_n \equiv V_0$ before applying the Shoelace formula. - The **absolute value** in area formulas is mandatory — area is always non-negative. - Geometric objects in $\mathbb{R}^3$ include: planes, polyhedra, spheres, cylinders, cones, and general surfaces. - The **chain of complexity** is: Point → Line Segment → Polygon → Polyhedron → General Mesh.

topics.title: and Their Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Objects and Their Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Geometric Objects in Computational Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> In Computational Geometry (PECST418), a **geometric object** is a mathematical entity defined in a Euclidean space (typically $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$) that possesses measurable spatial properties such as position, dimension, and orientation. These objects form the foundational primitives upon which all spatial algorithms, data structures, and geometric computations are constructed.

The fundamental geometric objects studied in this course are organized into a hierarchy based on their topological dimension:

| Dimension | Geometric Object | Living in Space |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **0-D** | Point | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **1-D** | Line Segment, Ray, Line, Polyline | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **2-D** | Triangle, Polygon, Circle, Convex Hull | $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
| **3-D** | Tetrahedron, Polyhedron, Sphere | $\mathbb{R}^3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECST418):**
> The first module establishes a unified vocabulary for the entire course. Every later algorithm (convex hull, line sweep, Voronoi diagram) operates on these primitive objects, so their **mathematical representation** is not merely notation — it directly determines the **time complexity** and **numerical robustness** of the algorithm.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blueprint View":**
> Imagine you are an architect drafting a building plan. A **point** is a single dot on paper (a nail in a wall). A **line segment** is the edge of a ruler connecting two nails. A **polygon** is the outline of a room formed by chaining these ruler edges. Now imagine two different ways to describe that room:
>
> 1. **Verbal description**: *"The room has four corners, and the walls connect them in order."*
> 2. **Coordinate description**: *"Corners are at (0,0), (4,0), (4,3), (0,3)."*
>
> Both describe the *same* room, but the **representation** (how the data is stored in the computer) is drastically different. Computational Geometry is the science of choosing the *best* representation so that the computer can answer spatial questions — *"Which two walls are longest?"*, *"Does the chair fit inside?"* — as fast and as accurately as possible.

## 1.3 The Three Standard Mathematical Representations

A geometric object in the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ can be described mathematically using one of three canonical schemes:

1. **Explicit (Parametric) Form** — Defines the object by tracing it with a parameter $t$.
2. **Implicit Form** — Defines the object as the solution set of an equation $f(x,y) = 0$.
3. **Vector / Coordinate Form** — Lists the vertex coordinates of the object.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:**
> Many students believe "explicit form" means the equation $y = mx + c$. In rigorous Computational Geometry terminology, that equation is actually a special case of the *implicit* form (with $f(x,y) = mx + c - y$). The **parametric** form is the one that traces the object using a parameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Representation 1 — Explicit (Parametric) Form

A geometric object is given as the image of a parameter $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (or a bounded interval) under a mapping function $\mathbf{p}(t)$.

### 2.1.1 Parametric Line in $\mathbb{R}^2$

A straight line passing through two distinct points $\mathbf{p}_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathbf{p}_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ is represented as:

$$
\mathbf{L}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t \cdot (\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}
$$

- $t = 0$ → point $\mathbf{p}_1$
- $t = 1$ → point $\mathbf{p}_2$
- $t < 0$ → ray extending beyond $\mathbf{p}_1$
- $t > 1$ → ray extending beyond $\mathbf{p}_2$

### 2.1.2 Parametric Line Segment

$$
\mathbf{S}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t \cdot (\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1), \quad t \in [0, 1]
$$

### 2.1.3 Parametric Circle

A circle with center $\mathbf{c} = (c_x, c_y)$ and radius $r$:

$$
\mathbf{C}(\theta) = (c_x + r\cos\theta, \; c_y + r\sin\theta), \quad \theta \in [0, 2\pi)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use parametric form?**
> It is the most natural way to **traverse** the object point-by-point (e.g., for rendering or ray tracing). It also handles bounded objects (segments, arcs) cleanly by restricting the parameter domain.

---

## 2.2 Representation 2 — Implicit Form

An object is described as the **zero set** of a real-valued function $f : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$.

### 2.2.1 General Implicit Form

$$
\mathcal{O} = \{ (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid f(x, y) = 0 \}
$$

### 2.2.2 Implicit Line

Given a normal vector $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$ and a point $\mathbf{p}_0$ on the line:

$$
\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p}_0) = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
a(x - x_0) + b(y - y_0) = 0 \implies ax + by + c = 0
$$

where $c = -(a x_0 + b y_0)$.

### 2.2.3 Implicit Circle

$$
(x - c_x)^2 + (y - c_y)^2 - r^2 = 0
$$

### 2.2.4 The Half-Plane Test (Crucial KTU Concept)

The implicit form gives a **free point-location test**: for any point $\mathbf{q} = (x_q, y_q)$,

$$
f(\mathbf{q}) = a x_q + b y_q + c
$$

| Sign of $f(\mathbf{q})$ | Geometric Meaning |
| :---: | :--- |
| $f(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | $\mathbf{q}$ lies **on** the line |
| $f(\mathbf{q}) > 0$ | $\mathbf{q}$ lies on the **positive** side of $\mathbf{n}$ |
| $f(\mathbf{q}) < 0$ | $\mathbf{q}$ lies on the **negative** side of $\mathbf{n}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why implicit form matters in algorithms:**
> The signed-distance test $f(\mathbf{q})$ is the engine behind **convex hull algorithms** (orientation tests), **line-sweep methods**, and **BSP trees**. It is preferred whenever the algorithm needs to classify points relative to a line.

---

## 2.3 Representation 3 — Coordinate (Vertex) Form

A polygonal object is stored as an **ordered list of vertex coordinates**.

### 2.3.1 Simple Polygon

$$
P = [ \mathbf{v}_0, \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_{n-1} ]
$$

with edges $E_i = \overline{\mathbf{v}_i \mathbf{v}_{(i+1) \bmod n}}$.

### 2.3.2 Triangle

$$
T = \{ \mathbf{v}_0, \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2 \}, \quad \text{with } 3 \text{ edges and } 1 \text{ interior face}
$$

### 2.3.3 Convex Polygon

A polygon is **convex** iff every internal angle is $\leq 180°$, equivalently:

$$
\forall i,\; \text{cross}(\mathbf{v}_{i+1} - \mathbf{v}_i,\; \mathbf{v}_{i+2} - \mathbf{v}_{i+1}) \text{ has the same sign}
$$

where $\text{cross}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = u_x v_y - u_y v_x$ is the 2-D cross product.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following table — it covers roughly **80% of the 3-mark questions** in Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula / Representation | Typical Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **F1** | Parametric line | $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$ | Ray tracing, segment traversal |
| **F2** | Implicit line | $ax + by + c = 0$ | Half-plane test, orientation |
| **F3** | 2-D cross product | $\text{cross}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = u_x v_y - u_y v_x$ | Orientation (CCW / CW) |
| **F4** | Dot product | $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = u_x v_x + u_y v_y$ | Angle test, projection |
| **F5** | Distance point-to-line | $d = \dfrac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Closest-point queries |
| **F6** | Signed area of triangle | $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \text{cross}(\mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_0,\; \mathbf{v}_2 - \mathbf{v}_0)$ | Polygon area, orientation |
| **F7** | Shoelace area (polygon) | $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Total area computation |
| **F8** | Parametric circle | $\mathbf{C}(\theta) = (c_x + r\cos\theta, c_y + r\sin\theta)$ | Arc rendering, sampling |
| **F9** | Implicit circle | $(x-c_x)^2 + (y-c_y)^2 - r^2 = 0$ | Inside/outside test |
| **F10** | Convex polygon condition | Same-sign cross products on all consecutive triples | Convexity check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These representations are the bedrock of **CAD systems** (AutoCAD, SolidWorks), **GIS** (Google Maps, QGIS), **computer graphics** (OpenGL, DirectX), **robot motion planning**, and **VLSI physical design**. Choosing the right representation can reduce a query from $O(n)$ to $O(\log n)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Parametric Form to Implicit Form

> **Goal:** Convert the parametric line passing through $\mathbf{p}_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathbf{p}_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ into the implicit equation $ax + by + c = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the parametric equations componentwise.**

From $\mathbf{L}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$, we have:

$$
x(t) = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1)
$$
$$
y(t) = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve each equation for the parameter $t$.**

Assuming $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $y_1 \neq y_2$ (non-vertical, non-horizontal generic case):

$$
t = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$
$$
t = \frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $t$.**

$$
\frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply to eliminate the fractions.**

$$
(x - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) = (y - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand both sides.**

$$
x y_2 - x y_1 - x_1 y_2 + x_1 y_1 = y x_2 - y x_1 - y_1 x_2 + y_1 x_1
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the common term $x_1 y_1 = y_1 x_1$ and rearrange.**

$$
x y_2 - x y_1 - y x_2 + y x_1 = x_1 y_2 - y_1 x_2
$$

**Step 7 — Group the $x$ and $y$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$
x(y_2 - y_1) + y(x_1 - x_2) = x_1 y_2 - y_1 x_2
$$

**Step 8 — Identify $a$, $b$, $c$ in $ax + by + c = 0$.**

$$
a = (y_2 - y_1), \qquad b = (x_1 - x_2), \qquad c = -\,x_1 y_2 + y_1 x_2
$$

Equivalently, the coefficients are obtained from the **normal vector** $\mathbf{n} = (a, b) = (y_2 - y_1,\; x_1 - x_2) = \mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1$ rotated by $90°$ counter-clockwise. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signed Distance from a Point to a Line

> **Goal:** Given implicit line $L: ax + by + c = 0$ and a query point $\mathbf{q} = (x_0, y_0)$, derive the shortest distance.

**Step 1 — Recall the distance from a point to a line in the plane.**

For a line with normal vector $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$, any vector from a point on the line to $\mathbf{q}$ has length component along $\mathbf{n}$ equal to the signed distance.

**Step 2 — The signed projection of $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}_0)$ onto the unit normal $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$.**

$$
d_{\text{signed}} = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}_0)}{\vert \mathbf{n} \vert} = \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 - a x_{p_0} - b y_{p_0}}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Since $\mathbf{p}_0$ lies on the line, $a x_{p_0} + b y_{p_0} = -c$. Substitute.**

$$
d_{\text{signed}} = \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 + c}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take absolute value for unsigned (geometric) distance.**

$$
d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** It is the foundation of *point-in-polygon* algorithms (ray casting), *line-sweep distance events*, and the construction of *Voronoi diagrams* (where each edge is equidistant from two sites).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Representation

> [!TIP]
> The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU exam aid. For a given **operation**, select the most efficient representation.

| Operation | Best Representation | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Render object point-by-point** | Parametric | Trivial sampling via $t$ or $\theta$ |
| **Determine which side of a line a point is on** | Implicit | Single plug-in $f(\mathbf{q})$ |
| **Compute polygon area** | Coordinate (vertices) | Direct shoelace summation |
| **Check if polygon is convex** | Coordinate (vertices) | Sequential cross-product sign test |
| **Test if two line segments intersect** | Parametric + Implicit | Solve for $t, s \in [0,1]$ |
| **Compute shortest distance to a wall** | Implicit | Direct signed-distance formula |
| **Store large mesh for 3-D printing** | Coordinate (vertex list) | Compatible with .STL, .OBJ formats |
| **Find intersection of two circles** | Implicit | Solve the system of two equations |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Convexity Test (Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** Test whether the polygon with vertices $\mathbf{v}_0 = (0,0),\; \mathbf{v}_1 = (4,0),\; \mathbf{v}_2 = (4,3),\; \mathbf{v}_3 = (0,3)$ is convex.

**Step 1 — Compute consecutive edge vectors.**

$$
\mathbf{e}_0 = \mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_0 = (4, 0)
$$
$$
\mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{v}_2 - \mathbf{v}_1 = (0, 3)
$$
$$
\mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{v}_3 - \mathbf{v}_2 = (-4, 0)
$$
$$
\mathbf{e}_3 = \mathbf{v}_0 - \mathbf{v}_3 = (0, -3)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cross product of each consecutive pair.**

Using $\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_{i+1}) = e_{i,x} e_{i+1, y} - e_{i, y} e_{i+1, x}$:

$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_0, \mathbf{e}_1) = 4 \cdot 3 - 0 \cdot 0 = +12
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2) = 0 \cdot 0 - 3 \cdot (-4) = +12
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_2, \mathbf{e}_3) = (-4) \cdot (-3) - 0 \cdot 0 = +12
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_3, \mathbf{e}_0) = 0 \cdot 0 - (-3) \cdot 4 = +12
$$

**Step 3 — Check sign consistency.**

All four cross products equal $+12 > 0$. They all share the same sign (positive).

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The polygon is **convex** and is oriented **counter-clockwise** (CCW). $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Geometric Objects

The following block-level diagram maps the hierarchy of geometric primitives and their standard representations, illustrating how each object class maps to its preferred mathematical form.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["GEOMETRIC OBJECTS IN R2"] --> B0["0 DIMENSIONAL"]
    A0 --> C0["1 DIMENSIONAL"]
    A0 --> D0["2 DIMENSIONAL"]
    A0 --> E0["3 DIMENSIONAL"]

    B0 --> B1["POINT"]
    B1 --> B1A["Coordinates x comma y"]
    B1 --> B1B["Vector form"]

    C0 --> C1["LINE SEGMENT"]
    C0 --> C2["RAY"]
    C0 --> C3["INFINITE LINE"]
    C0 --> C4["CIRCLE ARC"]
    C1 --> C1A["Parametric p1 plus t times p2 minus p1"]
    C1 --> C1B["Implicit via two endpoints"]
    C3 --> C3A["Parametric full line"]
    C3 --> C3B["Implicit ax plus by plus c equals 0"]

    D0 --> D1["TRIANGLE"]
    D0 --> D2["SIMPLE POLYGON"]
    D0 --> D3["CONVEX POLYGON"]
    D0 --> D4["CIRCLE"]
    D2 --> D2A["Vertex list v0 to vn minus 1"]
    D2 --> D2B["Edge list"]
    D3 --> D3A["Vertex list CCW ordered"]
    D3 --> D3B["Same sign cross products"]
    D4 --> D4A["Implicit x minus cx squared plus y minus cy squared minus r squared"]
    D4 --> D4B["Parametric angle theta"]

    E0 --> E1["TETRAHEDRON"]
    E0 --> E2["POLYHEDRON"]
    E0 --> E3["SPHERE"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Representation Engine

The following schematic shows the **functional flow** of a typical Computational Geometry software pipeline — from the raw geometric data, through the chosen representation, to the final query.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["INPUT GEOMETRY"] --> A2["RAW DATA FILE"]
    A2 --> B1["REPRESENTATION SELECTOR"]
    B1 --> C1["PARAMETRIC STORE"]
    B1 --> C2["IMPLICIT STORE"]
    B1 --> C3["COORDINATE STORE"]
    C1 --> D1["TRAVERSAL ENGINE"]
    C2 --> D2["SIDE TEST ENGINE"]
    C3 --> D3["AREA AND ORIENTATION ENGINE"]
    D1 --> E1["RENDERING OUTPUT"]
    D1 --> E2["RAY CASTING"]
    D2 --> E3["CONVEX HULL"]
    D2 --> E4["LINE INTERSECTION"]
    D3 --> E5["AREA COMPUTATION"]
    D3 --> E6["CONVEXITY CHECK"]
    E1 --> F1["FINAL VISUALIZATION"]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Implicit Line and the Half-Plane Test

The following diagram visualizes the implicit line $L: ax + by + c = 0$, the normal vector $\mathbf{n}$, and the two half-planes defined by the sign of $f(\mathbf{q})$.

```mermaid
graph TB
    L0["ORIGIN O"] --> P0["POINT p0 ON LINE L"]
    P0 --> N0["NORMAL VECTOR n OF LINE L"]
    P0 --> P1["POSITIVE HALF PLANE f q greater than 0"]
    P0 --> M1["NEGATIVE HALF PLANE f q less than 0"]
    P0 --> Z1["LINE L f x y equals 0"]
    Q1["QUERY POINT q in R2"] --> S0["EVALUATE f q equals a xq plus b yq plus c"]
    S0 --> R1["IF f q greater than 0 then POSITIVE SIDE"]
    S0 --> R2["IF f q less than 0 then NEGATIVE SIDE"]
    S0 --> R3["IF f q equals 0 then ON LINE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The block `EVALUATE f q` is the *single arithmetic operation* that classifies $\mathbf{q}$. This is precisely why the implicit form is the representation of choice for the orientation predicate in algorithms like **Graham's Scan** and **QuickHull**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

> **Q1.** With suitable diagrams, explain the three standard mathematical representations of a line in computational geometry. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Parametric / Explicit form:** $\mathbf{L}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$, $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
   *Advantage:* Easily traverses the line by varying the parameter $t$. Useful in ray tracing and segment rendering.

2. **Implicit form:** $ax + by + c = 0$.
   *Advantage:* Enables the **half-plane test** in $O(1)$ time — plugging a point into $f(\mathbf{q}) = ax + by + c$ instantly tells on which side of the line the point lies.

3. **Coordinate / two-point form:** Lists two endpoint coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$.
   *Advantage:* Compact storage and direct use in intersection algorithms (e.g., segment intersection by solving two parametric equations).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

> **Q2.** Distinguish between a simple polygon and a convex polygon. Give one example of a non-convex (concave) polygon and explain why it fails the convexity test.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **simple polygon** is a closed planar figure whose edges do not intersect except at shared vertices. A **convex polygon** is a simple polygon in which every line segment between any two points inside or on the polygon lies entirely within the polygon.

**Example of a concave polygon:** The arrow-shaped polygon with vertices $(0,0), (4,0), (4,2), (2,1), (4,2)$ — wait, this is degenerate. A cleaner example is a **chevron** (arrow-head) with vertices:
$(2,0), (4,2), (3,1), (4,0), (2,2), (0,0), (1,1)$. Here, the interior angle at vertex $(3,1)$ exceeds $180°$, so the cross product at that corner has the *opposite sign* to the rest, failing the **same-sign cross-product test** for convexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q A (a).** Derive the implicit equation $ax + by + c = 0$ of a line passing through two given points $\mathbf{p}_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathbf{p}_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in the Cartesian plane. Clearly state the geometric meaning of the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Begin with the parametric form (this is the **start of the derivation**, **1 mark**):
$$
x = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1), \quad y = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1)
$$

Step 2: Solve for $t$ in each equation (**1 mark**):
$$
t = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}
$$

Step 3: Cross-multiply to eliminate $t$ (**1 mark**):
$$
(x - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) = (y - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)
$$

Step 4: Expand and cancel the common term $x_1 y_1$ (**1 mark**):
$$
x(y_2 - y_1) - y(x_2 - x_1) = x_1 y_2 - y_1 x_2
$$

Step 5: Identify the coefficients (**1 mark**):
$$
a = (y_2 - y_1), \quad b = -(x_2 - x_1) = (x_1 - x_2), \quad c = -(x_1 y_2 - y_1 x_2) = y_1 x_2 - x_1 y_2
$$

Step 6: Geometric meaning (**2 marks**):
- The vector $(a, b) = (y_2 - y_1, x_1 - x_2)$ is the **direction vector $(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$ rotated by $+90°$** — it is the outward **normal** to the line.
- $c$ is determined by the line's offset from the origin; it equals $-(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p}_1)$.

---

> **Q A (b).** For the line obtained above, derive the formula for the (unsigned) distance from an arbitrary point $\mathbf{q} = (x_0, y_0)$ to the line. Use it to compute the distance from the point $(2, 5)$ to the line passing through $(0, 0)$ and $(3, 4)$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Start with the implicit line $ax + by + c = 0$ and a point $\mathbf{p}_0$ on the line (**1 mark**).

Step 2: The signed distance equals the projection of $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}_0)$ onto the unit normal $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ (**1 mark**):
$$
d_{\text{signed}} = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}_0)}{\vert \mathbf{n} \vert}
$$

Step 3: Substitute $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p}_0 = -c$ to obtain (**1 mark**):
$$
d_{\text{signed}} = \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 + c}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

Step 4: Take absolute value for the unsigned (geometric) distance (**1 mark**):
$$
d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

Step 5: Compute $a, b, c$ for the line through $(0,0)$ and $(3,4)$ (**1 mark**):
$$
a = y_2 - y_1 = 4 - 0 = 4, \quad b = x_1 - x_2 = 0 - 3 = -3, \quad c = y_1 x_2 - x_1 y_2 = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

So the line is $4x - 3y = 0$.

Step 6: Plug in $\mathbf{q} = (2, 5)$ (**1 mark**):
$$
d = \frac{\vert 4(2) - 3(5) + 0 \vert}{\sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2}} = \frac{\vert 8 - 15 \vert}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4
$$

**Final Answer:** $d = 1.4$ units.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q B (a).** Define the orientation test for three points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ using the 2-D cross product. Explain with a diagram how the sign of the cross product determines whether the triple is in counter-clockwise (CCW), clockwise (CW), or collinear configuration. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Definition (**1 mark**): Given three points $\mathbf{p}$, $\mathbf{q}$, $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^2$, the **orientation test** computes the signed area:
$$
\text{Orient}(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}, \mathbf{r}) = \text{cross}(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p},\; \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{p}) = (q_x - p_x)(r_y - p_y) - (q_y - p_y)(r_x - p_x)
$$

Step 2: Interpretation of the sign (**2 marks**):
- If $\text{Orient} > 0$ → the triple $(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}, \mathbf{r})$ is in **counter-clockwise (CCW)** order.
- If $\text{Orient} < 0$ → **clockwise (CW)** order.
- If $\text{Orient} = 0$ → the three points are **collinear**.

Step 3: Diagram description (**2 marks**): (a) CCW configuration: starting from $\mathbf{p}$, the path to $\mathbf{q}$ to $\mathbf{r}$ turns left. (b) CW: turns right. (c) Collinear: the three points lie on a single straight line; the "turn" angle is exactly $0°$ or $180°$.

Step 4: Geometric meaning of the cross product (**1 mark**): $\text{cross}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = u_x v_y - u_y v_x$ equals $\vert \mathbf{u} \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{v} \vert \cdot \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle from $\mathbf{u}$ to $\mathbf{v}$. Its sign equals the sign of $\sin\theta$.

Step 5: Worked example (**1 mark**): For $\mathbf{p} = (0,0), \mathbf{q} = (1,0), \mathbf{r} = (1,1)$:
$$
\text{Orient} = (1)(1) - (0)(1) = +1 > 0 \implies \text{CCW}.
$$

---

> **Q B (b).** Using the convexity-test cross-product algorithm, verify whether the polygon with vertices $\mathbf{v}_0 = (0,0), \mathbf{v}_1 = (5,0), \mathbf{v}_2 = (5,5), \mathbf{v}_3 = (2,2), \mathbf{v}_4 = (0,5)$ is convex. If not, identify the reflex vertex. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the five edge vectors (**1 mark**):
$$
\mathbf{e}_0 = (5, 0), \quad \mathbf{e}_1 = (0, 5), \quad \mathbf{e}_2 = (-3, -3), \quad \mathbf{e}_3 = (-2, 3), \quad \mathbf{e}_4 = (0, -5)
$$

Step 2: Compute the cross products $\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_{i+1})$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ (**3 marks**):
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_0, \mathbf{e}_1) = (5)(5) - (0)(0) = +25
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2) = (0)(-3) - (5)(-3) = +15
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_2, \mathbf{e}_3) = (-3)(3) - (-3)(-2) = -9 - 6 = -15
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_3, \mathbf{e}_4) = (-2)(-5) - (3)(0) = +10
$$
$$
\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_4, \mathbf{e}_0) = (0)(0) - (-5)(5) = +25
$$

Step 3: Check for sign consistency (**1 mark**): The signs are $(+, +, -, +, +)$. Since the third cross product (at vertex $\mathbf{v}_3 = (2, 2)$) is **negative** while the others are positive, the polygon is **NOT convex**.
