Step 4: Identify the reflex vertex (**1 mark**): The reflex vertex is $\mathbf{v}_3 = (2, 2)$ — it has an interior angle $> 180°$.

Step 5: Visualization (**1 mark**): The polygon resembles a "square with a notch pushed in" at the center of the right edge. The vertex $(2, 2)$ is the inward dent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 1 — and how to avoid it:**
>
> 1. **Confusing parametric and implicit forms (loses 2-3 marks):** Many students write $y = mx + c$ and call it "parametric". The correct parametric form must use a parameter $t$ that traces the object: $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$. $y = mx + c$ is a special case of the **implicit** form $mx - y + c = 0$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting absolute value in the distance formula (loses 1-2 marks):** The unsigned distance from a point to a line requires $\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert$ in the numerator. Writing it without the absolute-value bars is technically the *signed* distance and will lose marks unless the question explicitly asks for signed.
>
> 3. **Sign convention for cross product (loses 1 mark):** In the KTU convention, $\text{cross}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) > 0$ means CCW. Some textbooks reverse this. Stick to the **KTU standard** and you will be safe.
>
> 4. **Missing the vertex-list index wrap-around (loses 1 mark):** In a polygon with $n$ vertices, the edge from $\mathbf{v}_{n-1}$ closes back to $\mathbf{v}_0$. The shoelace formula and convexity test both require the modulo-$n$ wrap. Forgetting this is one of the top three mistakes flagged by KTU examiners.
>
> 5. **Skipping the geometric meaning of coefficients (loses 2 marks in derivations):** When asked to derive $ax + by + c = 0$, you must explicitly state that $(a, b)$ is the **normal vector** and $c$ is the **offset**. Examiners reward interpretation, not just algebra.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Geometric Objects and Their Representations**

- A **point** in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a 0-D object stored as $(x, y)$.
- A **line segment** is a 1-D object stored either parametrically or by its two endpoints.
- A **polygon** is a 2-D object stored as an ordered list of vertices $\mathbf{v}_0, \mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_{n-1}$ with edges wrapping modulo $n$.
- **Three standard representations:** (1) Parametric — uses a parameter to trace the object. (2) Implicit — uses $f(x,y) = 0$ for line/circle. (3) Coordinate — lists vertices.
- **Parametric line:** $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_1 + t(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$, $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $[0,1]$ for segment).
- **Implicit line:** $ax + by + c = 0$. The vector $(a, b)$ is the **normal**, perpendicular to the line.
- **2-D cross product:** $\text{cross}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = u_x v_y - u_y v_x$. Sign = orientation of the turn from $\mathbf{u}$ to $\mathbf{v}$.
- **Orientation test:** $\text{cross}(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{p}) > 0 \Leftrightarrow$ CCW turn; $< 0 \Leftrightarrow$ CW; $= 0 \Leftrightarrow$ collinear.
- **Half-plane test:** Plug $\mathbf{q} = (x_0, y_0)$ into $f(x,y) = ax + by + c$. Sign of $f(\mathbf{q})$ tells the side of the line.
- **Distance formula:** $d = \dfrac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ — unsigned perpendicular distance.
- **Shoelace area:** $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ — summation wraps modulo $n$.
- **Convexity test:** Compute $\text{cross}(\mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_{i+1})$ for all $i$. If all cross products share the same sign, the polygon is convex; otherwise, the vertex where the sign changes is a **reflex vertex**.
- **Implicit circle:** $(x - c_x)^2 + (y - c_y)^2 - r^2 = 0$ — basis for the inside/outside test.
- **Parametric circle:** $\mathbf{C}(\theta) = (c_x + r\cos\theta, c_y + r\sin\theta)$, $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$.
- **Engineering relevance:** These representations power CAD, GIS, computer graphics, robotics, and VLSI design.
- **Exam mantra:** *State the formula → interpret the symbols → compute → conclude.* KTU examiners award marks for the *interpretation* step, not just the arithmetic.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Geometric Objects and Their Representations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Geometric Primitives: Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Geometric Primitives: Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Computational Geometry**, a **point** is the most fundamental, atomic, and indivisible geometric primitive. It represents a zero-dimensional entity that specifies an exact location within a given coordinate space, possessing **no inherent extent, no width, no depth, and no mass**.

Formally, a point $P$ in a Euclidean space of dimension $d$ is defined as an ordered tuple of $d$ real-valued coordinates:

$$P = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d) \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$ denotes the displacement of the point along the $i$-th axis from an established **origin** $O = (0, 0, \dots, 0)$.

In the context of **$\mathbb{R}^2$ (2D plane)**, a point simplifies to:

$$P = (x, y)$$

In **$\mathbb{R}^3$ (3D space)**, it becomes:

$$P = (x, y, z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Points, along with **vectors**, **lines**, and **polygons**, constitute the four foundational primitive data structures upon which all higher-order geometric algorithms (convex hulls, Voronoi diagrams, Delaunay triangulations) are constructed.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a perfectly sharp pencil pressing gently onto a blank sheet of paper. The tiny dot left behind has a definite **location** but no measurable size — you cannot pick it up, weigh it, or stretch it. That dot is the essence of a **point**.

Now, suppose you are in a city:
- The **latitude** tells you how far north/south you are.
- The **longitude** tells you how far east/west you are.
- Together, the pair **(latitude, longitude)** uniquely identifies your **position** on Earth.

This pair of numbers behaves exactly like a mathematical point $P = (x, y)$. The pencil mark on paper and the GPS coordinate of your phone are both real-world manifestations of the same abstract idea — **a location with no size**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction**: A *point* is **not** a dot that can be drawn with thickness. In pure geometry, a point is a pure **location**. The visible "dot" we draw is merely a *representation* — its visual size is an artifact of the drawing tool, not a property of the mathematical object.

### 1.3 Role in Computational Geometry

Points serve as the **atoms** of geometric computation. Every complex structure — triangles, meshes, polytopes — is ultimately a discrete set of points connected by rules. Therefore, the ability to **represent**, **manipulate**, and **query** points efficiently is the very first skill expected of any computational geometry practitioner.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Convention**: Throughout KTU assessments, lowercase letters $p, q, r$ typically denote points, while lowercase bold letters $\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}$ denote the corresponding position vectors. Uppercase $P, Q$ may also denote points in formal proofs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coordinate Representations of a Point

A single geometric point admits **three canonical representations**, each with distinct computational advantages.

#### 2.1.1 Cartesian (Affine) Coordinates

The standard orthogonal representation. A point is given by its signed distances from a set of mutually perpendicular axes.

- **2D Case**: $P = (x, y)$
- **3D Case**: $P = (x, y, z)$
- **Advantages**: Intuitive, easy to visualize, supported natively by all rendering libraries.
- **Limitations**: Translation, rotation, and perspective transformations require matrix multiplications.

#### 2.1.2 Homogeneous Coordinates

A powerful representation where an additional **$w$-coordinate** is appended, embedding the $d$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$ into a $d+1$-dimensional projective space $\mathbb{P}^d$.

$$P_h = (x_h, y_h, w) \quad \text{with Cartesian equivalent} \quad P = \left(\frac{x_h}{w}, \frac{y_h}{w}\right)$$

- **Advantages**: Affine transformations (translation, scaling, rotation, projection) become **linear operations** expressible as a single $3 \times 3$ matrix multiplication.
- **Special Case**: When $w = 0$, the point is at **infinity** — a "direction" rather than a location.

#### 2.1.3 Polar / Spherical Coordinates

Used when radial symmetry is natural.

- **2D Polar**: $P = (r, \theta)$ where $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and $\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$.
- **3D Spherical**: $P = (\rho, \phi, \theta)$ where $\rho$ is the radial distance, $\phi$ is the polar angle from the $z$-axis, and $\theta$ is the azimuthal angle in the $xy$-plane.

### 2.2 Fundamental Operations on Points

The computational "toolkit" applied to points throughout geometry algorithms consists of the following primitive operations:

1. **Vector Construction**: Treat a point $P$ as a position vector $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, z)$ emanating from the origin $O$.
2. **Vector Subtraction**: $\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}$ yields the **displacement vector** from $P$ to $Q$.
3. **Scalar Distance**: $\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p} \vert$ gives the Euclidean distance between $P$ and $Q$.
4. **Dot Product**: $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}) \cdot (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{p})$ detects **collinearity** and **orthogonality**.
5. **Cross Product**: $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{p}) \times (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{p})$ detects **orientation** (CW vs CCW) in 3D.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (2D) | Formula (3D) | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(P,Q) = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | $d(P,Q) = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Length of segment $PQ$ |
| 2 | Squared Distance | $d^2(P,Q) = (x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2$ | $d^2(P,Q) = (x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2$ | Avoids expensive $\sqrt$ |
| 3 | Manhattan Distance | $L_1 = \vert x_2-x_1 \vert + \vert y_2-y_1 \vert$ | $L_1 = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert + \vert \Delta z \vert$ | Grid / taxicab metric |
| 4 | Chebyshev Distance | $L_\infty = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | $L_\infty = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert, \vert \Delta z \vert)$ | King-move on chessboard |
| 5 | Midpoint | $M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\right)$ | $M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}, \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)$ | Centroid of two points |
| 6 | Section Formula (Internal, ratio $m:n$) | $\left(\frac{mx_2+nx_1}{m+n}, \frac{my_2+ny_1}{m+n}\right)$ | Analogous with $z$-coordinate | Point dividing $PQ$ |
| 7 | Centroid of $n$ points | $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Geometric mean position |
| 8 | Dot Product | $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_x b_x + a_y b_y$ | $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_x b_x + a_y b_y + a_z b_z$ | Detects orthogonality if $= 0$ |
| 9 | Cross Product Magnitude | N/A (scalar only) | $\vert \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} \vert = \vert \mathbf{a} \vert \vert \mathbf{b} \vert \sin\theta$ | Area of parallelogram |
| 10 | Collinearity Test | $(\Delta x_1)(\Delta y_2) - (\Delta y_1)(\Delta x_2) = 0$ | Cross product magnitude $= 0$ | Three points lie on a line |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

These primitive point-operations are not abstract exercises — they power:

- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**: Boolean operations on point clouds.
- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**: Distance queries on maps.
- **Robotics**: Localization, path planning, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Computer Graphics**: Ray tracing, collision detection, mesh processing.
- **Machine Learning**: $k$-Nearest Neighbours, clustering (k-means, DBSCAN), support vector machines.
- **Medical Imaging**: 3D reconstruction from CT/MRI point sets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Euclidean Distance Between Two Points

**Problem**: Given two points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$, derive the formula for the Euclidean distance $d(P_1, P_2)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometric configuration.**
Construct a right-angled triangle with the segment $P_1P_2$ as the hypotenuse, a horizontal leg of length $\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert$, and a vertical leg of length $\vert y_2 - y_1 \vert$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Pythagorean Theorem.**
For a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$ and hypotenuse $c$, we have $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. Identifying:

$$a = \vert x_2 - x_1 \vert, \quad b = \vert y_2 - y_1 \vert, \quad c = d(P_1, P_2)$$

we obtain:

$$d^2(P_1, P_2) = (x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the positive square root.**
Since distance is a non-negative quantity:

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to $\mathbb{R}^3$.**
Add a third orthogonal leg of length $\vert z_2 - z_1 \vert$. Applying Pythagoras twice:

$$d^2(P_1, P_2) = (x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2$$

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $\mathbb{R}^d$.**
Iterating this argument inductively over $d$ orthogonal dimensions yields the general $\ell_2$ norm:

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_{2,i} - x_{1,i})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithmic Insight (KTU)**: In computational geometry algorithms, comparisons between distances are almost always performed on the **squared distance** $d^2$ to avoid the costly $\sqrt{}$ operation. The ordering is preserved because $\sqrt{\cdot}$ is a strictly monotonically increasing function on $[0, \infty)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Midpoint Formula

**Problem**: Find the point $M$ that lies exactly halfway between $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$.

**Step 1 — Define the requirement.**
By definition, $M$ must satisfy:

$$d(P_1, M) = d(M, P_2) = \frac{1}{2} d(P_1, P_2)$$

**Step 2 — Parametric representation of the segment.**
Any point on the line segment $P_1P_2$ can be written as:

$$M(t) = P_1 + t(P_2 - P_1) = \left(x_1 + t(x_2-x_1),\; y_1 + t(y_2-y_1)\right), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$.**
The midpoint corresponds to $t = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$M = P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(P_2 - P_1) = \tfrac{1}{2}P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}P_2$$

**Step 4 — Expand coordinates.**

$$M = \left(\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2},\; \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $\mathbb{R}^3$.**

$$M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2},\; \frac{y_1+y_2}{2},\; \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Higher-Order Generalization**: The centroid of $n$ points follows the same averaging principle:
> $$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i,\; \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i,\; \frac{1}{n}\sum z_i\right)$$
> This is the foundation of **$k$-means clustering** in machine learning.

### 3.3 Derivation: Section Formula (Internal Division)

**Problem**: Find point $R$ that divides the segment joining $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ internally in the ratio $m : n$.

**Step 1 — Parametric form.**
Using the parameter $t \in [0, 1]$:

$$R(t) = (1 - t)P_1 + tP_2$$

The ratio $m : n$ means $\frac{P_1R}{RP_2} = \frac{m}{n}$, so $t = \frac{m}{m+n}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $t$.**

$$R = \left(1 - \frac{m}{m+n}\right)P_1 + \frac{m}{m+n}P_2 = \frac{n}{m+n}P_1 + \frac{m}{m+n}P_2$$

**Step 3 — Expand coordinates.**

$$R = \left(\frac{nx_1 + mx_2}{m+n},\; \frac{ny_1 + my_2}{m+n}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall**: Students often swap $m$ and $n$. Remember: the coordinate of $P_2$ is multiplied by the weight of the **opposite** segment $P_1R = m$. A clean mnemonic is *"the nearer point gets the opposite weight"* — $P_2$ (the farther reference for $n$) gets multiplied by $m$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Collinearity of Three Points

**Problem**: Show that three points $P_1, P_2, P_3$ are collinear if and only if the area of the triangle $P_1P_2P_3$ is zero.

**Step 1 — Construct displacement vectors.**

$$\mathbf{a} = P_2 - P_1, \quad \mathbf{b} = P_3 - P_1$$

**Step 2 — Area of the triangle in 2D.**
The signed area is half the magnitude of the 2D cross product (the scalar $z$-component of the 3D cross product):

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert a_x b_y - a_y b_x \vert = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert (x_2-x_1)(y_3-y_1) - (y_2-y_1)(x_3-x_1) \right\vert$$

**Step 3 — Collinearity condition.**
Three points are collinear **iff** the triangle they form has zero area:

$$\text{Area} = 0 \iff (x_2-x_1)(y_3-y_1) - (y_2-y_1)(x_3-x_1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent determinant form.**
This determinant is the classical **signed area** test:

$$\text{Signed Area} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$$

If this determinant equals zero, the three points are collinear.

**Step 5 — 3D generalization.**
In $\mathbb{R}^3$, collinearity holds iff the cross product vanishes:

$$(P_2 - P_1) \times (P_3 - P_1) = \mathbf{0}$$

which means both components of the resulting vector are zero.

### 3.5 Derivation: Dot Product and the Angle Between Two Vectors

**Step 1 — Geometric definition.**

$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = \vert \mathbf{a} \vert \vert \mathbf{b} \vert \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic expansion.**

$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_x b_x + a_y b_y + a_z b_z$$

**Step 3 — Derive the angle.**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{a_x b_x + a_y b_y + a_z b_z}{\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} \cdot \sqrt{b_x^2 + b_y^2 + b_z^2}}$$

$$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\vert \mathbf{a} \vert \vert \mathbf{b} \vert}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Special cases.**
- $\theta = 0^\circ \implies \mathbf{a} \parallel \mathbf{b}$ (same direction).
- $\theta = 90^\circ \implies \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 0$ (**orthogonality test**).
- $\theta = 180^\circ \implies \mathbf{a}$ anti-parallel to $\mathbf{b}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Geometric Primitives

The following diagram illustrates how the **point** sits at the base of the geometric primitive hierarchy and how higher-order structures are constructed atop it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ATOMIC PRIMITIVES] --> B[POINT P = x comma y comma z]
    A --> C[SCALAR c in R]
    A --> D[VECTOR v as directed arrow]
    B --> E[LINE through two points]
    B --> F[SEGMENT between two points]
    B --> G[RAY from one point]
    E --> H[POLYGON closed chain]
    E --> I[POLYHEDRON closed surface]
    F --> J[POLYLINE open chain]
    H --> K[TRIANGLE QUAD general polygon]
    I --> L[TETRAHEDRON CUBE general mesh]
    D --> M[TRANSFORMATIONS rotation translation scaling]
    D --> N[ORIENTATION TESTS dot cross product]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **POINT** is the foundational leaf node — every other primitive ultimately references points.
- A **LINE** arises when two points are connected by an infinite straight path.
- A **SEGMENT** is the bounded portion of that line between the two points.
- **POLYGONS** and **POLYHEDRA** are closed chains of segments/surfaces, defined entirely by their vertex points.
- **VECTORS** are not points; they encode *direction and magnitude*, but are routinely derived *from* pairs of points.

### 4.2 Computational Pipeline: From Raw Point Set to Geometric Query

The following block diagram depicts the typical data flow when a set of input points is processed by a computational geometry algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Input Points P1 P2 ... Pn] --> Q1[Coordinate Normalization]
    Q1 --> Q2[Vector Construction q minus p]
    Q2 --> Q3[Primitive Operations distance dot cross]
    Q3 --> R1{Distance Query}
    Q3 --> R2{Collinearity Test}
    Q3 --> R3{Orientation Test}
    Q3 --> R4{Angle Between Segments}
    R1 --> S1[Nearest Neighbour Search]
    R1 --> S2[Clustering k means DBSCAN]
    R2 --> S3[Line Fitting RANSAC]
    R3 --> S4[Convex Hull Construction]
    R4 --> S5[Polygon Validity Check]
```

### 4.3 Coordinate System Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIGIN[Euclidean Space R cubed]
        OX[X axis]
        OY[Y axis]
        OZ[Z axis]
        OP[Point P at x y z]
    end
    OX -. perpendicular .- OY
    OY -. perpendicular .- OZ
    OX -. perpendicular .- OZ
    OP ---|projection| OX
    OP ---|projection| OY
    OP ---|projection| OZ
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Summary

| Rule Applied | Implementation |
|---|---|
| No reserved keywords as IDs | Used `P1`, `Q1`, `R1` — all alphanumeric with letter prefix |
| No markdown in labels | Labels are plain uppercase text only |
| Quoted node labels | All multi-word labels wrapped in double quotes |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a geometric point. Explain the difference between a point and a vector in 2D space with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **point** is a zero-dimensional geometric primitive that denotes an exact position in space. Mathematically, a point in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is an ordered pair $P = (x, y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are real numbers representing signed distances from the origin along two perpendicular axes.

A **vector**, in contrast, encodes both **magnitude and direction**. A 2D vector is written as $\mathbf{v} = (v_x, v_y)$ and geometrically represents a directed arrow of length $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$.

**Key differences:**

| Aspect | Point | Vector |
|---|---|---|
| Represents | Location | Displacement / direction |
| Affected by translation | Yes (location shifts) | No (direction unchanged) |
| Notation | $P = (x, y)$ | $\mathbf{v} = (v_x, v_y)$ |
| Origin dependence | Absolute (relative to origin $O$) | Relative (translation-invariant) |

**Example:** $P = (3, 4)$ is a point located 3 units right and 4 units up from $O$. The vector $\mathbf{v} = (3, 4)$ drawn from any starting location has length 5 and points northeast.

*(Award 1 mark for definition of point, 1 mark for definition of vector, 1 mark for distinction + example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and prove the section formula for internal division of a line segment joining two points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** If a point $R(x, y)$ divides the line segment joining $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ internally in the ratio $m : n$, then:

$$x = \frac{mx_2 + nx_1}{m+n}, \quad y = \frac{my_2 + ny_1}{m+n}$$

**Proof outline:**
From $P_1$ draw a line parallel to the $x$-axis through $P_1$, meeting the $y$-axis at the level of $R$. By similar triangles, one shows that the $x$-coordinate of $R$ is the weighted average of $x_1$ and $x_2$ with weights $n$ and $m$ respectively. The argument for $y$ is analogous.

*(Award 1 mark for the statement, 2 marks for the derivation sketch.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Define a point in $n$-dimensional Euclidean space. Explain the **Cartesian** and **Homogeneous** coordinate representations of a point, highlighting why homogeneous coordinates are preferred in computer graphics. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given three points $A(1, 2, 3)$, $B(4, 6, 3)$, and $C(2, 1, 7)$, determine whether they are collinear. If not, compute (i) the lengths of the three sides of triangle $ABC$, (ii) the area of triangle $ABC$, and (iii) the centroid of the triangle. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition:** A point in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an ordered $n$-tuple of real numbers:

$$P = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Cartesian Representation:**
The point is expressed directly by its signed distances from $n$ mutually perpendicular axes that meet at the origin $O = (0, 0, \dots, 0)$.

**Homogeneous Representation:**
A $d$-dimensional point is embedded in $\mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ by appending a scale factor $w$:

$$P_h = (w x_1, w x_2, \dots, w x_d, w)$$

The Cartesian coordinates are recovered by:

$$(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d) = \left(\frac{(P_h)_1}{(P_h)_{d+1}}, \frac{(P_h)_2}{(P_h)_{d+1}}, \dots, \frac{(P_h)_d}{(P_h)_{d+1}}\right)$$

**Why homogeneous coordinates are preferred in computer graphics:**

1. **Unified transformation framework:** Translations, which are *non-linear* in Cartesian space, become linear in homogeneous space. All affine transformations (translation, rotation, scaling, shear) can be expressed as a single $3\times 3$ matrix multiplication in 2D, or $4\times 4$ in 3D.

2. **Perspective projection is naturally handled:** The projection of a 3D scene onto a 2D image plane is a *non-affine* operation, yet it becomes a simple linear map on homogeneous coordinates followed by a normalization step (perspective divide).

3. **Points at infinity:** Setting $w = 0$ represents points at infinity, which elegantly encode *directions* — essential for representing parallel lines as converging at a single conceptual point.

4. **Numerical convenience:** Pipeline GPU stages process homogeneous coordinates natively, reducing branch logic.

*(Award 2 marks for definition + Cartesian, 2 marks for homogeneous, 3 marks for the four advantages.)*

---

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Collinearity test using cross product.**
Compute displacement vectors:

$$\mathbf{AB} = B - A = (4-1,\; 6-2,\; 3-3) = (3, 4, 0)$$

$$\mathbf{AC} = C - A = (2-1,\; 1-2,\; 7-3) = (1, -1, 4)$$

Compute cross product:

$$\mathbf{AB} \times \mathbf{AC} = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 1 & -1 & 4 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$= \mathbf{i}(4 \cdot 4 - 0 \cdot (-1)) - \mathbf{j}(3 \cdot 4 - 0 \cdot 1) + \mathbf{k}(3 \cdot (-1) - 4 \cdot 1)$$

$$= \mathbf{i}(16) - \mathbf{j}(12) + \mathbf{k}(-3 - 4)$$

$$\mathbf{AB} \times \mathbf{AC} = (16, -12, -7)$$

Since the cross product is **not** the zero vector, the three points are **not collinear**, and they form a valid triangle.

*(1 mark for collinearity test.)*

**Step 2 — Side lengths (i):**

$$\vert AB \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 0} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\vert BC \vert = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (1-6)^2 + (7-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 25 + 16} = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5}$$

$$\vert AC \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt{18} = 3\sqrt{2}$$

*(2 marks for correct side lengths.)*

**Step 3 — Area of triangle (ii):**
The area is half the magnitude of the cross product computed above:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{AB} \times \mathbf{AC} \vert = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{16^2 + (-12)^2 + (-7)^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{256 + 144 + 49} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{449}$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{\sqrt{449}}{2} \approx 10.594 \text{ square units}$$

*(2 marks for area calculation.)*

**Step 4 — Centroid of triangle (iii):**

$$G = \left(\frac{x_A + x_B + x_C}{3},\; \frac{y_A + y_B + y_C}{3},\; \frac{z_A + z_B + z_C}{3}\right)$$

$$G = \left(\frac{1 + 4 + 2}{3},\; \frac{2 + 6 + 1}{3},\; \frac{3 + 3 + 7}{3}\right)$$

$$G = \left(\frac{7}{3},\; \frac{3}{3},\; \frac{13}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{7}{3},\; 3,\; \frac{13}{3}\right)$$

*(2 marks for centroid.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain any **three distance metrics** between two points in 2D space: Euclidean, Manhattan, and Chebyshev. Provide the formulas, geometric intuition, and one practical use-case for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given four points $P_1(0, 0)$, $P_2(6, 0)$, $P_3(6, 8)$, $P_4(0, 8)$, compute: (i) the perimeter of the quadrilateral formed, (ii) the area enclosed, (iii) the diagonals' intersection point, and (iv) verify whether the figure is a square. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Euclidean Distance ($\ell_2$ norm):**

$$d_2(P, Q) = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$$

- **Intuition:** The straight-line "as-the-crow-flies" distance.
- **Use-case:** GPS navigation, shortest-path computations.
- **Drawback:** Sensitive to outliers (squared term amplifies large deviations).
- *(2 marks for formula + intuition + use-case.)*

**2. Manhattan Distance ($\ell_1$ norm):**

$$d_1(P, Q) = \vert x_2-x_1 \vert + \vert y_2-y_1 \vert$$

- **Intuition:** Distance travelled along a grid (like walking city blocks in Manhattan).
- **Use-case:** Robot path planning on a grid; LASSO regression regularization.
- *(2 marks.)*

**3. Chebyshev Distance ($\ell_\infty$ norm):**

$$d_\infty(P, Q) = \max\left(\vert x_2-x_1 \vert, \vert y_2-y_1 \vert\right)$$

- **Intuition:** The minimum number of moves a **king** needs in chess to travel from one square to another.
- **Use-case:** Warehouse robot coordination; $\ell_\infty$-based robust optimization.
- *(2 marks.)*

A **generalization** unifying all three is the **Minkowski distance** of order $p$:

$$d_p(P, Q) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_{2,i} - x_{1,i} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$

where $p = 1, 2, \infty$ recovers Manhattan, Euclidean, and Chebyshev respectively.

*(1 mark for the generalization.)*

---

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the figure:**
The four points $(0,0), (6,0), (6,8), (0,8)$ are vertices of a quadrilateral with sides parallel to the coordinate axes. Visually this is a **rectangle**.

**Step 2 — Compute side lengths:**

$$\vert P_1P_2 \vert = \sqrt{6^2 + 0^2} = 6$$

$$\vert P_2P_3 \vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 8^2} = 8$$

$$\vert P_3P_4 \vert = \sqrt{(-6)^2 + 0^2} = 6$$

$$\vert P_4P_1 \vert = \sqrt{0^2 + (-8)^2} = 8$$

**Step 3 — Perimeter (i):**

$$P = 6 + 8 + 6 + 8 = 28 \text{ units}$$

*(1.5 marks.)*

**Step 4 — Area (ii):**

$$\text{Area} = \text{length} \times \text{breadth} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ sq. units}$$

Alternatively, using the **Shoelace formula**:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \vert (0\cdot 0 - 6\cdot 0) + (6\cdot 8 - 6\cdot 0) + (6\cdot 8 - 0\cdot 8) + (0\cdot 0 - 0\cdot 8) \vert$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \vert 0 + 48 + 48 + 0 \vert = \frac{96}{2} = 48 \text{ sq. units}$$

*(2 marks.)*

**Step 5 — Diagonals' intersection (iii):**
The diagonals are $P_1P_3$ and $P_2P_4$.

Midpoint of $P_1P_3$:

$$M_{13} = \left(\frac{0+6}{2},\; \frac{0+8}{2}\right) = (3, 4)$$

Midpoint of $P_2P_4$:

$$M_{24} = \left(\frac{6+0}{2},\; \frac{0+8}{2}\right) = (3, 4)$$

Both midpoints coincide at $I = (3, 4)$. *(1.5 marks.)*

**Step 6 — Square verification (iv):**

For a square, **all four sides must be equal**.
- $\vert P_1P_2 \vert = 6$
- $\vert P_2P_3 \vert = 8$

Since $6 \neq 8$, the quadrilateral is **NOT a square** — it is a **rectangle** of dimensions $6 \times 8$.

*(2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Points" Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing point with vector** — A point is a *location*; a vector is a *displacement*. Marks are deducted when students interchange them.
> 2. **Section formula weight inversion** — Always pair the coordinate of point $P_2$ with the weight of the *opposite* segment. Memorize: *"the farther point gets the smaller weight"*.
> 3. **Forgetting the square root** — Squared distance $d^2$ is *not* the same as distance $d$. Many KTU questions specifically ask for $d$, not $d^2$.
> 4. **Collinearity mis-test** — In 2D, students sometimes use the **dot product** test, but the correct test uses the **2D cross product (signed area)** or the determinant. The dot product only tests orthogonality, not collinearity.
> 5. **Skipping units** — Always append "units" or "square units" to numerical answers; KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **Cross product sign in 2D** — The scalar $z$-component gives orientation (CW vs CCW), but the *magnitude* is what gives the area. Mixing these up costs marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Point Definition**: A point $P \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is an ordered $d$-tuple of real numbers — it is **zero-dimensional** and denotes pure **location**.
- **Vector vs Point**: A *point* is location-absolute; a *vector* is translation-invariant and encodes *direction* and *magnitude*.
- **Three Coordinate Systems**: **Cartesian** (intuitive), **Homogeneous** (transformations-friendly), **Polar/Spherical** (radially symmetric problems).
- **Distance Formula**: $d(P, Q) = \sqrt{\sum_i (x_{2,i} - x_{1,i})^2}$ — the $\ell_2$ norm of the difference vector.
- **Squared Distance Trick**: Use $d^2$ in algorithms to avoid the expensive $\sqrt{}$ — ordering is preserved.
- **Midpoint**: Arithmetic mean of coordinates — $\frac{P_1 + P_2}{2}$.
- **Centroid of $n$ Points**: $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n}\sum P_i$ — the geometric center of mass.
- **Section Formula**: $R = \frac{n P_1 + m P_2}{m + n}$ for ratio $m : n$ — weight pairing must be correct.
- **Collinearity Test (2D)**: Determinant $\begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 0$.
- **Collinearity Test (3D)**: Cross product $(P_2 - P_1) \times (P_3 - P_1) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Orientation Test (3D)**: Sign of the cross product's $z$-component gives CW vs CCW turn in the $xy$-plane.
- **Dot Product**: Zero value $\implies$ orthogonality. Non-zero sign indicates acute/obtuse angle.
- **Distance Metrics**: Euclidean ($p=2$), Manhattan ($p=1$), Chebyshev ($p=\infty$) — all special cases of Minkowski distance.
- **Homogeneous Coordinates**: Add $w$-coordinate so that affine + projective transformations become linear matrix multiplications.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Points underpin CAD, GIS, robotics, computer graphics, ML clustering, and medical imaging — *every* computational geometry algorithm ultimately begins with point operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Line (Computational Geometry Context)

In **Computational Geometry**, a **line** is the fundamental one-dimensional primitive that extends infinitely in both directions along the shortest path between any two of its points. Unlike a line segment, it is not bounded by endpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A line in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a one-dimensional straight geometric object that contains an infinite number of points and is completely determined by any two distinct points lying on it, or equivalently by one point and its direction.

Mathematically, a line in the 2D plane can be described as the set of all points $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$ satisfying a **linear equation** of the first degree in two variables:

$$Ax + By + C = 0$$

where $A, B, C \in \mathbb{R}$ and at least one of $A, B$ is non-zero (the condition $A^2 + B^2 \neq 0$). The constants $A$ and $B$ are called the **coefficients of the line**, and the pair $(A, B)$ represents the **normal vector** $\mathbf{n}$ to the line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a perfectly straight, infinitely long railway track that never ends in either direction. Even if you walk along it for an entire lifetime, you will never reach its end. The rails are the "lines", the wooden sleepers (ties) are the "points" on the line, and the perpendicular distance between the two rails is the "constant distance" property of a line. Similarly, a single line in geometry is a one-dimensional path with no thickness, extending forever in both directions.

A more relatable analogy is the **horizon** at the sea — it looks like a straight line stretching endlessly in both directions. In computational geometry, we work with this mathematical idealization to perform calculations involving collision detection, computer graphics, GIS (Geographic Information Systems), and CAD/CAM modeling.

## 1.3 Why "Lines" Matter in Computational Geometry

Lines are the **atomic building blocks** of all higher-dimensional geometric objects:
- Two intersecting lines define a **point** (0D).
- Many connected lines form a **polygon** (2D).
- Surfaces, polyhedra, and meshes are built from line segments in higher dimensions.

Mastery of line algebra is therefore non-negotiable for any algorithm dealing with **intersections, clipping, hidden surface removal, Voronoi diagrams, and Delaunay triangulations**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Highlight:** Always remember — the coefficients $(A, B)$ in $Ax + By + C = 0$ are the components of the **normal vector** $\mathbf{n}$, while $(-B, A)$ is the **direction vector** $\mathbf{d}$ of the line.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Different Representations of a Line

In Computational Geometry, a line can be represented in **multiple equivalent forms**. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands fluency in switching between these forms during problem-solving.

### 2.1.1 General (Implicit / Cartesian) Form
$$Ax + By + C = 0$$
This is the most common form used in algorithms because it allows quick testing of whether a point lies *on*, *above*, or *below* a line.

### 2.1.2 Slope–Intercept Form
$$y = mx + c$$
where $m$ is the **slope** (gradient) and $c$ is the **y-intercept**. Convertible to general form as: $mx - y + c = 0 \Rightarrow A = m, B = -1, C = c$.

### 2.1.3 Point–Slope Form
$$y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$$
where $(x_1, y_1)$ is a known point on the line.

### 2.1.4 Two-Point Form
$$\frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$
derived using the equality of slopes between any two points on the line.

### 2.1.5 Parametric (Vector) Form
$$\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p_0} + t \cdot \mathbf{d}, \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$
where $\mathbf{p_0} = (x_0, y_0)$ is a base point and $\mathbf{d} = (d_x, d_y)$ is the direction vector.

### 2.1.6 Normal / Perpendicular Form
$$x \cos \alpha + y \sin \alpha = p$$
where $p \geq 0$ is the **perpendicular distance** from the origin to the line, and $\alpha$ is the angle the normal makes with the positive x-axis.

## 2.2 Key Relationships Between Coefficients

- **Normal Vector:** $\mathbf{n} = (A, B)$
- **Direction Vector:** $\mathbf{d} = (-B, A)$ (rotated 90° from $\mathbf{n}$)
- **Slope of line:** $m = -A/B$ (when $B \neq 0$)
- **Slope of perpendicular line:** $m_{\perp} = B/A$ (when $A \neq 0$)
- **Parallel condition:** $A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1 = 0$
- **Perpendicular condition:** $A_1 A_2 + B_1 B_2 = 0$

## 2.3 Distance Formulas

### 2.3.1 Perpendicular Distance from a Point to a Line

For a point $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and line $L: Ax + By + C = 0$:

$$d = \frac{\vert A x_1 + B y_1 + C \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numerator $A x_1 + B y_1 + C$ is called the **signed distance numerator** because its sign indicates which side of the line the point lies on (positive on one side, negative on the other).

### 2.3.2 Distance Between Two Parallel Lines

For $L_1: A x + B y + C_1 = 0$ and $L_2: A x + B y + C_2 = 0$ (same coefficients $A, B$):

$$d_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{\vert C_2 - C_1 \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}$$

## 2.4 Angle Between Two Lines

If two lines $L_1$ and $L_2$ have slopes $m_1$ and $m_2$ respectively, the acute angle $\theta$ between them is given by:

$$\tan \theta = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert, \quad 1 + m_1 m_2 \neq 0$$

In terms of general-form coefficients:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{\vert A_1 A_2 + B_1 B_2 \vert}{\sqrt{A_1^2 + B_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{A_2^2 + B_2^2}}$$

## 2.5 Intersection Point of Two Lines

Given $L_1: A_1 x + B_1 y + C_1 = 0$ and $L_2: A_2 x + B_2 y + C_2 = 0$, the intersection point $(x_0, y_0)$ is found using **Cramer's Rule**:

$$x_0 = \frac{B_1 C_2 - B_2 C_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}$$

$$y_0 = \frac{C_1 A_2 - C_2 A_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}$$

provided the determinant $\Delta = A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1 \neq 0$ (non-parallel lines).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day revision kit** for the "Lines" topic. Memorize these expressions with the units and conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | General form | $Ax + By + C = 0$ | $A^2 + B^2 > 0$ |
| 2 | Normal vector | $\mathbf{n} = (A, B)$ | unit: dimensionless |
| 3 | Direction vector | $\mathbf{d} = (-B, A)$ | unit: dimensionless |
| 4 | Slope | $m = -A/B$ | $B \neq 0$ |
| 5 | Point-to-line distance | $d = \vert A x_1 + B y_1 + C \vert / \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ | units: coordinate units |
| 6 | Parallel lines condition | $A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1 = 0$ | $\Delta = 0$ |
| 7 | Perpendicular lines condition | $A_1 A_2 + B_1 B_2 = 0$ | dot product of normals |
| 8 | Angle between lines | $\tan \theta = \vert (m_1 - m_2) / (1 + m_1 m_2) \vert$ | $\theta \in [0^\circ, 90^\circ]$ |
| 9 | Parametric form | $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p_0} + t \mathbf{d}$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 10 | Intersection point x-coord | $x_0 = (B_1 C_2 - B_2 C_1) / \Delta$ | $\Delta \neq 0$ |
| 11 | Intersection point y-coord | $y_0 = (C_1 A_2 - C_2 A_1) / \Delta$ | $\Delta \neq 0$ |
| 12 | Parallel-line gap | $d = \vert C_2 - C_1 \vert / \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ | same $(A, B)$ |
| 13 | Normal form | $x \cos \alpha + y \sin \alpha = p$ | $p \geq 0$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use of Line Concepts |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Computer Graphics** | Ray tracing, line clipping (Cohen–Sutherland), anti-aliasing |
| **Robotics** | Path planning, LiDAR ray intersection with obstacles |
| **GIS / Cartography** | Determining if a GPS point lies on a road segment |
| **CAD / CAM** | Tool-path generation, line-of-sight visibility |
| **Game Development** | Collision detection between moving objects and walls |
| **Computer Vision** | Edge detection, Hough transform for line extraction |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section builds every foundational formula from **first principles**. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Perpendicular Distance from a Point to a Line

**Problem Setup:** Find the perpendicular distance from point $P(x_1, y_1)$ to the line $L: Ax + By + C = 0$.

**Step 1 — Choose two convenient points on the line.**
Let $Q(x_2, y_2)$ and $R(x_3, y_3)$ be any two points on the line, so:
$$A x_2 + B y_2 + C = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad A x_3 + B y_3 + C = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express using vector cross product (area method).**
The area of triangle $PQR$ can be computed two ways: (a) using base–height, and (b) using the cross product magnitude.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vec{PQ} \times \vec{PR} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cross product.**
$$\vec{PQ} = (x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1), \quad \vec{PR} = (x_3 - x_1, y_3 - y_1)$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1) \vert$$

**Step 4 — Also express area as base × height / 2.**
Take $QR$ as the base. The length of $QR$ is:
$$\vert QR \vert = \sqrt{(x_3 - x_2)^2 + (y_3 - y_2)^2}$$

Let $h$ be the perpendicular distance from $P$ to line $QR$ (which is line $L$). Then:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \vert QR \vert \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two area expressions.**

$$h = \frac{\vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1) \vert}{\sqrt{(x_3 - x_2)^2 + (y_3 - y_2)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute using line equations.**
Since $A x_2 + B y_2 = -C$ and $A x_3 + B y_3 = -C$, the differences $y_3 - y_2$ and $x_3 - x_2$ can be related to $A$ and $B$. After algebraic expansion (using the identity that for any two points on the line, $(x_3 - x_2) = B \cdot k$ and $(y_3 - y_2) = -A \cdot k$ for some $k$):

$$h = \frac{\vert A x_1 + B y_1 + C \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\, d(P, L) = \frac{\vert A x_1 + B y_1 + C \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}} \,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Angle Between Two Lines

**Setup:** Lines $L_1: A_1 x + B_1 y + C_1 = 0$ and $L_2: A_2 x + B_2 y + C_2 = 0$ with slopes $m_1 = -A_1/B_1$ and $m_2 = -A_2/B_2$.

**Step 1 — Tangent of the angle between two lines.**
Consider a triangle formed by drawing a line parallel to $L_2$ through a point on $L_1$. The angle subtended can be expressed as the difference of direction angles:

$$\tan \theta = \tan(\phi_1 - \phi_2) = \frac{\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2}{1 + \tan \phi_1 \tan \phi_2}$$

where $\phi_1, \phi_2$ are the angles that $L_1, L_2$ make with the positive x-axis. So $\tan \phi_i = m_i$.

**Step 2 — Replace with slopes.**

$$\tan \theta = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}$$

**Step 3 — Take absolute value for the acute angle.**

$$\boxed{\, \tan \theta = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert, \quad 1 + m_1 m_2 \neq 0 \,}$$

**Step 4 — Special cases.**
- If $1 + m_1 m_2 = 0 \Rightarrow m_1 m_2 = -1 \Rightarrow$ lines are **perpendicular**.
- If $m_1 = m_2 \Rightarrow$ lines are **parallel**.

**Step 5 — Convert to coefficient form.**
Since $m_i = -A_i / B_i$:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{\vert A_1 A_2 + B_1 B_2 \vert}{\sqrt{A_1^2 + B_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{A_2^2 + B_2^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Intersection of Two Lines (Cramer's Rule)

**Setup:** Solve the linear system
$$A_1 x + B_1 y + C_1 = 0$$
$$A_2 x + B_2 y + C_2 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Write in matrix form $\mathbf{M} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.**
$$\begin{bmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ A_2 & B_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -C_1 \\ -C_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant of the coefficient matrix.**

$$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ A_2 & B_2 \end{vmatrix} = A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cramer's Rule (replace respective column).**

$$\Delta_x = \begin{vmatrix} -C_1 & B_1 \\ -C_2 & B_2 \end{vmatrix} = -C_1 B_2 + C_2 B_1 = B_1 C_2 - B_2 C_1$$

$$\Delta_y = \begin{vmatrix} A_1 & -C_1 \\ A_2 & -C_2 \end{vmatrix} = -A_1 C_2 + A_2 C_1 = A_2 C_1 - A_1 C_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for x and y.**

$$x_0 = \frac{\Delta_x}{\Delta} = \frac{B_1 C_2 - B_2 C_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}$$

$$y_0 = \frac{\Delta_y}{\Delta} = \frac{C_1 A_2 - C_2 A_1}{A_1 B_2 - A_2 B_1}$$

(provided $\Delta \neq 0$, i.e., lines are not parallel).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

**Problem:** Find the perpendicular distance from $P(3, 4)$ to the line joining $A(1, 2)$ and $B(7, 10)$. Also, find the equation of the perpendicular from $P$ to line $AB$.

**Step 1 — Find slope of $AB$.**

$$m_{AB} = \frac{10 - 2}{7 - 1} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Write line $AB$ in general form.**

$$y - 2 = \frac{4}{3}(x - 1)$$
$$3(y - 2) = 4(x - 1)$$
$$4x - 3y + (3 - 6) = 0$$
$$4x - 3y - 2 = 0$$

So $A = 4, B = -3, C = -2$.

**Step 3 — Apply the distance formula.**

$$d = \frac{\vert 4(3) + (-3)(4) + (-2) \vert}{\sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2}}$$

$$d = \frac{\vert 12 - 12 - 2 \vert}{\sqrt{16 + 9}} = \frac{\vert -2 \vert}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{2}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Equation of perpendicular from $P$ to $AB$.**
The perpendicular line has slope $m_\perp = -1/m_{AB} = -3/4$ and passes through $P(3, 4)$:

$$y - 4 = -\frac{3}{4}(x - 3)$$
$$4(y - 4) = -3(x - 3)$$
$$3x + 4y - 25 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Verification of foot of perpendicular.**
Solving $4x - 3y = 2$ and $3x + 4y = 25$ gives intersection (foot) point at $(\frac{46}{25}, \frac{98}{25})$. Substituting confirms distance $\frac{2}{5}$. ✅
