## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> State the time and space complexity of Graham's Scan algorithm. What is the role of the cross product in this algorithm?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ — dominated by the polar-angle sorting step. The stack-based scan runs in $O(n)$ amortized time.
>
> **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ — for the stack and the sorted array of points.
>
> **Role of the Cross Product:** The cross product $Cross(A, B, C) = (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1)$ acts as the **turn-direction predicate**. Its sign indicates whether the path $A \to B \to C$ makes a left turn (positive), right turn (negative), or is collinear (zero). In Graham's Scan, if the cross product of the top two stack points and the new candidate point is $\le 0$, the middle point is popped (it is interior to the hull). This ensures only points forming strictly counter-clockwise turns are retained, producing a valid convex hull.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> Why is the point with minimum $y$-coordinate (and minimum $x$ in case of tie) chosen as the anchor in Graham's Scan? What happens if we choose an arbitrary point as anchor?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Why minimum-$y$ anchor:** The point with the minimum $y$-coordinate is guaranteed to lie on the convex hull (no other point can be vertically below it to exclude it). Choosing the leftmost among tied lowest points ensures a unique, deterministic anchor.
>
> **Mathematical utility:** All other points can then be measured by their **polar angle** from $p_0$ in the range $[0, 2\pi)$, and sorting by this angle produces a counter-clockwise traversal around the hull.
>
> **Consequence of arbitrary anchor:** If an arbitrary interior point is chosen, polar angles still sort the points, but the "turn" predicate (left vs. right) would not correspond to the hull's boundary direction. The stack invariant would break: an interior point might never be popped (it always satisfies a turn) and hull vertices might be incorrectly discarded. Hence the algorithm's correctness depends on the anchor being a hull vertex with the lowest polar reference.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using `Cross < 0` instead of `Cross ≤ 0` (or vice versa) without specifying the collinear-point policy. **Always state your convention explicitly** at the start of your answer.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the distance-based tie-breaking when multiple points share the same polar angle. This **costs 1–2 marks** in the sorting step.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Stating "$O(n^2)$" complexity because of the nested `while` loop. **Justify the amortized $O(n)$ bound** with a push/pop argument — this is a frequently tested nuance.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not explaining *why* the anchor point is on the convex hull. Examiners specifically test this geometric insight.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing Graham's Scan with Jarvis March (gift wrapping) in terms of complexity. Graham's Scan is $O(n \log n)$; Jarvis March is $O(nh)$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the Graham's Scan algorithm for computing the convex hull of a set of $n$ points in the plane. Clearly describe all three phases with their individual time complexities. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the set of points $\{(0,3), (1,1), (2,2), (4,4), (3,0), (0,0), (3,3), (5,1)\}$, trace Graham's Scan step-by-step using the cross product to determine turn directions. Show the contents of the stack at every iteration and state the final convex hull. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Graham's Scan computes the convex hull of $n$ points in three phases:

**Phase 1 — Anchor Selection (2 Marks):**
Find the point $p_0$ with the smallest $y$-coordinate. Break ties by choosing the smallest $x$. This point lies on the convex hull and serves as the polar reference.
- **Time:** $O(n)$ linear scan.

**Phase 2 — Polar Angle Sorting (2 Marks):**
For each remaining point $p_i$, compute the polar angle $\theta_i$ from $p_0$ using $\tan \theta_i = (y_i - y_0)/(x_i - x_0)$. Sort all points by $\theta_i$ in increasing order. For equal angles, retain only the farthest point.
- **Time:** $O(n \log n)$ dominated by sorting.

**Phase 3 — Stack-Based Construction (3 Marks):**
- Initialize an empty stack $\mathcal{S}$ and push $p_0$, $p_1$ (the first sorted point).
- For each next point $p_i$ (from $i = 2$ to $n-1$):
  - While $|\mathcal{S}| \ge 2$ and $Cross(\mathcal{S}_{top-1}, \mathcal{S}_{top}, p_i) \le 0$, pop $\mathcal{S}_{top}$.
  - Push $p_i$ onto $\mathcal{S}$.
- The final stack contains the convex hull vertices in counter-clockwise order.
- **Time:** $O(n)$ amortized (each point pushed and popped at most once).

**Total Time:** $O(n \log n) + O(n) + O(n) = O(n \log n)$.
**Space:** $O(n)$ for stack and sorted array.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Input Points:** $P = \{(0,3), (1,1), (2,2), (4,4), (3,0), (0,0), (3,3), (5,1)\}$

**Step 1 — Anchor Selection:**
Minimum $y$ is $0$, attained by $(0,0)$ and $(3,0)$. Tie-break by minimum $x$: choose $p_0 = (0,0)$.

**Step 2 — Polar Angle Computation:**

| Point | Coordinates | $\tan\theta = y/x$ | $\theta$ (degrees) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(0,3)$ | $A$ | $\infty$ | $90.00$ |
| $(1,1)$ | $B$ | $1.000$ | $45.00$ |
| $(2,2)$ | $C$ | $1.000$ | $45.00$ |
| $(4,4)$ | $D$ | $1.000$ | $45.00$ |
| $(3,0)$ | $E$ | $0$ | $0.00$ |
| $(3,3)$ | $F$ | $1.000$ | $45.00$ |
| $(5,1)$ | $G$ | $0.200$ | $11.31$ |

**Step 3 — Sort (with collinear tie-breaking):**
Points $B, C, D, F$ are collinear with $p_0$ at angle $45°$. Retain only the farthest, $D(4,4)$, and discard $B, C, F$.

**Sorted sequence:** $p_0=(0,0) \to E(3,0) \to G(5,1) \to D(4,4) \to A(0,3)$.

**Step 4 — Stack Processing:**

Initialize: $\mathcal{S} = [(0,0), (3,0)]$.

**Process $G(5,1)$:**
$Cross((0,0), (3,0), (5,1)) = (3-0)(1-0) - (0-0)(5-0) = 3 - 0 = 3 > 0$. Push $G$. $\mathcal{S} = [(0,0), (3,0), (5,1)]$.

**Process $D(4,4)$:**
$Cross((3,0), (5,1), (4,4)) = (5-3)(4-0) - (1-0)(4-3) = 8 - 1 = 7 > 0$. Push $D$. $\mathcal{S} = [(0,0), (3,0), (5,1), (4,4)]$.

**Process $A(0,3)$:**
$Cross((5,1), (4,4), (0,3)) = (4-5)(3-1) - (4-1)(0-5) = -2 - (-15) = 13 > 0$. Push $A$. $\mathcal{S} = [(0,0), (3,0), (5,1), (4,4), (0,3)]$.

**Final Convex Hull:** $(0,0) \to (3,0) \to (5,1) \to (4,4) \to (0,3) \to (0,0)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the working of Graham's Scan algorithm. State and justify its time complexity. Compare it briefly with the Jarvis March algorithm. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the point set $\{(2,2), (4,3), (5,1), (1,5), (7,4), (3,6), (6,0)\}$:
>   (i) Identify the anchor point and sort the remaining points by polar angle.  
>   (ii) Apply Graham's Scan and show the final hull with all cross-product computations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Description (4 Marks):**
- Graham's Scan begins by selecting the **lowest-leftmost point** $p_0$ as anchor.
- All other points are sorted by **polar angle** from $p_0$, creating a counter-clockwise sequence.
- A **stack** $\mathcal{S}$ is maintained, initialized with $p_0$ and $p_1$.
- For each subsequent point, the algorithm repeatedly **pops** the top of the stack while the last two stack points and the new point form a **non-left turn** (cross product $\le 0$).
- The new point is then **pushed**.
- The final stack reads out the convex hull in counter-clockwise order.

(Refer to Section 4 diagrams for visual representation.)

**Time Complexity Justification (2 Marks):**
- **Phase 1** (anchor): $O(n)$.
- **Phase 2** (sorting): $O(n \log n)$.
- **Phase 3** (stack scan): $O(n)$ amortized, because each point is pushed and popped at most once across the entire algorithm.
- **Total:** $O(n \log n)$.

**Comparison with Jarvis March (1 Mark):**

| Feature | Graham's Scan | Jarvis March |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(nh)$ |
| Approach | Sort then sweep | Gift-wrapping |
| Output-sensitivity | No | Yes ($h$ = hull vertices) |
| Preprocessing | Required | Not required |
| Best for | All points averaged | Few hull vertices |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Input Points:** $P = \{(2,2), (4,3), (5,1), (1,5), (7,4), (3,6), (6,0)\}$

**(i) Anchor & Sort (3 Marks):**

Minimum $y$ is $0$, attained uniquely by $(6,0)$. So $p_0 = (6,0)$.

Polar angles from $p_0$:

| Point | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ | $\tan\theta = \Delta y/\Delta x$ | $\theta$ (degrees) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(5,1)$ | $-1$ | $1$ | $-1.000$ | $135.00$ |
| $(4,3)$ | $-2$ | $3$ | $-1.500$ | $123.69$ |
| $(2,2)$ | $-4$ | $2$ | $-0.500$ | $153.43$ |
| $(1,5)$ | $-5$ | $5$ | $-1.000$ | $135.00$ |
| $(3,6)$ | $-3$ | $6$ | $-2.000$ | $116.57$ |
| $(7,4)$ | $1$ | $4$ | $4.000$ | $75.96$ |

**Tie-breaking:** $(5,1)$ and $(1,5)$ are collinear with $p_0$ at $135°$. Distances² from $p_0$: $(5,1) \to 2$; $(1,5) \to 50$. Keep $(1,5)$, discard $(5,1)$.

**Sorted sequence:** $p_0 = (6,0) \to (7,4) \to (3,6) \to (1,5) \to (4,3) \to (2,2)$.

**(ii) Apply Graham's Scan (4 Marks):**

Initialize $\mathcal{S} = [(6,0), (7,4)]$.

**Process $(3,6)$:**
$Cross((6,0),(7,4),(3,6)) = (7-6)(6-0) - (4-0)(3-6) = 6 - (-12) = 18 > 0$. Push. $\mathcal{S} = [(6,0), (7,4), (3,6)]$.

**Process $(1,5)$:**
$Cross((7,4),(3,6),(1,5)) = (3-7)(5-4) - (6-4)(1-7) = -4 - (-12) = 8 > 0$. Push. $\mathcal{S} = [(6,0), (7,4), (3,6), (1,5)]$.

**Process $(4,3)$:**
$Cross((3,6),(1,5),(4,3)) = (1-3)(3-6) - (5-6)(4-3) = 6 - (-1) = 7 > 0$. Push. $\mathcal{S} = [(6,0), (7,4), (3,6), (1,5), (4,3)]$.

**Process $(2,2)$:**
$Cross((1,5),(4,3),(2,2)) = (4-1)(2-5) - (3-5)(2-1) = -9 - (-2) = -7 < 0$. **Pop $(4,3)$**.
$Cross((3,6),(1,5),(2,2)) = (1-3)(2-6) - (5-6)(2-3) = 8 - 1 = 7 > 0$. Push $(2,2)$.
$\mathcal{S} = [(6,0), (7,4), (3,6), (1,5), (2,2)]$.

**Final Convex Hull:** $(6,0) \to (7,4) \to (3,6) \to (1,5) \to (2,2) \to (6,0)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Graham's Scan Algorithm**

- ✅ **Purpose:** Computes the convex hull of a set of $n$ points in the plane.
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ — sorting dominates.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$.
- ✅ **Anchor Point:** Lowest $y$-coordinate; tie-break by lowest $x$. Must be a hull vertex.
- ✅ **Sorting Key:** Polar angle from anchor; for ties, farthest point wins.
- ✅ **Data Structure:** Stack (LIFO) — the core mechanism.
- ✅ **Turn Predicate:** Cross product $Cross(A,B,C) = (x_2-x_1)(y_3-y_1) - (y_2-y_1)(x_3-x_1)$.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** $> 0$ = Left Turn (CCW, keep), $< 0$ = Right Turn (CW, pop), $= 0$ = Collinear (policy-dependent).
- ✅ **Loop Condition:** Pop while `Cross ≤ 0` (strict hull) or `Cross < 0` (boundary inclusive).
- ✅ **Amortization Argument:** Each point pushed and popped at most once → $O(n)$ for Phase 3.
- ✅ **Output Format:** Stack read bottom-to-top yields counter-clockwise hull.
- ✅ **Edge Case 1:** Collinear points on hull boundary — handled by tie-breaking in sorting.
- ✅ **Edge Case 2:** Duplicate points — handle by pre-dedup or by `Cross > 0` strict inequality.
- ✅ **Edge Case 3:** All points collinear — output is the two extreme points.
- ✅ **Comparison:** $O(n \log n)$ for Graham's Scan vs. $O(nh)$ for Jarvis March — Graham is asymptotically faster for large hulls.
- ✅ **Stable Since:** Published by Ronald Graham in 1972; one of the earliest optimal convex hull algorithms.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Hot Topic:** Algorithm trace with cross-product table, complexity derivation with amortization, and the collinearity policy distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jarvis's March (Gift Wrapping) Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Jarvis's March (Gift Wrapping) Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Jarvis's March**, also known as the **Gift Wrapping Algorithm**, is a classical output-sensitive algorithm in computational geometry used to compute the **Convex Hull** of a finite set of points in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$. Formally, given a set $S = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_n\}$ of $n$ points in the plane, Jarvis's March constructs the convex hull $\text{CH}(S)$ — the smallest convex polygon containing all points of $S$ — by simulating the process of wrapping a string tightly around the outermost points. The algorithm is classified as an **O(nh) algorithm**, where $h$ denotes the number of points on the convex hull (output-sensitive complexity). In the worst case (when $h = n$, e.g., when all points lie on the hull), the complexity degrades to **O(n²)**. ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Wrapped Gift" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Explanation (The Gift Wrapping Analogy):** > > Imagine you have a set of **pins (nails)** stuck on a flat board, representing the input points. Now take a piece of **wrapping paper (string)** and a pin at the **leftmost position** (your starting point). Hold the string taut against this starting pin and slowly rotate it counter-clockwise. The first pin the string **touches** as it rotates becomes the next vertex of the hull. You then fix the string to this new pin and repeat the rotation process. You continue wrapping until the string returns to the starting pin — the resulting polygon traced by the string is the **convex hull**. This analogy precisely captures the algorithm's core logic: - The **taut string** = the line segment from the current hull vertex to the candidate. - **Counter-clockwise rotation** = scanning for the most counter-clockwise point (using the cross product test). - **Termination** = returning to the leftmost starting point. ### 1.3 Salient Features > [!NOTE] > **Key Properties of Jarvis's March:** > > - **Output-sensitive:** Runtime depends on the number of hull points, not just input size. > - **Simplicity:** One of the easiest convex hull algorithms to understand and implement. > - **In-place:** Operates directly on the input set with O(1) auxiliary space (excluding output). > - **No preprocessing required:** Unlike Graham's Scan, it does not need sorting or angle computation. > - **Worst-case O(n²):** Occurs when all input points lie on the convex hull. > - **Best-case O(n):** Occurs when $h$ (hull points) is a small constant (e.g., triangle). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown The Jarvis's March algorithm proceeds through the following structured phases: **Phase 1: Initialization** - Find the **leftmost point** $p_0$ (minimum $x$-coordinate; lowest $y$ in case of ties). This point is guaranteed to lie on the convex hull. - Initialize the hull list $H \leftarrow \{p_0\}$. - Set the current point $p_{\text{curr}} \leftarrow p_0$. - Set the initial candidate $p_{\text{cand}} \leftarrow$ any point other than $p_0$ (say, $p_1$). **Phase 2: Hull Point Selection (Core Loop)** - For every point $r \in S \setminus \{p_{\text{curr}}\}$: - Compute the **cross product** $\text{cross}(p_{\text{cand}} - p_{\text{curr}},\ r - p_{\text{curr}})$. - If the cross product is **positive** ($> 0$), then $r$ lies to the **left** of the line from $p_{\text{curr}}$ to $p_{\text{cand}}$, meaning $r$ is a *more counter-clockwise* candidate. - Update $p_{\text{cand}} \leftarrow r$. - After scanning all points, $p_{\text{cand}}$ is the next hull vertex. **Phase 3: Termination Check** - If $p_{\text{cand}} = p_0$, we have completed the hull. Output $H$ and terminate. - Otherwise, append $p_{\text{cand}}$ to $H$, set $p_{\text{curr}} \leftarrow p_{\text{cand}}$, and return to Phase 2. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the "Most Counter-Clockwise" Point is the Next Hull Vertex:** > > Among all candidates from $p_{\text{curr}}$, the point that makes the smallest rightward turn (i.e., the largest leftward angle) is the one such that **all other points lie strictly to the right** of the directed segment from $p_{\text{curr}}$ to that point. This is exactly the definition of a convex hull edge — the entire set $S$ lies on or to one side. ### 2.2 The Orientation Test (Cross Product) For three points $p(x_1, y_1)$, $q(x_2, y_2)$, $r(x_3, y_3)$, the **2D cross product** of vectors $\vec{pq}$ and $\vec{pr}$ is the scalar: $$ \text{cross}(\vec{pq}, \vec{pr}) = (q.x - p.x)(r.y - p.y) - (q.y - p.y)(r.x - p.x) $$ This scalar is twice the **signed area** of the triangle $\triangle pqr$: $$ \text{cross}(\vec{pq}, \vec{pr}) = 2 \cdot \text{SignedArea}(\triangle pqr) $$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Rule | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **2D Cross Product** | $\text{cross}(p,q,r) = (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1)$ | Orientation primitive | | 2 | **Counter-Clockwise (CCW) Test** | $\text{cross}(p,q,r) > 0$ | $r$ is left of $\overline{pq}$ | | 3 | **Clockwise (CW) Test** | $\text{cross}(p,q,r) < 0$ | $r$ is right of $\overline{pq}$ | | 4 | **Collinearity Test** | $\text{cross}(p,q,r) = 0$ | $p, q, r$ are collinear | | 5 | **Signed Area of Triangle** | $A = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1) \vert$ | Geom. interpretation of cross | | 6 | **Time Complexity** | $T(n, h) = O(nh)$ | Output-sensitive | | 7 | **Worst-Case Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ | When $h = n$ | | 8 | **Best-Case Complexity** | $O(n)$ | When $h = O(1)$ | | 9 | **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ auxiliary (in-place) | Excluding output hull | | 10 | **Starting Point Criterion** | $p_0 = \arg\min_i\ (x_i)$, tie-broken by $y_i$ | Guaranteed hull point | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the absolute value is rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid conflicting with markdown table delimiters. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Jarvis's March and the underlying convex hull computation have profound engineering applications: - **Geometric Modeling & CAD/CAM:** Computing the outer boundary of 3D-modeled regions in computer-aided design. - **Pattern Recognition & Image Processing:** Identifying the outer contour of digital shapes; character recognition. - **Robotics & Motion Planning:** Defining the collision-free workspace boundary. - **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Computing the boundary of land parcels or the convex envelope of sensor data. - **Machine Learning:** Used in **Support Vector Machines (SVMs)** and **Convex Hull-based clustering** algorithms. - **Computational Finance:** Computing risk boundaries for portfolios of assets. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Cross Product Orientation Test The cross product of two 2D vectors $\vec{u} = (u_x, u_y)$ and $\vec{v} = (v_x, v_y)$ is defined as the scalar: $$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = u_x v_y - u_y v_x $$ For points $p, q, r$, treating $\vec{pq} = (q.x - p.x,\ q.y - p.y)$ and $\vec{pr} = (r.x - p.x,\ r.y - p.y)$: $$ \text{cross}(p, q, r) = (q.x - p.x)(r.y - p.y) - (q.y - p.y)(r.x - p.x) $$ **Sign Interpretation (Geometric Proof Sketch):** The cross product equals $\vert \vec{pq} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{pr} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the angle from $\vec{pq}$ to $\vec{pr}$ (counter-clockwise positive). - If $\theta \in (0, \pi)$: $\sin(\theta) > 0 \Rightarrow$ cross $> 0 \Rightarrow$ $r$ is to the **left** of $\overline{pq}$ (CCW turn). - If $\theta \in (-\pi, 0)$: $\sin(\theta) < 0 \Rightarrow$ cross $< 0 \Rightarrow$ $r$ is to the **right** of $\overline{pq}$ (CW turn). - If $\theta = 0$ or $\pi$: $\sin(\theta) = 0 \Rightarrow$ cross $= 0 \Rightarrow$ $p, q, r$ are **collinear**. This is the foundational tool that drives Jarvis's March. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Tracing Jarvis's March **Problem:** Compute the convex hull of the set $$S = \{A(0,0),\ B(1,1),\ C(2,0),\ D(2,2),\ E(0,3),\ F(3,1)\}$$ **Step 1 — Initialization (Locate Leftmost Point):** Scanning all $x$-coordinates, the minimum $x = 0$ is shared by $A(0,0)$ and $E(0,3)$. Tie-breaking by minimum $y$, we select: $$p_0 = A(0,0)$$ Set $H = \{A\}$, $p_{\text{curr}} = A$, and initial candidate $p_{\text{cand}} = B(1,1)$. **Step 2 — Find Next Hull Point After $A$:** For each candidate $q \in S \setminus \{A\}$, we check if all other points lie to the right of $\overline{Aq}$ (i.e., cross product of $\vec{Aq}$ and $\vec{A,r}$ is $\leq 0$ for all $r$). **Trial: $q = B(1,1)$:** | Point $r$ | $\vec{AB} = (1,1)$, $\vec{Ar}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{AB}, \vec{Ar})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $C(2,0)$ | $(2,0)$ | $(1)(0) - (1)(2) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $D(2,2)$ | $(2,2)$ | $(1)(2) - (1)(2) = 0$ | On line | | $E(0,3)$ | $(0,3)$ | $(1)(3) - (1)(0) = 3$ | **LEFT ✗** | | $F(3,1)$ | $(3,1)$ | $(1)(1) - (1)(3) = -2$ | Right ✓ | Since $E$ lies to the left, $B$ is **not** the next hull point. **Trial: $q = C(2,0)$:** $\vec{AC} = (2,0)$. For $r = B(1,1)$: $\text{cross} = (2)(1) - (0)(1) = 2 > 0$ (left). **Reject.** **Trial: $q = D(2,2)$:** $\vec{AD} = (2,2)$. For $r = E(0,3)$: $\text{cross} = (2)(3) - (2)(0) = 6 > 0$ (left). **Reject.** **Trial: $q = E(0,3)$:** $\vec{AE} = (0,3)$. | Point $r$ | $\vec{Ar}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{AE}, \vec{Ar})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $B(1,1)$ | $(1,1)$ | $(0)(1) - (3)(1) = -3$ | Right ✓ | | $C(2,0)$ | $(2,0)$ | $(0)(0) - (3)(2) = -6$ | Right ✓ | | $D(2,2)$ | $(2,2)$ | $(0)(2) - (3)(2) = -6$ | Right ✓ | | $F(3,1)$ | $(3,1)$ | $(0)(1) - (3)(3) = -9$ | Right ✓ | All points lie to the right (or on the line). Therefore: $$p_{\text{cand}} = E(0,3)$$ Append to hull: $H = \{A, E\}$. Set $p_{\text{curr}} = E$. **Step 3 — Find Next Hull Point After $E(0,3)$:** Set initial candidate $p_{\text{cand}} = A(0,0)$. (Any point works as initial seed.) **Trial: $q = A(0,0)$:** $\vec{EA} = (0, -3)$. For $r = B(1,1)$: $\vec{EB} = (1, -2)$, $\text{cross} = (0)(-2) - (-3)(1) = 3 > 0$ (left). **Reject.** **Trial: $q = B(1,1)$:** $\vec{EB} = (1, -2)$. For $r = C(2,0)$: $\vec{EC} = (2, -3)$, $\text{cross} = (1)(-3) - (-2)(2) = 1 > 0$ (left). **Reject.** **Trial: $q = C(2,0)$:** $\vec{EC} = (2, -3)$. | Point $r$ | $\vec{Er}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{EC}, \vec{Er})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $A(0,0)$ | $(0,-3)$ | $(2)(-3) - (-3)(0) = -6$ | Right ✓ | | $B(1,1)$ | $(1,-2)$ | $(2)(-2) - (-3)(1) = -1$ | Right ✓ | | $D(2,2)$ | $(2,-1)$ | $(2)(-1) - (-3)(2) = 4$ | **LEFT ✗** | | $F(3,1)$ | $(3,-2)$ | $(2)(-2) - (-3)(3) = 5$ | **LEFT ✗** | Points $D$ and $F$ are to the left. **Reject.** **Trial: $q = D(2,2)$:** $\vec{ED} = (2, -1)$. | Point $r$ | $\vec{Er}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{ED}, \vec{Er})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $A(0,0)$ | $(0,-3)$ | $(2)(-3) - (-1)(0) = -6$ | Right ✓ | | $B(1,1)$ | $(1,-2)$ | $(2)(-2) - (-1)(1) = -3$ | Right ✓ | | $C(2,0)$ | $(2,-3)$ | $(2)(-3) - (-1)(2) = -4$ | Right ✓ | | $F(3,1)$ | $(3,-2)$ | $(2)(-2) - (-1)(3) = -1$ | Right ✓ | All points to the right. Therefore: $$p_{\text{cand}} = D(2,2)$$ Append to hull: $H = \{A, E, D\}$. Set $p_{\text{curr}} = D$. **Step 4 — Find Next Hull Point After $D(2,2)$:** **Trial: $q = F(3,1)$:** $\vec{DF} = (1, -1)$. | Point $r$ | $\vec{Dr}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{DF}, \vec{Dr})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $A(0,0)$ | $(-2,-2)$ | $(1)(-2) - (-1)(-2) = -4$ | Right ✓ | | $B(1,1)$ | $(-1,-1)$ | $(1)(-1) - (-1)(-1) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $C(2,0)$ | $(0,-2)$ | $(1)(-2) - (-1)(0) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $E(0,3)$ | $(-2,1)$ | $(1)(1) - (-1)(-2) = -1$ | Right ✓ | All points to the right. Therefore: $$p_{\text{cand}} = F(3,1)$$ Append: $H = \{A, E, D, F\}$. Set $p_{\text{curr}} = F$. **Step 5 — Find Next Hull Point After $F(3,1)$:** **Trial: $q = C(2,0)$:** $\vec{FC} = (-1, -1)$. | Point $r$ | $\vec{Fr}$ | $\text{cross}(\vec{FC}, \vec{Fr})$ | Position | |---|---|---|---| | $A(0,0)$ | $(-3,-1)$ | $(-1)(-1) - (-1)(-3) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $B(1,1)$ | $(-2,0)$ | $(-1)(0) - (-1)(-2) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $D(2,2)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $(-1)(1) - (-1)(-1) = -2$ | Right ✓ | | $E(0,3)$ | $(-3,2)$ | $(-1)(2) - (-1)(-3) = -5$ | Right ✓ | All points to the right. Therefore: $$p_{\text{cand}} = C(2,0)$$ Append: $H = \{A, E, D, F, C\}$. Set $p_{\text{curr}} = C$. **Step 6 — Termination Check:** From $C(2,0)$, the next hull point selection: $\vec{CA} = (-2, 0)$. Checking all $r$: $\text{cross}(\vec{CA}, \vec{Cr})$ for $r = B, D, E, F$ yields non-positive values, so $A$ is the next hull point. **Since $p_{\text{cand}} = p_0 = A$, the algorithm terminates.** ### 3.3 Final Convex Hull $$ \boxed{\text{CH}(S) = A(0,0) \rightarrow E(0,3) \rightarrow D(2,2) \rightarrow F(3,1) \rightarrow C(2,0) \rightarrow A(0,0)} $$ The point $B(1,1)$ is **inside** the hull and is therefore correctly excluded. ### 3.4 Complexity Analysis of the Example - **Input size:** $n = 6$. - **Hull size:** $h = 5$. - **Operations:** Each hull point selection scans $O(n)$ points; the loop runs $h$ times. - **Total:** $O(n \cdot h) = O(6 \cdot 5) = 30$ cross product evaluations — consistent with the theoretical bound. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Jarvis's March Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([START Algorithm]) --> init1[Find leftmost point p0
min x, tie-break by min y] init1 --> init2[Initialize hull list H = p0
Set current = p0
Set candidate = p0] init2 --> loop1[Begin Outer Loop
for each iteration i = 1, 2, 3, ...] loop1 --> init3[Set candidate = first point
in S different from current] init3 --> loop2{Inner Loop
For every point r
in S minus current} loop2 -->|yes| crossOp[Compute cross product
CP = cross candidate minus current
AND r minus current] crossOp --> decision{CP greater than 0?} decision -->|YES| updateCand[Update candidate = r
r is more counter-clockwise] decision -->|NO| skipR[Skip point r
r is not a hull candidate] updateCand --> loop2 skipR --> loop2 loop2 -->|no more points| endInner[Inner Loop Ends
candidate is the next hull vertex] endInner --> termCheck{candidate equals p0?} termCheck -->|NO| appendHull[Append candidate to H
Set current = candidate] termCheck -->|YES| outputHull[Output hull H
and terminate] appendHull --> loop1 outputHull([END Algorithm]) ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR inputBlock[Input Set S
n points in plane] --> preBlock[Preprocessing
Locate p0] preBlock --> ctrlBlock[Control Unit
Maintains current
and candidate] ctrlBlock --> orientUnit[Orientation Engine
Cross Product Test] orientUnit --> selectUnit[Selector Unit
Pick Most CCW Point] selectUnit --> memBlock[Hull Memory H
Stores hull vertices] memBlock --> termBlock[Termination Logic
Checks for closure] termBlock -->|Continue| ctrlBlock termBlock -->|Stop| outputBlock[Output
Convex Hull H] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Hull Construction Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD pA((A 0,0)) --> pE((E 0,3)) pE --> pD((D 2,2)) pD --> pF((F 3,1)) pF --> pC((C 2,0)) pC --> pA pB((B 1,1)) -.->|Inside Hull| pA pB -.->|Rejected| pE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The solid arrows represent the **directed hull edges** traversed by Jarvis's March, while the dashed lines from $B$ indicate that $B$ is an **interior point** and is correctly excluded from the convex hull. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the **convex hull** of a set of points. Mention the time complexity of Jarvis's March algorithm. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **convex hull** of a finite set of points $S$ in the plane, denoted $\text{CH}(S)$, is the smallest convex polygon that contains all points of $S$. Equivalently, it is the intersection of all convex sets containing $S$, or the set of all convex combinations of points in $S$. Jarvis's March algorithm computes $\text{CH}(S)$ in **$O(nh)$ time**, where $n$ is the number of input points and $h$ is the number of points on the hull. In the worst case (all points on the hull), this becomes $O(n^2)$, and in the best case (constant hull size), it becomes $O(n)$. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` What is the role of the **cross product** in Jarvis's March algorithm? State the formula. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **cross product** is used as an **orientation test** to determine whether a point $r$ lies to the left, right, or on the directed line from $p$ to $q$ in $O(1)$ time. The formula is: $$ \text{cross}(p, q, r) = (q.x - p.x)(r.y - p.y) - (q.y - p.y)(r.x - p.x) $$ - $\text{cross} > 0$: $r$ is to the **left** of $\overline{pq}$ (CCW). - $\text{cross} < 0$: $r$ is to the **right** of $\overline{pq}$ (CW). - $\text{cross} = 0$: $p, q, r$ are **collinear**. In Jarvis's March, the cross product is used to find the next hull vertex by selecting the candidate that makes the most counter-clockwise turn. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **orientation test** using the 2D cross product. How is it used to determine if a point lies to the left, right, or on a line? (7 Marks) **(b)** Apply **Jarvis's March algorithm** on the following set of points and find the convex hull. Show all iterations clearly: $$S = \{(0,0),\ (4,0),\ (4,4),\ (2,2),\ (0,4),\ (3,1)\}$$ (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Orientation Test (7 Marks):** **Definition:** Given three points $p(x_1, y_1)$, $q(x_2, y_2)$, and $r(x_3, y_3)$, the **2D cross product** is defined as the scalar: $$ \text{cross}(p, q, r) = (q.x - p.x)(r.y - p.y) - (q.y - p.y)(r.x - p.x) $$ **Geometric Interpretation:** The cross product equals twice the signed area of the triangle $\triangle pqr$: $$ \text{cross}(p, q, r) = 2 \cdot A_{\text{signed}}(\triangle pqr) = \vert \vec{pq} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{pr} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta) $$ where $\theta$ is the angle measured counter-clockwise from $\vec{pq}$ to $\vec{pr}$. **Three Cases:** 1. **Counter-Clockwise (Left Turn):** If $\text{cross} > 0$, then $\sin(\theta) > 0$, meaning $\theta \in (0, \pi)$. The point $r$ lies strictly to the **left** of the directed line from $p$ to $q$. 2. **Clockwise (Right Turn):** If $\text{cross} < 0$, then $\sin(\theta) < 0$, meaning $\theta \in (-\pi, 0)$. The point $r$ lies strictly to the **right** of the directed line from $p$ to $q$. 3. **Collinear:** If $\text{cross} = 0$, the three points $p, q, r$ lie on a single straight line. **Application in Jarvis's March:** For each candidate $q$ from the current point $p_{\text{curr}}$, we compute $\text{cross}(p_{\text{curr}}, q, r)$ for every other point $r$. The candidate $q$ that makes all cross products non-positive (i.e., all other points are to the right or on the line) is selected as the next hull vertex. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Jarvis's March Application (7 Marks):** Let $P_1 = (0,0)$, $P_2 = (4,0)$, $P_3 = (4,4)$, $P_4 = (2,2)$, $P_5 = (0,4)$, $P_6 = (3,1)$. **Step 1:** Leftmost point is $P_1(0,0)$. Set $H = \{P_1\}$, $p_{\text{curr}} = P_1$. **Step 2:** From $P_1$, test candidates: - $P_5(0,4)$: $\vec{P_1P_5} = (0,4)$. For $P_2(4,0)$: $\text{cross} = (0)(0) - (4)(4) = -16 \leq 0$. For all others, cross is also $\leq 0$. So $P_5$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5\}$. **Step 3:** From $P_5(0,4)$, test $P_3(4,4)$: $\vec{P_5P_3} = (4,0)$. For $P_1(0,0)$: $\text{cross} = (4)(-4) - (0)(0) = -16 \leq 0$. All others also give $\leq 0$. So $P_3$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5, P_3\}$. **Step 4:** From $P_3(4,4)$, the next hull vertex is $P_2(4,0)$ (verified by cross product scan). $H = \{P_1, P_5, P_3, P_2\}$. **Step 5:** From $P_2(4,0)$, the next hull vertex is $P_1(0,0)$ — which equals the starting point. **Algorithm terminates.** **Final Convex Hull:** $P_1(0,0) \rightarrow P_5(0,4) \rightarrow P_3(4,4) \rightarrow P_2(4,0) \rightarrow P_1(0,0)$. The points $P_4(2,2)$ and $P_6(3,1)$ lie strictly inside the hull. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Compare **Jarvis's March** with **Graham's Scan** algorithm for computing the convex hull. Discuss time complexity, approach, and applicability. (7 Marks) **(b)** Trace **Jarvis's March algorithm** step-by-step on the point set: $$S = \{(1,2),\ (3,1),\ (5,3),\ (4,5),\ (2,4),\ (3,3)\}$$ List the vertices of the convex hull in order. (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Jarvis's March | Graham's Scan | |---|---|---| | **Approach** | Gift wrapping; iterative | Sort + stack-based sweep | | **Time Complexity** | $O(nh)$ output-sensitive | $O(n \log n)$ always | | **Best Case** | $O(n)$ (small $h$) | $O(n \log n)$ | | **Worst Case** | $O(n^2)$ (all points on hull) | $O(n \log n)$ | | **Preprocessing** | None required | Requires sorting by polar angle | | **Data Structure** | None (in-place) | Stack for candidate vertices | | **Ease of Implementation** | Simple, intuitive | Moderate (angle sort is tricky) | | **Numerical Stability** | Robust (uses cross product only) | Sensitive to angle computations | | **When to Use** | When $h$ is small | When worst-case guarantee needed | **Conclusion:** Jarvis's March is preferred when the number of hull points is expected to be small (e.g., point clusters, small polygons), while Graham's Scan is preferred when a guaranteed $O(n \log n)$ bound is essential, regardless of hull size. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Tracing Jarvis's March (7 Marks):** Let $P_1 = (1,2)$, $P_2 = (3,1)$, $P_3 = (5,3)$, $P_4 = (4,5)$, $P_5 = (2,4)$, $P_6 = (3,3)$. **Step 1:** Leftmost point is $P_1(1,2)$. Set $H = \{P_1\}$. **Step 2:** From $P_1$, find the most CCW point. Testing $P_4(4,5)$: $\vec{P_1P_4} = (3, 3)$. For $P_2(3,1)$: $\text{cross} = (3)(-1) - (3)(2) = -9 \leq 0$. For $P_3$: $\text{cross} = (3)(1) - (3)(4) = -9 \leq 0$. For $P_5$: $\text{cross} = (3)(2) - (3)(1) = 3 > 0$ (left). So $P_4$ is **not** the next hull point. Testing $P_5(2,4)$: $\vec{P_1P_5} = (1, 2)$. For $P_2$: $\text{cross} = (1)(-1) - (2)(2) = -5 \leq 0$. For $P_3$: $\text{cross} = (1)(1) - (2)(4) = -7 \leq 0$. For $P_4$: $\text{cross} = (1)(3) - (2)(3) = -3 \leq 0$. For $P_6$: $\text{cross} = (1)(1) - (2)(2) = -3 \leq 0$. All non-positive → $P_5$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5\}$. **Step 3:** From $P_5(2,4)$, test $P_4(4,5)$: $\vec{P_5P_4} = (2, 1)$. For $P_1$: $\text{cross} = (2)(-2) - (1)(-1) = -3 \leq 0$. For $P_2$: $\text{cross} = (2)(-3) - (1)(1) = -7 \leq 0$. For $P_3$: $\text{cross} = (2)(-1) - (1)(3) = -5 \leq 0$. For $P_6$: $\text{cross} = (2)(-1) - (1)(1) = -3 \leq 0$. All non-positive → $P_4$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5, P_4\}$. **Step 4:** From $P_4(4,5)$, test $P_3(5,3)$: $\vec{P_4P_3} = (1, -2)$. For $P_1$: $\text{cross} = (1)(-3) - (-2)(-3) = -9 \leq 0$. For $P_2$: $\text{cross} = (1)(-4) - (-2)(-1) = -6 \leq 0$. For $P_5$: $\text{cross} = (1)(-1) - (-2)(-2) = -5 \leq 0$. For $P_6$: $\text{cross} = (1)(-2) - (-2)(-1) = -4 \leq 0$. All non-positive → $P_3$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5, P_4, P_3\}$. **Step 5:** From $P_3(5,3)$, test $P_2(3,1)$: $\vec{P_3P_2} = (-2, -2)$. For all other points, cross products are $\leq 0$. So $P_2$ is next. $H = \{P_1, P_5, P_4, P_3, P_2\}$. **Step 6:** From $P_2(3,1)$, the next hull vertex is $P_1(1,2)$ — equal to the starting point. **Algorithm terminates.** **Final Convex Hull:** $P_1(1,2) \rightarrow P_5(2,4) \rightarrow P_4(4,5) \rightarrow P_3(5,3) \rightarrow P_2(3,1) \rightarrow P_1(1,2)$. The point $P_6(3,3)$ is interior. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Cross Product Sign Reversal:** The single most common error is interpreting the sign of the cross product in the wrong direction. **Remember:** Cross product $> 0$ means **CCW (left)**, and the algorithm seeks the candidate such that **all other points are to the right or on the line** (cross $\leq 0$). Reversing this logic will yield the **concave hull** (interior wrapping) — an entirely incorrect answer. > > 2. **Forgetting the Termination Condition:** Many students continue adding points without checking whether the candidate equals the starting point $p_0$. Always include the explicit termination check. > > 3. **Incorrect Starting Point:** Using the rightmost, topmost, or arbitrary point instead of the leftmost leads to a hull that may miss vertices or include them in the wrong order. > > 4. **Ignoring Collinearity:** The problem statement should specify whether collinear points on the hull boundary are to be included. The default behavior of Jarvis's March may include all collinear points or only the endpoints, depending on the strict/non-strict inequality used. > > 5. **Confusing Complexity Notations:** Clearly distinguish between $O(nh)$ (output-sensitive, best/average case behavior) and $O(n^2)$ (worst case). Examiners will deduct marks if you state only $O(n^2)$ without justification. > > 6. **Skipping Intermediate Steps in Trace Problems:** In 7-mark trace questions, KTU examiners expect **every cross product computation** to be shown. Skipping steps is a guaranteed mark loss. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Jarvis's March (Gift Wrapping) Algorithm** > > - **Algorithm Type:** Output-sensitive convex hull algorithm. > - **Time Complexity:** $O(nh)$ general; $O(n^2)$ worst case; $O(n)$ best case. > - **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ auxiliary. > - **Starting Point:** Leftmost point (min $x$; tie-broken by min $y$) — guaranteed hull vertex. > - **Core Primitive:** 2D cross product for orientation testing in $O(1)$ time. > - **Cross Product Formula:** $\text{cross}(p,q,r) = (q.x - p.x)(r.y - p.y) - (q.y - p.y)(r.x - p.x)$. > - **Sign Convention:** $> 0$ = CCW (left); $< 0$ = CW (right); $= 0$ = collinear. > - **Selection Rule:** Pick the candidate such that all other points are to the right of (or on) the directed segment from current to candidate. > - **Termination:** When the next selected vertex equals the starting point $p_0$. > - **Key Advantage:** Output-sensitive — fast when hull size $h$ is small. > - **Key Disadvantage:** Degrades to $O(n^2)$ when $h = n$. > - **Comparison with Graham's Scan:** Jarvis is $O(nh)$ vs. Graham's $O(n \log n)$; Jarvis is in-place, Graham needs a stack. > - **Use Cases:** Prefer Jarvis when $h \ll n$; prefer Graham when worst-case guarantees matter. > - **Applications:** CAD/CAM, GIS, pattern recognition, motion planning, ML clustering. > - **No Preprocessing:** Unlike Graham's Scan, no sorting or angle computation is needed. > - **Geometric Insight:** Equivalent to wrapping a taut string around the outermost points starting from the leftmost.

topics.title: Divide and Conquer Algorithm for Convex Hulls; Line Segment Intersection - Problem Definition and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Divide and Conquer Algorithm for Convex Hulls; Line Segment Intersection - Problem Definition and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Convex Hull — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Convex Hull (CH)**: Given a finite set of points $S = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ in the plane, the convex hull $CH(S)$ is the **smallest convex polygon** that contains all points of $S$. Equivalently, it is the intersection of all convex sets containing $S$, or the set of all convex combinations of points in $S$.

Formally, the convex hull is defined as:

$$CH(S) = \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i p_i \;\middle|\; p_i \in S,\; \lambda_i \geq 0,\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = 1 \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The convex hull is one of the most fundamental structures in computational geometry. It serves as a building block for many other geometric algorithms including line segment intersection, Voronoi diagrams, Delaunay triangulations, and range searching.

## 1.2 Divide and Conquer Paradigm — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Divide and Conquer (D&C)**: An algorithm design paradigm that recursively breaks a problem into two or more sub-problems of the same or related type, until these become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then combined to give a solution to the original problem.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Map"

Imagine you are a cartographer drawing a boundary around a set of villages on a map. Instead of trying to draw the boundary around all 1000 villages at once (a complex task), you:

1. **Divide**: Split the villages into two groups — those on the eastern side and those on the western side (by drawing a vertical line).
2. **Conquer**: Recursively draw the boundary for each half independently. With only 500 villages per group, the task is much simpler.
3. **Merge**: Find the two tangent lines (one on the top, one on the bottom) that connect the eastern and western boundaries, and "stitch" them together to form a single convex polygon enclosing all villages.

This is precisely the spirit of the **Divide and Conquer Convex Hull Algorithm** proposed by Preparata and Hong (1977).

## 1.3 Line Segment Intersection — Problem Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Line Segment Intersection Problem**: Given a set $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ of $n$ line segments in the plane, determine whether **any two segments $s_i$ and $s_j$ (with $i \neq j$) intersect**, and if so, report all such intersection points.

Two line segments $s_1 = \overline{p_1 p_2}$ and $s_2 = \overline{p_3 p_4}$ **intersect** if and only if they share at least one common point in the plane. This is tested via the **orientation test** (cross-product test):

$$s_1 \text{ and } s_2 \text{ intersect} \iff \text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_4) \leq 0$$
$$\text{and} \quad \text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_1) \cdot \text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_2) \leq 0$$

where $\text{orient}(a, b, c) = (b_x - a_x)(c_y - a_y) - (b_y - a_y)(c_x - a_x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note**: The orientation function returns positive for a **counter-clockwise (CCW) turn**, negative for a **clockwise (CW) turn**, and zero for **collinear** points. The sign change indicates that the point lies on opposite sides of the line.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy — Line Segment Intersection

Think of a city's road network as a set of line segments. Civil engineers and GPS routing systems need to know **where two roads cross** (an intersection) so they can place traffic signals, plan detours, and render maps. The computational problem of detecting all such crossings efficiently is exactly the line segment intersection problem.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Divide and Conquer Convex Hull — Operational Phases

The D&C convex hull algorithm (Preparata-Hong) operates in three precise phases:

### Phase 1 — DIVIDE

- Sort the input points by **x-coordinate** (and break ties by y-coordinate): $O(n \log n)$.
- Find the median $x$-value $x_m$ and split the set into:
  - **Left half** $S_L$: points with $x \leq x_m$.
  - **Right half** $S_R$: points with $x > x_m$.
- Each half contains at most $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ points.

### Phase 2 — CONQUER (Recursive)

- Recursively compute $CH(S_L)$ and $CH(S_R)$.
- **Base case**: When a subset has $\leq 3$ points, the convex hull is computed trivially (a point, a line segment, or a triangle).
- Output of recursion: two disjoint convex polygons (the hulls of each half), represented as ordered vertex lists (CCW order).

### Phase 3 — MERGE (Hull Merging)

This is the most critical step. We need to "stitch" the two convex polygons $H_L$ and $H_R$ into a single convex hull $H$.

**Algorithm to find the Upper Tangent:**
1. Let $p \in H_L$ be the rightmost point of $H_L$ and $q \in H_R$ be the leftmost point of $H_R$ (initial candidates).
2. While $(p, q)$ is **not** the upper tangent, do:
   - Check the vertex $p_{\text{next}}$ immediately clockwise from $p$ on $H_L$. If $p_{\text{next}}$ lies **above** the line $\overline{pq}$, move $p \leftarrow p_{\text{next}}$.
   - Check the vertex $q_{\text{prev}}$ immediately counter-clockwise from $q$ on $H_R$. If $q_{\text{prev}}$ lies **above** the line $\overline{pq}$, move $q \leftarrow q_{\text{prev}}$.
3. The pair $(p, q)$ is the upper tangent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mirror procedure** gives the lower tangent by checking **below** the line and moving vertices in the opposite directions. The two tangents divide the boundary of $H_L \cup H_R$ into four chains; the outer two chains together form the merged convex hull $H$.

### Why It Works — The Core Logic

- Since $H_L$ and $H_R$ are convex, the **upper tangent** is unique and is found by a "rotational sweep" that converges monotonically.
- The combined procedure visits each vertex of $H_L$ and $H_R$ at most once during tangent finding, leading to **linear-time merging**.

## 2.2 Recurrence Relation & Complexity

The time complexity satisfies the recurrence:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$$

**Solving by Master Theorem (Case 2):**

$$T(n) = O(n \log n)$$

Including the initial sort:

$$T_{\text{total}}(n) = O(n \log n) + O(n \log n) = O(n \log n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result**: The D&C convex hull algorithm matches the optimal $\Omega(n \log n)$ lower bound for sorting-based convex hull algorithms and is asymptotically optimal in the **algebraic decision tree model**.

## 2.3 Line Segment Intersection — Detailed Analysis

### Brute-Force Approach

- Test every pair of segments: $\binom{n}{2} = O(n^2)$ tests.
- Each orientation test: $O(1)$.
- **Total: $O(n^2)$** — feasible only for small $n$.

### Plane Sweep Algorithm (Shamos-Hoey, 1976) — Optimal Approach

The classical optimal algorithm uses the **Bentley-Ottmann paradigm**:

1. **Sweep Line**: A vertical line $L$ sweeps from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$.
2. **Event Queue (Q)**: Ordered set of $x$-coordinates of segment endpoints.
3. **Status Structure (T)**: Ordered set of segments currently intersected by $L$, ordered by $y$-coordinate at $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Event Types**:
> - **Upper endpoint**: Segment enters the sweep.
> - **Lower endpoint**: Segment leaves the sweep.
> - **Intersection**: Two segments swap their order in the status structure.

**Complexity:**

$$T(n, k) = O((n + k) \log n)$$

where $k$ is the number of intersection points reported. This is optimal because we must at least read the input ($O(n)$) and report the output ($O(k)$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Use Case | Complexity |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------|-----------:|
| 1 | Convex Hull Definition | $CH(S) = \left\{ \sum \lambda_i p_i \;\vert\; \lambda_i \geq 0, \sum \lambda_i = 1 \right\}$ | Formal mathematical definition | — |
| 2 | D&C Recurrence | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$ | Convex hull time | $O(n \log n)$ |
| 3 | Master Theorem Case 2 | $a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = n \Rightarrow n^{\log_b a} = n$ | Solving recurrences | $O(n \log n)$ |
| 4 | Orientation Test | $\text{orient}(a,b,c) = (b_x - a_x)(c_y - a_y) - (b_y - a_y)(c_x - a_x)$ | CCW/CW/Collinear test | $O(1)$ |
| 5 | Segment Intersection Condition | $\text{orient}(p_1,p_2,p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1,p_2,p_4) \leq 0$ and $\text{orient}(p_3,p_4,p_1) \cdot \text{orient}(p_3,p_4,p_2) \leq 0$ | Two segments intersect | $O(1)$ per pair |
| 6 | On-Segment Test | $x = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1),\; y = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1),\; 0 \leq t \leq 1$ | Collinear overlap check | $O(1)$ |
| 7 | Brute-Force Intersection | $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs | Naive baseline | $O(n^2)$ |
| 8 | Plane Sweep Complexity | $O((n + k) \log n)$ | Optimal intersection | Optimal |
| 9 | Lower Bound for Convex Hull | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | Sorting-based model | Lower bound |
| 10 | Kirkpatrick–Seidel | $O(n \log h)$ | Output-sensitive hull | Optimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas underpin GIS systems (boundary detection), VLSI design (wire crossings), robotics (collision detection), computer graphics (visibility culling), and computational fluid dynamics (mesh generation).

## 2.5 Why These Algorithms Matter in Real Engineering

- **VLSI Design**: Chips contain millions of wire segments. Detecting crossings before fabrication saves millions in manufacturing cost.
- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**: Road/utility network analysis requires fast intersection queries.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles**: Path planning needs real-time collision detection with moving obstacles.
- **Computer Graphics**: Ray-tracing and hidden surface removal rely on efficient segment-polygon intersection.
- **CAD/CAM Systems**: Tool path planning in CNC machines must avoid intersecting tool trajectories.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Complexity of Divide and Conquer Convex Hull

We derive the time complexity $T(n)$ of the Preparata-Hong D&C convex hull algorithm in full, step by step.

### Step 1: Decompose the Cost

The total cost $T(n)$ consists of three components:

$$T(n) = \underbrace{T_{\text{divide}}(n)}_{\text{splitting}} + \underbrace{2 \cdot T(n/2)}_{\text{recursive hulls}} + \underbrace{T_{\text{merge}}(n)}_{\text{combining tangents}}$$

### Step 2: Cost of the Divide Step

- Sorting the $n$ points by $x$-coordinate once at the top level: $O(n \log n)$.
- However, since the points can be pre-sorted globally before recursion begins, this cost is amortized to $O(n \log n)$ overall.
- Finding the median and partitioning: $O(n)$.

$$T_{\text{divide}}(n) = O(n)$$

### Step 3: Cost of the Merge Step

- **Finding the upper tangent**: A two-pointer walk. The pointer on $H_L$ moves only in the clockwise direction, the pointer on $H_R$ moves only in the counter-clockwise direction. Each vertex of $H_L$ and $H_R$ is examined at most **once**. The total number of vertices is at most $\vert H_L \vert + \vert H_R \vert \leq n$.

$$T_{\text{upper tangent}} = O(\vert H_L \vert + \vert H_R \vert) = O(n)$$

- **Finding the lower tangent**: Symmetric argument, also $O(n)$.

$$T_{\text{merge}}(n) = O(n) + O(n) = O(n)$$

### Step 4: Form the Recurrence

Substituting into Step 1:

$$T(n) = O(n) + 2 T(n/2) + O(n)$$

Combining the linear terms:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + c \cdot n$$

for some constant $c > 0$, with the base case $T(1) = T(2) = T(3) = O(1)$.

### Step 5: Solve by Recursion Tree (Verification of Master Theorem)

Each level $i$ of the recursion tree has $2^i$ subproblems, each of size $n / 2^i$, contributing $c \cdot (n / 2^i)$ work per subproblem.

**Work at level $i$:**

$$W_i = 2^i \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = c \cdot n$$

**Number of levels** (depth of recursion): $\log_2 n$.

**Total work:**

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} W_i = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} c \cdot n = c \cdot n \cdot \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n \log n)}$$

### Step 6: Adding the Pre-Sorting Cost

The initial sort costs $O(n \log n)$. Since the divide step reuses sorted subarrays (no re-sorting within recursion), the **amortized total** is:

$$T_{\text{total}}(n) = O(n \log n) + O(n \log n) = O(n \log n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: The asymptotic complexity is $O(n \log n)$, which is **optimal** for the algebraic decision tree model (as shown by the sorting reduction). The D&C algorithm is therefore **asymptotically optimal** among comparison-based convex hull algorithms.

## 3.2 Derivation: Line Segment Intersection via Plane Sweep

### Step 1: Event Point Definition

For each segment $s_i = \overline{(x_i^{\text{left}}, y_i^{\text{left}})(x_i^{\text{right}}, y_i^{\text{right}})}$, we define two events:
- $u_i = (x_i^{\text{left}}, y_i^{\text{left}})$: upper (or left) endpoint.
- $l_i = (x_i^{\text{right}}, y_i^{\text{right}})$: lower (or right) endpoint.

**Total events**: $2n$ endpoint events $+ k$ intersection events.

### Step 2: Status Structure

The status structure $T$ is a balanced binary search tree (e.g., AVL or Red-Black tree) that maintains the segments intersecting the sweep line $L$, ordered by their $y$-coordinate at $L$.

- **Insertion at upper endpoint**: $O(\log n)$.
- **Deletion at lower endpoint**: $O(\log n)$.
- **Swap at intersection**: Two adjacent segments in $T$ are swapped — $O(\log n)$.

### Step 3: Total Cost

Each of the $2n$ endpoint events triggers $O(\log n)$ work:

$$T_{\text{endpoints}} = 2n \cdot O(\log n) = O(n \log n)$$

Each of the $k$ intersection events also triggers $O(\log n)$ work:

$$T_{\text{intersections}} = k \cdot O(\log n) = O(k \log n)$$

**Total cost:**

$$T(n, k) = O(n \log n) + O(k \log n) = O((n + k) \log n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Sensitivity**: The $O((n + k) \log n)$ complexity is **output-sensitive** — the running time depends on $k$, the number of intersection points actually reported. This is highly desirable in sparse intersection scenarios.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Tangent Merging

Consider two convex polygons:
- $H_L$ (left hull) with vertices: $A = (1, 3)$, $B = (2, 1)$, $C = (3, 2)$ (CCW order).
- $H_R$ (right hull) with vertices: $D = (5, 1)$, $E = (6, 3)$, $F = (7, 2)$ (CCW order).

**Step 1**: Identify rightmost of $H_L$ and leftmost of $H_R$ as initial candidates.
- Rightmost of $H_L$: $C = (3, 2)$.
- Leftmost of $H_R$: $D = (5, 1)$.

**Step 2**: Check if $\overline{CD}$ is the upper tangent. Compute the orientation of the candidate line with respect to neighboring vertices.

**Step 3**: Adjust pointers by checking whether the next CW vertex of $H_L$ or next CCW vertex of $H_R$ lies above the current line.

**Step 4**: Iterate until both checks confirm the upper tangent.

**Step 5**: Mirror the procedure for the lower tangent.

**Step 6**: The merged hull $H$ contains the upper chain of $H_R$ + upper tangent + upper chain of $H_L$ (reversed) + lower tangent.

> [!NOTE]
> The total number of orientation tests in the merge is at most $2 \cdot (\vert H_L \vert + \vert H_R \vert)$, confirming the **linear merge time**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Approaches

| Method | Preprocessing | Query/Detection | Total Complexity | Output-Sensitive | Optimal |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|---------|
| Convex Hull — Gift Wrapping (Jarvis March) | $O(1)$ | $O(nh)$ | $O(nh)$ | Yes | For $h \ll n$ |
| Convex Hull — Graham Scan | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | No | Yes (general) |
| Convex Hull — D&C (Preparata-Hong) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | No | Yes |
| Convex Hull — Kirkpatrick-Seidel | $O(n \log h)$ | — | $O(n \log h)$ | Yes | Yes (optimal output-sensitive) |
| Segment Intersection — Brute Force | $O(1)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | No | No |
| Segment Intersection — Plane Sweep | $O(n \log n)$ | $O((n+k)\log n)$ | $O((n+k)\log n)$ | Yes | Yes |

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        V3[Vertex p further CW]
    end

    subgraph RightHull[Right Hull HR]

        W2[Vertex q CCW next]

    end

    V1 -- candidate upper tangent --> W1
    V2 -- after move --> W2
    V3 -- converges --> W3

    Check1[Orientation Test: pnext above line pq]
    Check2[Orientation Test: qprev above line pq]
    Loop{Is upper tangent found?}

    V2 --> Check1
    W2 --> Check2
    Check1 --> Loop
    Check2 --> Loop
    Loop -- No --> Move[Move p or q pointer]
    Move --> V3
```

```mermaid

    EventType -- Lower Endpoint --> DeleteS[Delete segment from Status T]
```
