# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Define the convex hull of a set of points. State and justify the time complexity of the divide and conquer algorithm for computing the convex hull.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The convex hull of a finite set of points $S$ in the plane is the smallest convex polygon containing all points of $S$. Formally, it is the set of all convex combinations of points in $S$:

$$CH(S) = \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i p_i \;\vert\; \lambda_i \geq 0, \sum \lambda_i = 1 \right\}$$

The D&C algorithm sorts the points by $x$-coordinate ($O(n \log n)$), recursively computes hulls of left and right halves, and merges them by finding upper and lower tangent lines in $O(n)$ time. This gives the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$, which solves by the Master Theorem to $\boxed{T(n) = O(n \log n)}$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> What is the line segment intersection problem? Mention any two real-world applications.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The line segment intersection problem: given $n$ line segments $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ in the plane, determine whether any pair of segments intersect and report all such intersection points.

**Two Real-World Applications:**

1. **VLSI Circuit Design**: Detecting crossings in millions of wire segments before fabrication prevents costly re-spins.
2. **GIS / Road Networks**: Computing road intersections for navigation, traffic analysis, and map rendering.
3. *(Bonus)* **Computer Graphics / Ray Tracing**: Determining where light rays intersect scene geometry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The KTU pattern requires **internal choice**, so two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Explain the divide and conquer algorithm for computing the convex hull of a set of $n$ points in the plane. Derive its time complexity.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Pre-sort** all $n$ points by $x$-coordinate (and $y$ as tie-breaker). Cost: $O(n \log n)$.

2. **Divide**: Split the set at the median $x$-coordinate into left subset $S_L$ (size $\lceil n/2 \rceil$) and right subset $S_R$ (size $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$). Cost: $O(n)$.

3. **Conquer**: Recursively compute $CH(S_L)$ and $CH(S_R)$. Base case: $\leq 3$ points returns the trivial hull.

4. **Merge**: Stitch the two hulls $H_L$ and $H_R$ using the **upper tangent** and **lower tangent**:
   - **Upper Tangent Algorithm** (Preparata-Hong):
     - Initialize $p$ = rightmost vertex of $H_L$, $q$ = leftmost vertex of $H_R$.
     - Loop:
       - If $p_{\text{next CW}}$ lies above $\overline{pq}$, advance $p \leftarrow p_{\text{next}}$.
       - If $q_{\text{prev CCW}}$ lies above $\overline{pq}$, advance $q \leftarrow q_{\text{prev}}$.
     - Terminate when no advance is needed.
   - **Lower Tangent**: Mirror procedure.
   - The merged hull consists of: upper chain of $H_R$ + upper tangent + upper chain of $H_L$ reversed + lower tangent.

5. **Merge Cost**: $O(\vert H_L \vert + \vert H_R \vert) = O(n)$.

**Recurrence:**

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), \quad T(3) = O(1)$$

**Solving by Master Theorem** ($a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = n$): Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2})$, this is **Case 2**:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n \log n)}$$

**Verification by Recursion Tree** (as shown in Section 3.1): Each of $\log_2 n$ levels contributes $O(n)$ work, yielding $O(n \log n)$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> Describe the plane sweep algorithm for detecting all intersections among $n$ line segments. State its time complexity and explain why it is optimal in the output-sensitive sense.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Bentley-Ottmann / Shamos-Hoey):**

1. **Event Queue $Q$**: A priority queue (sorted by $x$-coordinate) containing:
   - **Upper endpoints** of all segments: $2n$ events.
   - **Lower endpoints** of all segments: as discovered.
   - **Intersection points**: dynamically inserted.

2. **Status Structure $T$**: A balanced BST (Red-Black or AVL) maintaining segments currently intersecting the sweep line $L$, ordered by their $y$-coordinate at $L$.

3. **Event Processing Loop**:
   - Extract the next event $e$ from $Q$.
   - **Upper endpoint**: Insert segment into $T$. Check its new neighbors in $T$ for future intersections; insert those events into $Q$.
   - **Lower endpoint**: Remove segment from $T$. Its neighbors may now intersect; insert that event into $Q$.
   - **Intersection event**: Swap the two segments in $T$. Re-check the new neighbors of each; insert events into $Q$.

4. **Termination**: When $Q$ is empty, all detected intersections have been reported.

**Time Complexity:**

- Each of the $2n$ endpoint events: $O(\log n)$ for queue/status operations.
- Each of the $k$ intersection events: $O(\log n)$ for swapping and updating neighbors.

$$T(n, k) = 2n \cdot O(\log n) + k \cdot O(\log n) = \boxed{O((n + k) \log n)}$$

**Optimality Argument:**

- **Lower bound on input processing**: We must read all $n$ segments $\Rightarrow \Omega(n)$.
- **Lower bound on output reporting**: We must report all $k$ intersections $\Rightarrow \Omega(k)$.
- The plane sweep achieves $O((n + k) \log n)$, which is within a $\log n$ factor of the trivial lower bound $\Omega(n + k)$.
- This is **optimal** in the **output-sensitive model** (Chazelle, 1988; shows that with appropriate modifications, the $\log n$ factor can be removed for reporting all $k$ intersections, achieving $O(n \log n + k)$).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Define orientation of three points. Using the orientation test, derive the conditions for two line segments to intersect.

**Model Solution:**

**Orientation Definition:**

The orientation of an ordered triple of points $(a, b, c)$ in the plane is given by the sign of the cross product:

$$\text{orient}(a, b, c) = (b_x - a_x)(c_y - a_y) - (b_y - a_y)(c_x - a_x)$$

- $\text{orient}(a, b, c) > 0$: counter-clockwise (left turn) — $c$ lies to the **left** of $\overrightarrow{ab}$.
- $\text{orient}(a, b, c) < 0$: clockwise (right turn) — $c$ lies to the **right** of $\overrightarrow{ab}$.
- $\text{orient}(a, b, c) = 0$: collinear — $a, b, c$ lie on a single line.

**Intersection Conditions for $s_1 = \overline{p_1 p_2}$ and $s_2 = \overline{p_3 p_4}$:**

Two segments intersect if and only if **both** of the following hold:

**Condition 1 (Straddle Test):**

$$\text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_4) \leq 0$$

This means $p_3$ and $p_4$ lie on **opposite sides** (or one is collinear) with respect to the line $\overline{p_1 p_2}$.

**Condition 2 (Straddle Test):**

$$\text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_1) \cdot \text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_2) \leq 0$$

This means $p_1$ and $p_2$ lie on **opposite sides** (or one is collinear) with respect to the line $\overline{p_3 p_4}$.

**Special Case (Collinearity)**:

If both segments are collinear ($\text{orient} = 0$ for all triples), they intersect if and only if their **1D projections** on either $x$ or $y$ axis overlap. Specifically, define:

$$t_1 = \frac{(p_3 - p_1) \cdot (p_2 - p_1)}{\vert p_2 - p_1 \vert^2}, \quad t_2 = \frac{(p_4 - p_1) \cdot (p_2 - p_1)}{\vert p_2 - p_1 \vert^2}$$

The segments intersect iff $\min(t_1, t_2) \leq 1$ and $\max(t_1, t_2) \geq 0$.

**Combined Decision Rule:**

$$s_1 \cap s_2 \neq \emptyset \iff \big[\text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_4) \leq 0\big] \wedge \big[\text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_1) \cdot \text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_2) \leq 0\big]$$

Cost per test: $O(1)$ — four orientation evaluations plus at most two interval checks.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> Compare the brute-force and plane sweep approaches for line segment intersection. Discuss when each is preferred.

**Model Solution:**

**Brute-Force Approach:**

- **Method**: For every pair of segments $(s_i, s_j)$ with $1 \leq i < j \leq n$, apply the orientation-based intersection test.
- **Time Complexity**: $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs $\times$ $O(1)$ test $= O(n^2)$.
- **Space**: $O(1)$ auxiliary.
- **Implementation**: Trivial; no preprocessing.
- **Output**: All intersecting pairs.

**Plane Sweep Approach (Bentley-Ottmann / Shamos-Hoey):**

- **Method**: Sweep a vertical line from left to right; maintain a status structure of currently intersected segments; detect intersection events dynamically.
- **Time Complexity**: $O((n + k) \log n)$ where $k$ is the number of intersections.
- **Space**: $O(n)$ for the status structure and event queue.
- **Implementation**: More complex; requires balanced BST and careful event scheduling.
- **Output**: All intersection points (with multiplicities).

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Brute-Force | Plane Sweep |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| Time Complexity | $O(n^2)$ | $O((n+k) \log n)$ |
| Space | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Implementation Difficulty | Trivial | Moderate to High |
| Preprocessing | None | $O(n \log n)$ sort |
| When $k = 0$ (no intersections) | Still $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| When $k = \Theta(n^2)$ (dense) | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2 \log n)$ — worse! |
| Output Type | Pairs of segments | Intersection points |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to Prefer Each:**

- **Prefer Brute-Force when**:
  - $n$ is small (e.g., $n < 100$).
  - $k$ is expected to be $\Theta(n^2)$ (dense intersections).
  - Implementation simplicity is critical.
  - Hardware has very limited memory.

- **Prefer Plane Sweep when**:
  - $n$ is large (e.g., $n \geq 10^3$).
  - $k$ is expected to be small or moderate ($k \ll n^2$).
  - Real-time performance is required (e.g., GIS, robotics).
  - Memory is sufficient to maintain the status structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Students often forget to handle the **collinear case** when deriving intersection conditions. The inequality should be $\leq 0$, not $< 0$. Missing this loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: In the D&C convex hull merge step, students frequently confuse the **upper** and **lower tangent** directions. Remember: upper tangent uses the **top chain** of both hulls; lower tangent uses the **bottom chain**. Drawing a wrong sketch costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: When asked for time complexity, students write "$O(n \log n)$" without showing the **Master Theorem application** or the **recurrence**. KTU examiners require the **derivation** — show $a, b, f(n)$ and the case number.
>
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Confusing **orient** sign convention. KTU uses the convention $\text{orient}(a,b,c) > 0$ for **CCW** (left turn). Reversing this loses marks.
>
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: In plane sweep, omitting the **status structure** in the explanation. KTU specifically tests whether students understand the role of the **status** (active segments) vs. the **event queue**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Convex Hull — D&C Algorithm
- [ ] **Definition**: Smallest convex polygon containing all input points.
- [ ] **Three Phases**: Divide (by $x$-coordinate) → Conquer (recursive hulls) → Merge (upper & lower tangents).
- [ ] **Recurrence**: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$.
- [ ] **Complexity**: $O(n \log n)$ — **asymptotically optimal**.
- [ ] **Pre-sorting**: $O(n \log n)$ global sort amortizes over recursion.
- [ ] **Tangent Finding**: Two-pointer walk; each vertex examined at most once.
- [ ] **Base Case**: $\leq 3$ points → trivial hull (point, segment, or triangle).
- [ ] **Lower Bound**: $\Omega(n \log n)$ in algebraic decision tree model.

### Line Segment Intersection
- [ ] **Problem**: Detect/report all pairs of intersecting segments among $n$ segments.
- [ ] **Brute-Force**: $O(n^2)$ — test all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs via orientation test.
- [ ] **Orientation Function**: $\text{orient}(a,b,c) = (b_x - a_x)(c_y - a_y) - (b_y - a_y)(c_x - a_x)$.
- [ ] **Sign Convention**: Positive = CCW (left turn), Negative = CW (right turn), Zero = Collinear.
- [ ] **Intersection Condition**: Both straddle tests must satisfy $\leq 0$.
- [ ] **Plane Sweep Complexity**: $O((n + k) \log n)$ — **output-sensitive**.
- [ ] **Status Structure**: Balanced BST of active segments ordered by $y$-coordinate.
- [ ] **Event Queue**: Priority queue of endpoints and intersection events.
- [ ] **Event Types**: Upper endpoint, lower endpoint, intersection.
- [ ] **Real-World Apps**: VLSI, GIS, robotics, computer graphics, CAD/CAM.

### Key Identifiers for KTU Board Exams
- [ ] Master Theorem cases: **Case 1** ($f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$), **Case 2** ($f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$), **Case 3** ($f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$).
- [ ] For D&C convex hull: $a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = n \Rightarrow$ **Case 2** $\Rightarrow O(n \log n)$.
- [ ] Output-sensitive complexity is preferred in modern CG algorithms.
- [ ] Kirkpatrick-Seidel algorithm achieves $O(n \log h)$ — optimal for **output-sensitive** convex hull.

### Cross-Reference to KTU 2024 Course Outcomes
- **CO1**: Apply fundamental geometric primitives (orientation, intersection tests).
- **CO2**: Analyze time and space complexity of CG algorithms.
- **CO3**: Implement divide-and-conquer and sweep-line paradigms.
- **CO4**: Identify real-world applications of convex hull and intersection problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plane Sweep Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plane Sweep Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Plane Sweep Algorithm** (also called the **Sweep Line Paradigm**) is a fundamental algorithmic technique in computational geometry where a conceptual line (the *sweep line*) is translated across the embedding plane in a fixed direction, typically from left to right (i.e., along increasing x-coordinate). The algorithm maintains a dynamic data structure of geometric objects that are currently *active* (i.e., intersecting the sweep line) and processes *events* that occur as the sweep line encounters significant geometric features (such as endpoints of line segments or intersection points).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The plane sweep paradigm is a *meta-algorithm*; it is not a single algorithm but a framework that, when specialized with a specific problem, yields concrete algorithms like the **Bentley–Ottmann** algorithm (line segment intersection), **Andrew's monotone chain** (convex hull), and the **Fortune's algorithm** (Voronoi diagrams).

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Components of a Plane Sweep Algorithm

Every plane sweep algorithm, irrespective of the problem it solves, is composed of the following four logical components:

1. **The Sweep Line (L)**: An imaginary vertical line $\ell$ defined by the equation $x = c$, where $c$ is the current sweep position. The line moves monotonically from $x = -\infty$ to $x = +\infty$.

2. **The Event Queue (Q)**: A priority queue (typically a binary heap) that stores all *event points* sorted by their x-coordinate (and secondarily by y-coordinate, to break ties). The minimum of the queue represents the next point the sweep line will reach.

3. **The Sweep Line Status Structure (T)**: A dynamic data structure (usually a balanced binary search tree, BBST) that maintains the set of geometric objects currently *intersecting* the sweep line $\ell$, ordered by their y-coordinate (or by some natural ordering) at the current sweep position.

4. **The Event Handler**: A set of procedures that update the sweep line status structure $T$ when an event is processed. Depending on the event type (left endpoint, right endpoint, intersection, etc.), the handler inserts, removes, or swaps objects in $T$, and schedules new events as a consequence.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Windshield Wiper and the Road.**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car at night, and your windshield wiper is sweeping across the windshield, revealing the road in front of you in vertical strips. As the wiper moves:
>
> - The wiper itself is the **sweep line** $\ell$.
> - Each new strip of road revealed corresponds to the **current sweep position** $x = c$.
> - The "things" of interest on the road (lane markings, potholes, signposts) are the **geometric objects** (e.g., line segments).
> - The intersections between lane markings and the wiper's current path are the **active objects** tracked in the **sweep line status structure**.
> - When the wiper reaches a road sign, that is an **event** in the event queue.
> - Just as the driver does not have to scan the *entire* road at once but only the strip currently revealed, the plane sweep algorithm avoids quadratic scanning by processing only objects that are spatially local to the sweep line.

A second, equally effective analogy is the **air traffic control radar**: the radar dish rotates (analogous to the sweep line) and only tracks aircraft currently in the *beam* (the active set), while aircraft that have left the beam are removed from the active list.

## 1.4 Why is Plane Sweep Efficient?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The plane sweep paradigm achieves efficiency by exploiting a **temporal–spatial locality principle**: geometric problems that are *non-local* in the static plane (e.g., "do these two segments intersect?") often become **local** along the sweep line. This allows the algorithm to focus its computational effort only on objects that are "close" to the current sweep position, avoiding the $O(n^2)$ pairwise comparison overhead of the naive approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: The General Plane Sweep Pseudocode

The general structure of a plane sweep algorithm can be expressed as the following sequence of operations:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Identify all *event points* of the input (e.g., endpoints of segments, query points, candidate intersections).
   - Insert all event points into a priority queue $Q$ keyed by $(x, y)$ lexicographic order.
   - Initialize the sweep line status structure $T$ as empty.

2. **Main Loop: While $Q$ is not empty**
   - **Extract-Min Operation:** Pop the event point $p$ with the smallest x-coordinate from $Q$.
   - **Advance the Sweep Line:** Update $\ell$ so that $\ell$ now passes through $p$ (i.e., set $c \leftarrow p_x$).
   - **Event Classification:** Determine the type of $p$ (left endpoint, right endpoint, intersection, query, etc.).
   - **Dispatch to Event Handler:** Invoke the handler corresponding to the event type.
   - **Event Handler Actions:**
     - **Insertion events** (e.g., left endpoint of a segment): Insert the new object into $T$. Query $T$ to find the *neighbors* of the new object (the objects immediately above and below it in $T$). Test these neighbors for new intersections. If new intersections are found *in the future* (i.e., to the right of $p$), add them to $Q$.
     - **Deletion events** (e.g., right endpoint of a segment): Find the neighbors of the object in $T$. Remove the object from $T$. Test the *new pair of neighbors* for an intersection occurring in the future, and add it to $Q$ if found.
     - **Intersection events**: The two intersecting objects swap their relative order in $T$. Test the *new pairs of neighbors* (now different due to the swap) for future intersections and add them to $Q$.

3. **Termination Phase**
   - When $Q$ is empty, the algorithm terminates. All discovered intersections (or other results) have been reported.

## 2.2 The Invariant: Why the Algorithm is Correct

The plane sweep algorithm maintains the following **invariant** at all times:

> The sweep line status structure $T$ contains exactly those objects that intersect the sweep line $\ell$ at the current x-coordinate $c$, ordered by their intersection with $\ell$.

This invariant guarantees that when two objects $s_i$ and $s_j$ in $T$ are *consecutive* (no other object lies between them in $T$), then if they are to intersect in the future, the intersection will occur before any other object "passes between" them. This is the key insight that limits the number of candidate intersections to $O(n + k)$ instead of $\binom{n}{2}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **time and space complexity formulas** that examiners expect students to memorize for plane sweep algorithms. Note: $\vert S \vert$ denotes cardinality of set $S$.

| Algorithm / Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Key Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| General plane sweep (segment intersection) | $O\big((n + k) \log n\big)$ | $O(n + k)$ | $n$ = number of input segments, $k$ = number of intersections |
| Building the initial event queue | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Sorting $2n$ endpoints (or equivalent) |
| Each event-queue operation (extract-min, insert) | $O(\log n)$ | - | Priority queue with $O(n + k)$ entries |
| Each sweep-line status operation | $O(\log n)$ | - | BBST augmented with $O(1)$ neighbor queries |
| Closest pair of points (with plane sweep) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $n$ = number of points |
| 2D Range counting (offline) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $n$ = number of points and query rectangles |
| Trapezoidal decomposition | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $n$ = number of segments |
| Bentley–Ottmann (degenerate case: $k = O(n^2)$) | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | Worst case when all segments pairwise intersect |
| Convex hull (Andrew's monotone chain) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $n$ = number of points |

**Key asymptotic relationships:**

$$
T_{\text{plane sweep}} = T_{\text{sort}} + T_{\text{events}} = O(n \log n) + O((n + k) \log n) = O((n + k) \log n)
$$

$$
S_{\text{plane sweep}} = S_{\text{event queue}} + S_{\text{status}} = O(n + k) + O(n) = O(n + k)
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where do plane sweep algorithms appear in industry?**
>
> - **VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) Design Rule Checking:** Verifying that millions of wire segments on a chip do not violate minimum-spacing rules (a direct application of Bentley–Ottmann).
> - **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) and CAM:** Boolean operations on polygons, mesh generation, and offset curve computation.
> - **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Computing intersections of road networks, polygon overlay for map analysis.
> - **Computer Graphics:** Hidden surface removal, ray tracing acceleration, polygon clipping (Sutherland–Hodgman is a degenerate 1D plane sweep).
> - **Robotics and Motion Planning:** Collision detection between moving obstacles, configuration space analysis.
> - **Computational Biology:** Aligning genomic maps, finding common intervals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Detecting All Intersections Using Plane Sweep

To make the plane sweep paradigm concrete, we work through a complete numerical example. Consider the following set of three line segments:

$$
S_1 : (0,\, 0) \rightarrow (6,\, 6), \quad \text{slope} = 1, \quad y = x
$$

$$
S_2 : (0,\, 6) \rightarrow (6,\, 0), \quad \text{slope} = -1, \quad y = -x + 6
$$

$$
S_3 : (2,\, 1) \rightarrow (5,\, 3), \quad \text{slope} = \tfrac{2}{3}, \quad y = \tfrac{2}{3}x - \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

### Step 1: Identify the Ground-Truth Intersections (Analytical Sanity Check)

We will verify our plane sweep output against direct algebra. The intersection of $S_1$ and $S_2$:

$$
x = -x + 6 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2x = 6 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 3, \; y = 3
$$

This is the point $I_1 = (3, 3)$, and both $x = 3$ lies in $[0, 6]$ for both segments.

The intersection of $S_1$ and $S_3$:

$$
x = \tfrac{2}{3}x - \tfrac{1}{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{3}x = -\tfrac{1}{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; x = -1
$$

Since $-1 \notin [0, 6]$, the segments $S_1$ and $S_3$ do **not** intersect.

The intersection of $S_2$ and $S_3$:

$$
-x + 6 = \tfrac{2}{3}x - \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\Longrightarrow\; -3x + 18 = 2x - 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; -5x = -19 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = \tfrac{19}{5} = 3.8
$$

$$
y = -3.8 + 6 = 2.2
$$

This is the point $I_2 = (3.8,\; 2.2)$, and both segments' x-ranges contain $3.8$.

> **Ground truth:** Two intersections: $I_1 = (3, 3)$ and $I_2 = (3.8, 2.2)$.

### Step 2: Build and Sort the Event Queue

We extract all 6 endpoints and sort them lexicographically by $(x, y)$:

| Event ID | Coordinates | Type | Segment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_1$ | $(0, 0)$ | Left endpoint | $S_1$ |
| $E_2$ | $(0, 6)$ | Left endpoint | $S_2$ |
| $E_3$ | $(2, 1)$ | Left endpoint | $S_3$ |
| $E_4$ | $(3, 3)$ | Intersection | $S_1 \cap S_2$ |
| $E_5$ | $(3.8, 2.2)$ | Intersection | $S_2 \cap S_3$ |
| $E_6$ | $(5, 3)$ | Right endpoint | $S_3$ |
| $E_7$ | $(6, 0)$ | Right endpoint | $S_2$ |
| $E_8$ | $(6, 6)$ | Right endpoint | $S_1$ |

> **Note:** $E_4$ and $E_5$ are *predicted* intersection events that the algorithm will discover and add dynamically — they are not pre-loaded into the queue.

### Step 3: Trace the Plane Sweep Execution

We now execute the main loop. The status structure $T$ is initially empty. The y-coordinates of segments at the current sweep position are denoted $y_i(c)$.

#### Event $E_1 = (0, 0)$: Left endpoint of $S_1$

- **Action:** Insert $S_1$ into $T$.
- $T$ at $x = 0$ (bottom to top): $\{S_1\}$
- **Neighbor check:** $S_1$ has no neighbor above and no neighbor below.
- **New events scheduled:** None.

#### Event $E_2 = (0, 6)$: Left endpoint of $S_2$

- **Action:** Insert $S_2$ into $T$. At $x = 0$: $y_1(0) = 0$, $y_2(0) = 6$. So $S_1$ is below $S_2$.
- $T$ at $x = 0$: $\{S_1, S_2\}$
- **Neighbor check:** $S_2$'s lower neighbor is $S_1$. Test $S_1$ and $S_2$ for future intersection.
  - Solve $y_1 = y_2$ (already done in Step 1): $x = 3, y = 3$.
  - Since $3 > 0$, the intersection is in the *future*.
- **New events scheduled:** Insert $E_4 = (3, 3)$ into $Q$ as an intersection event.

#### Event $E_3 = (2, 1)$: Left endpoint of $S_3$

- **Action:** Insert $S_3$ into $T$. At $x = 2$: $y_1(2) = 2$, $y_2(2) = 4$, $y_3(2) = 1$. So $S_3$ (y=1) is the lowest, then $S_1$ (y=2), then $S_2$ (y=4).
- $T$ at $x = 2$: $\{S_3, S_1, S_2\}$
- **Neighbor check:**
  - $S_3$'s upper neighbor is $S_1$. Test $S_3 \cap S_1$: $x = -1$ (computed in Step 1). Not in $[2, 5] \cap [0, 6]$ within future window. **No event.**
  - $S_3$'s lower neighbor: none. **No event.**

#### Event $E_4 = (3, 3)$: Intersection of $S_1$ and $S_2$

- **Action:** This is an *intersection* event. $S_1$ and $S_2$ swap their relative order in $T$ (because, just past $x = 3$, the order of $y$-values inverts: $y_1 > y_2$ for $x > 3$).
- $T$ just after $x = 3$ (bottom to top): $\{S_3, S_2, S_1\}$
- **Neighbor check (new neighbors formed by the swap):**
  - New pair $(S_3, S_2)$: Test $S_3 \cap S_2$. Solution: $x = 3.8, y = 2.2$ (from Step 1). Since $3.8 > 3$, this is in the future.
  - **New events scheduled:** Insert $E_5 = (3.8, 2.2)$ into $Q$.
  - New pair $(S_2, S_1)$: Test $S_2 \cap S_1$. Solution: $x = 3, y = 3$. This is the *current* event, not a future one. **No new event.**

#### Event $E_5 = (3.8, 2.2)$: Intersection of $S_2$ and $S_3$

- **Action:** Intersection event. $S_2$ and $S_3$ swap their relative order in $T$.
- At $x = 3.8^+$, the new $y$-order is: $y_3(3.8) = 2.2$ exactly (they touch), and just after, $S_2$ is below $S_3$ (since for $x > 3.8$, $y_2 < y_3$).
- $T$ just after $x = 3.8$ (bottom to top): $\{S_2, S_3, S_1\}$
- **Neighbor check (new neighbors):**
  - New pair $(S_2, S_3)$: Test $S_2 \cap S_3$. Solution is $x = 3.8$ (current). **No new event.**
  - New pair $(S_3, S_1)$: Test $S_3 \cap S_1$. Solution: $x = -1$ (in the past). **No new event.**

#### Event $E_6 = (5, 3)$: Right endpoint of $S_3$

- **Action:** Remove $S_3$ from $T$. The neighbors of $S_3$ were $S_2$ (below) and $S_1$ (above).
- **New pair formed after removal:** $(S_2, S_1)$.
- **Neighbor check:** $S_2 \cap S_1$ at $x = 3$ (in the past). **No new event.**
- $T$ at $x = 5$: $\{S_2, S_1\}$

#### Event $E_7 = (6, 0)$: Right endpoint of $S_2$

- **Action:** Remove $S_2$ from $T$. The only neighbor of $S_2$ was $S_1$.
- **New pair formed:** None (S_1 is alone).
- $T$ at $x = 6$: $\{S_1\}$

#### Event $E_8 = (6, 6)$: Right endpoint of $S_1$

- **Action:** Remove $S_1$ from $T$. $T$ becomes empty.
- **Termination condition:** $Q$ is now empty. Algorithm halts.

### Step 4: Verify the Final Output

The algorithm discovered exactly two intersections, $I_1$ and $I_2$, which **matches the ground truth** computed algebraically in Step 1. The plane sweep produced these results using only **8 events** (vs. the $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ candidate pairs of the naive approach; with more segments the savings are exponential in $n$).

### Step 5: Complexity Analysis of This Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of input events: } & n_{\text{endpoints}} = 6 \\
\text{Number of intersection events scheduled: } & k = 2 \\
\text{Total events processed: } & n + k = 6 + 2 = 8 \\
\text{Time complexity: } & T(n, k) = O((n + k) \log n) = O(8 \log 3) = O(8 \cdot 1.585) \approx O(12.68) \\
\text{Space complexity: } & S(n, k) = O(n + k) = O(8)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The plane sweep algorithm runs in **$O((n + k) \log n)$** time, which, for typical inputs where $k \ll n^2$, is dramatically faster than the naive $O(n^2)$ approach of checking all pairs.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Handler Operations

| Event Type | $T$ Update | New Pairs to Test | Events Added |
|---|---|---|---|
| Left endpoint of $s$ | Insert $s$ into $T$ | (above, $s$) and ($s$, below) | At most 2 new intersection events |
| Right endpoint of $s$ | Remove $s$ from $T$ | (above, below) — the new neighbors of $s$ | At most 1 new intersection event |
| Intersection of $s_i$ and $s_j$ | Swap $s_i$ and $s_j$ in $T$ | (new above, $s_j$) and ($s_i$, new below) | At most 2 new intersection events |

> **Crucial observation:** Each event handler invocation performs only a *constant* number of insertions, deletions, and intersection tests. The total work is therefore $O(\text{number of events}) \times O(\log n) = O((n + k) \log n)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the architecture, event flow, and state transitions of the plane sweep paradigm. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean alphanumeric text as required by the rendering engine.

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: The General Plane Sweep Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start Algorithm] --> stepB[Identify All Event Points from Input]
    stepB --> stepC[Insert Event Points into Priority Queue Q]
    stepC --> stepD[Sort Q Lexicographically by x then y]
    stepD --> stepE[Initialize Empty Status Structure T]
    stepE --> stepF{Is Q Empty?}
    stepF -- No --> stepG[Extract Min Event p from Q]
    stepG --> stepH[Advance Sweep Line to x = p_x]
    stepH --> stepI[Classify Event Type of p]
    stepI --> stepJ[Invoke Corresponding Event Handler]
    stepJ --> stepK[Handler Modifies T]
    stepK --> stepL[Handler Detects New Future Events]
    stepL --> stepM{New Events Found?}
    stepM -- Yes --> stepN[Insert New Events into Q]
    stepM -- No --> stepF
    stepN --> stepF
    stepF -- Yes --> stepO[Report All Discovered Results]
    stepO --> stepP[End Algorithm]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Event Handler: Bentley-Ottmann Segment Intersection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hndlStart[Event Handler Dispatcher] --> hndlType{Event Type?}
    hndlType -- Left Endpoint --> leftH[Insert Segment s into T]
    leftH --> leftN1[Find Above Neighbor s_up of s]
    leftH --> leftN2[Find Below Neighbor s_down of s]
    leftN1 --> leftChk1{Test s_up and s: Future Intersection?}
    leftN2 --> leftChk2{Test s_down and s: Future Intersection?}
    leftChk1 -- Yes --> leftAdd1[Schedule Intersection Event]
    leftChk1 -- No --> leftEnd[Return]
    leftChk2 -- Yes --> leftAdd1
    leftChk2 -- No --> leftEnd
    leftAdd1 --> leftEnd
    
    hndlType -- Right Endpoint --> rightN[Find Above and Below Neighbors of s in T]
    rightN --> rightDel[Remove s from T]
    rightDel --> rightChk{Test New Pair of Neighbors: Future Intersection?}
    rightChk -- Yes --> rightAdd[Schedule Intersection Event]
    rightChk -- No --> rightEnd[Return]
    rightAdd --> rightEnd
    
    hndlType -- Intersection Event --> intSwap[Swap s_i and s_j in T]
    intSwap --> intChk1{Test New Pair: Future Intersection?}
    intSwap --> intChk2{Test New Pair: Future Intersection?}
    intChk1 -- Yes --> intAdd[Schedule Intersection Event]
    intChk2 -- Yes --> intAdd
    intChk1 -- No --> intEnd[Return]
    intChk2 -- No --> intEnd
    intAdd --> intEnd
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram: Sweep Line Progression

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateA: Initial State: Q has 6 endpoints, T is empty
    stateB: After E1: T contains S1
    stateC: After E2: T contains S1, S2; I1 scheduled
    stateD: After E3: T contains S3, S1, S2
    stateE: After E4: I1 detected, swap S1 and S2, I2 scheduled
    stateF: After E5: I2 detected, swap S2 and S3
    stateG: After E6: S3 removed from T
    stateH: After E7: S2 removed from T
    stateI: After E8: S1 removed, T empty
    stateJ: Final: Q empty, two intersections reported
    
    stateA --> stateB : E1 left of S1
    stateB --> stateC : E2 left of S2
    stateC --> stateD : E3 left of S3
    stateD --> stateE : E4 intersection S1 and S2
    stateE --> stateF : E5 intersection S2 and S3
    stateF --> stateG : E6 right of S3
    stateG --> stateH : E7 right of S2
    stateH --> stateI : E8 right of S1
    stateI --> stateJ : Q empty, terminate
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Plane Sweep Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        inp[Input Geometric Objects<br>n segments or points]
    end
    
    subgraph initLayer[INITIALIZATION LAYER]
        ext[Endpoint Extractor]
        srt[Lexicographic Sorter]
    end
    
    subgraph coreLayer[CORE SWEEP ENGINE]
        eq[Event Queue<br>Priority Queue Heap]
        sl[Sweep Line<br>Virtual Line x = c]
        ss[Sweep Line Status<br>Balanced BST]
        eh[Event Handler<br>Dispatcher and Procedures]
    end
    
    subgraph outLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        rep[Reporter]
        res[Result Set<br>k intersections or features]
    end
    
    inp --> ext
    ext --> srt
    srt --> eq
    eq --> eh
    sl -. tracks .-> ss
    eh --> ss
    eh --> eq
    ss --> eh
    eh --> rep
    rep --> res
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is modeled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for PECST418 - Computational Geometry. Part A questions carry **3 marks each**; Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with internal choice and sub-parts of 7 marks each.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the plane sweep paradigm. List its four major components and state the role of each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The plane sweep paradigm is a computational geometry technique in which an imaginary line (the sweep line) is moved across the plane, typically in a fixed direction (left to right), while a data structure tracks geometric objects intersecting the line and events are processed in order of occurrence.

The four major components are:
1. **Sweep Line (L):** The moving line $x = c$ that defines the current "frontier" of the algorithm. *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Event Queue (Q):** A priority queue of event points sorted by x-coordinate, dictating the order of processing. *(0.75 marks)*
3. **Sweep Line Status (T):** A dynamic data structure (usually a BBST) that maintains the set of objects currently intersecting the sweep line, ordered by y-coordinate. *(0.75 marks)*
4. **Event Handler:** A set of procedures that update $T$ and $Q$ in response to each event type. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is an event in a plane sweep algorithm? Identify the three standard event types in the Bentley-Ottmann segment intersection algorithm and explain each in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *event* is a discrete point in the plane at which the combinatorial structure of the sweep line status changes, requiring an update of the data structures.

The three event types in Bentley-Ottmann are: *(1 mark for naming, 2 marks for explanations)*
1. **Left Endpoint Event:** Occurs at the left endpoint of a segment; the segment is inserted into the status structure $T$. *(0.67 marks)*
2. **Right Endpoint Event:** Occurs at the right endpoint of a segment; the segment is removed from $T$. *(0.67 marks)*
3. **Intersection Event:** Occurs at a point where two segments cross; the two segments swap their relative order in $T$. *(0.66 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the general structure of a plane sweep algorithm with a neat diagram. Discuss in detail the roles of the event queue and the sweep line status structure. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Initialization Phase (1.5 marks)**
- Identify all event points from the input (e.g., $2n$ endpoints for $n$ line segments).
- Insert all event points into a priority queue $Q$ keyed by $(x, y)$ lexicographic order.
- Initialize the sweep line status structure $T$ as empty. Set the sweep line to $x = -\infty$.

**2. Main Loop (3.5 marks)**
- **Extract-Min:** Remove the event $p$ with smallest x-coordinate from $Q$.
- **Advance Sweep Line:** Move $\ell$ to $x = p_x$.
- **Classify and Dispatch:** Determine the type of $p$ (left/right endpoint, intersection, etc.) and invoke the appropriate event handler.
- **Event Handler Actions**:
  - Update $T$ (insert, delete, or swap objects).
  - Query $T$ for the *neighbors* of any newly inserted or swapped object (e.g., the segment immediately above and below).
  - Test these neighbor pairs for *future* intersections.
  - If a future intersection is found, add it to $Q$ as a new event.

**3. Termination Phase (0.5 marks)**
- When $Q$ is empty, the algorithm halts and reports all discovered results (e.g., all intersection points).

**Role of the Event Queue (Q):** *(0.75 marks)*
- Acts as the "scheduler" of the algorithm.
- Sorted by x-coordinate, it determines the temporal order in which events are processed.
- Implemented as a binary heap or balanced BST, supporting $O(\log n)$ insert and extract-min.

**Role of the Sweep Line Status (T):** *(0.75 marks)*
- Maintains the "current world view" of the algorithm, namely the set of objects intersecting the sweep line at the current x-coordinate.
- Ordered by y-coordinate of intersection, it enables $O(\log n)$ neighbor queries and updates.
- The key invariant: $T$ contains exactly those objects that intersect the sweep line.

#### (b) Apply the plane sweep technique to detect all intersections among the following three line segments. Show all events and status updates clearly, and derive the time complexity. (7 marks)

$$
S_1: (0, 0) \rightarrow (6, 6), \quad S_2: (0, 6) \rightarrow (6, 0), \quad S_3: (2, 1) \rightarrow (5, 3)
$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Sort Endpoints (1 mark)**
The 6 endpoints sorted by x-coordinate are: $(0,0), (0,6), (2,1), (5,3), (6,0), (6,6)$.

**Step 2: Trace the Algorithm (4 marks)**

| Event | Coordinates | Type | Action | Status T (bottom to top) | New Events |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0, 0)$ | Left $S_1$ | Insert $S_1$ | $\{S_1\}$ | None |
| 2 | $(0, 6)$ | Left $S_2$ | Insert $S_2$ | $\{S_1, S_2\}$ | $S_1 \cap S_2$ at $(3, 3)$ |
| 3 | $(2, 1)$ | Left $S_3$ | Insert $S_3$ | $\{S_3, S_1, S_2\}$ | $S_3 \cap S_1$? No (past). |
| 4 | $(3, 3)$ | Intersection | Swap $S_1, S_2$ | $\{S_3, S_2, S_1\}$ | $S_3 \cap S_2$ at $(3.8, 2.2)$ |
| 5 | $(3.8, 2.2)$ | Intersection | Swap $S_2, S_3$ | $\{S_2, S_3, S_1\}$ | None new |
| 6 | $(5, 3)$ | Right $S_3$ | Remove $S_3$ | $\{S_2, S_1\}$ | $S_2 \cap S_1$? Past, none. |
| 7 | $(6, 0)$ | Right $S_2$ | Remove $S_2$ | $\{S_1\}$ | None |
| 8 | $(6, 6)$ | Right $S_1$ | Remove $S_1$ | $\{\}$ | None |

**Result:** Two intersections detected: $I_1 = (3, 3)$ and $I_2 = (3.8, 2.2)$.

**Step 3: Time Complexity (2 marks)**
- Total events processed: $n + k = 6 + 2 = 8$.
- Each event involves $O(\log n)$ work in the priority queue and $O(\log n)$ work in the status structure.
- Total time:

$$
T(n, k) = O((n + k) \log n) = O((6 + 2) \log 3) = O(8 \log 3) \approx O(12.68)
$$

For $n$ segments in general, the time is $O((n + k) \log n)$, which is asymptotically superior to the naive $O(n^2)$ pairwise check when $k = o(n^2 / \log n)$.

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) Describe the Bentley-Ottmann algorithm for computing all intersections among a set of line segments in the plane. How does it use the plane sweep paradigm? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Problem Statement (1 mark)**
Given a set $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ of $n$ line segments in the plane, find all $k$ intersection points among them.

**2. Algorithm Description (4.5 marks)**
The Bentley-Ottmann algorithm is a direct application of the plane sweep paradigm. The steps are:

- **Event Queue Q:** Initially contains all $2n$ endpoints of the segments, sorted by x-coordinate.
- **Sweep Line Status T:** A balanced BST (e.g., red-black tree) that stores the segments intersecting the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the current x-position.
- **Initialization:** $T$ is empty; sweep line is at $x = -\infty$.
- **Main Loop:**
  - Extract the next event $p$ from $Q$.
  - **Left endpoint event:** Insert the segment $s$ into $T$. Test the neighbors of $s$ (above and below) for future intersections; schedule them in $Q$ if found.
  - **Right endpoint event:** Remove the segment $s$ from $T$. Test the new pair of neighbors (above and below) for future intersections; schedule them in $Q$ if found.
  - **Intersection event:** Swap the two intersecting segments in $T$. Test the new neighbor pairs (formed by the swap) for future intersections; schedule them in $Q$ if found.
- **Output:** When $Q$ is empty, report all intersection events that were processed.

**3. Plane Sweep Connection (1.5 marks)**
Bentley-Ottmann is a *textbook specialization* of the plane sweep paradigm. The four components are realized as:
- **Sweep line:** Vertical line at $x = c$.
- **Event queue:** A priority queue of endpoint and intersection events.
- **Sweep line status:** BBST of active segments.
- **Event handler:** Three distinct procedures for the three event types.

The algorithm achieves its efficiency by observing that two non-adjacent segments in $T$ cannot be the next pair to intersect, so the number of intersection tests is bounded by $O(n + k)$ rather than $O(n^2)$.

#### (b) Analyze the time and space complexity of the Bentley-Ottmann algorithm. Under what conditions does it degenerate to $O(n^2)$? Compare it with the naive $O(n^2)$ approach. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Time Complexity (2.5 marks)**
- Each of the $n$ segment endpoints produces one event: $O(n)$ events from endpoints.
- Each of the $k$ intersections produces one event: $O(k)$ events from intersections.
- Total events: $O(n + k)$.
- Each event handler invocation does $O(1)$ updates to $T$ (BBST operations) and $O(1)$ insertions to $Q$ (heap operations).
- Each BBST operation: $O(\log n)$ since $T$ has at most $n$ elements.
- Each heap operation: $O(\log (n + k))$.
- Total time:

$$
T(n, k) = O((n + k) \log n)
$$

**2. Space Complexity (1.5 marks)**
- Event queue: $O(n + k)$ entries.
- Status structure: $O(n)$ entries.
- Total space:

$$
S(n, k) = O(n + k)
$$

**3. Degeneracy Analysis (2 marks)**
Bentley-Ottmann *degrades* to $O(n^2 \log n)$ time and $O(n^2)$ space when $k = \Theta(n^2)$, i.e., when every pair of segments intersects. Examples of such pathological inputs:
- A "star" configuration where $n/2$ horizontal segments are all crossed by $n/2$ vertical segments, producing $\Theta(n^2)$ intersections.
- A convex polygon with all diagonals drawn as segments.
- Any "dense" arrangement.

In such cases, the algorithm is asymptotically *worse* than the naive $O(n^2)$ approach because of the $\log n$ factor in the sweep, though in practice the constants are small.

**4. Comparison with Naive $O(n^2)$ Approach (1 mark)**

| Metric | Bentley-Ottmann | Naive Pairwise |
|---|---|---|
| Time (typical, $k \ll n^2$) | $O((n + k) \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Time (worst, $k = n^2$) | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Space | $O(n + k)$ | $O(1)$ (just a counter) |
| Robustness to degeneracies | Requires care (e.g., vertical segments, collinear overlaps) | Naturally handles all cases |

> **Conclusion:** Bentley-Ottmann is *output-sensitive*: its complexity depends on $k$ (the size of the output), making it highly efficient for sparse intersection patterns but vulnerable to the dense-arrangement worst case.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose marks in the following ways on plane sweep questions:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to schedule new intersection events:** When two segments $s_i$ and $s_j$ are tested for an intersection during an event handler, students often forget to *add the intersection point to $Q$* if it lies in the future. **Tip:** Always check whether the intersection's x-coordinate is *strictly greater* than the current sweep position.
>
> 2. **Confusing event types:** Some students mix up "right endpoint" and "left endpoint" handler logic. The left endpoint *inserts* into $T$ and tests the new upper/lower neighbors; the right endpoint *removes* from $T$ and tests the *former* upper/lower neighbors' new adjacency.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the priority of intersections:** When two segments have an intersection at the same x-coordinate as a left or right endpoint, the tie-breaking rule must be specified. The default is: process the intersection *first* if it lies at the same x as an endpoint, to avoid missing the swap.
>
> 4. **Omitting the status structure invariant:** A common omission in long-answer questions is the explicit statement of the invariant: "$T$ contains exactly those segments that intersect the sweep line, ordered by their y-coordinate at the sweep line." Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 5. **Conflating time and space complexity:** Students often write "$O(n \log n)$" for both time and space. The correct time is $O((n + k) \log n)$; the space is $O(n + k)$. Do not conflate these.
>
> 6. **Neglecting the degenerate case:** When asked for complexity, students often give only the typical-case formula. Always state the *worst case* separately and identify the conditions under which it occurs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Plane Sweep Algorithm**
>
> - **Definition:** The plane sweep paradigm moves a line (typically vertical, $x = c$) across the plane, processing events in order, while a status structure tracks active objects.
> - **Four Components (memorize verbatim):**
>   1. Sweep Line ($\ell$)
>   2. Event Queue ($Q$, priority queue)
>   3. Sweep Line Status ($T$, balanced BST)
>   4. Event Handler
> - **Three Event Types in Bentley-Ottmann:**
>   - **L**eft endpoint: **I**nsert into $T$.
>   - **R**ight endpoint: **R**emove from $T$.
>   - **I**ntersection: **S**wap in $T$. (Mnemonic: "LIS-RIS" or "Insert-Remove-Swap")
> - **Core Invariant:** $T$ contains exactly those objects intersecting $\ell$, ordered by y-coordinate.
> - **Time Complexity:** $O((n + k) \log n)$ — *output-sensitive*.
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(n + k)$.
> - **Worst-Case Behavior:** $O(n^2 \log n)$ time when $k = \Theta(n^2)$ (dense arrangements).
> - **Default Sweep Direction:** Left to right (along +x axis); some algorithms use bottom-to-top.
> - **General Position Assumption:** No two endpoints share the same x-coordinate; no three segments are concurrent. Ties require explicit tie-breaking rules.
> - **Key Insight:** Two non-adjacent segments in $T$ cannot be the next pair to intersect — this bounds the candidate pairs to $O(n + k)$.
> - **Canonical Applications:** Segment intersection (Bentley-Ottmann), convex hull (Andrew's monotone chain), closest pair of points, trapezoidal decomposition, Fortune's Voronoi diagram.
> - **Engineering Applications:** VLSI design rule checking, GIS map overlay, CAD/CAM polygon operations, robotics collision detection, computational biology.
> - **What to draw in exams:** A flowchart of the four components, a table of event handlers, and a worked numerical example with $n = 3$ or $n = 4$ segments.

---
