Wait — this needs correction. The full expected analysis must also account for the cost of **searching** for the tangent points in each step, which involves looking at $O(h_i)$ edges where $h_i$ is the hull size after $i$ insertions.

Through the theory of **randomized incremental algorithms** (de Berg et al., CLRS-style analysis), the expected cost of each insertion is:

$$
E[\text{Insertion Cost}] = O(\log n)
$$

Summing:

$$
E[T_{\text{expected}}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log n) = O(n \log n)
$$

**Best-Case:** $O(n)$ — when all points after the initial triangle are interior.

**(b) Comparison Table [7 marks]:**

| Parameter | Incremental Algorithm | Graham's Scan |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Preprocessing** | None required (or optional random shuffle) | Mandatory sorting by polar angle, $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Worst-Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Expected Time** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Output Sensitivity** | Not naturally output-sensitive; $O(n \log n)$ expected regardless of hull size | Not output-sensitive in basic form; $O(n \log n)$ dominated by sort |
| **Online Property** | Fully online — can process streaming input | Offline — needs all points before sorting |
| **Degenerate Cases** | Requires careful handling of collinear points (use strict vs. non-strict inequality) | Collinear points handled by removing middle points after sort |
| **Memory Pattern** | Local updates, in-place modifications possible | Stack-based with push/pop operations |
| **Implementation** | More complex (tangent search logic) | Simpler (single stack + sorted scan) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign Convention Error:** Students frequently confuse the orientation test sign convention. **Always state explicitly** that positive = CCW, negative = CW, zero = collinear. Inverting this leads to incorrect tangent identification and wrong hull.
> - **Skipping the Inside Test:** Some students jump directly to tangent finding without checking if the point is interior. This wastes computation and can produce incorrect edge removals.
> - **Wrong Tangent Order:** The upper tangent is where the sign changes from $+$ to $-$ (in CCW traversal), and the lower tangent is where it changes from $-$ to $+$. Reversing this causes incorrect hull reconstruction.
> - **Forgetting Non-Collinearity Check:** If the first 3 points are collinear, the algorithm fails at initialization. Always verify $\text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_3) \neq 0$ before forming the base triangle.
> - **Missing Final Output Verification:** After constructing the hull, students often forget to verify all edges maintain consistent orientation (all should have the same sign relative to the interior).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Concept:** Incremental algorithms build structures by adding one element at a time, performing **local updates** to maintain a global invariant.
- **Base Case:** Always start with **3 non-collinear points** forming a CCW triangle.
- **Invariant:** After processing $i$ points, the current structure is the convex hull of those $i$ points.
- **Three-Step Insertion:** (1) Inside test using orientation signs, (2) Tangent point identification at sign changes, (3) Edge replacement along the visible chain.
- **Orientation Formula:** $\text{orient}(a,b,c) = (b_x - a_x)(c_y - a_y) - (b_y - a_y)(c_x - a_x)$. Positive $\Rightarrow$ CCW.
- **Inside Test:** A point is **inside** the hull if all orientation values with consecutive edges have the **same sign**.
- **Tangent Detection:** Tangent points are where the orientation **sign changes** during CCW traversal of hull edges.
- **Time Complexities to Memorize:**
  - Worst case: $O(n^2)$
  - Expected (random input): $O(n \log n)$
  - Best case: $O(n)$
- **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$.
- **Key Advantage:** **Online** algorithm — can handle streaming input.
- **Key Disadvantage:** Worst-case $O(n^2)$ when points are pre-sorted angularly.
- **Randomization Trick:** Shuffle the input randomly to achieve expected $O(n \log n)$ performance.
- **Real-World Applications:** CAD boundary construction, GIS territorial mapping, robot safe-region planning, pattern recognition, facility location.
- **Connection to Other Algorithms:** Forms the basis for **randomized incremental algorithms** used in Voronoi diagram and Delaunay triangulation construction (Module 3 connection).
- **Examiner's Focus Areas:** Orientation test computation, tangent identification logic, hull update steps, and time complexity derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fortune's Sweep Line Algorithm; Delaunay Triangulations - Definition and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fortune's Sweep Line Algorithm; Delaunay Triangulations - Definition and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fortune's Sweep Line Algorithm — Formal Definition

**Fortune's Sweep Line Algorithm** is an **O(n log n)** plane-sweep procedure introduced by Steven Fortune (1986) that constructs the **Voronoi diagram** of *n* given sites by sweeping a horizontal line from top to bottom across the plane, while dynamically maintaining a *breakpoint curve* (the **beach line**) that separates regions already decided from regions not yet processed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Fortune's algorithm is the canonical optimal method for Voronoi diagram construction and forms the theoretical backbone of many mesh-generation, geographic-information-system (GIS), and proximity-based engineering solvers.

Formally, the algorithm maintains three dynamic structures:

| Structure | Type | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Sweep Line** *L* | Geometric line, y = y(s) | Drives event processing in order of decreasing y |
| **Beach Line** *B* | Sequence of parabolic arcs | Boundary between "known" and "unknown" Voronoi regions |
| **Event Queue** *Q* | Priority queue | Stores site events and circle events |

### 1.2 Delaunay Triangulation — Formal Definition

Let **P** = {p₁, p₂, …, p_n} be a set of points in general position in **ℝ²**. A **Delaunay Triangulation** *D(P)* is a triangulation such that, for every triangle in *D(P)*, the **circumcircle** of that triangle contains **no point of P in its interior**.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Position Assumption:** No three points are collinear and no four points are cocircular. This guarantees uniqueness and avoids degenerate edge cases during evaluation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

#### Analogy 1 — Fortune's Algorithm as a "Tide Rolling Out"
Imagine a beach on the ocean. As the tide recedes downward (the sweep line moving down), it exposes the wet sand behind it. Wherever a sandcastle (a **site**) sits, the waterline forms a **parabolic bulge** around it. The intersection of two such bulges is a **breakpoint**—a marker of an upcoming Voronoi edge. When three neighboring bulges simultaneously collapse to a single point (because their crests meet in a circle), the tide has just exposed a **Voronoi vertex**—this is a *circle event*.

#### Analogy 2 — Delaunay as "Triangle of Closest Neighbors"
Think of each point as a firefly that wants to be connected to its **nearest non-collinear neighbors** without ever enclosing any other firefly inside its connecting triangle. The Delaunay triangulation is the wiring diagram of these connections—every triangle is "loose" in the sense that no other point invades its circumcircle. This produces triangles that are as **fat (equilateral-like)** as possible, avoiding the long, sliver-like triangles that would make downstream numerical solvers unstable.

> [!TIP]
> The **Delaunay triangulation is the straight-line dual of the Voronoi diagram**—this single duality underlies nearly every modern mesh generator (Triangle, CGAL, Qhull) used in finite-element analysis and CAD.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Fortune's Beach Line

When the sweep line is at height *y = y_L*, each site *p_i = (x_i, y_i)* generates a parabola whose locus is the set of points **equidistant** from *p_i* and the line *L*.

#### The Parabola Equation (KTU Exam Favourite)

Starting from the equidistance condition:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = (y - y_L)^2$$

Solving for *y* in terms of *x*:

$$y = \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{y_i + y_L}{2}$$

**Interpretation of parameters:**
- **Focus:** *(x_i, y_i)* — the site itself
- **Directrix:** *y = y_L* — the moving sweep line
- **Vertex:** *(x_i, (y_i + y_L)/2)* — the parabola's apex
- **Axis of symmetry:** *x = x_i* (vertical line through the site)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since sites lie above the descending sweep line (*y_i > y_L*), the parabola **opens upward** (toward the focus). The beach line is the **upper envelope** of these parabolas.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars: Breakpoints, Site Events, Circle Events

#### Pillar 1 — Breakpoints
A **breakpoint** is the intersection between two consecutive arcs on the beach line belonging to sites *p_i* and *p_j*. The breakpoint lies on the **perpendicular bisector** of segment *p_i p_j*, because at that point the distance to *p_i* equals the distance to *p_j*:

$$\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} = \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}$$

These breakpoints **trace out the Voronoi edges** as the sweep progresses.

#### Pillar 2 — Site Event (Insertion)
When the sweep line crosses a new site *p_k*, a new parabolic arc is **inserted** into the beach line at position *x = x_k*. This typically splits one existing arc into two, creating a new triple of consecutive arcs.

#### Pillar 3 — Circle Event (Deletion)
When three consecutive sites *p_i, p_j, p_k* on the beach line have their circumcircle's **lowest point** descend to (or below) the sweep line, the middle arc *p_j* **collapses to a point**. This point is the corresponding **Voronoi vertex**. The algorithm:
1. Records the Voronoi vertex coordinates.
2. Closes the two outer arcs into a single arc.
3. Emits two new edges into the output.

> [!WARNING]
> A circle event becomes **invalid** if any of its three sites is consumed by another event first. The algorithm must therefore verify event validity before processing.

### 2.3 Delaunay Triangulation — Properties Catalogue

| # | Property | Statement |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Empty Circumcircle** | Every triangle's circumcircle is empty of any other input point |
| 2 | **Max-Min Angle** | Among all triangulations of P, Delaunay **maximizes the minimum angle** |
| 3 | **Voronoi Duality** | Each Delaunay edge crosses exactly one Voronoi edge perpendicularly |
| 4 | **Edge-Flip Locality** | Edge *e* shared by △pqr and △pqs is non-Delaunay iff *∠prq + ∠psq > π* |
| 5 | **Local ⇒ Global** | If every edge is locally Delaunay, the whole triangulation is Delaunay |
| 6 | **Uniqueness** | In general position, the Delaunay triangulation is **unique** |
| 7 | **Combinatorial Size** | For *n* points with *k* on the convex hull: triangles = *2n − 2 − k*, edges = *3n − 3 − k* |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Parabola (beach-line arc) | $$y = \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{y_i + y_L}{2}$$ | Focus at *p_i*, directrix *y = y_L* |
| Breakpoint (between *p_i*, *p_j*) | $$(x - x_i)^2 - (x - x_j)^2 = (y - y_j)^2 - (y - y_i)^2$$ | Perpendicular bisector locus |
| Circumcircle condition (Delaunay) | $$\vert p_k - c_{ijk} \vert \;>\; r_{ijk} \; \forall k \neq i,j,m$$ | Centre *c*, radius *r* of △p_i p_j p_m |
| Edge flip criterion | $$\angle prq + \angle psq > \pi \;\Rightarrow\; \text{flip } pq \to rs$$ | Locally non-Delaunay edge *pq* |
| Delaunay triangle count | $$T = 2n - 2 - k$$ | *n* points, *k* hull points |
| Delaunay edge count | $$E = 3n - 3 - k$$ | Same parameters |
| Fortune's complexity | **O(n log n)** | Optimal in algebraic decision-tree model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all formulas above, the symbol **π** denotes the geometric constant *≈ 3.14159265…*, and angles are measured in **radians** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Finite Element Meshing** | Delaunay's max-min angle property prevents sliver elements that degrade FEM accuracy |
| **Robot Path Planning** | Voronoi edges represent maximum clearance paths between obstacles |
| **GIS / Cartography** | Nearest-neighbor queries use Delaunay/Voronoi for O(log n) lookup |
| **Wireless Network Design** | Cell towers placed at Voronoi sites yield uniform coverage regions |
| **Molecular Modelling** | Alpha-shapes (generalized Delaunay) reconstruct molecular surfaces |
| **Computer Graphics** | Real-time terrain triangulation in games uses incremental Delaunay |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beach-Line Parabola Equation

**Given:** Site *p_i = (x_i, y_i)*; sweep line *L: y = y_L*.

**Goal:** Find the locus *B_i(x)* of points equidistant from *p_i* and *L*.

**Step 1 — Set up equidistance.**
A point *(x, y)* on the parabola satisfies:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = (y - y_L)^2$$

*Logic:* The squared Euclidean distance to *p_i* equals the squared perpendicular distance to *L* (the latter is simply *(y − y_L)²* because *L* is horizontal).

**Step 2 — Expand both squares.**

$$x^2 - 2x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = y^2 - 2y y_L + y_L^2$$

*Logic:* Apply *(a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²* to every product on both sides.

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms.**

The terms *x²* and *y²* cancel out immediately:

$$- 2x x_i + x_i^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = - 2y y_L + y_L^2$$

**Step 4 — Group *y*-terms on one side.**

$$2y(y_L - y_i) = x_i^2 - 2x x_i + y_L^2 - y_i^2$$

*Logic:* Move all *y* terms to the left, all *x* and constants to the right.

**Step 5 — Isolate *y*.**

$$y = \frac{x_i^2 - 2x x_i + y_L^2 - y_i^2}{2(y_L - y_i)}$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite in canonical parabolic form.**

Recognise that *x_i² − 2x x_i = −(x − x_i)² + x_i²*:

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{-(x - x_i)^2 + x_i^2 + y_L^2 - y_i^2}{2(y_L - y_i)} \\
  &= \frac{-(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_L - y_i)} + \frac{x_i^2 + y_L^2 - y_i^2}{2(y_L - y_i)} \\
  &= \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{(y_i - y_L)(y_i + y_L) + 2x_i^2 \cdot (\text{adjustment})}{2(y_i - y_L)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

After careful algebraic simplification using the identity *y_L² − y_i² = −(y_i − y_L)(y_i + y_L)*:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{y_i + y_L}{2}}$$

*Logic of the final form:* The first term is a standard upward-opening parabola with axis at *x = x_i*; the second term is the constant vertical offset equal to the midpoint of the focus and directrix.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Circle-Event Condition

**Given:** Three consecutive sites *p_i, p_j, p_k* on the beach line, with circumcircle centre *c* and radius *r*. Let *y_bottom = c_y − r* be the lowest point of the circumcircle.

**Claim:** The arc belonging to *p_j* shrinks to zero at the moment *y_L = y_bottom*.

**Step 1 — Beach-line definition.**
At height *y_L*, the arc of *p_j* is the portion of the parabola *B_j* that dominates all other parabolas locally.

**Step 2 — Arc vanishes when its three defining breakpoints coincide.**
The arc of *p_j* is bounded above by the parabola and on the sides by two breakpoints — call them *x*₁ (with *p_i*) and *x*₂ (with *p_k*). The arc has zero length when *B_j(x) = B_i(x) = B_k(x)* at a single point.

**Step 3 — Equidistance implies common circumcircle.**
If a point *v = (x_v, y_v)* is equidistant from *p_i, p_j, p_k* and from the sweep line *L*, then:

$$\vert v - p_i \vert^2 = \vert v - p_j \vert^2 = \vert v - p_k \vert^2 = (y_v - y_L)^2$$

*Logic:* The common distance is exactly *y_v − y_L* (distance to horizontal line).

**Step 4 — Therefore *v* is the circumcenter of △p_i p_j p_k, and *y_L = y_v − r*.**

Because *v* is equidistant from all three sites and from *L*, the circle centred at *v* passing through the sites is tangent to *L* at the point *directly below v*.

$$\boxed{y_{\text{trigger}} = c_y - r = y_{\text{bottom}}}$$

*Engineering interpretation:* The moment the sweep line touches the bottom of the circumcircle, the three arcs converge, defining a Voronoi vertex.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Edge-Flip Criterion (Delaunay Locality)

**Given:** Edge *pq* shared by triangles △pqr and △pqs.

**Step 1 — Construct the two circumcircles.**
Let *C₁* be the circumcircle of △pqr (through *p, q, r*) and *C₂* be the circumcircle of △pqs (through *p, q, s*).

**Step 2 — Local Delaunay condition.**
Edge *pq* is **locally Delaunay** iff *s ∉ interior(C₁)* AND *r ∉ interior(C₂)*.

**Step 3 — Equivalence with angle sum.**
By the **Inscribed Angle Theorem**, points inside a circle see any chord at an angle *greater* than points on the circle. Therefore:

$$s \in \text{interior}(C_1) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \angle psq > \angle prq$$

**Step 4 — Sum-of-opposite-angles form.**
Let *α = ∠prq* (angle at *r* in △pqr, opposite to *pq*) and *β = ∠psq* (angle at *s* in △pqs, opposite to *pq*). The edge *pq* is locally Delaunay iff:

$$\boxed{\alpha + \beta \leq \pi}$$

**Step 5 — Edge flip algorithm.**
If *α + β > π*, then *pq* violates the Delaunay condition. **Flip** *pq* to *rs*, recompute the four neighbouring edge tests, and repeat until no flip is possible. This greedy procedure terminates because each flip strictly increases the minimum angle.

> [!TIP]
> This flip-based **Bowyer–Watson / incremental Delaunay** algorithm is *O(n²)* worst-case but extremely simple to implement and is the standard approach in textbooks and CGAL.

---

### 3.4 Combinatorial Counting (Number of Delaunay Triangles)

**Step 1 — Euler's formula.**
For a planar connected graph with *V* vertices, *E* edges, *F* faces:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Delaunay counts.**
Let *V = n*, *F = T + 1* (triangles *T* + 1 unbounded outer face):

$$n - E + T + 1 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; E = T + n - 1$$

**Step 3 — Use 3-edge sum property.**
Each triangle contributes 3 edge incidences; the outer face contributes *k* hull edges (each counted once again):

$$3T + k = 2E$$

*Logic:* Each interior edge is shared by 2 triangles (counted twice in *3T*); each hull edge is in exactly 1 triangle but also bounds the outer face, so each hull edge contributes 1 to *3T* (from the triangle side) and 1 to the outer face (counted by *k*). Total = *3T + k = 2E*.

**Step 4 — Solve simultaneously.**

$$\begin{aligned}
3T + k &= 2(T + n - 1) \\
3T + k &= 2T + 2n - 2 \\
T &= 2n - 2 - k
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= T + n - 1 \\
  &= (2n - 2 - k) + n - 1 \\
  &= 3n - 3 - k
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2n - 2 - k \quad,\quad E = 3n - 3 - k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These bounds are tight: equality holds for *k = 3* (a triangle convex hull, where *T = 2n − 5*, *E = 3n − 6*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fortune's Algorithm — Event-Driven State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Event Queue Q with all site events sorted by y] --> B[Set sweep line y_L at + infinity]
    B --> C{Is Q empty?}
    C -->|No| D[Pop event with smallest y]
    D --> E{Event type?}
    E -->|Site Event| F[Insert new parabolic arc into beach line]
    F --> G[Update breakpoints and check for new circle events]
    G --> H[Add detected circle events to Q]
    H --> C
    E -->|Circle Event| I[Validate event: all 3 sites still active]
    I -->|Invalid| C
    I -->|Valid| J[Record Voronoi vertex at event point]
    J --> K[Delete middle arc; merge outer two arcs]
    K --> L[Add Voronoi edges to output]
    L --> M[Update neighbouring circle events]
    M --> C
    C -->|Yes| N[Output complete Voronoi diagram]
    N --> O[END]
```

*Reading Guide:* Each node is a procedural step. Site events trigger insertions; circle events trigger deletions and Voronoi-vertex creation. The *Validate* gate prevents processing stale circle events.

---

### 4.2 Beach-Line Geometric Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sweep_Region[Sweep Line Region]
        SL[Horizontal Sweep Line at y = y_L]
    end
    subgraph Beach_Band[Beach Line Band]
        B1[Arc of site p_1]
        BP1[Breakpoint x_12]
        B2[Arc of site p_2]
        BP2[Breakpoint x_23]
        B3[Arc of site p_3]
    end
    subgraph Decided[Decided Region Above]
        V1[Known Voronoi edges]
        V2[Already-emitted vertices]
    end
    subgraph Undecided[Undecided Region Below]
        U1[Future Voronoi edges]
        U2[Future vertices]
    end

    SL --- B1
    B1 --- BP1
    BP1 --- B2
    B2 --- BP2
    BP2 --- B3
    B3 --- SL
    V1 --> BP1
    V2 --> BP2
    U1 -.Future.-> BP1
    U2 -.Future.-> BP2
```

*Reading Guide:* The beach line is the boundary between the decided region (where Voronoi edges are already finalized) and the undecided region. Each arc corresponds to one site; each breakpoint corresponds to one Voronoi edge endpoint being traced.

---

### 4.3 Delaunay Empty-Circumcircle Illustration (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input_Set[Input Point Set P]
        P1[Point p_1]
        P2[Point p_2]
        P3[Point p_3]
        P4[Point p_4]
        P5[Point p_5]
        P6[Point p_6]
    end
    subgraph Triangles[Delaunay Triangles]
        T1[Triangle p_1 p_2 p_3]
        T2[Triangle p_1 p_3 p_4]
        T3[Triangle p_1 p_4 p_5]
        T4[Triangle p_3 p_4 p_6]
    end
    subgraph Circumcircles[Circumcircle Tests]
        CC1[Circumcircle of T_1: EMPTY of other points]
        CC2[Circumcircle of T_2: EMPTY of other points]
        CC3[Circumcircle of T_3: EMPTY of other points]
        CC4[Circumcircle of T_4: EMPTY of other points]
    end

    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T1
    P3 --> T1
    T1 --> CC1

    P1 --> T2
    P3 --> T2
    P4 --> T2
    T2 --> CC2

    P1 --> T3
    P4 --> T3
    P5 --> T3
    T3 --> CC3

    P3 --> T4
    P4 --> T4
    P6 --> T4
    T4 --> CC4
```

*Reading Guide:* Every triangle's circumcircle must be tested for emptiness. If any point of P lies strictly inside a circumcircle, that triangle is **not** Delaunay and the offending edge must be flipped.

---

### 4.4 Voronoi–Delaunay Duality Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Voronoi_Side[Voronoi Diagram]
        V1[Cell c_1]
        V2[Cell c_2]
        V3[Cell c_3]
        VE1[Voronoi edge between c_1 and c_2]
        VV1[Voronoi vertex meeting c_1 c_2 c_3]
    end
    subgraph Delaunay_Side[Delaunay Triangulation]
        S1[Site p_1]
        S2[Site p_2]
        S3[Site p_3]
        DE1[Delaunay edge between p_1 and p_2]
        DT1[Delaunay triangle p_1 p_2 p_3]
    end

    V1 -.dual.-> S1
    V2 -.dual.-> S2
    V3 -.dual.-> S3
    VE1 -.perpendicular crossing.-> DE1
    VV1 -.circumcenter of.-> DT1
```

*Reading Guide:* Each Voronoi cell corresponds to one site. Each Voronoi edge perpendicularly crosses exactly one Delaunay edge. Each Voronoi vertex is the circumcenter of exactly one Delaunay triangle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]`
**Define the Delaunay triangulation of a point set P. State and justify the *empty circumcircle property*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Delaunay triangulation** *D(P)* of a point set *P* in general position is a triangulation in which **the circumcircle of every triangle is empty**, i.e., contains no point of *P* in its interior.

**Justification of empty circumcircle property:** The empty circumcircle condition is equivalent to the statement that for every triangle △pqr ∈ D(P), the circumcenter *c* of △pqr satisfies *c ∉ interior(any Voronoi cell other than those of p, q, r)*. Geometrically, the three sites are the *closest* points to their common Voronoi vertex, so no fourth site can "steal" the cell — this is the unique triangulation that maximizes the minimum angle of all triangles.

*Key marking cues:* Definition (1 mark), property statement (1 mark), geometric justification (1 mark).

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the *beach line* in Fortune's algorithm? Why is it composed of parabolic arcs rather than circles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **beach line** is a moving *x-monotone breakpoint curve* maintained during the plane sweep that demarcates the boundary between the **decided region** (above, where Voronoi edges have already been computed) and the **undecided region** (below, where Voronoi structure is still unknown).

**Why parabolic?** Each point on the beach line is equidistant from some site *p_i = (x_i, y_i)* and from the current sweep line *y = y_L*. The locus of points equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix) is, by classical geometry, a **parabola**. Circles would only arise if the sweep were by a *point* (e.g., a growing disk), not by a line.

*Key marking cues:* Beach line definition (1 mark), x-monotonicity (1 mark), parabola-from-focus-directrix reasoning (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B (Choice A).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the working of Fortune's sweep line algorithm for computing the Voronoi diagram of *n* point sites. Clearly distinguish between **site events** and **circle events** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the equidistance condition, derive the equation of the parabolic arc traced on the beach line by a site *p_i = (x_i, y_i)* when the sweep line is at *y = y_L*. Show that this parabola opens upward when the site lies above the sweep line. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to B (a):**

Fortune's algorithm sweeps a horizontal line *L: y = y_L* from *y = +∞* downward. It uses a priority queue of events and a balanced binary search tree (BBST) for the beach line.

**Step 1 — Initialization.** Insert all *n* site events into the event queue *Q*, sorted in decreasing order of *y_i* (so the topmost site is processed first).

**Step 2 — Site event handling.** When the sweep line crosses a new site *p_k*, the beach line must be updated. A new parabolic arc belonging to *p_k* is inserted at horizontal position *x = x_k*. Typically, this **bisects** one existing arc into two, producing one new breakpoint on each side. Any newly formed triple of consecutive arcs is tested for a potential circle event, and valid events are inserted into *Q*.

**Step 3 — Circle event handling.** For three consecutive sites *p_i, p_j, p_k* on the beach line, if the bottom of their circumcircle is at or above the sweep line, a circle event is detected. When processed:
- The Voronoi vertex at the circumcircle's bottom is output.
- The middle arc *p_j* is removed; outer arcs merge into a single arc.
- Two Voronoi edges are extended from the new vertex.
- Neighbouring circle events involving *p_j* are **invalidated**.

**Step 4 — Termination.** When *Q* is empty, all Voronoi cells, edges, and vertices have been emitted. Total complexity: **O(n log n)**.

**Diagram (schematic ASCII for board work):**

```
   p_1 •     p_2 •
       \    /
        \  /  <-- Voronoi edge (already decided)
   ─────●────────  Beach line (parabolic arcs)
        |
   y_L  ↓ sweep direction
        |
```

**Site event** = new arc insertion; **circle event** = three arcs collapse to a vertex.

*Marking scheme:* Initialisation (1), site event logic (2), circle event logic (2), complexity + termination (2).

---

##### **Solution to B (b):**

Let *p_i = (x_i, y_i)* be the focus and *y = y_L* the directrix. A point *(x, y)* on the parabola satisfies the equidistance condition:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = (y - y_L)^2$$

Expanding both sides:

$$x^2 - 2x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = y^2 - 2y y_L + y_L^2$$

Cancelling *x²* and *y²*:

$$- 2x x_i + x_i^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 = - 2y y_L + y_L^2$$

Rearranging to isolate *y*:

$$2y(y_L - y_i) = x_i^2 - 2x x_i + y_L^2 - y_i^2$$

$$y = \frac{x_i^2 - 2x x_i + y_L^2 - y_i^2}{2(y_L - y_i)}$$

Applying the completing-the-square identity *x_i² − 2x x_i = −(x − x_i)² + x_i²*:

$$y = \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{y_i + y_L}{2}$$

**Verification of upward opening (y_i > y_L):**
- Coefficient of *(x − x_i)²* is *1 / (2(y_i − y_L))*, which is **positive** when *y_i > y_L*.
- Therefore the parabola **opens upward**, with vertex at *(x_i, (y_i + y_L)/2)*.

When *y_i < y_L* (site below sweep line, hypothetical), the coefficient becomes negative and the parabola opens downward — but this never occurs during a top-down sweep because sites are encountered before the line passes below them.

*Marking scheme:* Equidistance setup (1), expansion (1), cancellation (1), solving for y (2), canonical form (1), sign analysis for opening direction (1).

---

#### **Question B (Choice B — Alternative).** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define Delaunay triangulation formally. Prove the equivalence between the **empty circumcircle property** and the **max-min angle property** using the edge-flip criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For *n = 10* points with *k = 4* points on the convex hull, compute the number of triangles and edges in the Delaunay triangulation. Justify using Euler's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to B-alt (a):**

**Definition:** *D(P)* is a Delaunay triangulation of *P* iff for every triangle △pqr ∈ D(P), the circumcircle of △pqr contains no other point of *P* in its interior.

**Edge-flip equivalence proof:**

*Step 1 — Local Delaunay condition.* Edge *e = pq* shared by △pqr and △pqs is locally Delaunay iff *s* is outside *C₁* (circumcircle of △pqr) and *r* is outside *C₂* (circumcircle of △pqs).

*Step 2 — Inscribed Angle Theorem application.* For chord *pq* in *C₁*, every point on the arc not containing *r* sees *pq* at the **same angle** *α = ∠prq*. Points *inside* the circle see *pq* at a **larger angle**, points *outside* see *pq* at a **smaller angle**. So:

$$s \in \text{interior}(C_1) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \angle psq > \angle prq = \alpha$$

Similarly, *r ∈ interior(C₂)* iff *∠prq > ∠psq*.

*Step 3 — Combined local condition.* Edge *pq* is locally Delaunay iff:

$$\angle psq \leq \alpha \quad \text{AND} \quad \angle prq \leq \beta \quad \text{where} \; \beta = \angle psq$$

But since *α + β* are opposite angles in a quadrilateral *prqs*, and for the quadrilateral to be convex *α + β ≤ π*. By inspection, *pq* is locally Delaunay **iff** *α + β ≤ π*.

*Step 4 — Connection to max-min angle.* Each edge flip replaces two triangles *T₁, T₂* with two new triangles *T₁', T₂'*. By the law of cosines and the inscribed-angle relationship, the **minimum angle strictly increases** after each flip (this is a classical result by Lawson, 1977). Since the minimum angle is bounded above by *60°* (equilateral case), the flip process terminates. At termination, every edge is locally Delaunay; by **local ⇒ global** Delaunay equivalence (proved by contradiction using the empty-circle criterion), the triangulation is globally Delaunay and thus maximises the minimum angle.

*Marking scheme:* Formal definition (1), inscribed-angle argument (2), α + β ≤ π criterion (2), Lawson monotone-increase lemma (1), local-global equivalence (1).

---

##### **Solution to B-alt (b):**

**Given:** *n = 10*, *k = 4*.

**Step 1 — Apply the triangle-count formula.**

$$T = 2n - 2 - k = 2(10) - 2 - 4 = 20 - 6 = 14$$

**Step 2 — Apply the edge-count formula.**

$$E = 3n - 3 - k = 3(10) - 3 - 4 = 30 - 7 = 23$$

**Step 3 — Verification by Euler's formula.**
For a connected planar graph with *V = n = 10*, *E = 23*, *F = T + 1 = 15* (including the unbounded face):

$$V - E + F = 10 - 23 + 15 = 2 \;\checkmark$$

Also, verify the 3-edge sum: *3T + k = 3(14) + 4 = 46 = 2E = 2(23) = 46* ✓

**Answer:** The Delaunay triangulation contains **14 triangles** and **23 edges**.

*Marking scheme:* Formula application (3), substitution (1), Euler verification (2), final answer (1).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "*in general position*" qualifier** when defining Delaunay — KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks if you do not state the assumption about non-cocircular / non-collinear points.
> 2. **Confusing the parabola's directrix with the sweep line itself.** Students frequently write the directrix as *y = 0*; the correct directrix is *y = y_L*, the *current* sweep-line height.
> 3. **Edge-flip direction errors.** The criterion is *∠prq + ∠psq > π → flip*, NOT *< π → flip*. Getting the inequality backwards is the single most frequent error and costs 2–3 marks outright.
> 4. **Omitting the "local ⇒ global" lemma proof.** When asked to prove equivalence between empty-circle and max-min angle, you must explicitly invoke this lemma; merely stating it is insufficient for full marks.
> 5. **Beach-line complexity argument.** Students often claim O(n log n) without justifying the O(log n) cost of beach-line insertion (achieved via the BBST) and the O(log n) priority-queue operations. Examiners expect both structures named.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fortune's algorithm** sweeps a line from top to bottom and maintains a **parabolic beach line** composed of arcs with foci at the sites and directrix on the sweep line.
- **Beach-line equation:** $$y = \frac{(x - x_i)^2}{2(y_i - y_L)} + \frac{y_i + y_L}{2}$$ — must be derivable from scratch in the exam.
- **Two event types:** *Site events* insert arcs; *circle events* delete arcs and emit Voronoi vertices.
- **Circle event trigger:** Sweep line reaches the bottom of the circumcircle of three consecutive sites.
- **Complexity:** O(n log n) — optimal among comparison-based algorithms.
- **Delaunay definition:** Triangulation where **every triangle's circumcircle contains no other input point in its interior**.
- **Delaunay = Voronoi dual:** Each Voronoi cell ↔ site; each Voronoi edge ↔ Delaunay edge; each Voronoi vertex ↔ Delaunay triangle circumcenter.
- **Max-min angle property:** Delaunay triangulation **maximises the minimum angle** — critical for FEM mesh quality.
- **Edge flip test:** Sum of opposite angles *α + β > π* ⇒ flip edge; this is the **local Delaunay criterion**.
- **Local ⇒ Global:** A triangulation is globally Delaunay iff every edge is locally Delaunay.
- **Combinatorial bounds:** Triangles = *2n − 2 − k*; Edges = *3n − 3 − k*; always verify with Euler's formula.
- **Uniqueness:** In general position (no 4 cocircular, no 3 collinear), the Delaunay triangulation is unique.
- **Engineering impact:** Used in FEM, GIS, robotics, computer graphics, and molecular modelling — emphasize this in long answers for "application" marks.
- **General position assumption** must always be explicitly stated to avoid losing definitional marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship with Voronoi Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship with Voronoi Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voronoi Diagram — Formal Definition

Given a finite set of points $S = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, called **sites** (or *generators*), the **Voronoi diagram** $\mathcal{V}(S)$ is the subdivision of the plane into $n$ cells, one per site, such that a point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$ lies in the cell $V(p_i)$ of site $p_i$ if and only if:

$$V(p_i) = \left\{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\middle|\; d(q, p_i) \le d(q, p_j) \;\; \forall\, j \ne i \right\}$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the Euclidean distance. The boundary between two cells $V(p_i)$ and $V(p_j)$ is the **perpendicular bisector** of the segment $\overline{p_i p_j}$, called a **Voronoi edge**. A **Voronoi vertex** is a point equidistant from three (or more) sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Voronoi diagram is the *fundamental proximity structure* in computational geometry. It is the **dual** of the Delaunay triangulation, and its construction in $O(n \log n)$ time (via Fortune's sweep or divide-and-conquer) is a board-favourite topic.

## 1.2 Delaunay Triangulation — Formal Definition

The **Delaunay triangulation** $\mathcal{D}(S)$ is a triangulation of the convex hull of $S$ such that **no point of $S$ lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle** in $\mathcal{D}(S)$. This is known as the **Empty Circumcircle Property** (or *Delaunay condition*).

For any Delaunay triangle $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$ with circumcenter $c_{ijk}$ and circumradius $R_{ijk}$:

$$R_{ijk} \;\le\; \min_{p_\ell \in S \setminus \{p_i, p_j, p_k\}} d(c_{ijk}, p_\ell)$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏥

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Ambulance Dispatch in Kerala Districts**
>
> Imagine the government wants to place ambulance stations across Kerala such that every village is served by the *nearest* station. You draw boundaries around each station — every boundary line lies exactly halfway between two stations. This map of districts **is a Voronoi diagram**.
>
> Now, draw straight-line connections between stations that share a boundary. The resulting network of triangles — where no village is "trapped" inside a triangle's circle — **is the Delaunay triangulation**.
>
> - Voronoi = "Whose territory am I in?"
> - Delaunay = "Who are my natural neighbours?"

## 1.4 Why This Relationship Matters in Engineering

| Domain | Voronoi Use | Delaunay Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Telecoms** | Cell-tower coverage zones | Network backbone mesh |
| **Robotics** | Path-planning safe corridors | SLAM feature graphs |
| **GIS** | Service-area allocation | Terrain TIN models |
| **Crystallography** | Wigner–Seitz cells | Lattice adjacency |
| **Bio-informatics** | Protein contact regions | Molecular mesh generation |

The duality between these structures means an algorithm designed for one can often be *converted* into an algorithm for the other at the same time complexity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Voronoi Diagrams

Let $n = \vert S \vert \ge 3$ and assume the points are in **general position** (no four cocircular, no three collinear).

- **P1 — Convexity:** Each Voronoi cell $V(p_i)$ is a (possibly unbounded) **convex polygon**.
- **P2 — Empty-circle property (vertex form):** A point $v$ is a Voronoi vertex iff there exists an **empty circle** centered at $v$ passing through three (or more) sites, with no other site inside.
- **P3 — Combinatorial bounds:**
  - Number of Voronoi vertices $n_V \le 2n - 5$
  - Number of Voronoi edges $n_E \le 3n - 6$
  - Number of Voronoi cells $n_C = n$
- **P4 — Average degree:** The average number of edges meeting at a Voronoi vertex is **less than 6** (in fact, exactly 6 for a regular hexagonal tiling).
- **P5 — Unbounded cells:** The set of sites whose cells are unbounded equals the set of vertices of the **convex hull** of $S$.

## 2.2 Fundamental Properties of Delaunay Triangulations

- **D1 — Planarity:** $\mathcal{D}(S)$ is a **planar graph** (no crossing edges).
- **D2 — Maximizes minimum angle:** Among all possible triangulations of $S$, Delaunay maximizes the minimum angle of any triangle, avoiding "sliver" triangles.
- **D3 — Combinatorial bounds:**
  - Number of Delaunay edges $e \le 3n - 6$
  - Number of Delaunay triangles $t \le 2n - 5$
- **D4 — Local Delaunay property:** A triangulation is Delaunay iff for every edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$, there exists a circle through $p_i, p_j$ containing no other site on either side.

## 2.3 The Duality Relationship — Step by Step

The relationship between Voronoi and Delaunay structures is a **geometric duality**:

**Step 1 — Cell ↔ Site:** Each Voronoi cell $V(p_i)$ corresponds to the Delaunay site $p_i$.

**Step 2 — Edge ↔ Edge:** A Voronoi edge separates $V(p_i)$ and $V(p_j)$ **iff** the Delaunay triangulation contains the edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$.

**Step 3 — Vertex ↔ Triangle:** Each Voronoi vertex $v$ (equidistant from $p_i, p_j, p_k$) corresponds to the Delaunay triangle $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$, and $v$ is precisely the **circumcenter** of that triangle.

**Step 4 — Convex hull ↔ Unbounded cells:** The convex hull of $S$ is the union of Voronoi cells whose sites are on the convex hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Computing $\mathcal{V}(S)$ and $\mathcal{D}(S)$ are computationally equivalent — any $O(f(n))$ algorithm for one yields an $O(f(n))$ algorithm for the other.

## 2.4 Subgraph Relationships

Some classical proximity graphs are **subgraphs** of the Delaunay triangulation:

$$\text{Nearest-Neighbor Graph (NNG)} \;\subseteq\; \text{Delaunay Triangulation} \;\supseteq\; \text{Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)} \;\supseteq\; \text{NNG}$$

**Why MST ⊆ Delaunay?**
Consider the MST edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$ with length $\ell$. The lune (lens) formed by two circles of radius $\ell$ centered at $p_i$ and $p_j$ must be empty — otherwise, a shorter edge would exist, contradicting MST minimality. This lune is contained in a circle through $p_i, p_j$, so the edge satisfies the Delaunay condition.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Property | Formula / Bound | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voronoi cell of $p_i$ | $V(p_i) = \{q \mid d(q,p_i) \le d(q,p_j)\;\forall j\}$ | Set $S$ finite |
| 2 | Number of Voronoi vertices $n_V$ | $n_V \le 2n - 5$ | $n \ge 3$, general position |
| 3 | Number of Voronoi edges $n_E$ | $n_E \le 3n - 6$ | Planar graph bound |
| 4 | Number of Delaunay edges | $e \le 3n - 6$ | Triangulation bound |
| 5 | Number of Delaunay triangles | $t \le 2n - 5$ | Euler + planarity |
| 6 | Average Voronoi degree | $< 6$ | Asymptotic |
| 7 | Circumcenter of Delaunay triangle | $v = (x_v, y_v)$ solving perpendicular bisectors | Unique if non-degenerate |
| 8 | Edge-emptiness condition | $\exists$ empty circle through $\overline{p_i p_j}$ | Local Delaunay |
| 9 | Total combinatorial size | $O(n)$ | Both structures |
| 10 | Construction time | $O(n \log n)$ | Fortune / D&C |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finite Element Meshing (CFD/FEA):** Delaunay triangulations produce well-shaped triangles that improve numerical solver conditioning.
- **Wireless Sensor Networks:** Voronoi cells define the broadcast region of each sensor; the Delaunay graph gives the *minimum-energy* routing backbone.
- **Computer Graphics:** Voronoi shaders (Voronoi noise) generate procedural textures resembling cracked mud, scales, or stained glass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combinatorial Bounds via Euler's Formula

For a connected planar graph $G$ with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces:

$$V - E + F = 2 \quad \text{(Euler's formula)}$$

For the Delaunay triangulation of $n$ sites in general position:

- Number of triangles $t = 2F_{\text{ext}} - 2$ ... wait — let us carefully count.
- Let $h$ = number of sites on the convex hull. The hull itself has $h$ edges and contributes 1 outer face.
- Inner faces = number of triangles = $t$.
- Total faces $F = t + 1$.

Now count edges. Each triangle has 3 edges, and **each interior edge is shared by 2 triangles**, while the **$h$ hull edges are shared by only 1 triangle**. So:

$$\begin{aligned}
3t &= 2E_{\text{interior}} + E_{\text{hull}} \\
   &= 2(E - h) + h \\
   &= 2E - h
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into Euler's formula with $V = n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
n - E + (t + 1) &= 2 \\
n - E + t &= 1 \\
t &= E - n + 1
\end{aligned}$$

Combining with $3t = 2E - h$:

$$\begin{aligned}
3(E - n + 1) &= 2E - h \\
3E - 3n + 3 &= 2E - h \\
E &= 3n - h - 3
\end{aligned}$$

Since $h \ge 3$ for a non-degenerate point set:

$$E \le 3n - 6$$

And substituting back:

$$t = (3n - h - 3) - n + 1 = 2n - h - 2 \le 2n - 5$$

✅ **Confirmed:** Delaunay has at most $3n - 6$ edges and $2n - 5$ triangles.

## 3.2 Derivation: Empty-Circle Property ⇔ Voronoi Vertex

**Claim:** A point $v$ is a Voronoi vertex of $\mathcal{V}(S)$ **iff** the largest empty circle centered at $v$ passes through at least three sites of $S$ and contains no site in its interior.

**(⇒) Forward direction:**

Assume $v$ is a Voronoi vertex where cells $V(p_1), V(p_2), V(p_3)$ meet. Then $v$ is equidistant from $p_1, p_2, p_3$ by definition of the boundaries. The perpendicular bisectors of $\overline{vp_1}, \overline{vp_2}, \overline{vp_3}$ all pass through $v$, so $v$ is the unique common point.

Draw the circle $C$ with center $v$ and radius $r = d(v, p_1) = d(v, p_2) = d(v, p_3)$.

Suppose for contradiction some site $p_4$ lies strictly inside $C$, i.e., $d(v, p_4) < r$. Then $p_4$ would be strictly closer to $v$ than $p_1, p_2, p_3$ are. Hence $v$ would lie in $V(p_4)$ — contradicting the assumption that $v$ is on the boundary of $V(p_1), V(p_2), V(p_3)$ simultaneously.

Therefore $C$ is empty. ✅

**(⇐) Reverse direction:**

Assume a circle $C$ centered at $v$ with radius $r$ passes through three sites $p_1, p_2, p_3$ and contains no other sites. We must show $v$ is a Voronoi vertex.

For any site $p \in S$:

- If $p \in \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}$: $d(v, p) = r$.
- Otherwise (since $C$ is empty): $d(v, p) \ge r$.

Thus $v$ is no closer to any site than to $p_1, p_2, p_3$. Moreover, by moving $v$ slightly toward any of $p_1, p_2, p_3$, the distance to that site strictly decreases while distances to the others remain equal or increase slightly. Hence $v$ lies on the boundary of all three cells — i.e., it is a Voronoi vertex. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation: MST is a Subgraph of the Delaunay Triangulation

**Theorem:** For any site set $S$, the Euclidean Minimum Spanning Tree $\text{MST}(S)$ is a subgraph of $\mathcal{D}(S)$.

**Proof:**

Let $e = \overline{p_i p_j}$ be any edge in $\text{MST}(S)$ with length $\ell = d(p_i, p_j)$. We show that $e$ is a Delaunay edge.

Construct the **lune** $L$ of $e$: the intersection of the two open disks $D(p_i, \ell)$ and $D(p_j, \ell)$. By the **cut property of MSTs**, removing $e$ from the MST splits it into two components $T_1$ (containing $p_i$) and $T_2$ (containing $p_j$).

**Claim:** No site of $S$ lies inside the lune $L$.

Suppose for contradiction that some site $p_k$ lies in $L$. Then $d(p_i, p_k) < \ell$ and $d(p_j, p_k) < \ell$. Since $p_k$ is in one of the two components, say $T_1$ (containing $p_i$), the edge $\overline{p_j, p_k}$ would have length $< \ell = w(e)$, the **maximum-weight** edge in the cycle formed by adding $\overline{p_j, p_k}$ to the MST. By the cycle property, this is a contradiction.

Therefore the lune is empty. Now consider the circle through $p_i$ and $p_j$ centered at the midpoint of the lune's "diameter" — this circle strictly contains the lune, so it is also empty. Hence the local Delaunay condition holds and $e \in \mathcal{D}(S)$. ✅

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Let $S = \{(0,0), (4,0), (2,3)\}$. Compute the Voronoi diagram and Delaunay triangulation.

**Solution:**

The three sites are non-collinear and form a triangle. The Voronoi diagram has exactly **one vertex** (equidistant from all three) — the **circumcenter**.

The circumcenter is the intersection of perpendicular bisectors. From $\overline{(0,0)(4,0)}$, the bisector is $x = 2$. From $\overline{(0,0)(2,3)}$, the bisector is $2x + 3y = (0+2)/2 \cdot 2 \dots$ let's compute:

Midpoint of $(0,0)(2,3)$ is $(1, 1.5)$; slope of segment is $3/2$, so perpendicular slope is $-2/3$:

$$y - 1.5 = -\tfrac{2}{3}(x - 1)$$

At $x = 2$: $y - 1.5 = -\tfrac{2}{3}(1) = -\tfrac{2}{3}$, so $y = 1.5 - 0.667 = 0.833$.

**Voronoi vertex:** $v = (2, 0.833)$.

Voronoi cells: each is unbounded, opening away from the triangle's opposite side.

**Delaunay triangulation:** the single triangle $\triangle (0,0)(4,0)(2,3)$, with the Voronoi vertex as its circumcenter.

**Counts check:** $n = 3$, so $3n - 6 = 3$ edges (matches the triangle), $2n - 5 = 1$ triangle (matches), $2n - 5 = 1$ Voronoi vertex (matches). ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Voronoi–Delaunay Duality Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Site Set S equals p1, p2, ... , pn"] --> B["Voronoi Diagram V of S"]
    A --> C["Delaunay Triangulation D of S"]

    B --> B1["Voronoi Cell V pi"]
    B --> B2["Voronoi Edge = bisector of pi pj"]
    B --> B3["Voronoi Vertex = equidistant from 3+ sites"]

    C --> C1["Delaunay Site pi"]
    C --> C2["Delaunay Edge pi pj"]
    C --> C3["Delaunay Triangle pi pj pk"]

    B1 -.Dual.-> C1
    B2 -.Dual.-> C2
    B3 -.Dual.-> C3

    B3 --> D1["Empty Circle Property"]
    C3 --> D1

    C2 --> E1["MST is subgraph"]
    C2 --> E2["Nearest Neighbor Graph is subgraph"]
    C2 --> E3["Gabriel Graph is subgraph"]
    C2 --> E4["Relative Neighborhood Graph is subgraph"]

    B --> F["Convex Hull = union of unbounded cells"]
    C --> G["Convex Hull = boundary of triangulation"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    I["Input: Point Set S of size n"] --> P1["Preprocessing: General Position Check"]
    P1 --> P2["Compute Convex Hull CH of S"]
    P2 --> A1["Algorithm A: Fortune Sweep"]
    P2 --> A2["Algorithm B: Divide and Conquer"]
    P2 --> A3["Algorithm C: Incremental with Flip"]

    A1 --> O1["Output: Voronoi Diagram V"]
    A2 --> O1
    O1 --> D1["Compute Circumcenters of Voronoi Vertices"]
    D1 --> D2["Connect Adjacent Site-Pairs to Form Triangles"]
    D2 --> O2["Output: Delaunay Triangulation D"]

    A3 --> O3["Direct: Delaunay Triangulation D"]
    O3 --> O1R["Recover Voronoi via Duality"]

    O1 --> APP1["Application: Cell Coverage Maps"]
    O1 --> APP2["Application: Nearest Facility Queries"]
    O2 --> APP3["Application: Mesh Generation for FEM"]
    O2 --> APP4["Application: Terrain Modelling TIN"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Empty-Circle Property

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Site p_i"] --> C["Empty Circle C centered at Voronoi vertex v"]
    P2["Site p_j"] --> C
    P3["Site p_k"] --> C
    C --> V["Voronoi vertex v is the circumcenter of triangle pi pj pk"]
    V --> T["Delaunay Triangle pi pj pk"]
    T --> EC["Condition: No site of S lies strictly inside C"]
    EC --> DEL["pi pj pk is a Delaunay Triangle"]
```

## 4.4 Algorithm Complexity Comparison Table

| Construction Method | Voronoi Time | Delaunay Time | Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fortune's Sweep | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ via duality | $O(n)$ |
| Divide & Conquer | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Incremental + Flip | $O(n^2)$ worst | $O(n^2)$ worst | $O(n)$ |
| Randomized Incremental | Expected $O(n \log n)$ | Expected $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Voronoi diagram. State any two properties of the Voronoi diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A Voronoi diagram of a finite set of points $S = \{p_1, \dots, p_n\}$ in the plane is the subdivision of the plane into $n$ cells, where cell $V(p_i)$ contains all points closer to $p_i$ than to any other site.

$$V(p_i) = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(q, p_i) \le d(q, p_j),\; \forall j \ne i\}$$

**Properties:**
1. Each Voronoi cell is a **convex polygon** (possibly unbounded).
2. The number of Voronoi vertices is at most $\mathbf{2n - 5}$ and the number of edges is at most $\mathbf{3n - 6}$ for $n \ge 3$ sites in general position.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the relationship between the Voronoi diagram and the Delaunay triangulation? Mention the empty circle property.**

**Model Answer:**

The Delaunay triangulation $\mathcal{D}(S)$ is the **geometric dual** of the Voronoi diagram $\mathcal{V}(S)$:
- A Voronoi edge between cells $V(p_i)$ and $V(p_j)$ corresponds to a Delaunay edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$.
- A Voronoi vertex (meeting point of three cells) corresponds to a Delaunay triangle whose circumcenter is that vertex.

**Empty Circle Property:** For every Delaunay triangle $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$, the circumcircle contains no other site of $S$ in its interior. This is the defining characterization of the Delaunay triangulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks for (a) failing to state assumptions like "general position" (no three collinear, no four cocircular), (b) confusing Voronoi vertices with sites, and (c) writing combinatorial bounds without justifying via Euler's formula. Always show the formula sheet values **with conditions** attached.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the Voronoi diagram for a set of $n$ sites. With a neat sketch, explain the construction using the perpendicular bisector method for $n = 4$ sites. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the number of Voronoi edges in the diagram is at most $3n - 6$. Show that the Delaunay triangulation is the dual of the Voronoi diagram by enumerating the duality correspondences. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

For $n$ sites, the Voronoi diagram partitions the plane such that $V(p_i)$ contains all points closer to $p_i$ than to any other $p_j$. The boundary between $V(p_i)$ and $V(p_j)$ lies on the **perpendicular bisector** of the segment $\overline{p_i p_j}$.

**Construction steps for $n = 4$ sites** $p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4$:

1. For each pair of sites $(p_i, p_j)$, construct the perpendicular bisector line $\ell_{ij}$.
2. For each site $p_i$, intersect the half-planes $\{q \mid d(q, p_i) \le d(q, p_j)\}$ over all $j \ne i$.
3. The intersection of these $n - 1$ half-planes gives the convex (possibly unbounded) cell $V(p_i)$.
4. The cells tile the plane.

A neat sketch shows four sites, six bisectors, and four cells meeting at a central Voronoi vertex (the circumcenter of the Delaunay triangle in the interior).

**Key points:** $n$ cells, $3n - 6 = 6$ edges for $n = 4$ (when general position holds).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Proof of the bound $n_E \le 3n - 6$:**

The Voronoi diagram of $n$ sites in general position is a planar graph with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces. By Euler's formula:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

Let $n$ = number of sites, $n_V$ = Voronoi vertices, $n_E$ = edges, and $n_C = n$ = cells (faces, excluding the unbounded outer face only if we treat the diagram as embedded in the plane). Including the outer face, $F = n + 1$.

Hence:

$$n_V - n_E + (n + 1) = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_V - n_E + n = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_E = n_V + n - 1$$

Now, each Voronoi vertex is the meeting point of at least 3 cells, contributing at least 3 incident edge-endpoints. By handshake:

$$2 n_E \ge 3 n_V \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_V \le \tfrac{2}{3} n_E$$

Substituting:

$$n_E \le \tfrac{2}{3} n_E + n - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tfrac{1}{3} n_E \le n - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_E \le 3n - 3$$

With the tighter "no three cocircular" general position assumption, equality rarely holds and the bound becomes $3n - 6$ for $n \ge 3$.

**Duality correspondences (table form):**

| Voronoi | ↔ | Delaunay |
|---|---|---|
| Cell $V(p_i)$ | ↔ | Site $p_i$ |
| Edge between $V(p_i), V(p_j)$ | ↔ | Edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$ |
| Vertex where $V(p_i), V(p_j), V(p_k)$ meet | ↔ | Triangle $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$ (with that vertex as circumcenter) |

Each Voronoi cell corresponds to one Delaunay site (1-to-1), each Voronoi edge corresponds to exactly one Delaunay edge, and each Voronoi vertex corresponds to exactly one Delaunay triangle. Thus the two structures are **duals**. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define Delaunay triangulation. State and prove the empty circle (empty circumcircle) property. Explain its connection to the Voronoi diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Show that the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of a set of points is a subgraph of its Delaunay triangulation. Hence explain why the Delaunay triangulation is a "good" structure for proximity-based applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** A Delaunay triangulation of a point set $S$ is a triangulation of the convex hull of $S$ such that the circumcircle of every triangle contains no other point of $S$ in its interior.

**Empty Circle Property (Statement):** A triangulation of $S$ is Delaunay **iff** the circumcircle of every triangle in it is empty of other sites.

**Proof of (⇒):** Suppose triangle $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$ is Delaunay. Assume for contradiction that some site $p_\ell$ lies strictly inside its circumcircle $C$ with center $c$ and radius $R$. Then $d(c, p_\ell) < R$. But $c$ is equidistant from $p_i, p_j, p_k$, so by definition $c$ is a Voronoi vertex (by the converse argument in §3.2). However, the presence of $p_\ell$ inside $C$ would mean $p_\ell$ is closer to $c$ than $p_i$ is, contradicting that $c \in V(p_i)$ (and the other two). Hence $C$ must be empty. ✅

**Proof of (⇐):** Suppose every triangle in the triangulation has an empty circumcircle. For any edge $\overline{p_i p_j}$ shared by two triangles $\triangle p_i p_j p_k$ and $\triangle p_i p_j p_\ell$, the local empty-circle property holds: there exists a circle through $p_i, p_j$ empty of other points on each side (the two circumcircles). This is exactly the *local Delaunay condition*, which by the well-known equivalence theorem (local ⇔ global) implies the triangulation is globally Delaunay. ✅

**Connection to Voronoi diagram:** The center of the empty circumcircle is precisely the **Voronoi vertex** where the three cells $V(p_i), V(p_j), V(p_k)$ meet. This is the fundamental duality between the two structures.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Theorem:** $\text{MST}(S) \subseteq \mathcal{D}(S)$.

**Proof:** Let $e = \overline{p_i p_j}$ be any edge of $\text{MST}(S)$ with length $\ell = d(p_i, p_j)$. Remove $e$; the MST splits into two components $T_1 \ni p_i$ and $T_2 \ni p_j$. Consider the **lune** $L = D(p_i, \ell) \cap D(p_j, \ell)$, where $D(c, r)$ is the open disk of radius $r$ around $c$.

**Claim:** $L$ contains no site of $S$.

*Proof of claim:* Suppose $p_k \in L$. Then $d(p_i, p_k) < \ell$ and $d(p_j, p_k) < \ell$. Without loss of generality, $p_k \in T_1$. The edge $\overline{p_j, p_k}$ when added to the MST creates a cycle. The unique heaviest edge in this cycle is $e$ (otherwise MST would not be minimum). But $d(p_j, p_k) < \ell = w(e)$, contradicting the cycle property. Hence $L$ is empty. ✅

The circle $C$ with diameter $\overline{p_i p_j}$ (radius $\ell/2$) is strictly contained inside the lune's circumscribing structure and is therefore also empty. By the local Delaunay condition, $e$ is a Delaunay edge. Since $e$ was arbitrary, $\text{MST}(S) \subseteq \mathcal{D}(S)$. ✅

**Why Delaunay is "good" for proximity applications:**

1. **Edge count is linear:** $O(n)$ edges, so proximity queries (nearest neighbour) take $O(\log n)$ with a suitable search structure.
2. **Contains all "natural" proximity graphs:** MST, NNG, GG, RNG are all subgraphs — so Delaunay is a *superset* carrying all useful proximity information.
3. **Avoids slivers:** Maximum-minimum-angle property gives well-shaped triangles, critical for FEM/CFD meshing.
4. **Constructible in $O(n \log n)$:** Efficient algorithms (Fortune's sweep) make it practical for large datasets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

- ✅ Voronoi diagram = **proximity-based partition** of the plane; each cell contains all points closest to one site.
- ✅ Delaunay triangulation = **dual of Voronoi**; circumcircle of every triangle is empty.
- ✅ Voronoi vertex (3 cells meet) ↔ Delaunay triangle (circumcenter is that vertex).
- ✅ Voronoi edge (boundary of 2 cells) ↔ Delaunay edge.
- ✅ Voronoi cell ↔ Delaunay site (1-to-1).
- ✅ Combinatorial bounds: vertices $\le 2n - 5$, edges $\le 3n - 6$, triangles $\le 2n - 5$.
- ✅ Construction time: $O(n \log n)$ (Fortune's sweep / divide-and-conquer).
- ✅ $\text{NNG} \subseteq \text{MST} \subseteq \text{Delaunay}$.
- ✅ Sites on the convex hull = sites with unbounded Voronoi cells.
- ✅ Maximum-minimum-angle property of Delaunay is critical for mesh generation.
- ✅ Always assume **general position** (no 3 collinear, no 4 cocircular) when stating bounds.
- ✅ Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2$ is the standard proof engine for combinatorial bounds.
- ✅ For $n = 3$ sites, the Voronoi diagram has **exactly 1 vertex** and **3 unbounded cells**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bowyer-Watson Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bowyer-Watson Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bowyer-Watson Algorithm** is an elegant **incremental insertion algorithm** used to construct the **Delaunay Triangulation** of a finite set of points in the Euclidean plane (ℝ²). It was independently discovered by **Adrian Bowyer** (1981) and **David F. Watson** (1981). The algorithm maintains the fundamental **Empty Circumcircle Property** (also called the Delaunay condition) at every intermediate stage by detecting and locally repairing violations as new points are inserted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delaunay Triangulation Maximizes the Minimum Angle** among all possible triangulations, producing well-shaped (non-sliver) triangles — this is the "Empty Circle Property." A triangulation is Delaunay if and only if the circumcircle of every triangle contains no other input point in its interior.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Peg Board and Rubber Bands"

Imagine a **wooden board with pegs** hammered into it at various positions. Now take a set of **rubber bands** and stretch them around the pegs:
- If you start with a **giant triangle (super-triangle)** that encloses *all* pegs, you have an initial rubber band outline.
- When you insert a new peg, you must **cut the rubber bands** of every triangle whose "circle of influence" (circumcircle) is violated by the new peg.
- A **polygonal hole (cavity)** opens up.
- You then **stretch new rubber bands** from the new peg to every exposed edge of the cavity.
- Repeating this for every peg builds a clean, well-shaped triangulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Exams:**
> - **Super-Triangle** — Initial bounding triangle containing all input points.
> - **Bad Triangle** — A triangle whose circumcircle contains the new point.
> - **Cavity (Star-shaped Polygon)** — The union of all bad triangles removed at each step.
> - **Boundary Polygon (Hole)** — The polygonal cavity formed after removing bad triangles.
> - **Legal/Illegal Edge** — An edge is *legal* if it satisfies the Delaunay (empty circumcircle) condition.

### 1.3 Why is the Bowyer-Watson Algorithm Important in Engineering?

- **Finite Element Meshing (FEM/CFD):** Generates high-quality adaptive meshes that avoid degenerate (sliver) triangles, crucial for convergence of solvers.
- **Computer Graphics & Terrain Rendering:** Used to build TIN (Triangulated Irregular Networks) for surface reconstruction.
- **GPS / GIS Mapping:** Surface reconstruction from scattered point cloud data.
- **Medical Imaging:** 3D reconstruction from CT/MRI scan points.
- **Robotics & Path Planning:** Configuration space triangulation.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Bowyer-Watson algorithm proceeds in **5 systematic stages** for every inserted point:

**Step 1 — Initialization with a Super-Triangle**
- Construct a large triangle (the *super-triangle*) that strictly contains all input points.
- This guarantees that the initial triangulation is valid (a single triangle trivially satisfies the Delaunay condition).

**Step 2 — Locate All "Bad" Triangles**
- For the point $P$ being inserted, identify every triangle $T_i$ in the current triangulation whose **circumcircle strictly contains $P$**.
- A triangle is *bad* if and only if it violates the empty circumcircle property with respect to $P$.

**Step 3 — Extract the Cavity**
- The union of all bad triangles forms a **star-shaped polygonal region** (the *cavity*).
- Remove these bad triangles from the triangulation, leaving an unfilled polygonal hole.

**Step 4 — Identify the Boundary Polygon**
- Determine all edges of the cavity that are **not shared** by any *other* bad triangle.
- These exposed edges form the *boundary polygon* (hole boundary) of the cavity.
- The number of boundary edges is $k$, where $3 \le k \le 2n - h$ (with $h$ the number of holes, but typically $h = 1$ in Bowyer-Watson).

**Step 5 — Retriangulate the Cavity**
- Connect $P$ to every boundary edge of the cavity.
- This creates $k$ new triangles, all of which now satisfy the Delaunay (empty circumcircle) property by construction.

**Step 6 — Iterate and Cleanup**
- Repeat Steps 2–5 for every input point.
- Finally, **remove all triangles incident to the super-triangle's vertices**, leaving only the Delaunay triangulation of the original input points.

### 2.2 Core Geometric Predicates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two predicates drive the entire algorithm:**
> 1. **In-Circumcircle Test (Orient2D/InCircle)** — decides if a point lies inside a triangle's circumcircle.
> 2. **Circumcenter Computation** — used to compute the geometric center of a triangle's circumcircle.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **In-Circle Determinant Test** | $ \text{InCircle}(A,B,C,D) = \begin{vmatrix} a_x - d_x & a_y - d_y & (a_x - d_x)^2 + (a_y - d_y)^2 \\ b_x - d_x & b_y - d_y & (b_x - d_x)^2 + (b_y - d_y)^2 \\ c_x - d_x & c_y - d_y & (c_x - d_x)^2 + (c_y - d_y)^2 \end{vmatrix} $ | $D$ inside circumcircle of $\triangle ABC$ if det $> 0$ (CCW) |
| 2 | **Circumcenter X-Coordinate** | $U_x = \dfrac{(x_1^2 + y_1^2)(y_2 - y_3) + (x_2^2 + y_2^2)(y_3 - y_1) + (x_3^2 + y_3^2)(y_1 - y_2)}{D}$ | $x$-coord of triangle's circumcenter |
| 3 | **Circumcenter Y-Coordinate** | $U_y = \dfrac{(x_1^2 + y_1^2)(x_3 - x_2) + (x_2^2 + y_2^2)(x_1 - x_3) + (x_3^2 + y_3^2)(x_2 - x_1)}{D}$ | $y$-coord of triangle's circumcenter |
| 4 | **Denominator of Circumcenter** | $D = 2\left[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)\right]$ | Common denominator; $D \ne 0$ iff non-collinear |
| 5 | **Circumradius** | $R = \dfrac{\vert AB \vert \cdot \vert BC \vert \cdot \vert CA \vert}{4 \cdot [\triangle ABC]}$ | Where $[\triangle ABC]$ is the triangle's signed area |
| 6 | **Area of Triangle** | $[\triangle ABC] = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)\vert$ | Standard shoelace formula |
| 7 | **Algorithm Complexity (Worst Case)** | $T(n) = O(n^2)$ | $n$ = number of input points |
| 8 | **Algorithm Complexity (Average / Planar)** | $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ | Expected case for random points |
| 9 | **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ triangles in 2D (since planar graph has $\le 2n - 2 - h$ triangles) | Euler's formula in action |
| 10 | **Cavity Star Property** | Every bad triangle's edge is shared with at most one other bad triangle on the cavity boundary | Guarantees hole is topologically valid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Focus:** The **InCircle determinant** is the *single most important* formula in KTU exams for this topic. Memorize its sign convention: **CCW orientation of $A,B,C$ ⇒ $D$ inside circumcircle iff determinant $> 0$.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bowyer-Watson algorithm is the workhorse behind:
- **ANSYS / COMSOL Meshing:** Adaptive mesh refinement in finite element analysis.
- **Unity / Unreal Engine:** Procedural terrain generation.
- **OpenStreetMap Rendering:** Triangulated surface layers.
- **3D Scanning Pipelines (LiDAR):** Point cloud triangulation in autonomous vehicles.
- **Weather Forecasting:** Adaptive atmospheric mesh refinement.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Circumcenter Coordinates of a Triangle

**Problem:** Given three non-collinear points $A(x_1, y_1)$, $B(x_2, y_2)$, $C(x_3, y_3)$, derive the circumcenter $O(U_x, U_y)$.

**Step 1 — Setup the perpendicular bisector equations.**

The circumcenter is equidistant from all three vertices:
$$
\vert OA \vert^2 = \vert OB \vert^2 = \vert OC \vert^2
$$

Equating $\vert OA \vert^2 = \vert OB \vert^2$:
$$
(U_x - x_1)^2 + (U_y - y_1)^2 = (U_x - x_2)^2 + (U_y - y_2)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expand and simplify.**

Expanding the squares:
$$
U_x^2 - 2 U_x x_1 + x_1^2 + U_y^2 - 2 U_y y_1 + y_1^2 = U_x^2 - 2 U_x x_2 + x_2^2 + U_y^2 - 2 U_y y_2 + y_2^2
$$

Cancel $U_x^2$ and $U_y^2$ from both sides:
$$
-2 U_x x_1 + x_1^2 - 2 U_y y_1 + y_1^2 = -2 U_x x_2 + x_2^2 - 2 U_y y_2 + y_2^2
$$

Rearranging:
$$
2 U_x (x_2 - x_1) + 2 U_y (y_2 - y_1) = (x_2^2 + y_2^2) - (x_1^2 + y_1^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Form the first linear equation.**
$$
2 U_x (x_2 - x_1) + 2 U_y (y_2 - y_1) = x_2^2 + y_2^2 - x_1^2 - y_1^2 \quad \text{...(I)}
$$

**Step 4 — Similarly, equate $\vert OB \vert^2 = \vert OC \vert^2$.**
$$
2 U_x (x_3 - x_2) + 2 U_y (y_3 - y_2) = x_3^2 + y_3^2 - x_2^2 - y_2^2 \quad \text{...(II)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the linear system using Cramer's Rule.**

The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:
$$
D = \begin{vmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_2) & 2(y_3 - y_2) \end{vmatrix}
$$

Expanding:
$$
D = 4\left[(x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_2) - (x_3 - x_2)(y_2 - y_1)\right]
$$

Factoring the standard area form (and noting that $4 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 2$):
$$
D = 2\left[x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Cramer's Rule for $U_x$.**

Replace the first column with the RHS constants:
$$
U_x = \frac{1}{D} \begin{vmatrix} (x_2^2+y_2^2) - (x_1^2+y_1^2) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ (x_3^2+y_3^2) - (x_2^2+y_2^2) & 2(y_3 - y_2) \end{vmatrix}
$$

Expanding the determinant:
$$
U_x = \frac{1}{D}\left[2(x_1^2+y_1^2)(y_2-y_3) + 2(x_2^2+y_2^2)(y_3-y_1) + 2(x_3^2+y_3^2)(y_1-y_2)\right]
$$

Dividing by $D = 2[\,\cdot\,]$:
$$
U_x = \frac{(x_1^2+y_1^2)(y_2-y_3) + (x_2^2+y_2^2)(y_3-y_1) + (x_3^2+y_3^2)(y_1-y_2)}{x_1(y_2-y_3) + x_2(y_3-y_1) + x_3(y_1-y_2)}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Cramer's Rule for $U_y$.**

Similarly, replacing the second column:
$$
U_y = \frac{1}{D} \begin{vmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & (x_2^2+y_2^2) - (x_1^2+y_1^2) \\ 2(x_3 - x_2) & (x_3^2+y_3^2) - (x_2^2+y_2^2) \end{vmatrix}
$$

This yields:
$$
U_y = \frac{(x_1^2+y_1^2)(x_3-x_2) + (x_2^2+y_2^2)(x_1-x_3) + (x_3^2+y_3^2)(x_2-x_1)}{x_1(y_2-y_3) + x_2(y_3-y_1) + x_3(y_1-y_2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both formulas have the same denominator, which is **twice the signed area** of the triangle. If this denominator is zero, the three points are **collinear** and no circumcircle exists.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: In-Circumcircle Determinant Test

**Problem:** Given triangle $A(x_1,y_1), B(x_2,y_2), C(x_3,y_3)$ (oriented CCW) and a query point $D(x,y)$, determine whether $D$ lies inside the circumcircle.

**Step 1 — Equation of a general circle.**

A circle with center $(U_x, U_y)$ and radius $R$ satisfies:
$$
(x - U_x)^2 + (y - U_y)^2 = R^2
$$

Expanding:
$$
x^2 - 2U_x x + U_x^2 + y^2 - 2U_y y + U_y^2 - R^2 = 0
$$

Let $\alpha = -2U_x$, $\beta = -2U_y$, $\gamma = U_x^2 + U_y^2 - R^2$. Then:
$$
x^2 + y^2 + \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Use points $A$, $B$, $C$ to solve for $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ (in matrix form).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -(x_1^2 + y_1^2) \\ -(x_2^2 + y_2^2) \\ -(x_3^2 + y_3^2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute point $D$ into the circle equation.**

If $D$ lies **on** the circumcircle:
$$
x^2 + y^2 + \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the system into a single determinant condition.**

For $D$ to lie inside/outside/on the circumcircle, we can combine the 3 linear equations and the $D$-equation into a $4 \times 4$ determinant. The sign of the determinant (when $A,B,C$ are CCW) tells us:
- $\Delta > 0$: $D$ is **inside** the circumcircle (bad triangle)
- $\Delta = 0$: $D$ is **on** the circumcircle
- $\Delta < 0$: $D$ is **outside** the circumcircle

After factoring, the equivalent **$3 \times 3$ in-circle determinant** is:
$$
\text{InCircle}(A,B,C,D) = \begin{vmatrix} x_1 - x & y_1 - y & (x_1 - x)^2 + (y_1 - y)^2 \\ x_2 - x & y_2 - y & (x_2 - x)^2 + (y_2 - y)^2 \\ x_3 - x & y_3 - y & (x_3 - x)^2 + (y_3 - y)^2 \end{vmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The translation by $(x, y)$ (i.e., using $(x_i - x, y_i - y)$) makes this formulation **numerically more stable** than the unshifted $4 \times 4$ version. Always use this form in KTU exam answers.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical Application

**Given Points:** $A(0,0)$, $B(4,0)$, $C(0,3)$, and query $D(1,1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute denominator $D$.**
$$
D = 2\left[0(0-3) + 4(3-0) + 0(0-0)\right] = 2(0 + 12 + 0) = 24
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $U_x$ (circumcenter x-coordinate).**
$$
U_x = \frac{(0+0)(0-3) + (16+0)(3-0) + (0+9)(0-0)}{12} = \frac{0 + 48 + 0}{12} = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $U_y$.**
$$
U_y = \frac{(0+0)(0-4) + (16+0)(0-0) + (0+9)(4-0)}{12} = \frac{0 + 0 + 36}{12} = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the circumcircle.**
- Center: $(4, 3)$, Radius: $\sqrt{16+9} = 5$.
- Distance from $(4,3)$ to $D(1,1)$: $\sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 < 5$.
- **Conclusion:** $D$ lies **inside** the circumcircle ⇒ $\triangle ABC$ is a *bad triangle* with respect to $D$.

**Step 5 — InCircle determinant verification.**
$$
\text{InCircle} = \begin{vmatrix} 0-1 & 0-1 & 1+1 \\ 4-1 & 0-1 & 9+1 \\ 0-1 & 3-1 & 9+1 \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 & 10 \\ -1 & 2 & 10 \end{vmatrix}
$$

Expanding along row 1:
$$
= -1[(-1)(10) - (10)(2)] - (-1)[(3)(10) - (10)(-1)] + 2[(3)(2) - (-1)(-1)]
$$
$$
= -1[-10 - 20] + 1[30 + 10] + 2[6 - 1]
$$
$$
= -1(-30) + 1(40) + 2(5) = 30 + 40 + 10 = \mathbf{+80}
$$

> **Positive determinant $\Rightarrow$ $D$ is inside the circumcircle $\Rightarrow$ $\triangle ABC$ is BAD with respect to $D$.** ✓ Result matches.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — Bowyer-Watson

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Start - Create Super-Triangle Enclosing All Input Points] --> B[Step 2: Initialize Triangulation T with Super-Triangle]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select Next Input Point P from Set S]
    C --> D[Step 4: For Each Triangle in T - Test InCircle with P]
    D --> E[Step 5: Identify All Bad Triangles - Circumcircle Contains P]
    E --> F[Step 6: Remove All Bad Triangles from T]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute Boundary Polygon - Edges Not Shared by Two Bad Triangles]
    G --> H[Step 8: Form New Triangles by Connecting P to Each Boundary Edge]
    H --> I[Step 9: Add New Triangles to T]
    I --> J{Step 10: All Points Inserted?}
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 11: Remove All Triangles Incident to Super-Triangle Vertices]
    K --> L[Step 12: Output Final Delaunay Triangulation T]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Bowyer-Watson

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input: Set of Points S] --> SUPERTRI[Super-Triangle Module]
    SUPERTRI --> INITTRI[Initial Triangulation Buffer]
    INITTRI --> INSERTLOOP[Insertion Loop Controller]
    INSERTLOOP --> BADFIND[Bad Triangle Finder: InCircle Test]
    BADFIND --> CAVITYFORM[Cavity Formation Module]
    CAVITYFORM --> BOUNDEDGE[Boundary Edge Extractor]
    BOUNDEDGE --> RETRI[Retriangulation Engine]
    RETRI --> UPDATEBUF[Update Triangulation Buffer]
    UPDATEBUF --> INSERTLOOP
    INSERTLOOP --> CLEANUP{Cleanup Phase}
    CLEANUP --> SUPERREMOVE[Remove Super-Triangle Vertices]
    SUPERREMOVE --> OUTPUT[Output: Delaunay Triangulation]
```

### 4.3 Cavity Construction — Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE1["Phase 1: BEFORE Insertion of P"]
        T1[Triangle T1 - Legal]:::legal
        T2[Triangle T2 - Bad]:::bad
        T3[Triangle T3 - Bad]:::bad
        T4[Triangle T4 - Legal]:::legal
        PNEW[New Point P]:::newpoint
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["Phase 2: AFTER Cavity Extraction"]
        HOLE[Polygonal Cavity Hole]:::cavity
        PEDGES[Boundary Edges Exposed]:::edges
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["Phase 3: AFTER Retriangulation"]
        NT1[New Triangle P-A-B]:::newtri
        NT2[New Triangle P-B-C]:::newtri
        NT3[New Triangle P-C-D]:::newtri
        NT4[New Triangle P-D-E]:::newtri
    end

    T2 -. "Removed (Circumcircle contains P)" .-> HOLE
    T3 -. "Removed (Circumcircle contains P)" .-> HOLE
    HOLE --> PEDGES
    PEDGES --> NT1
    PEDGES --> NT2
    PEDGES --> NT3
    PEDGES --> NT4

    classDef legal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef newpoint fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef cavity fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    classDef edges fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef newtri fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Computational Phases — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PREP["Preparation Phase"]
        P1[Read Input Points]
        P2[Compute Bounding Box]
        P3[Generate Super-Triangle 3x larger than bounding box]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Incremental Insertion Phase - Hot Path"]
        C1[For Each Point Pi]
        C2[InCircle Predicate Test on all current triangles]
        C3[Mark Violating Triangles as Bad]
        C4[Build Cavity Boundary Loop]
        C5[Emit New Triangles with Pi as Apex]
    end

    subgraph FINAL["Finalization Phase"]
        F1[Delete Super-Triangle Vertices]
        F2[Compact Edge List]
        F3[Return Final Triangulation]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    C5 -->|Next Point| C1
    C5 -->|All Points Done| F1 --> F2 --> F3
```
