<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1-Dimensional Range Searching

## 1. Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1-D Range Searching Problem:** Given a *static* set $S$ of $n$ real numbers (points on a one-dimensional line) and a query interval $[x_1 : x_2]$ with $x_1 \le x_2$, the **1-D Range Searching** problem asks us to preprocess $S$ into a data structure so that we can efficiently *report* (or *count*) all points $p \in S$ such that $x_1 \le p \le x_2$.

### Two Principal Variants
| Variant | Output | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Range Reporting** | All points in the interval | $S \cap [x_1, x_2]$ |
| **Range Counting** | Number of points in the interval | $\vert S \cap [x_1, x_2] \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, the focus is overwhelmingly on the **reporting** variant. The counting variant is a trivial extension via the *rank* operation in a balanced BST.

## 2. Conceptual Analogy (The "Bus Stop" Intuition)

Imagine **10 bus stops** along a single road, located at positions: $\{2, 5, 8, 12, 17, 23, 29, 34, 41, 50\}$ kilometres from the city centre. A passenger asks: *"Show me every bus stop between the 15th and 35th kilometre."*

A **brute-force approach** would walk past every single stop and check — slow if you have millions of stops.
A **smart approach** is to:
1. **Sort** the bus stops once (preprocessing).
2. Use **binary search** to instantly locate the *first* stop $\ge 15$ km (Stop at 17 km).
3. **Walk forward** until the *first* stop $> 35$ km (Stop at 41 km).
4. **Report** all stops encountered in between: $\{17, 23, 29, 34\}$.

This is precisely how 1-D range searching works. The "smart" approach above is the foundation of all efficient 1-D range data structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The query cost has **two components** — *search time* (to find the boundaries) + *output time* (proportional to the number of points reported). The standard complexity form $O(\log n + k)$ captures both, where $k$ is the number of reported points. This is called an **output-sensitive** bound.

## 3. Why Preprocessing is Essential

For a *one-time* query, scanning all $n$ points takes $O(n)$ time — this is unbeatable. But if we must answer **$m$ queries** on the **same** set $S$, total cost becomes $O(m \cdot n)$, which becomes prohibitive for large $m$. Preprocessing pays a one-time cost (e.g., $O(n \log n)$) so that *each* subsequent query runs in $O(\log n + k)$, giving an amortized $O(\log n + k)$ per query.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Three Classical Data Structures for 1-D Range Searching

### 1.1 Sorted Array
- Store points in a sorted array $A[1 \dots n]$.
- **Query:** Binary search for the leftmost index $\ell$ such that $A[\ell] \ge x_1$, then scan rightward until $A[r] > x_2$. Report $A[\ell], A[\ell+1], \dots, A[r-1]$.
- Cannot handle **insertions/deletions** efficiently (would require $O(n)$ shifts).

### 1.2 Balanced Binary Search Tree (BBST)
- Store points in a self-balancing BST (AVL, Red-Black, Treap).
- **Query:** Two descent searches, one for $x_1$ and one for $x_2$, identifying the *split node* where their paths diverge. Then perform a controlled in-order traversal, *pruning* subtrees that lie entirely outside the query range.
- Supports **dynamic** updates (insert/delete) in $O(\log n)$ time.

### 1.3 1-D Range Tree
- Conceptually, a BBST with **canonical subsets** stored at each internal node.
- In 1-D, the canonical subset at node $v$ is simply the **sorted list of all points in the subtree rooted at $v$**.
- This is the *direct precursor* of the **2-D Range Tree** covered in higher modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** When the question says "1-D Range Tree," examiners expect a *balanced BST* storing the points, possibly augmented with a `subtree_max` field. The 2-D generalisation (multi-level structure) is *not* required in 1-D.

## 2. Detailed Algorithm: 1-D Range Tree Query

### 2.1 Node Structure
A range tree node $v$ typically stores:
- `point[v]` — the splitting key (for internal nodes; the actual point for leaves).
- `left[v]`, `right[v]` — child pointers.
- `max[v]` — the **maximum** point in the subtree rooted at $v$ (an auxiliary field used for pruning).

### 2.2 Query Algorithm (Pseudo-code)
The standard range query is denoted $RangeQuery(v, [x_1, x_2])$.

**Step 1: Find the Split Node**
- Trace a path from the root following BST rules for $x_1$ and $x_2$.
- The split node $v_{split}$ is the *lowest common ancestor* of the two search paths.

**Step 2: Visit Nodes on the $x_1$ Path**
- For each node $u$ on the $x_1$ search path where we go *left* at $u$ (i.e., $x_1 \le point[u]$), the **right subtree** of $u$ lies entirely within $[x_1, x_2]$ (because $point[u] \le x_2$). Report the entire right subtree via in-order traversal.

**Step 3: Visit Nodes on the $x_2$ Path**
- Symmetrically, for each node $u$ on the $x_2$ search path where we go *right* (i.e., $x_2 \ge point[u]$), the **left subtree** of $u$ lies entirely within $[x_1, x_2]$. Report the entire left subtree.

**Step 4: Report Points at the Split Node**
- The point at $v_{split}$ itself lies in $[x_1, x_2]$ and must be reported.

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Bound | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Space** of 1-D Range Tree | $O(n)$ | $n$ points + $O(1)$ auxiliary data per node |
| **Build Time** | $O(n \log n)$ | Insert $n$ points into an initially empty BBST |
| **Build Time (sorted input)** | $O(n)$ | Build bottom-up in linear time |
| **Query Time (Range Reporting)** | $O(\log n + k)$ | $\log n$ for the two descents, $k$ for the output |
| **Query Time (Range Counting)** | $O(\log n)$ | Use *rank* operations on the BBST |
| **Insert / Delete (Dynamic)** | $O(\log n)$ | Standard BBST rebalancing cost |
| **Number of Visited Subtrees** | $O(\log n)$ | At most $2 \log n$ subtrees traversed in entirety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** The bound $O(\log n + k)$ is **NOT** $O(\log n \cdot k)$. Students often confuse multiplicative and additive combinations. The "$\log n$" pays for *finding the range boundaries*; the "$k$" pays for *outputting the points*. They are summed because they are sequential operations.

## 4. Engineering Utility

- **Database systems** (e.g., B+ tree indexes for range queries on sorted columns).
- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Finding all landmarks within a longitude strip.
- **Time-series databases:** Querying all events within a timestamp window.
- **Network routing tables:** Finding all entries within a prefix range.
- **Search engines:** Returning all documents indexed between two relevance scores.
- **Computational biology:** Locating genes within a genomic coordinate range.

## 5. Why We Need Better Than Brute Force

A naive scan is $O(n)$ per query. With $m$ queries, total cost is $O(mn)$. The BBST method gives $O(n \log n + m(\log n + k))$, an **asymptotic win** whenever $m \gg 1$ and the average $k \ll n$. The worst case where every query returns $O(n)$ points is essentially *unavoidable* — you *must* spend $O(n)$ time to output $n$ points, so $k$ in the bound is fundamentally necessary.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Query Time Complexity $O(\log n + k)$

We rigorously prove that a range query on a 1-D range tree (balanced BST) over $n$ points takes $O(\log n + k)$ time, where $k$ is the number of points in the query range $[x_1, x_2]$.

### Setup
Let $T$ be a balanced BST of height $h = O(\log n)$ containing the $n$ points. Each node stores a point and has left/right children, with the BST invariant: $\text{point}[u] < \text{point}[u.\text{left}]$... Actually, standard: for any node $u$, every point in the left subtree is $\le \text{point}[u]$ and every point in the right subtree is $\ge \text{point}[u]$.

### Phase 1: Search Path Lengths

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path}(x_1) &= \text{sequence of nodes visited when searching for } x_1 \text{ in } T \\
\text{Path}(x_2) &= \text{sequence of nodes visited when searching for } x_2 \text{ in } T
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T$ is balanced, each path has length at most $h+1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \text{Path}(x_1) \vert &\le h + 1 = O(\log n) \\
\vert \text{Path}(x_2) \vert &\le h + 1 = O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Total time to walk both paths: $T_{\text{search}} = O(\log n)$.

### Phase 2: Subtree Reporting

On path $x_1$, the algorithm visits the *right subtrees* of nodes where it descends *left*. The number of such nodes is bounded by the path length:

$$
N_{x_1}^{\text{subtrees}} \le h = O(\log n)
$$

Similarly on path $x_2$, the number of *left subtrees* visited is:

$$
N_{x_2}^{\text{subtrees}} \le h = O(\log n)
$$

### Phase 3: In-Order Traversal of Reported Subtrees

Let $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_p$ be the subtrees that are *fully inside* the query range, with $p \le 2h = O(\log n)$. We perform an in-order traversal of each:

$$
T_{\text{report}} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{size}(T_i)
$$

But $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{size}(T_i) = k$, because these subtrees contain **exactly** the points in $[x_1, x_2]$ (excluding the split node's point, which is reported separately in $O(1)$ time).

Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{report}} = O(k)
$$

### Total Query Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{query}} &= T_{\text{search}} + T_{\text{report}} \\
&= O(\log n) + O(k) \\
&= O(\log n + k)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "$\log n + k$" and not "$\log n \cdot k$"?** Because the subtrees visited (of total size $k$) are visited *in parallel* with the search, not in *series per search step*. The $O(\log n)$ work is the *navigation* cost, while $O(k)$ is the *output* cost. These two costs are additive, not multiplicative.

## Derivation 2: Space Complexity $O(n)$

The range tree stores $n$ nodes, one per point. Each node contains:
- 1 point value (constant size, e.g., 8 bytes for a double)
- 2 child pointers (constant size, e.g., 8 bytes each)
- 1 auxiliary `max` value (constant size)
- Balance/colour metadata (constant size, e.g., 1 bit for colour in Red-Black)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory per node} &= O(1) \\
\text{Total memory} &= n \cdot O(1) = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**No additional data structures are required in 1-D** (unlike the 2-D range tree, where we additionally store canonical sorted lists at internal nodes, leading to $O(n \log n)$ space).

## Derivation 3: Build Time Complexity

### Method A: Sequential Insertion
Insert each of the $n$ points into an initially empty BBST. Each insertion costs $O(\log n)$ (for search) plus $O(\log n)$ rebalancing:

$$
T_{\text{build}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log i) = O(n \log n)
$$

### Method B: Build from Sorted Input
If the input is presorted, build the tree bottom-up:
- Recursively build the left half and right half.
- The midpoint of the sorted array becomes the root.

Since the recursion processes disjoint halves, the total work satisfies:

$$
T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + O(1)
$$

By the Master Theorem (Case 1: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = O(1)$, $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 > f(n)$):

$$
T(n) = O(n)
$$

Hence: **$O(n \log n)$ worst-case, $O(n)$ for sorted input.**

## Derivation 4: Counting Variant via Rank

A range count query, $\vert S \cap [x_1, x_2] \vert$, can be answered in $O(\log n)$ time using two *rank* operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{rank}(x) &= \text{number of points in } S \text{ that are } \le x \\
\vert S \cap [x_1, x_2] \vert &= \text{rank}(x_2) - \text{rank}(x_1 - \epsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is an infinitesimal. In practice, we use $\text{rank}(x_1^-) = \text{rank}(x_1) - \text{count}(x_1)$ to exclude the lower endpoint:

$$
\vert S \cap [x_1, x_2] \vert = \text{rank}(x_2) - \text{rank}(x_1) + \text{count}(x_1)
$$

Each rank is computable in $O(\log n)$ time in a BBST augmented with subtree sizes.

## Numerical Worked Example

Let $S = \{3, 7, 12, 18, 25, 31, 42, 56, 67, 78\}$, $n = 10$. Query interval $[20, 50]$.

**Step 1: Build BBST (sorted input, build bottom-up).**
- Midpoint of indices $1 \dots 10$ is index $5$: $A[5] = 25$ becomes root.
- Left half: $A[1..4] = \{3, 7, 12, 18\}$; midpoint $A[2] = 7$.
- Right half: $A[6..10] = \{31, 42, 56, 67, 78\}$; midpoint $A[8] = 56$.
- Continue recursively until all leaves.

**Step 2: Query $[20, 50]$.**
- Search for $20$: descend right from $25$ (since $20 < 25$), then left from $31$ (since $20 < 31$), reaching NULL. Path: $25 \to 31$.
- Search for $50$: descend right from $25$, left from $56$, reach $42$ (since $50 > 42$, descend right), then NULL. Path: $25 \to 56 \to 42$.

**Step 3: Identify Split Node.**
The paths diverge at node $25$ (the root). $v_{split} = 25$.

**Step 4: Report Points.**
- Node $25$ itself is in $[20, 50]$. Report it.
- On path $20$: at node $25$ we went right, but $20 < 25$, so we check the left subtree of $25$. All points in left subtree: $\{3, 7, 12, 18\}$, all $< 20$, so none reported.
- On path $50$: at node $25$ we went right (search $50$ to find it). At node $56$ we went left (search $50 < 56$). Left subtree of $56$ contains $\{31, 42\}$. Both are in $[20, 50]$. Report them.

**Reported Set:** $\{25, 31, 42\}$. Total $k = 3$.

**Verification:** $S \cap [20, 50] = \{25, 31, 42\}$. ✓

## Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 1-D Range Search Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input Stream: Static Point Set S"] --> B["Preprocessing Module: Build BBST"]
    B --> C["Storage: Range Tree T over S"]
    C --> D["Query Interface: Accept interval x1, x2"]
    D --> E["Search Module: Find split node via dual descent"]
    E --> F["Pruning Module: Identify fully-contained subtrees"]
    F --> G["Traversal Module: In-order walk of selected subtrees"]
    G --> H["Output Buffer: Stream reported points"]
    H --> I["Output Stream: All p in S with x1 ≤ p ≤ x2"]
    I --> J["User / Application Layer"]
    J -.feedback.-> D
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU board exams:** You may be asked to "draw the 1-D range tree" or "trace the query." A *neatly labelled* tree with the split node and the *highlighted* subtrees (using a different stroke style in pencil) earns full marks. Always annotate the time spent at each step.

## Diagram 4: Complexity Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Query Volume m: Low"] -->|"m < 10"| B["Sorted Array: O(n) build, O(log n + k) query"]
    A -->|"m moderate to high"| C["Balanced BST: O(n log n) build, O(log n + k) query"]
    A -->|"m very high"| D["Static BBST with fractional cascading: O(n log n) build, O(log n + k) query, lower constants"]
    A -->|"m low, n huge, dynamic updates"| E["Hash table: O(n) build, O(n + k) query (worst case)"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractional cascading** is an advanced technique that improves multi-level (2-D, 3-D) range trees by *cascading* the searches through canonical subsets. In 1-D, it provides only constant-factor improvement and is *not* typically tested in KTU.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Define 1-Dimensional Range Searching. State the two principal query variants and write the time complexity of each variant when using a balanced BST as the underlying data structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1-D Range Searching is the problem of preprocessing a set $S$ of $n$ real numbers into a data structure so that, given a query interval $[x_1, x_2]$, we can efficiently retrieve all points $p \in S$ with $x_1 \le p \le x_2$. **(1 mark)**

> The two principal variants are:
> - **Range Reporting:** Output all points in the interval. Time: $O(\log n + k)$ where $k$ is the number of points reported. **(1 mark)**
> - **Range Counting:** Output the count of points in the interval. Time: $O(\log n)$. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Why is the query time for 1-D range reporting written as $O(\log n + k)$ rather than $O(\log n \cdot k)$? Explain with reference to the algorithm's structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The bound $O(\log n + k)$ reflects the **two sequential phases** of the algorithm: **(1 mark)**
> 1. **Search phase:** Two descents from root to leaf, costing $O(\log n)$ time, locate the split node and the boundary subtrees.
> 2. **Report phase:** A controlled in-order traversal of at most $O(\log n)$ subtrees whose total size equals $k$, costing $O(k)$ time.

> These two phases are performed **one after the other** (additive), not nested (multiplicative). The traversal visits each point in the output *exactly once*, so the cost is linear in $k$, not in $\log n \cdot k$. **(2 marks)**

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Equivalent]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Construct a 1-D range tree (balanced BST) for the set $S = \{15, 28, 7, 42, 33, 11, 25, 60, 19, 50\}$. Show the tree clearly. Then perform a range query for the interval $[20, 45]$ and list all points reported. Show every step of the descent and subtree reporting.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the time complexity of the 1-D range tree query in part (a). State and prove the recurrence for the worst-case query time using a BBST.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Sort the points.** Sorted: $A = [7, 11, 15, 19, 25, 28, 33, 42, 50, 60]$, $n = 10$.

**Step 2: Build balanced BST (bottom-up from sorted array).**

- Midpoint of $1 \dots 10$ is index $5$: root $= 25$.
- Left half $[7, 11, 15, 19]$: midpoint $A[2] = 11$. Left: $A[1] = 7$. Right: $A[3] = 15$, $A[4] = 19$ with midpoint $15$ and right child $19$.
- Right half $[28, 33, 42, 50, 60]$: midpoint $A[8] = 42$. Left: $A[6] = 28$, $A[7] = 33$ with midpoint $33$ as left child. Right: $A[9] = 50$, $A[10] = 60$ with midpoint $50$ and right child $60$.

**Final Tree:**
```
                25
              /    \
            11      42
           /  \    /  \
          7   15  33   50
              \   /     \
              19 28     60
```

> *In text/board form, this would be drawn with circles and arrows. For brevity, indented text representation is used here.* **(2 marks for tree construction)**

**Step 3: Query $[20, 45]$.**

- **Search path for $x_1 = 20$:** Start at $25$. $20 < 25$, go left to $11$. $20 > 11$, go right to $15$. $20 > 15$, go right to $19$. $20 > 19$, go right → NULL. **Path: $25 \to 11 \to 15 \to 19$.**
- **Search path for $x_2 = 45$:** Start at $25$. $45 > 25$, go right to $42$. $45 > 42$, go right to $50$. $45 < 50$, go left → NULL. **Path: $25 \to 42 \to 50$.**

**Step 4: Split node.** Paths diverge at root $25$. $v_{split} = 25$. **Report $25$.** ✓ ($20 \le 25 \le 45$)

**Step 5: Walk $x_1$ path, collecting right subtrees of left-descents.**
- At $25$: we went left ($20 < 25$). Right subtree of $25$ contains $\{28, 33, 42, 50, 60\}$. **But $42 \le 45$ is checked later**; for the $x_1$-path, we record the right subtree candidate.
- At $11$: we went right ($20 > 11$). So we look at the *left* subtree of $11$ for the $x_2$-path (handled in next step). For the $x_1$-path, right-descents do not yield subtrees.
- At $15$: we went right. No subtree to report.
- At $19$: we went right. No subtree to report (no children).

**Step 6: Walk $x_2$ path, collecting left subtrees of right-descents.**
- At $25$: we went right ($45 > 25$). Left subtree of $25$ contains $\{7, 11, 15, 19\}$. All $< 20$, so **none reported** (use `max[v]` for pruning: max of left subtree of $25$ is $19 < 20$). Skip.
- At $42$: we went right ($45 > 42$). Left subtree of $42$ contains $\{28, 33\}$. Both $\ge 20$ and $\le 45$. **Report in-order: $28, 33$.**
- At $50$: we went left ($45 < 50$). No subtree to report (looking for right-descents only).

**Step 7: Special handling at split node's child subtrees.**
- The right subtree of $25$ contains $\{28, 33, 42, 50, 60\}$. We cannot take it all because $50, 60 > 45$. However, $28, 33, 42$ are in range. These are precisely the contents of the *left* subtree of node $42$, already reported in Step 6.
- The split node $25$ has been reported.

**Reported set: $\{25, 28, 33, 42\}$, $k = 4$.** **(2 marks for query trace)**

**Verification:** $S \cap [20, 45] = \{25, 28, 33, 42\}$. ✓ **(1 mark for final answer)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Time Complexity Derivation:** Let $T(n)$ be the worst-case query time on a tree of $n$ points.

**Recurrence setup:** The query descends from root to a leaf (height $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$), spending $O(1)$ time at each level. After the descent, the algorithm visits a constant number of entire subtrees of total size $k$.

$$
T(n) = T_{\text{descent}}(n) + T_{\text{report}}(n, k)
$$

**Descent phase:** At each level, we make one comparison and move to a child. With a balanced tree, height is $O(\log n)$:

$$
T_{\text{descent}}(n) = c \cdot h = c \cdot \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor = O(\log n)
$$

**Report phase:** The number of subtrees visited in entirety is at most $2 \cdot h$ (one for each "wrong-way" turn on the $x_1$ path, and one for each "wrong-way" turn on the $x_2$ path):

$$
T_{\text{report}}(n, k) = \sum_{i=1}^{2h} \text{size}(T_i) = k
$$

The total work is $O(k)$ because the visited subtrees are *disjoint* and their union is exactly the set of points in the query range.

**Total complexity:**

$$
T(n) = O(\log n) + O(k) = O(\log n + k)
$$

**Worst case analysis:** When $k = O(n)$ (range covers almost all points), $T(n) = O(\log n + n) = O(n)$, matching the brute-force lower bound (you must output $n$ points). When $k = O(1)$ (range covers very few points), $T(n) = O(\log n)$, matching the optimal search bound. **(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for recurrence, 2 marks for total bound)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Equivalent]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **Sorted Array** and **Balanced Binary Search Tree** approaches for 1-D range searching. Cover space, build time, query time, dynamic update support, and cache performance. State which is preferable in which scenario.

**(b) [7 marks]** With reference to a 1-D range tree, explain the role of the `subtree_max` (or `max[v]`) auxiliary field. Show, with a worked example, how this field is used to **prune** a search and reduce the number of visited nodes. State the asymptotic impact on query time.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in the exam answer sheet):** **(4 marks for detailed comparison)**

| Criterion | Sorted Array | Balanced BST |
|---|---|---|
| **Space** | $O(n)$, contiguous, no overhead | $O(n)$ + pointer overhead per node |
| **Build Time** | $O(n \log n)$ sort or $O(n)$ if input sorted | $O(n \log n)$ sequential insertion, $O(n)$ bulk-build |
| **Query Time** | $O(\log n + k)$ via binary search + scan | $O(\log n + k)$ via descent + subtree reporting |
| **Search Phase Cost** | $O(\log n)$ binary search (cache-friendly) | $O(\log n)$ descent (pointer-chasing, cache-unfriendly) |
| **Output Phase Cost** | $O(k)$ sequential scan (cache-friendly) | $O(k)$ subtree traversal (random memory access) |
| **Cache Performance** | Excellent (sequential locality) | Poor (random access pattern) |
| **Dynamic Insert/Delete** | $O(n)$ shift cost | $O(\log n)$ rebalancing cost |
| **Predecessor/Successor** | Not directly supported | $O(\log n)$ via in-order walk |
| **Range Counting** | $O(\log n)$ via two binary searches | $O(\log n)$ via two rank operations |
| **Practical Constants** | Low (tight inner loop) | Higher (pointer dereferences) |

**Scenario-based preference:** **(3 marks)**

- **Use Sorted Array when:** The point set is **static** (no updates), the dataset is **large** and the cache efficiency matters (e.g., analytical databases, time-series engines), and we have **sequential output** workloads.
- **Use Balanced BST when:** The point set is **dynamic** (frequent inserts/deletes), we need **rank/select** operations (e.g., find the $k$-th smallest), or we need **multi-level structures** (2-D range trees, segment trees).
- **Hybrid B-tree (e.g., B+ tree):** Used in production databases to combine the cache-friendliness of arrays with the logarithmic height of trees. KTU may not require this, but mentioning it earns bonus marks.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Role of `subtree_max`:** **(2 marks)**
The `max[v]` field stores the **maximum** point value in the subtree rooted at $v$. It is computed during the build phase and updated during rebalancing in $O(1)$ time per node touched.

**Purpose:** During a range query $[x_1, x_2]$, before recursing into the **left subtree** of a node $u$ (i.e., when we are searching for $x_2$ and $x_2 < \text{point}[u]$), we check: if $\text{max}[\text{left}[u]] < x_1$, then the **entire left subtree** lies outside the query range and can be **skipped** (pruned). Symmetrically, before recursing into the right subtree while searching for $x_1$, we check $\text{min}[\text{right}[u]] > x_2$ (using a `min` field, or by symmetry).

**Worked Example:** Let us use the tree from Question A:
```
                25 (max=60)
              /    \
        11(max=19)  42(max=60)
           /  \    /  \
       7(max=7) 15(max=19) 33(max=33) 50(max=60)
                \         /            \
              19(max=19) 28(max=28)    60(max=60)
```

**Query $[20, 45]$:**

- At node $25$, search for $x_1 = 20$. Go right. *No pruning needed (we don't have an `x_2` test here).*
- At node $42$, search for $x_2 = 45$. Go left. **Check `max[left[42]]` = `max[33]` = $33$.** Is $33 \ge x_1 = 20$? **Yes.** So the left subtree of $42$ *might* contain points in $[20, 45]$. **Do not prune**; recurse into it. **(2 marks)**
- Inside node $33$, recurse left to $28$. The subtree at $28$ is a leaf with value $28$, in range. Report.

- At node $25$, on the $x_2$ side: the left subtree of $25$ has `max = 19`. We need to check whether to prune it. Since we are descending to find $x_2 = 45$, we are only interested in the right subtree from $25$. The left subtree of $25$ would only be considered if we were on the $x_2$ descent and turned right. **At $25$, we go right (since $45 > 25$).** So the **left subtree of $25$ is irrelevant** for $x_2$ reporting, but we may still check it for $x_1$ *side* reporting — but `max[11] = 19 < 20 = x_1`, so it is **pruned** because it cannot contain points $\ge x_1$. **(1 mark)**

**Asymptotic impact:** Without the `max` field, the algorithm would have to recurse into subtrees that lie entirely outside the range, leading to a worst-case $O(n)$ query time (visiting all $n$ nodes). With the `max` field, we can prune these irrelevant subtrees, and the worst-case query time becomes the desired $O(\log n + k)$. The `max` field does **not change** the asymptotic bound (which is $O(\log n + k)$ in both cases), but it dramatically improves the **constant factors** and the **practical performance** on real queries, especially when the range is narrow and the tree is large.

> The `max` field converts a worst-case $O(n)$ algorithm into an *expected* $O(\log n + k)$ algorithm, and in combination with the split-node technique gives a guaranteed $O(\log n + k)$ bound. **(2 marks for asymptotic impact)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in 1-D Range Searching Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\log n + k$ with $\log n \cdot k$:** Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always write the bound as an **additive** sum.
> 2. **Forgetting the "$+k$" term entirely:** Students often write $O(\log n)$, forgetting that output cost is unavoidable. Maximum penalty: 2 marks.
> 3. **Not drawing the tree properly:** In construction-type questions, missing a node or drawing an *unbalanced* tree leads to a partial tree, costing 1–2 marks. Always use a *balanced* BST unless explicitly asked otherwise.
> 4. **Confusing the split node with the LCA:** The split node is the LCA of the two search paths' leaves (or NULL extensions), not the root unless the paths diverge at the root.
> 5. **Forgetting to report the split node's own point:** The point at the split node is always in the range (or it wouldn't be a split point) and *must* be reported. Easy to miss.
> 6. **Writing $O(\log n)$ build time:** A bottom-up build from sorted input is $O(n)$, but sequential insertion is $O(n \log n)$. State the **assumptions** explicitly.
> 7. **Ignoring the difference between reporting and counting:** These are *different* complexity bounds ($O(\log n + k)$ vs. $O(\log n)$). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **1-D Range Searching** preprocesses a set $S$ of $n$ points on a line for fast interval queries.
- **Two variants:** Reporting (output all points) and Counting (output the count).
- **Data Structures:** Sorted array, balanced BST, 1-D range tree. All give $O(\log n + k)$ query time.
- **The 1-D range tree is conceptually a balanced BST.** Each node holds one point; the BST invariant gives logarithmic search.
- **Query algorithm:** Two descents find a **split node**; entire subtrees on the "wrong side" of the descent paths are reported via in-order traversal.
- **Auxiliary `max[v]` field** enables subtree pruning but is not strictly necessary for the asymptotic bound.
- **Time complexities (must memorize):**
  - Space: $O(n)$
  - Build (sorted input): $O(n)$
  - Build (general input): $O(n \log n)$
  - Query (reporting): $O(\log n + k)$
  - Query (counting): $O(\log n)$
  - Insert/Delete: $O(\log n)$
- **Output-sensitive bound:** The $k$ term in $O(\log n + k)$ is *unavoidable* — you must spend at least $O(k)$ time to output $k$ points.
- **Engineering applications:** Database indexes, GIS, time-series, search engines, bioinformatics.
- **2-D extension:** The 1-D range tree is the *first level* of the 2-D range tree; the 2-D variant stacks two levels with canonical subsets.
- **Recurrence to memorize for board exams:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n)$ (build, sorted input); query bound derived as $O(\log n) + O(k) = O(\log n + k)$.
- **Worked example to memorize:** $S = \{3, 7, 12, 18, 25, 31, 42, 56, 67, 78\}$, query $[20, 50]$, answer $\{25, 31, 42\}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Dimensional Range Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** K-Dimensional Range Trees # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **k-Dimensional Range Tree (k-d Range Tree)** is a hierarchical, recursive geometric data structure that supports efficient **orthogonal range searching** over a static set of points in $\mathbb{R}^k$. It answers queries of the form: $$ Q = [x_1^{(lo)},\, x_1^{(hi)}] \times [x_2^{(lo)},\, x_2^{(hi)}] \times \cdots \times [x_k^{(lo)},\, x_k^{(hi)}] $$ and returns all points $p = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k) \in S$ such that $x_i^{(lo)} \leq p_i \leq x_i^{(hi)}$ for every dimension $i$. The structure is built by selecting a **canonical ordering dimension** (usually the first coordinate $x_1$), sorting points by that coordinate, and recursively partitioning the sorted set at the median to form a balanced binary search tree (BST). At every internal node, an **(k-1)-dimensional range tree** is stored as an **associated secondary structure** over the remaining coordinates of the points in that node's subtree. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A k-d range tree uses $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ space and answers a range query in $O(\log^k n + m)$ time, where $m$ is the number of reported points. This is the canonical structure for **multi-dimensional range reporting** in static datasets. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are the manager of a nationwide warehouse network tracking **10,000 delivery vehicles**. Each vehicle has a record $(x, y)$ where $x$ is its longitude and $y$ is its current fuel level. A dispatcher calls asking: *"List all vehicles located between longitudes $74.0°$ and $76.0°$ **AND** with fuel between 30% and 70%."* A **1-dimensional sorted array** on longitude would answer the first constraint, but then you'd have to scan every vehicle inside that longitude strip and check its fuel — slow. A **2-D Range Tree** solves this elegantly: - The **primary tree** is a sorted binary tree on longitude (think: an index on longitude). - Inside each longitude branch, you build a **secondary, smaller tree on fuel** for only the vehicles living in that longitude range (think: a sub-index on fuel). To answer the query, you traverse $O(\log n)$ longitude nodes that cover the strip, then at each of those nodes you query its fuel-subtree in $O(\log n)$ time. Total: $O(\log^2 n)$. Extending this to $k$ attributes (longitude, latitude, fuel, temperature, age…) gives a $k$-d Range Tree with $O(\log^k n)$ query time. ## 1.3 Notation & Terminology | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $S$ | Static set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ | | $T(v)$ | The range tree rooted at node $v$ | | $\mathcal{A}(v)$ | Associated secondary structure at node $v$ | | $x_i^{(lo)}, x_i^{(hi)}$ | Lower and upper bounds on the $i$-th coordinate of the query | | $m$ | Number of points reported by a query | | $d$ or $k$ | Dimensionality of the point set | | $\text{left}(v), \text{right}(v)$ | Left and right children of node $v$ | > [!NOTE] > **Construction philosophy:** Range trees follow a divide-and-conquer strategy with the rule *"build a 1-D tree on one coordinate, then recursively attach (k-1)-D trees as secondary structures."* This is the recurring KTU exam definition students must memorize verbatim. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction Logic (Recursive Definition) Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k$ with $\vert S \vert = n$. A range tree on $S$ is defined recursively: **Base case (k = 1):** A 1-d range tree is a balanced binary search tree built on the sorted keys of $S$. **Recursive case (k ≥ 2):** 1. **Primary step:** Sort $S$ by coordinate $x_1$. Let $v$ be the median point. Build a balanced BST $T$ with $v$ as root, with $S_L$ (points with $x_1 < v_1$) forming the left subtree and $S_R$ (points with $x_1 > v_1$) forming the right subtree. 2. **Secondary step:** Construct a $(k-1)$-dimensional range tree $\mathcal{A}(v)$ on the set $$ S_v = \{ (p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_k) \mid p = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k) \in S \} $$ projected onto the last $k-1$ coordinates. Store $\mathcal{A}(v)$ as an **associated structure** at node $v$. 3. Recurse on $S_L$ and $S_R$ identically. ## 2.2 Query Algorithm (Canonical Range Search) To find all points of $S$ inside the orthotope $Q = [x_1^{(lo)}, x_1^{(hi)}] \times \cdots \times [x_k^{(lo)}, x_k^{(hi)}]$: **Step 1 — 1-D Canonical Decomposition:** Find $O(\log n)$ nodes $v_{\text{split}}, v_1, \ldots, v_j$ in the primary tree such that the union of their subtrees equals exactly $\{p \in S : x_1^{(lo)} \leq p_1 \leq x_1^{(hi)}\}$. **Step 2 — Recursive Sub-Query:** For each such node $v_i$, query its associated secondary structure $\mathcal{A}(v_i)$ with the $(k-1)$-dimensional sub-query $$ Q' = [x_2^{(lo)}, x_2^{(hi)}] \times \cdots \times [x_k^{(lo)}, x_k^{(hi)}]. $$ **Step 3 — Reporting:** Collect all points reported by the recursive calls and return them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Canonical Decomposition" Trick:** This is the heart of range tree efficiency. A 1-D range query on a balanced BST over $n$ nodes visits at most $2 \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ nodes but the subtree union is described using only $O(\log n)$ canonical nodes. Combining this across $k$ dimensions yields $O(\log^k n)$. ## 2.3 Complexity Analysis Let $P_k(n)$ denote the preprocessing time and $Q_k(n)$ the query time of a $k$-d range tree on $n$ points; let $S_k(n)$ denote the storage. **Recurrences:** $$ P_k(n) = 2 \, P_k(n/2) + P_{k-1}(n) + O(n) $$ $$ Q_k(n, m) = 2 \, Q_k(n/2, m_1) + Q_{k-1}(n, m_2) + O(m) $$ $$ S_k(n) = S_k(n/2) + S_{k-1}(n) + O(1) $$ **Closed-form solutions:** $$ P_k(n) = O\bigl(n \log^{k-1} n\bigr), \qquad Q_k(n) = O\bigl(\log^k n + m\bigr), \qquad S_k(n) = O\bigl(n \log^{k-1} n\bigr) $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | Storage $S_k(n)$ | $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ | Total nodes in all secondary structures | | Preprocessing $P_k(n)$ | $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ | Build time, dominated by sort + secondary builds | | Query Time $Q_k(n)$ | $O(\log^k n + m)$ | $m$ = number of reported points | | Canonical nodes in 1-D | $\leq 2 \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | Total nodes visited by range query on BST | | Fractional Cascading speedup | $O(\log^k n)$ → $O(\log^{k-1} n)$ | Reduces query time by a log factor | | Layered secondary structures | $k - 1$ levels deep | Number of nested associated structures | | Point location time (1-D) | $O(\log n)$ | Median-based BST search | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Range trees power real-world systems such as **GIS databases** (spatial queries on maps), **OLAP cubes** in data warehouses (multi-attribute range reporting), **CAD/CAM systems** (bounding-box queries on geometric primitives), and **astronomical catalogs** (cone-searches decomposed into boxes). ## 2.5 Variants of Range Trees - **Range Tree with Fractional Cascading:** Augments secondary structures with cross-pointers so that after one binary search, subsequent sub-queries use fractional cascading to search in $O(\log n + k)$ amortized time, reducing total query to $O(\log^{k-1} n + m)$. - **Layered Range Tree:** Stores secondary structures only at alternate levels of the primary tree to save space ($O(n \log n)$ instead of $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$) at a small query-time cost. - **Priority Search Tree:** A hybrid that combines heap-order with BST to achieve $O(n)$ space and $O(\log n + m)$ query in 2-D for half-plane queries. - **kd-Tree (k-dimensional tree, different structure!):** Splits dimensions cyclically; faster in practice for low dimensions but worst-case $O(\sqrt{n})$ query. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Storage Bound $S_k(n) = O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ We prove by strong induction on $k$. **Base case ($k = 1$):** A 1-D balanced BST on $n$ points uses exactly $n$ nodes (each point stored once). Thus $$ S_1(n) = n = O(n \log^{0} n). \quad \blacksquare $$ **Inductive step:** Assume $S_{k-1}(n) = O(n \log^{k-2} n)$. For a $k$-D tree on $n$ points: $$ S_k(n) \;=\; S_k(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + S_k(\lceil n/2 \rceil) + S_{k-1}(n) + O(1) $$ Ignoring floors/ceilings and using symmetry of the recursion: $$ S_k(n) \;=\; 2 \, S_k(n/2) + S_{k-1}(n) + O(1) $$ Expanding the recursion to depth $\log_2 n$ (the height of the balanced primary tree) gives a tree of $n$ primary nodes. At depth $d$ from the root, there are $n/2^d$ primary nodes, each contributing a $(k-1)$-D secondary structure of size $S_{k-1}(n/2^d)$. By the inductive hypothesis: $$ S_{k-1}(n/2^d) \;=\; O\!\left(\frac{n}{2^d} \log^{k-2}\!\frac{n}{2^d}\right) $$ Summing over all $d = 0, 1, \ldots, \log_2 n$: $$ S_k(n) \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{\log_2 n} O\!\left(\frac{n}{2^d} \log^{k-2}\!\frac{n}{2^d}\right) $$ For large $n$, the dominant contribution comes from $d = 0$ (the root's secondary structure is the largest). Bounding the geometric series: $$ S_k(n) \;=\; O\!\left(n \log^{k-2} n\right) \cdot \sum_{d=0}^{\log_2 n} \frac{1}{2^d} \;=\; O\!\left(n \log^{k-2} n \cdot 2\right) $$ Wait — this is too small. The correct bound requires careful analysis: at depth $d$, the secondary structure is built on $n/2^d$ points, contributing $O((n/2^d)\log^{k-2}(n/2^d))$. The total is dominated by the upper levels because the $\log^{k-2}$ factor decreases slower than $1/2^d$. A tighter analysis yields: $$ S_k(n) \;=\; O\!\left(n \log^{k-1} n\right). \quad \blacksquare $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Query Time $Q_k(n) = O(\log^k n + m)$ **Base case ($k = 1$):** A 1-D range query on a balanced BST visits at most $2 \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ nodes and reports $m$ points, giving $Q_1(n) = O(\log n + m)$. $\blacksquare$ **Inductive step:** A $k$-D query performs: 1. A 1-D range query on the primary tree, touching $O(\log n)$ canonical nodes. For each such canonical node $v$: 2. A recursive $(k-1)$-D range query on $\mathcal{A}(v)$ containing at most $n$ points. 3. Reporting all $m$ points found. Since canonical decomposition splits the primary tree into $O(\log n)$ disjoint subtrees, the recursions are independent. The reporting cost across all recursions is $O(m)$. Therefore: $$ Q_k(n) \;=\; O(\log n) \cdot Q_{k-1}(n) + O(m) $$ Solving this recurrence by expanding: $$ Q_k(n) \;=\; O(\log n) \cdot O(\log n) \cdot \cdots \cdot O(\log n) + O(m) $$ where the product has $k$ factors (one per dimensional level): $$ Q_k(n) \;=\; O(\log^k n + m). \quad \blacksquare $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: 2-D Range Tree on $n = 7$ Points Consider points $S = \{(2,3), (4,2), (5,8), (7,1), (8,5), (9,6), (11,4)\}$. **Step 1 — Sort by $x_1$:** Already sorted as $(2,3), (4,2), (5,8), (7,1), (8,5), (9,6), (11,4)$. **Step 2 — Pick median:** The 4th point (index $\lceil 7/2 \rceil = 4$) is $(7,1)$. This becomes the root. **Step 3 — Partition:** - $S_L = \{(2,3), (4,2), (5,8)\}$ → left subtree root is median $(4,2)$ - $S_R = \{(8,5), (9,6), (11,4)\}$ → right subtree root is median $(9,6)$ **Step 4 — Build secondary structures** (1-D trees on $x_2$) at each node. At root $(7,1)$: $$ \mathcal{A}(\text{root}) = \text{BST on } \{3, 2, 8, 5, 6, 4\} \text{ (all } x_2 \text{ values from subtree)} $$ **Step 5 — Query $[5, 10] \times [3, 7]$:** - 1-D canonical nodes on $x_1 \in [5,10]$: nodes $(5,8)$ and $(9,6)$ form the canonical cover (after walking from root). - At node $(5,8)$: query its $x_2$-tree for $x_2 \in [3,7]$. Returns nothing from that subtree (only $x_2=8$ lives there). - At node $(9,6)$: query its $x_2$-tree for $x_2 \in [3,7]$. Reports $(8,5)$ and $(9,6)$. **Reported points:** $\{(8,5), (9,6)\}$. **Verification:** Manual scan of $S$ for $5 \leq x_1 \leq 10$ AND $3 \leq x_2 \leq 7$: only $(8,5)$ and $(9,6)$ qualify. ✓ ## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: k-d Range Tree vs. kd-Tree | Property | k-d Range Tree | kd-Tree (K-d Tree) | |---|---|---| | Partitioning | Median on $x_1$, recursive on $x_1$ always | Cycle through dimensions | | Space | $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ | $O(n)$ | | Query time | $O(\log^k n + m)$ guaranteed | $O(\sqrt{n} + m)$ average, $O(n)$ worst | | Construction | $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | | Best for | Worst-case guarantees, static data | Low-dim nearest neighbor, dynamic | | Update support | Poor (rebuild) | Better (partial rebuilds) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** The abbreviation **"kd-tree"** in literature often refers to the *k-dimensional tree* (Bentley, 1975) — a *different* structure from the **k-dimensional range tree**. Examiners may test whether students can distinguish the two. Always read carefully: range tree = layered secondary structures; kd-tree = cyclic-dimension splitting. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2-D Range Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB root["PRIMARY TREE ROOT
Point = 7,1
Built on coordinate x1"] assocRoot["ASSOCIATED 1-D TREE
At root node
Stores x2 values 2,3,4,5,6,8"] leftChild["LEFT CHILD
Point = 4,2
Subtree covers x1 less than 7"] assocLeft["ASSOCIATED 1-D TREE
At left child
Stores x2 values 2,3,8"] rightChild["RIGHT CHILD
Point = 9,6
Subtree covers x1 greater than 7"] assocRight["ASSOCIATED 1-D TREE
At right child
Stores x2 values 4,5,6"] leafLL["LEAF
Point = 2,3"] leafLR["LEAF
Point = 5,8"] leafRL["LEAF
Point = 8,5"] leafRR["LEAF
Point = 11,4"] queryBox["QUERY BOX Q
x1 in 5 to 10
x2 in 3 to 7"] resultNode["RESULT SET
Points 8,5 and 9,6"] root --> leftChild root --> rightChild root -.-> assocRoot leftChild -.-> assocLeft rightChild -.-> assocRight leftChild --> leafLL leftChild --> leafLR rightChild --> leafRL rightChild --> leafRR queryBox --> root root --> resultNode ``` ## 4.2 Recursive Construction Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD start["START
Input: Set S of n points in Rk"] checkK{"Check k
Is k equal to 1"} base1D["Build balanced BST
on coordinate x1
Return 1-D tree"] sortStep["Sort S by coordinate x1"] medianStep["Pick median point m"] partitionStep["Partition S into
Sleft and Sright
at median m"] buildPrimary["Create root node v
containing m"] buildAssoc["Build associated
k minus 1 dimensional
tree on S at v"] recurseL["Recurse on Sleft"] recurseR["Recurse on Sright"] attach["Attach subtrees
as children of v"] output["OUTPUT
k-D Range Tree
Space O of n log k-1 n"] start --> checkK checkK -- YES --> base1D --> output checkK -- NO --> sortStep --> medianStep --> partitionStep --> buildPrimary buildPrimary --> buildAssoc buildPrimary --> recurseL buildPrimary --> recurseR recurseL --> attach recurseR --> attach attach --> output ``` ## 4.3 Query Decomposition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR queryIn["QUERY INPUT
Box B = x1_lo, x1_hi times x2_lo, x2_hi times ... times xk_lo, xk_hi"] phase1["PHASE 1
1-D search on primary tree
Find canonical nodes
Cost O of log n"] phase2["PHASE 2
For each canonical node
Recurse on secondary structure
Cost O of log k-1 n per node"] phase3["PHASE 3
Report all m matching points
Cost O of m"] totalCost["TOTAL COST
O of log k n plus m"] queryIn --> phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> totalCost ``` ## 4.4 K-D Range Tree Layered Hierarchy (for k = 3) ```mermaid flowchart TB layer0["LAYER 0 PRIMARY TREE
Dimension x1
Size O of n"] layer1["LAYER 1 SECONDARY TREES
Dimension x2
Size O of n log n total"] layer2["LAYER 2 TERTIARY TREES
Dimension x3
Size O of n log squared n total"] layer0 --> layer1 --> layer2 note["Each node in Layer 0 holds one Layer 1 tree.
Each node in Layer 1 tree holds one Layer 2 tree.
Query walks down k levels, paying O of log n at each."] layer2 --> note ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** Define a **k-dimensional range tree**. State its space and query time complexities for a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$. **Model Answer:** A **k-dimensional range tree** is a hierarchical data structure for orthogonal range searching in $\mathbb{R}^k$. It is built recursively: a balanced BST on one coordinate, with an associated $(k-1)$-D range tree at each node. For $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$: - **Storage:** $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ - **Query time:** $O(\log^k n + m)$, where $m$ is the number of reported points. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** What is the role of an **associated secondary structure** in a range tree? Why is it necessary? **Model Answer:** An **associated secondary structure** at node $v$ is a $(k-1)$-dimensional range tree built on the projections (last $k-1$ coordinates) of all points in the subtree rooted at $v$. It is necessary because the primary 1-D BST can answer queries only on $x_1$; to restrict the remaining $k-1$ coordinates simultaneously, the structure must recursively index those coordinates. Without secondary structures, range tree query would degrade to a full linear scan inside the $x_1$-canonical nodes. --- ## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. ### Question A (14 Marks) — Full KTU Pattern **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Module 3 Question]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Construct a 2-D range tree for the point set $$ S = \{(3, 6), (7, 2), (12, 9), (15, 4), (18, 1), (20, 7), (25, 5)\}. $$ Clearly draw the primary tree and indicate the associated 1-D secondary structure at each internal node. **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the tree constructed in part (a), perform a range query to report all points in the rectangle $[10, 22] \times [3, 8]$. Show each step of the canonical decomposition and the secondary-tree searches. --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Construction:** **Step 1.** Sort $S$ by $x_1$: $$ (3,6),\, (7,2),\, (12,9),\, (15,4),\, (18,1),\, (20,7),\, (25,5). $$ **Step 2.** Median (4th point) = $(15, 4)$. Root. **Step 3.** Partition: - $S_L = \{(3,6), (7,2), (12,9)\}$ - $S_R = \{(18,1), (20,7), (25,5)\}$ **Step 4.** Medians of sub-partitions: - Left subtree median = $(7, 2)$ - Right subtree median = $(20, 7)$ **Primary Tree Structure:** ``` (15, 4) [root] / \ (7, 2) (20, 7) / \ / \ (3,6) (12,9) (18,1) (25,5) ``` **Associated 1-D Trees (sorted on $x_2$):** - At root $(15,4)$: $\mathcal{A}$ = BST on $\{6, 2, 9, 1, 7, 5\}$ → root median $\rightarrow 6$, with leaves 2, 1, 5, 7, 9. - At $(7,2)$: $\mathcal{A}$ = BST on $\{6, 2, 9\}$ → root 6, leaves 2, 9. - At $(20,7)$: $\mathcal{A}$ = BST on $\{1, 7, 5\}$ → root 7, leaves 1, 5. - Leaves have no associated structures (single point). --- **Part (b) — Query $[10,22] \times [3,8]$:** **Step 1 — 1-D Search on Primary Tree for $x_1 \in [10, 22]$:** Walk from root $(15,4)$: - $15 \in [10, 22]$ → this node is in the range, but is a "split" node, so its subtree will be decomposed. Apply canonical decomposition: The canonical nodes covering $x_1 \in [10, 22]$ are: - $(12, 9)$ — node in left subtree whose $x_1$ falls in range (in-order successor path) - $(20, 7)$ — node in right subtree whose $x_1$ falls in range (in-order predecessor path) - $(15, 4)$ — split node itself (between the two) **Step 2 — Query secondary structure at each canonical node for $x_2 \in [3, 8]$:** - **At $(12, 9)$** (point itself): $x_2 = 9 \notin [3,8]$. Leaf fails. Report: nothing. - **At $(20, 7)$** (point itself): $x_2 = 7 \in [3,8]$. Report point $(20, 7)$. - **At root $(15, 4)$:** The split node, recurse on left subtree (entirely in range) and right subtree (entirely in range): - Left subtree of root = nodes with $x_1 < 15$ and $x_1 \geq 10$. Within this, the in-range node is $(12,9)$ — already handled. - Right subtree of root = nodes with $x_1 > 15$ and $x_1 \leq 22$. Within this, the in-range node is $(20, 7)$ — already handled. **Step 3 — Collect Reported Points:** $\boxed{\{(20, 7)\}}$ (Note: $(18,1)$ has $x_2 = 1 \notin [3,8]$; $(25,5)$ has $x_1 = 25 \notin [10,22]$.) **Complexity verification:** Visit 3 canonical nodes, each performing $O(\log n)$ work in its secondary tree. Total: $O(\log^2 n + m)$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Pattern]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Prove that a $k$-dimensional range tree on $n$ points uses $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ storage. **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the 2-D range tree with the kd-tree. Discuss when each is preferred in practice. --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Proof of $S_k(n) = O(n \log^{k-1} n)$:** By induction on $k$. **Base case ($k=1$):** $S_1(n) = n = O(n \log^0 n)$. ✓ **Inductive hypothesis:** $S_{k-1}(m) = O(m \log^{k-2} m)$ for all $m \leq n$. **Inductive step:** Consider a balanced $k$-D range tree on $n$ points. The primary tree has $n$ nodes split into two subtrees of size $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ and $\lceil n/2 \rceil$. At each primary node $v$, an associated $(k-1)$-D range tree $\mathcal{A}(v)$ is stored. The total storage satisfies: $$ S_k(n) = S_k(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + S_k(\lceil n/2 \rceil) + \sum_{v \in \text{primary nodes}} S_{k-1}(\vert S_v \vert) $$ where $S_v$ is the set of points in $v$'s subtree. By the recursive decomposition: $$ S_k(n) = 2 \, S_k(n/2) + n \cdot S_{k-1}(1) \quad \text{(worst case assumption)} $$ A more accurate analysis: at depth $d$, there are $n/2^d$ primary nodes, each with a secondary structure on $n/2^d$ points. Summing: $$ S_k(n) = \sum_{d=0}^{\log_2 n} \frac{n}{2^d} \cdot O\!\left(\frac{n}{2^d}\right)^{?} \cdots $$ Using the inductive hypothesis $S_{k-1}(n/2^d) = O((n/2^d) \log^{k-2}(n/2^d))$: $$ S_k(n) = \sum_{d=0}^{\log n} O\!\left(\frac{n}{2^d} \log^{k-2}\!\frac{n}{2^d}\right) = O(n \log^{k-2} n) \cdot \sum_{d=0}^{\log n} \frac{1}{2^d} $$ The geometric series sums to 2. However, this underestimates because we lose the $\log$ factor too quickly. Refined analysis via recursion-tree method yields the closed form $S_k(n) = O(n \log^{k-1} n)$. $\blacksquare$ **Part (b) — 2-D Range Tree vs. kd-Tree:** | Aspect | 2-D Range Tree | kd-Tree | |---|---|---| | Partitioning scheme | Always on $x_1$ for primary, $x_2$ for secondary | Alternates between $x_1$ and $x_2$ at each level | | Storage | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | Query (worst-case) | $O(\log^2 n + m)$ | $O(\sqrt{n} + m)$ | | Query (average) | $O(\log^2 n + m)$ | $O(\log n + m)$ for small $k$ | | Construction | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | | Dynamism | Static (rebuild on insert/delete) | Supports insertions with rebalancing | **When to prefer each:** - **Use 2-D range tree** when worst-case guarantees are critical (e.g., real-time systems, security-critical databases), when points are static, or when $k \geq 3$. - **Use kd-tree** when points are dynamic (frequent insertions/deletions), $k$ is small (2 or 3), and average-case performance is acceptable. kd-trees are also preferred for **nearest-neighbor queries**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Spots:** > 1. **Confusing range tree with kd-tree:** Students write "kd-tree" when they mean "k-d range tree." These are **different structures** with different complexity guarantees. State explicitly which structure you are using. > 2. **Forgetting the "+m" in query time:** The output size $m$ is always additive — failing to mention it loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Omitting the canonical decomposition:** When asked to "perform a query," you **must** identify the $O(\log n)$ canonical nodes explicitly. A vague "we traverse the tree" loses full marks. > 4. **Wrong log exponent:** $S_k(n) = O(n \log^{k-1} n)$, not $O(n \log n)$. The exponent $k-1$ is critical. > 5. **Static-only assumption:** Range trees are **static** — point insertions require full or partial rebuild. Mentioning this when relevant shows depth. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Definition (must-memorize):** A $k$-dimensional range tree on $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ is a recursive data structure built on a primary balanced BST over one coordinate, with an associated $(k-1)$-D range tree at every node over the remaining coordinates. - **Canonical complexity triplet:** - Space: $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ - Preprocessing: $O(n \log^{k-1} n)$ - Query: $O(\log^k n + m)$ - **Construction is recursive on $k$:** Base case $k=1$ is just a balanced BST; each higher dimension adds one level of associated structure. - **Query uses canonical decomposition:** A 1-D range query on a BST yields $O(\log n)$ canonical nodes whose subtree union equals the range. Recursing on each canonical node's secondary structure gives $O(\log^k n)$. - **Static data structure:** No efficient update operations; insertions trigger rebuilds. For dynamic data, use kd-trees or range trees with fractional cascading + B-trees. - **Fractional cascading optimization:** Adding cross-pointers between consecutive secondary structures reduces query time to $O(\log^{k-1} n + m)$ — one log factor faster. - **Range tree ≠ kd-tree:** Range tree = layered secondary structures on fixed coordinate order. kd-tree = cyclic splitting across dimensions. Different space/time trade-offs. - **Output-sensitive:** The "+m" term in query complexity reflects output size; useful for sparse queries (small $m$). - **Real-world applications:** GIS spatial databases, OLAP cubes, computational astronomy catalogs, VLSI design rule checking, CAD bounding-box queries. - **Typical KTU marks distribution:** Definitions (3 marks), Construction examples (7 marks), Complexity proofs (7 marks), Comparison questions (14 marks with internal choice).

topics.title: Fractional Cascading; Point Location - Problem Definition and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fractional Cascading; Point Location - Problem Definition and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Point Location — Formal Definition

The **Point Location Problem** is one of the classical query problems in computational geometry. Formally, it is defined as follows:

> **Point Location Problem (Planar Subdivision Variant):**
> Let $\mathcal{S}$ be a planar subdivision of the plane induced by a planar straight-line graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$ vertices and $|F|$ faces. Given a query point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$, determine the face $f \in F$ such that $q \in f$.

The problem admits several natural variants based on the geometric structure of the subdivision:
1. **Point location in a convex polygon** — query point vs. convex hull faces.
2. **Point location in a planar subdivision** — the canonical KTU variant (triangulations, trapezoidations).
3. **Point location in arrangements** of lines or segments.
4. **3D point location** — finding the cell of a 3D arrangement.

A *planar subdivision* $\mathcal{S}$ partitions the plane into faces (open 2D regions), edges (1D segments), and vertices (0D points). The query returns the face label.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The board examiner expects a clear distinction between *preprocessing time*, *space complexity*, and *query time*. Memorize the trade-off between **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** ($O(n)$ space, $O(\log n)$ query) and the **Slab Method** ($O(n^2)$ space, $O(\log n)$ query). Fractional Cascading is the bridge that keeps the Slab Method's $O(\log n)$ query while reducing multiplicative constants in practice.

## 1.2 Fractional Cascading — Formal Definition

> **Fractional Cascading (Chazelle & Guibas, 1986):**
> A technique that speeds up binary search across $k$ sorted lists (each of size $O(n)$) from a naive $O(k \log n)$ total cost to $O(\log n + k)$, after $O(kn)$ preprocessing time and $O(kn)$ space. Each query takes $O(\log n)$ to enter the first list, then traverses the remaining lists in $O(1)$ amortized time each, because each successive list stores "fractional copies" of its predecessor.

It is a **search acceleration technique** for multiple lists with monotonic properties (e.g., sorted arrays of edges stacked vertically in slabs).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

### 🗺️ Analogy for Point Location
Imagine you are looking for a specific **district on a map of Kerala**:
1. You do not scan every district boundary.
2. You first locate the **state column** using the x-coordinate (longitude) — a binary search.
3. Within that column, you walk vertically (using y-coordinate / latitude) and check which district band your city lies in.

This is exactly the **Slab Method + Vertical Search** strategy for point location.

### 🪜 Analogy for Fractional Cascading
Imagine a **multi-floor staircase with handrails at every step**. On each floor, every handrail step has a small marker telling you exactly where the equivalent step is on the floor above. So once you climb the staircase and find the right step on the ground floor, you can match directly to the correct step on floor 1, then floor 2, and so on — without starting the climb all over again. The "fractional copies" are those marker projections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** "Fractional" = the lists share *fractions* of their elements with neighbours. "Cascading" = the binary search *cascades* from one list to the next in $O(1)$ amortized time.

---

## 1.4 Problem Statement (Standard KTU Form)

> Given a planar straight-line graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, pre-process it into a data structure such that for any query point $q$, we can return the face of the subdivision containing $q$ in $O(\log n)$ time. The structure must use $O(n)$ or $O(n^2)$ space (depending on the method).

**Input:** A planar subdivision $S$ (preprocessing phase) and a stream of query points $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_m\}$ (query phase).

**Output:** For each $q_i$, the face $f_i \in F$ such that $q_i \in f_i$.

## 1.5 Applications — Why This Problem Matters

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **GIS / Maps** | Determining the district, taluk, or state containing a GPS coordinate |
| **CAD / CAM** | Selecting which polygon (component) the cursor is over in a circuit board |
| **Computer Graphics** | Ray tracing (cell traversal), clipping, virtual reality picking |
| **Robotics / Path Planning** | Finding free-space cells in a configuration-space decomposition |
| **VLSI Design** | Locating which cell a coordinate belongs to in a chip layout |
| **Spatial Databases (R-trees)** | Bounding-box queries during indexing |
| **Game Engines** | Collision detection, terrain cell lookup |
| **Computational Physics** | Mesh-based simulations (which element contains a particle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real GIS systems (e.g., Bhuvan, Google Maps APIs), point location is performed millions of times per second. The theoretical $O(\log n)$ bound is the *baseline* — Fractional Cascading and Kirkpatrick's method are deployed when the underlying subdivision is updated dynamically or stored in memory-constrained environments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Point Location Methods

### Method A: Slab Method (Müller, Preparata, Newborn)

**Idea:** Slice the plane vertically through every vertex x-coordinate.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Sort all $n$ vertices by x-coordinate: $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$.
2. Draw $n + 1$ vertical lines $x = x_1, x = x_2, \ldots, x = x_{n+1}$ (extending to $\pm\infty$).
3. The plane is partitioned into **slabs** $S_i = [x_i, x_{i+1}] \times \mathbb{R}$.
4. Inside each slab, no edge crosses another edge (because no vertex lies strictly between $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$).
5. For each slab, sort the edges by their y-coordinates into an array.
6. Build a **range tree / binary search tree** on the slabs (sorted by x).

**Query Algorithm:**
1. Binary search the slab containing $q$ by x-coordinate — $O(\log n)$.
2. Binary search the slab's edge array for $q$ by y-coordinate — $O(\log n)$.
3. Total query: $O(\log n)$.

**Complexity Profile:**

| Phase | Time | Space |
|-------|------|-------|
| Preprocessing | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Query | $O(\log n)$ | — |

**Drawback:** The $O(n^2)$ space is the bottleneck. In the worst case, each of the $n$ slabs can contain $O(n)$ edges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $O(n^2)$ Space?** Each slab $S_i$ must independently store its own sorted edge array. The total number of edge-array entries summed over all slabs is $O(n^2)$ in the worst case (think of a "fan" of edges from a vertex).

### Method B: Slab Method + Fractional Cascading

**The Optimization Insight:**
Inside a slab $S_i$, we binary search the edges. As we move from slab $S_i$ to slab $S_{i+1}$, only **$O(1)$ edges** change relative order (the edges adjacent to the vertex $v_{i+1}$). So we should not start a fresh $O(\log n)$ binary search in each slab; instead, we should *cascade* the search.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Construct slabs as before.
2. For each slab $S_i$, store a sorted edge array $A_i$.
3. Augment $A_i$ with *bridge pointers* to corresponding elements in $A_{i+1}$ (Fractional Cascading structure).
4. Build the slab range tree with auxiliary catalogs.

**Query Algorithm:**
1. Locate slab via range tree — $O(\log n)$.
2. Binary search first array $A_1$ — $O(\log n)$.
3. Follow bridge pointers through remaining arrays — $O(1)$ amortized per array.
4. Total query: $O(\log n)$ (same asymptotic, but the multiplicative constant is drastically reduced).

### Method C: Kirkpatrick's Algorithm

**Idea:** Repeatedly find an independent set of vertices, remove them and their incident triangles, and store the removed triangles in a *search DAG*.

**Complexity Profile:**

| Phase | Time | Space |
|-------|------|-------|
| Preprocessing | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Query | $O(\log n)$ | — |

This is the **asymptotically optimal** structure for planar subdivisions, but the constants are large and it is complex to implement.

### Method D: Trapezoidal Map (Randomized)

**Idea:** Draw horizontal rays from each vertex to both $\pm\infty$, creating trapezoids. Build a **point location DAG** by inserting segments one-by-one in random order.

**Complexity Profile (Expected):**

| Phase | Time | Space |
|-------|------|-------|
| Preprocessing | $O(n \log n)$ expected | $O(n)$ expected |
| Query | $O(\log n)$ expected | — |

---

## 2.2 Fractional Cascading — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Setup

Suppose we have $k$ sorted lists $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k$, each of size at most $n$, with the property that every element of $L_{i+1}$ is *also* an element of $L_i$ (or roughly so). Without loss of generality, assume they are arranged in a linear sequence (as slabs in the Slab Method).

### 2.2.2 Construction

For each list $L_i$, build a *merged* array $M_i$ by merging $L_i$ with every other element of $L_{i+1}$. Specifically:

$$M_i = L_i \cup \{\text{every other element of } L_{i+1}\} \quad \text{(merged and re-sorted)}$$

Then store **forward pointers** from each element of $L_i$ to its nearest match in $L_{i+1}$, and **backward pointers** for symmetric navigation.

### 2.2.3 The Three Levels of Fractional Cascading

1. **Half-size merges** — Each $M_i$ has size $|L_i| + |L_{i+1}|/2$ approximately.
2. **Quarter-size merges** — $M_i$ itself merges with half of $M_{i+1}$, and so on.
3. **Recursive block structure** — A constant-depth cascade gives $O(\log n + k)$ query total.

### 2.2.4 Query in Cascaded Structure

1. Binary search $L_1$ for $x$ — $O(\log n)$.
2. Follow bridge pointer to $L_2$ — $O(1)$.
3. Use a 2-step refinement (forward + backward pointer) to find $x$ in $L_2$ — $O(1)$.
4. Repeat for $L_3, L_4, \ldots, L_k$ — each in $O(1)$.
5. Total: $O(\log n + k)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept / Equation | Complexity / Value | Constraints / Notes |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Slab Method** preprocessing time | $O(n^2)$ | Worst case, $n$ vertices |
| 2 | **Slab Method** query time | $O(\log n)$ | Binary search on slab + binary search on edges |
| 3 | **Slab Method** space | $O(n^2)$ | Sum of edge-array sizes across all slabs |
| 4 | **Slab + Fractional Cascading** query time | $O(\log n)$ | Same asymptotic; multiplicative constant reduced |
| 5 | **Fractional Cascading** query across $k$ lists | $O(\log n + k)$ | Versus naive $O(k \log n)$ |
| 6 | **Fractional Cascading** preprocessing | $O(kn)$ | Build merged arrays + bridge pointers |
| 7 | **Fractional Cascading** space | $O(kn)$ | Storing merged arrays |
| 8 | **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** query time | $O(\log n)$ | Optimal |
| 9 | **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** preprocessing | $O(n)$ | Independent-set peeling |
| 10 | **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** space | $O(n)$ | Linear |
| 11 | **Trapezoidal Map** expected query time | $O(\log n)$ | Randomized, expected |
| 12 | **Trapezoidal Map** expected space | $O(n)$ | Randomized, expected |
| 13 | Number of slabs | $n + 1$ | At most |
| 14 | Size of merged array $M_i$ | $\vert L_i \vert + \frac{\vert L_{i+1} \vert}{2}$ | After one level of cascade |
| 15 | Total cascade levels | $O(\log n)$ | For $k = n$ lists |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why These Algorithms Are Used |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Google Maps "reverse geocoding"** | Point location in polygon (district lookup); uses spatial indices (R-tree) and BVH variants |
| **AutoCAD / SolidWorks** | Cell selection on multi-million-face meshes; uses spatial subdivision + fractional cascading in B-rep kernels |
| **NVIDIA OptiX / Ray Tracing** | Accelerates per-pixel point location in BVH-trees (fractional cascading idea generalized to bounding volume hierarchies) |
| **VLSI Layout Verification** | DRC (Design Rule Check) requires repeated point location over polygons; Kirkpatrick's is preferred |
| **Robotic motion planning (RRT)** | Configuration-space decomposition requires online point location |
| **Mesh-based FEM solvers** | Every quadrature point must be located in its element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice:** Modern systems usually adopt the **Trapezoidal Map** (randomized, simpler) or **BVH-based spatial hashing** rather than the classical Slab Method, because of the $O(n^2)$ space blow-up. However, Fractional Cascading's *idea* — precomputing partial answers to amortize repeated queries — is ubiquitous.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Slab Method Construction

### Step 1: Vertex Sorting
Given the planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ with vertex set $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, we sort by x-coordinate.

Let $x_1 \leq x_2 \leq \ldots \leq x_n$ denote the sorted x-coordinates. Ties (vertical alignments) are broken arbitrarily.

### Step 2: Slab Creation
We draw vertical lines at each $x_i$, partitioning the plane into:

$$
S_i = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : x_i \leq x \leq x_{i+1}\}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

with $x_{n+1} = +\infty$ for the rightmost unbounded slab and $x_0 = -\infty$ for the leftmost.

The total number of slabs is at most $n + 1$.

### Step 3: Edge-Array per Slab
Inside slab $S_i$, every edge of the subdivision is fully contained or absent. We sort the edges by their y-coordinate (specifically, the y-coordinate at the midpoint of the slab, or equivalently by the y-coordinate at $x = (x_i + x_{i+1})/2$). Let the sorted array be:

$$
A_i = [e_{i,1}, e_{i,2}, \ldots, e_{i,m_i}]
$$

where $m_i = \vert A_i \vert$ is the number of edges crossing slab $S_i$.

### Step 4: Total Size Bound

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \leq n^2
$$

This is because each edge crosses at most $n$ slabs (one per vertex x-coordinate to its right). Summing over $n$ edges gives:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = O(n^2)
$$

### Step 5: Range Tree
Build a binary range tree $T$ on the slabs $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$, keyed by their x-coordinate interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$.

### Step 6: Query Algorithm

Given query point $q = (q_x, q_y)$:

```
FUNCTION PointLocateSlab(q):
    Step A: Use range tree T to find slab S_i containing q_x.
            This is binary search on slabs: O(log n).
    Step B: Binary search A_i for the edge whose y-band contains q_y.
            This is binary search on m_i edges: O(log n).
    Step C: Return the face adjacent to that edge in S_i.
```

**Total query:** $O(\log n + \log n) = O(\log n)$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Fractional Cascading Cascade Construction

### Step 1: Define Lists
Let $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k$ be sorted arrays (e.g., the edge arrays $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k$ from the Slab Method). Assume a *nesting property*: the keys in $L_{i+1}$ are also keys in $L_i$ (or we can augment $L_i$ with copies of $L_{i+1}$ elements).

### Step 2: Build Merged Array $M_i$
For each $i$ from $1$ to $k-1$, construct:

$$
M_i = \text{merge}(L_i, L_{i+1})
$$

But to control size, we keep all of $L_i$ and *every other* element of $L_{i+1}$:

$$
M_i = L_i \cup \{\text{even-indexed elements of } L_{i+1}\}
$$

Then sort $M_i$ and store it.

### Step 3: Size Analysis

$$
\vert M_i \vert = \vert L_i \vert + \frac{\vert L_{i+1} \vert}{2} = O(n)
$$

Total storage over all merged arrays:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert M_i \vert = O(kn)
$$

### Step 4: Bridge Pointers
For each element $x \in L_{i+1}$, store a pointer `bridge[i][x]` to the position of $x$ in $M_i$. For each element $y \in M_i$, store a pointer `next[i][y]` to the position of $y$ in $M_{i+1}$ (or its analog in $L_{i+1}$).

### Step 5: Cascading Search

**Query for value $x$ in all $k$ lists:**

```
FUNCTION FractionalCascadeSearch(x, L_1, ..., L_k, M_1, ..., M_{k-1}):
    pos := BinarySearch(L_1, x)              // O(log n)
    FOR i FROM 2 TO k:
        // Step A: Forward probe in M_{i-1}
        FORWARD pos by 1 step in M_{i-1}      // O(1) - at most 1 step
        IF M_{i-1}[pos] >= x THEN
            BACKWARD pos by 1 step           // O(1)
        END IF
        // Step B: Now pos points to the range in L_i
        Use bridge to jump to L_i            // O(1)
    END FOR
    RETURN pos
```

**Complexity:** One binary search $O(\log n)$, plus $k - 1$ iterations each $O(1)$ — total $O(\log n + k)$. ✓

### Step 6: Why the Forward+Backward Probe Works
The forward-backward probe exploits the fact that $M_i$ contains every element of $L_{i+1}$, so the target $x$'s position in $L_{i+1}$ differs from its position in $M_i$ by at most **2 steps** (since we kept every other element of $L_{i+1}$, the worst-case gap is bounded).

Formally: if $x$ has rank $r$ in $L_{i+1}$, then in $M_i$ (which contains every other element of $L_{i+1}$), the rank of $x$ is at most $r + 1$, and the candidate position is off by at most 2.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Time Complexity Proof for Slab Method

### Claim: Slab Method query time is $O(\log n)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. **Slab lookup:** The range tree $T$ contains $n$ leaves. Binary search on a balanced BST takes $O(\log n)$ time.

2. **Edge lookup:** Once slab $S_i$ is fixed, its edge array $A_i$ has size $m_i$. In the worst case $m_i = O(n)$. Binary search on $m_i$ elements takes $O(\log m_i) = O(\log n)$.

3. **Total:** $T(n) = T_{\text{slab}} + T_{\text{edge}} = O(\log n) + O(\log n) = O(\log n)$.

### Claim: Slab Method preprocessing time is $O(n^2)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

$$
T_{\text{preproc}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(m_i \log m_i) = O\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \log n\right) = O(n^2 \log n)
$$

In the worst case (a "fan" arrangement), $m_i = O(n)$, and the sum is dominated by the $n$ slabs. With careful implementation using merging (since $A_{i+1}$ is almost identical to $A_i$), we can reduce this to $O(n^2)$:

$$
T_{\text{preproc}} = O(n^2) \quad \text{(using merging)}
$$

This is because each edge crosses at most $n$ slabs, and sorting each slab independently takes $O(m_i \log m_i)$, summing to $O(n^2 \log n)$. Using a merge-sort-style incremental update, we get $O(n^2)$.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Fractional Cascading in Slab Method

### The Bridge: Connecting Slab Search to Fractional Cascading

In the Slab Method, the edge arrays $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ satisfy a key monotonicity property:

> **Monotonicity of Edge Arrays:** Moving from slab $S_i$ to slab $S_{i+1}$ (rightward by one vertex), only the edges incident to $v_{i+1}$ change relative order. All other edges maintain their relative positions.

This is precisely the setting for **Fractional Cascading**.

### Construction of the Fractional Cascade on Slabs

1. Build $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ as before.
2. Construct merged arrays $M_i$ between consecutive $A_i$ and $A_{i+1}$.
3. Store bridge pointers.
4. Build the range tree $T$ augmented with $M_i$ at each node.

### Query in Slab + Fractional Cascading

```
FUNCTION PointLocateFractional(q):
    // Step 1: Locate the leaf slab S_i containing q_x via range tree
    slab := RangeTreeSearch(T, q_x)              // O(log n)
    // Step 2: Binary search A_i for the position
    pos_i := BinarySearch(A_i, q_y)              // O(log n)
    // Step 3: Cascade through subsequent slabs (if needed)
    FOR each subsequent slab S_j (j > i) needed:
        pos_j := CascadeStep(pos_{j-1}, M_{j-1})  // O(1)
    END FOR
    // Step 4: Return face label
    RETURN face adjacent to A_j[pos_j] in slab S_j
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this improve over plain Slab Method?**
> The plain Slab Method requires $O(\log n)$ *per slab visited*. If we need to visit $\ell$ slabs (e.g., during a multi-slab operation), naive cost is $O(\ell \log n)$. With Fractional Cascading, it becomes $O(\log n + \ell)$. This is critical for **range queries** and **3D extensions**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison

| Method | Preproc Time | Space | Query Time | Dynamic? | Implementation Difficulty |
|--------|--------------|-------|------------|----------|---------------------------|
| Slab Method | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Static | Easy |
| Slab + Fractional Cascading | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ (improved const.) | Static | Moderate |
| Kirkpatrick's | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Static | Hard |
| Trapezoidal Map (Randomized) | $O(n \log n)$ exp. | $O(n)$ exp. | $O(\log n)$ exp. | Static | Moderate |
| Real-tree / BVH | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ avg. | Dynamic | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** "Which method is asymptotically optimal for *static* planar point location?" Answer: **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** ($O(n)$ space, $O(\log n)$ query). For *dynamic* updates, the answer is different.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Point Location Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram captures the end-to-end flow of a point location query using the Slab Method enhanced with Fractional Cascading.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[/Query Point QX QY/]
    RT[Range Tree T on Slab X-coordinates]
    SlabLookup[Slab Lookup via Binary Search]
    S1[Slab Si]
    A1[Edge Array Ai]
    BS1[Binary Search Ai at QY]
    FC1[Fractional Cascade Bridge to Ai+1]
    S2[Slab Si+1]
    A2[Edge Array Ai+1]
    FC2[Cascade Bridge to Ai+2]
    S3[Slab Si+2]
    A3[Edge Array Ai+2]
    Face[Return Face Label]
    Output[/Face Identifier F/]

    Input --> SlabLookup
    RT -.-> SlabLookup
    SlabLookup --> S1
    S1 --> A1
    A1 --> BS1
    BS1 --> FC1
    FC1 --> S2
    S2 --> A2
    A2 --> FC2
    FC2 --> S3
    S3 --> A3
    BS1 --> Face
    FC1 --> Face
    FC2 --> Face
    Face --> Output
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The dashed edge from `RT` to `SlabLookup` indicates that the **Range Tree** is consulted but is not part of the active call stack during cascade.
- `FC1` and `FC2` are the **Fractional Cascading bridges** — they provide $O(1)$ amortized transition between slabs.
- The output `Face Identifier F` represents the final answer to the point location query.

---

## 4.2 Fractional Cascading Data Structure

This diagram visualizes the structural relationship between sorted lists and their merged arrays with bridge pointers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ListLayer [Sorted Lists Layer]
        L1[L1 sorted array size n]
        L2[L2 sorted array size n]
        L3[L3 sorted array size n]
        Lk[Lk sorted array size n]
    end

    subgraph MergeLayer [Merged Arrays Layer]
        M1[M1 size 3n by 2]
        M2[M2 size 3n by 2]
        M3[M3 size 3n by 2]
    end

    subgraph BridgeLayer [Bridge Pointers]
        B12[Bridge L1 to L2]
        B23[Bridge L2 to L3]
        B34[Bridge L3 to Lk]
    end

    L1 --> M1
    L2 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L3 --> M2
    L3 --> M3
    Lk --> M3

    L1 --> B12
    B12 --> L2
    L2 --> B23
    B23 --> L3
    L3 --> B34
    B34 --> Lk

    style L1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style L2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style L3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Lk fill:#FFE4B5
    style M1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style M2 fill:#B0E0E6
    style M3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style B12 fill:#98FB98
    style B23 fill:#98FB98
    style B34 fill:#98FB98
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Sorted Lists Layer:** Each $L_i$ is the original edge array for slab $S_i$.
- **Merged Arrays Layer:** Each $M_i$ holds all of $L_i$ plus half of $L_{i+1}$.
- **Bridge Layer:** Forward+backward pointers allowing $O(1)$ transition.
- Colors: beige = original lists, blue = merged arrays, green = bridge pointers.

---

## 4.3 Cascading Query Walkthrough

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant RangeTree as Range Tree T
    participant L1 as L1
    participant M1 as M1
    participant L2 as L2
    participant M2 as M2
    participant L3 as L3

    User->>RangeTree: Query q = qx qy
    RangeTree-->>User: Slab index i
