```mermaid
    User->>L1: Binary search qy
    L1-->>User: Position p1 in O of log n
    User->>M1: Forward probe from p1
    M1-->>User: Adjusted position p1 prime
    User->>L2: Bridge jump to p2
    L2-->>User: Position p2 in O of 1
    User->>M2: Forward probe from p2
    M2-->>User: Adjusted position p2 prime
    User->>L3: Bridge jump to p3
    L3-->>User: Position p3 in O of 1
    User->>User: Return face label
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The first binary search costs $O(\log n)$.
- Every subsequent cascade step costs $O(1)$.
- Total cost: $O(\log n + k)$ where $k$ is the number of lists traversed.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Map of Point Location Methods

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Point Location Methods))
    Slab Method
      Vertical Slice Decomposition
      Range Tree on Slabs
      Edge Arrays per Slab
      Complexity O of n squared space
    Fractional Cascading
      Merged Arrays
      Bridge Pointers
      O of log n plus k query
      Cascade in O of 1 amortized
    Kirkpatricks Algorithm
      Independent Set Peeling
      Search DAG
      O of n preprocessing
      Optimal Linear Space
    Trapezoidal Map
      Horizontal Ray Decomposition
      Randomized Insertion
      Point Location DAG
      O of n Expected Space
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "draw the Slab Method structure," KTU examiners expect (1) the vertical lines through vertices, (2) labeled slabs, (3) sorted edges per slab, and (4) the binary search tree on slabs. Fractional Cascading additions are extra credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

> **Define the Point Location Problem in planar subdivisions. List any two of its real-world applications.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Point Location Problem** is defined as follows: given a planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ induced by a planar straight-line graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ vertices, pre-process $G$ into a data structure such that, for any query point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$, we can determine the face $f$ of $\mathcal{S}$ containing $q$ in time $O(\log n)$.

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Determining which administrative region (district, state, country) a GPS coordinate lies in.
2. **VLSI Design / CAD:** Locating the geometric cell on an integrated-circuit layout that contains a given point during design rule checking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note:** A complete answer must mention: planar subdivision, query point, and the face. Marks are deducted if "face" is replaced with "region" without further clarification.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

> **What is Fractional Cascading? State its time complexity for a query across $k$ sorted lists.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Fractional Cascading** is a data structure technique introduced by Chazelle and Guibas (1986) that speeds up binary search across multiple sorted lists. It precomputes merged arrays and bridge pointers between consecutive lists, so a single binary search in the first list allows $O(1)$ amortized search in each subsequent list.

**Time Complexity:** Given $k$ sorted lists, each of size at most $n$, after $O(kn)$ preprocessing, a query for element $x$ across all $k$ lists takes:

$$
T_{\text{query}} = O(\log n + k)
$$

This improves the naive $O(k \log n)$ cost when $k$ is large.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

> **(a)** Describe the Slab Method for planar point location. Discuss its preprocessing time, space complexity, and query time complexity with proper justification. [7 Marks]
>
> **(b)** Explain Fractional Cascading in detail. How does it enhance multi-list query performance? Show with an example how bridge pointers and forward-backward probes work. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Vertex Sorting (1 mark)**
Sort all $n$ vertices of the planar subdivision by their x-coordinate. Let the sorted x-coordinates be $x_1 \leq x_2 \leq \ldots \leq x_n$.

**Step 2: Slab Construction (2 marks)**
Draw vertical lines $x = x_1, x = x_2, \ldots, x = x_n$, partitioning the plane into $n + 1$ slabs $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$. Each slab is defined as:

$$
S_i = [x_i, x_{i+1}] \times \mathbb{R}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n
$$

**Step 3: Edge Arrays (1 mark)**
Inside each slab $S_i$, no edge crosses another (since no vertex lies strictly inside $S_i$). Sort the edges crossing $S_i$ by their y-coordinate into an array $A_i$.

**Step 4: Range Tree (1 mark)**
Build a binary search tree $T$ on the slabs keyed by their x-coordinate interval.

**Step 5: Complexity Analysis (2 marks)**
- **Preprocessing time:** $O(n^2)$. The dominant cost is summing over all slabs: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} O(m_i \log m_i)$, where $m_i$ is the number of edges in slab $S_i$. Worst case $\sum m_i = O(n^2)$.
- **Space:** $O(n^2)$ to store all edge arrays.
- **Query time:** $O(\log n)$. Two binary searches: one on slabs (over $n$ leaves), one on edges in slab (over $m_i \leq n$ elements).

**Total for (a): 7 marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Problem Setup (1 mark)**
We are given $k$ sorted lists $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k$, each of size $O(n)$. A naive query for element $x$ across all lists takes $O(k \log n)$.

**Step 2: Merged Array Construction (2 marks)**
For each pair of consecutive lists, build a merged array:

$$
M_i = L_i \cup \{\text{every other element of } L_{i+1}\}
$$

Size: $\vert M_i \vert = \vert L_i \vert + \vert L_{i+1} \vert / 2 = O(n)$.

**Step 3: Bridge Pointers (1 mark)**
For each element in $M_i$, store a pointer to its corresponding position in $L_{i+1}$. This is the "bridge."

**Step 4: Cascading Query Algorithm (2 marks)**
```
1. Binary search L_1 for x: O(log n)
2. Forward probe in M_1: O(1)
3. Backward correction if needed: O(1)
4. Bridge jump to L_2: O(1)
5. Repeat for L_3, ..., L_k
```

**Step 5: Complexity Analysis (1 mark)**
Total query: $O(\log n + k)$, since the first binary search costs $O(\log n)$ and each subsequent cascade costs $O(1)$ amortized.

**Example (1 mark):**
Let $L_1 = [2, 4, 6, 8]$, $L_2 = [3, 5, 7, 9]$, $L_3 = [1, 4, 7, 10]$. Query for $x = 5$.
- $L_1$: $5$ falls between positions of $4$ and $6$.
- Bridge to $L_2$: position of $5$ found in $O(1)$.
- Bridge to $L_3$: position of $5$ found in $O(1)$.
- Total: 1 binary search + 2 cascade steps.

**Total for (b): 7 marks**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

> **(a)** Define a planar subdivision. State and explain the Point Location Problem with a real-world analogy. Mention the four classical algorithmic approaches to solve it. [7 Marks]
>
> **(b)** Construct a Fractional Cascade data structure for the following three sorted lists:
> $L_1 = [3, 9, 15, 21, 27]$, $L_2 = [6, 12, 18, 24, 30]$, $L_3 = [3, 12, 21, 30, 39]$.
> Show step-by-step how a query for $x = 20$ is processed using bridge pointers. Compute the query time. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Planar Subdivision Definition (1 mark)**
A **planar subdivision** $\mathcal{S}$ is a partition of the plane into three types of features:
- **Faces** (open 2D regions),
- **Edges** (1D segments),
- **Vertices** (0D points),
induced by a planar straight-line graph $G = (V, E)$.

**Step 2: Point Location Problem Statement (2 marks)**
Given a planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ with $n$ vertices and a query point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$, find the face $f \in F$ such that $q \in f$.

**Step 3: Real-World Analogy (1 mark)**
Finding which district of Kerala contains a given city. Kerala's district map is a planar subdivision; the city coordinates are the query point; the answer is the face (district).

**Step 4: Four Classical Approaches (3 marks)**
1. **Slab Method:** $O(n^2)$ preprocessing, $O(n^2)$ space, $O(\log n)$ query.
2. **Slab Method + Fractional Cascading:** Same asymptotic; reduced constants.
3. **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm:** $O(n)$ preprocessing, $O(n)$ space, $O(\log n)$ query (optimal).
4. **Trapezoidal Map (Randomized):** $O(n \log n)$ expected preprocessing, $O(n)$ expected space, $O(\log n)$ expected query.

**Total for (a): 7 marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: List Setup (1 mark)**
Given:
- $L_1 = [3, 9, 15, 21, 27]$
- $L_2 = [6, 12, 18, 24, 30]$
- $L_3 = [3, 12, 21, 30, 39]$

**Step 2: Construct Merged Array $M_1$ (2 marks)**
Merge $L_1$ with every other element of $L_2$:
$M_1 = [3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30]$

**Step 3: Construct Merged Array $M_2$ (1 mark)**
Merge $L_2$ with every other element of $L_3$:
$M_2 = [3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 39]$

**Step 4: Bridge Pointers (1 mark)**
From each element of $M_1$, store a pointer to its position in $L_2$. From $M_2$, store a pointer to $L_3$.

**Step 5: Query for $x = 20$ (1 mark)**

```
Step A: Binary search L_1 = [3, 9, 15, 21, 27] for x = 20
        → 20 falls between 15 and 21 → position p_1 = 3 (0-indexed)
Step B: Forward probe in M_1 starting at position 3
        → M_1 = [3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30]
        → M_1[3] = 12 < 20; advance to position 4: M_1[4] = 15 < 20
        → M_1[5] = 18 < 20; M_1[6] = 21 ≥ 20
        → Position p'_1 = 6
Step C: Backward correction: M_1[5] = 18 < 20, so position in L_2 is between 18 and 21
        → Use bridge: in L_2, 20 falls between 18 and 24 → position p_2 = 3
Step D: Forward probe in M_2 from position 3
        → M_2[3] = 12 < 20; M_2[4] = 15 < 20; M_2[5] = 18 < 20
        → M_2[6] = 21 ≥ 20
        → Bridge to L_3: 20 falls between 12 and 21 → position p_3 = 2
```

**Step 6: Query Time Computation (1 mark)**
- Binary search in $L_1$: $O(\log 5) = O(1)$.
- Cascade to $L_2$: $O(1)$.
- Cascade to $L_3$: $O(1)$.
- Total: $O(\log n + k) = O(\log 5 + 3) = O(1)$ for this small example.

In general, for $n$ elements and $k$ lists: $O(\log n + k)$.

**Total for (b): 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $O(n^2)$ with $O(n \log n)$:** Students often mistakenly write "Slab Method preprocessing is $O(n \log n)$." The correct bound is $O(n^2)$ in the worst case.
>
> 2. **Missing the role of bridge pointers:** A model answer that only describes merged arrays without bridge pointers is incomplete. Bridge pointers are the heart of Fractional Cascading — they enable $O(1)$ amortized transitions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "fractional" part:** Simply merging $L_i$ and $L_{i+1}$ gives $O(n)$ extra space per merge. The *fractional* aspect (every other element) keeps the merged array size at $O(n)$ instead of $O(2n)$.
>
> 4. **Asymptotic vs. Practical Improvement:** Fractional Cascading does not improve the asymptotic query time ($O(\log n)$ vs. $O(\log n)$). It improves the *multi-list* scenario from $O(k \log n)$ to $O(\log n + k)$, which is critical when $k$ is large.
>
> 5. **Mixing up "Point Location in Point Sets" vs. "Point Location in Subdivision":** These are different problems! The first asks "is q in the convex hull / Voronoi cell of a point set"; the second asks "which face of a subdivision contains q." KTU examiners penalize confusion.
>
> 6. **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm Setup:** Students often describe it without mentioning the *independent set* of vertices, which is the core mechanism.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Points:**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Point Location:** Given a planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ with $n$ vertices, pre-process into a data structure so that for any query point $q$, the face containing $q$ can be found efficiently.
- **Planar Subdivision:** Partition of the plane into faces (2D), edges (1D), and vertices (0D) by a planar straight-line graph.
- **Fractional Cascading:** Speedup technique for multi-list binary search via merged arrays and bridge pointers.

### 🔑 Complexity Bounds (Memorize the Table)

| Method | Preprocess | Space | Query |
|--------|-----------|-------|-------|
| Slab | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Slab + FC | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Kirkpatrick | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Trapezoidal Map | $O(n \log n)$ exp. | $O(n)$ exp. | $O(\log n)$ exp. |
| FC across $k$ lists | $O(kn)$ | $O(kn)$ | $O(\log n + k)$ |

### 🔑 Slab Method — Key Properties
- Number of slabs: $n + 1$ at most.
- Inside each slab, no edge crossing occurs.
- Slab lookup via range tree: $O(\log n)$.
- Edge lookup via binary search: $O(\log n)$.

### 🔑 Fractional Cascading — Key Properties
- Merged array $M_i$ has size $\vert L_i \vert + \vert L_{i+1} \vert / 2 = O(n)$.
- Bridge pointers enable $O(1)$ transition between lists.
- Forward-backward probe guarantees position within 2 steps.
- Total query across $k$ lists: $O(\log n + k)$.

### 🔑 Real-World Applications to Mention in Answers
- GIS / reverse geocoding
- CAD / VLSI design
- Computer graphics / ray tracing
- Robotics / motion planning
- Mesh-based FEM solvers

### 🔑 Frequent Exam Keywords
- "Planar subdivision"
- "Face, edge, vertex"
- "Slab"
- "Range tree"
- "Bridge pointer"
- "Merged array"
- "Independent set" (Kirkpatrick)
- "Trapezoid" (Trapezoidal Map)

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls
- Confusing $O(n^2)$ vs. $O(n \log n)$ preprocessing.
- Forgetting "fractional" in Fractional Cascading (must be every other element, not full merge).
- Confusing point location in *point sets* vs. *subdivisions*.

### 🔑 Mnemonics
- **"SLEEK"** for the 4 methods: **S**lab, **L**ist (Fractional Cascading), **E**xponential (Kirkpatrick), **K**-Trapezoidal (Trapezoidal Map).
- **"FBP"** for Fractional Cascading query: **F**orward probe, **B**ackward correct, **P**ointer jump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme module 3 carries 20% weightage of the End Semester Exam (ESE). Questions on Point Location typically appear as **Part A (3 marks)** for definitions and **Part B (14 marks)** for algorithmic questions. Always pair your algorithm description with its complexity analysis table — that alone can earn 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trapezoidal Map and Randomized Incremental Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trapezoidal Map and Randomized Incremental Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Trapezoidal Decomposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trapezoidal Map (T(S))** — Given a set $S$ of $n$ non-crossing line segments in the plane, the *trapezoidal map* of $S$ is the subdivision of the plane obtained by drawing two vertical rays (one upward, one downward) from every endpoint of every segment in $S$, extending each ray until it hits another segment (or to infinity).

Each face of this subdivision is either:
- A **trapezoid** (bounded by two segment-edges on the left and right, and two vertical rays on the top and bottom), OR
- An **unbounded region** (often called an *infinite trapezoid* in the KTU convention).

The horizontal boundaries of each trapezoid are segments of the original input, while the vertical boundaries are the *shooting rays* from the endpoints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Flashlight in a Hall of Mirrors":**
>
> Imagine a dark room (the plane) in which several opaque, non-touching **glass panels** (line segments) are suspended. Stand at the endpoint of each panel and shine a flashlight *straight up* and *straight down*. The light beams travel until they hit the next panel above or below, or escape to the ceiling/floor. The illuminated patches of the room form the **trapezoids** — long horizontal regions pinched between two adjacent panels, and vertical slots (the rays) separating them.

This image makes it clear why:
- Each trapezoid is *horizontally* bounded by input segments.
- Each trapezoid is *vertically* bounded by the flashlight beams (rays).
- A point location query reduces to: *"In which illuminated patch does my point lie?"*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **$n$** — number of input line segments in $S$.
- **$t$** — number of trapezoids in the resulting map (expected $O(n)$ for non-crossing segments).
- **$m$** — number of queries for the point-location phase.
- **Expected query time** — $O(\log n)$ (randomized).
- **Expected preprocessing time** — $O(n \log n)$ (randomized).
- **Expected space** — $O(n)$.

### 1.4 Why a Trapezoidal Map?

> [!NOTE]
> The trapezoidal map is the *canonical subdivision* used by the **Randomized Incremental Construction (RIC)** paradigm in computational geometry. It serves two purposes simultaneously:
> 1. It *decomposes* the plane into simple constant-time-lookup cells.
> 2. It implicitly defines a *point-location data structure* (a directed acyclic graph / search structure) that is built at almost no extra cost during construction.

This is one of the rare cases in algorithm design where *building* the structure and *querying* it share the same asymptotic complexity as solving either one alone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Strategy: Randomized Incremental Algorithm

The construction proceeds by inserting segments one at a time in **random order**. The key operations during each insertion are:

1. **Locate** the trapezoid containing the *left endpoint* of the new segment $s_i$.
2. **Walk along** $s_i$ from left to right, traversing each trapezoid it crosses.
3. For each trapezoid crossed, *split it* into smaller trapezoids (removing the old trapezoid and replacing it with 2 or 3 new ones).
4. **Shoot vertical rays** from the (new) endpoints of $s_i$ upward and downward, splitting any trapezoid the ray passes through.

### 2.2 Expected-Case Analysis (Backwards Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backwards Analysis Principle:** Instead of asking *"How many trapezoids will my insertion split?"*, we ask the equivalent question in reverse: *"Given a trapezoid $\Delta$ of the **final** map, what is the probability that $\Delta$ was created (or destroyed) by the $i$-th insertion?"*

If the trapezoid $\Delta$ has $k(\Delta)$ *defining segments* (segments that bound it on top or bottom), then the probability that $\Delta$'s creation was triggered by the $i$-th random insertion is:
$$P[\Delta \text{ changed at step } i] = \frac{k(\Delta)}{i}$$

This is because the $i$-th segment is equally likely to be **any** of the $i$ segments seen so far, and only $k(\Delta)$ of them are "relevant" to $\Delta$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Expected Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of trapezoids | $t$ | $O(n)$ | Each insertion creates $O(k)$ new ones |
| Vertices in map | $V$ | $\le 2n + 2$ | 2 per endpoint, 2 for $x = -\infty, +\infty$ |
| Edges in map | $E$ | $\le 3n + 1$ | horizontal segments + vertical rays |
| Trapezoid faces | $F$ | $\le n + 1$ | By Euler's formula |
| Defining segments per trapezoid | $k(\Delta)$ | $2$ typically | Top and bottom segment |
| Preprocessing time | $T_{\text{prep}}$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Sorting + randomized insert |
| Query time | $T_{\text{query}}$ | $O(\log n)$ | Expected, point location |
| Space | $M$ | $O(n)$ | DAG of trapezoids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Euler relation used for verification:**
> $$V - E + F = 1 + C$$
> where $C$ is the number of connected components of the arrangement. For non-crossing segments $C = n + 1$ (each segment + the unbounded face).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GIS / Cartography:** Decompose a road network into trapezoidal cells for fast spatial lookups ("which road am I on?").
- **VLSI Design Rule Checking:** Partition layout into simple cells to check for geometric violations.
- **CAD / Collision Detection:** Pre-process a polygon environment for fast "where is the robot?" queries.
- **Database Spatial Indexing:** Foundation for $R$-tree-like structures.
- **Robotics Path Planning:** Rapid "which free-space region contains my point?" test.

### 2.5 The Point-Location Data Structure (DAG)

> [!NOTE]
> During construction, the algorithm also builds a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** $D$ whose leaves are the trapezoids. Internal nodes are of two types:
> - **$x$-node:** stores the $x$-coordinate of a segment endpoint; routes the query left/right.
> - **$y$-node:** stores a segment $s$; routes the query above/below $s$.
>
> The DAG has **$O(n)$ nodes** in expectation, and a query is answered by walking from the root to a leaf in $O(\log n)$ expected time.

### 2.6 Operations Summary

| Operation | Description | Cost (expected) |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{INSERT}(s_i)$ | Add a new segment randomly | $O(\log n + k_i)$ amortized |
| $\text{QUERY}(q)$ | Locate trapezoid containing $q$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| $\text{BUILD}(S)$ | Build the full structure | $O(n \log n)$ |
| $\text{VERIFY}$ | Confirm map correctness via Euler check | $O(n)$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Trapezoids $E[t]$

We will compute $E[t]$ using backwards analysis. Let $\mathcal{T}_i$ be the trapezoidal map after inserting the first $i$ random segments. Define the indicator random variable:
$$X_{\Delta} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if trapezoid } \Delta \text{ exists in the final map } \mathcal{T}_n \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Then the expected number of trapezoids in the final map is:
$$E[t] = E\left[\sum_{\Delta} X_{\Delta}\right] = \sum_{\Delta} P[X_{\Delta} = 1]$$

For a trapezoid $\Delta$ in the *final* map, it has $k(\Delta)$ defining segments (the segments forming its top and bottom horizontal edges). It was "born" at the insertion of the **last** of these $k(\Delta)$ defining segments. Let $i_\Delta$ be the step at which $\Delta$ was created. Then:
$$P[X_{\Delta} = 1] = P[i_\Delta \le n] = 1 \quad \text{(it exists in the final map)}$$

But we need the *change* count. A more refined analysis: the trapezoid $\Delta$ was *created or destroyed* at step $i$ iff one of its $k(\Delta)$ defining segments is the $i$-th segment inserted. Hence:
$$P[\Delta \text{ changed at step } i] = \frac{k(\Delta)}{i}$$

The total number of trapezoid *creation/destruction events* across all $n$ steps:
$$E[\text{events}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{\Delta \in \mathcal{T}_i} \frac{k(\Delta)}{i}$$

Because $k(\Delta) \le 2$ (a trapezoid has at most a top and a bottom segment), and each step starts with at most $O(i)$ trapezoids:
$$E[\text{events}] \le \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2 \cdot c \cdot i}{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2c = O(n)$$

This proves that the *expected* total number of trapezoids ever created during the algorithm is $O(n)$. Since trapezoids only grow in number monotonically (we never re-merge), the final count is also $O(n)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Preprocessing Time $E[T_{\text{prep}}]$

The preprocessing time breaks into:
$$T_{\text{prep}} = T_{\text{sort}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{insert}}(i)$$

- **Sorting:** $T_{\text{sort}} = O(n \log n)$ (lexicographic sort of endpoints).
- **Insertion time $T_{\text{insert}}(i)$:** Locate the left endpoint, then walk through trapezoids. If the new segment crosses $k_i$ trapezoids, the cost is $O(\log i + k_i)$.

Thus:
$$E[T_{\text{prep}}] = O(n \log n) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[\log i + k_i]$$

We need to bound $E[k_i]$, the expected number of trapezoids crossed by the $i$-th inserted segment. By linearity:
$$E[k_i] = \sum_{\Delta \in \mathcal{T}_{i-1}} P[\text{segment } i \text{ crosses } \Delta]$$

A segment $s_i$ crosses trapezoid $\Delta$ iff $\Delta$ has a defining segment on the *opposite* side. For a typical trapezoid with $k(\Delta) = 2$ (top and bottom), the probability is $\le \frac{2}{i}$. Hence:
$$E[k_i] \le \sum_{\Delta \in \mathcal{T}_{i-1}} \frac{2}{i} = \frac{2 \cdot O(i)}{i} = O(1)$$

Therefore:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[\log i + k_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log i) + O(1) = O(n \log n)$$

$$\boxed{E[T_{\text{prep}}] = O(n \log n)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Expected Query Time $E[T_{\text{query}}]$

Consider the search DAG $D$ with root $r$. A query point $q$ traces a path $r = v_0, v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_d$ where $d$ is the depth. The path makes two kinds of decisions:

- **$x$-node decisions:** depend on $x$-coordinates of randomly-ordered endpoints. There are $2i$ such endpoints after $i$ insertions.
- **$y$-node decisions:** depend on which segment is "above" $q$ in some interval. The probability that segment $s_j$ is the one tested at a given $y$-node is $\frac{1}{j}$ (random order).

Using backwards analysis again, a single trapezoid $\Delta$ in the final map is *queried into* with probability $\frac{1}{t} \le \frac{1}{c_1 n}$ (since $t = O(n)$). The depth of the path to $\Delta$ is bounded by the number of "relevant" insertions:
$$E[\text{depth to } \Delta] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P[\text{segment } j \text{ is on the path}] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{k_j(\Delta)}{j} \le \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{2}{j} = O(\log n)$$

Hence:
$$\boxed{E[T_{\text{query}}] = O(\log n)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Expected Space $E[M]$

The DAG has one node per:
- Segment endpoint (≤ $2n$)
- Segment (≤ $n$)
- Trapezoid in the final map ($O(n)$)

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E[M] = O(n)}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example: 3-Segment Configuration

Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ be three horizontal segments, equally spaced vertically. The random insertion order is $(s_2, s_1, s_3)$.

| Step | Action | New Trapezoids Created | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insert $s_2$ (middle) | 1 strip (above + below $s_2$) | 1 |
| 2 | Insert $s_1$ (top) | Splits upper strip into 2, lower untouched | 3 |
| 3 | Insert $s_3$ (bottom) | Splits lower strip into 2, adds rays | 5 |

Final map: 5 trapezoids. Notice the *expected* $O(n)$ bound: $5 = O(3)$.

### 3.6 Verification via Euler's Formula

For our example: 3 segments give $V = 8$ (2 per endpoint + 2 infinity), $E = 13$, $F = 6$ (5 trapezoids + 1 unbounded). Check:
$$V - E + F = 8 - 13 + 6 = 1 = 1 + C - C = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

with $C = 3 + 1 = 4$ connected components (3 segments + 1 unbounded face), giving $1 + C - $ faces $\ldots$ the formula is satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Build Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input set S of n segments]) --> sortB[Randomly permute S]
    sortB --> initC[Initialize empty trapezoidal map M0 and DAG D with single root trapezoid]
    initC --> loopD{For i = 1 to n}
    loopD --> locE[Locate trapezoid containing left endpoint of s_i using current DAG]
    locE --> walkF[Walk along s_i left to right, traversing trapezoids it crosses]
    walkF --> splitG[For each crossed trapezoid: remove it, add 2 or 3 new ones]
    splitG --> rayH[Shoot vertical rays from both endpoints of s_i]
    rayH --> updateI[Update DAG D: add x-nodes and y-nodes for new decisions]
    updateI --> loopD
    loopD --> doneJ([Done: Return map M_n and DAG D])
```

### 4.2 Trapezoidal Map Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph map1["Trapezoidal Map T(S) — Conceptual Layout"]
        seg1["Segment s_1 (top)"]:::seg
        seg2["Segment s_2 (middle)"]:::seg
        seg3["Segment s_3 (bottom)"]:::seg
        trap1["Trapezoid T1"]:::trap
        trap2["Trapezoid T2"]:::trap
        trap3["Trapezoid T3"]:::trap
        trap4["Trapezoid T4"]:::trap
        trap5["Trapezoid T5"]:::trap
        rayU["Upward ray"]:::ray
        rayD["Downward ray"]:::ray
        seg1 --- trap1
        seg1 --- trap2
        seg2 --- trap2
        seg2 --- trap3
        seg3 --- trap4
        seg3 --- trap5
        rayU --- trap1
        rayU --- trap3
        rayD --- trap2
        rayD --- trap4
    end
    classDef seg fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef trap fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#1E5AA8,color:#000
    classDef ray fill:#D0F0D0,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Search DAG Structure (Point-Location)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA([Root: Start]) --> xnode1{x-node: x = x_left of s_1?}
    xnode1 -->|q.x ≤ x_left| trapX1[Trapezoid T_left]:::leaf
    xnode1 -->|q.x > x_left| ynode1{y-node: Above s_1?}
    ynode1 -->|Yes| ynode2{y-node: Above s_2?}
    ynode2 -->|Yes| trapX2[Trapezoid T_above_above]:::leaf
    ynode2 -->|No| trapX3[Trapezoid T_above_below]:::leaf
    ynode1 -->|No| xnode2{x-node: x = x_left of s_3?}
    xnode2 -->|q.x ≤ x_left| trapX4[Trapezoid T_below_left]:::leaf
    xnode2 -->|q.x > x_left| trapX5[Trapezoid T_below_right]:::leaf
    classDef leaf fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Backwards-Analysis Probability Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    piA[Step i]:::step --> qA[What is the chance that the LAST of k defining segments is the i-th inserted?]:::q
    qA --> ansA["P = k / i"]:::ans
    ansA --> outA[Used to bound expected cost]:::out
    classDef step fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Architecture of the Full Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputA["Input Layer"]
        segset["Set S of n non-crossing segments"]:::in
    end
    subgraph buildB["Construction Layer"]
        sorter["Random Permutation Module"]:::mod
        locator["Endpoint Locator (uses DAG)"]:::mod
        walker["Trapezoid Walker (along s_i)"]:::mod
        splitter["Trapezoid Splitter"]:::mod
        rayShooter["Vertical Ray Shooter"]:::mod
    end
    subgraph dataC["Data Structure Layer"]
        mapDS["Trapezoidal Map M_n"]:::data
        dagDS["Search DAG D"]:::data
    end
    subgraph queryD["Query Layer"]
        pointQ["Point-Location Query q"]:::query
        resultQ["Result: Trapezoid containing q"]:::query
    end
    segset --> sorter
    sorter --> locator
    locator --> walker
    walker --> splitter
    splitter --> rayShooter
    rayShooter --> mapDS
    rayShooter --> dagDS
    pointQ --> dagDS
    dagDS --> resultQ
    classDef in fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef mod fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef query fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
Define the *trapezoidal map* $T(S)$ of a set $S$ of line segments. How is it constructed from the segments?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The trapezoidal map $T(S)$ of a set of $n$ non-crossing line segments $S$ is the subdivision of the plane obtained by drawing a vertical ray **upward** and a vertical ray **downward** from every endpoint of every segment in $S$. Each ray extends until it hits another segment or continues to infinity.
>
> The faces of this subdivision are called **trapezoids**. Each trapezoid is bounded by:
> - Two (portions of) input segments on the top and bottom (horizontal boundaries).
> - Two vertical ray segments on the left and right (vertical boundaries).
>
> If a face is not bounded above or below, it is treated as an *infinite trapezoid* with one of its vertical edges going to infinity. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
State the expected time and space complexity of the *Randomized Incremental Construction* of the trapezoidal map and its associated point-location structure. Justify the bound for query time briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The expected preprocessing time is $O(n \log n)$, the expected query time is $O(\log n)$, and the expected space is $O(n)$.
>
> **Justification for query time:** Using *backwards analysis*, a trapezoid $\Delta$ in the final map has $k(\Delta)$ defining segments. The probability that any particular one of the $i$ random segments was responsible for placing $\Delta$ on the search path is $\frac{k(\Delta)}{i} \le \frac{2}{i}$. Summing over $i = 1 \ldots n$ gives an expected path length of $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2}{i} = O(\log n)$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to mention both upward and downward rays** when defining the trapezoidal map — this costs a full mark.
> - In backwards-analysis problems, students write the probability as $\frac{1}{k(\Delta)}$ instead of $\frac{k(\Delta)}{i}$ — read the question carefully (whose probability? at which step?).
> - The expected query time is **$O(\log n)$, not $O(\log^2 n)$** — confusion with segment trees is common.
> - Always state the *randomness assumption* (segments inserted in random order) before applying the expected-case analysis.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Randomized Incremental Algorithm** for constructing the trapezoidal map of a set $S$ of $n$ non-crossing line segments. State the data structures used and explain the role of the search DAG. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Random Permutation:** Randomly order the $n$ segments of $S$ into a sequence $\sigma = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$. This randomness is crucial for the expected-time bounds.

2. **Initialization:** Start with the empty arrangement $\mathcal{T}_0$ containing exactly one trapezoid — the entire plane. The associated DAG $D$ has a single leaf node representing this trapezoid.

3. **Iterative Insertion:** For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:
   - **Step 3.1 — Locate:** Using the current DAG $D$, locate the trapezoid $T_L$ containing the **left endpoint** of $s_i$. This takes $O(\log i)$ expected time.
   - **Step 3.2 — Walk:** Trace the segment $s_i$ from left to right through the current map. Maintain a list of trapezoids it crosses.
   - **Step 3.3 — Split:** For each trapezoid $T$ crossed by $s_i$:
     - If $s_i$ passes through the *interior* of $T$ (from left edge to right edge), replace $T$ by **3 new trapezoids**: one above $s_i$, one below $s_i$, and one straddling it (depending on the shape).
     - If $s_i$ enters/exits through a *vertical* edge, replace $T$ by **2 trapezoids** split along the vertical edge.
   - **Step 3.4 — Shoot Rays:** From the **right endpoint** of $s_i$, shoot vertical rays upward and downward. Each ray splits any trapezoid it passes through into two.
   - **Step 3.5 — Update DAG:** Create new internal nodes (one $x$-node for the right endpoint, two $y$-nodes for the new defining segments) and new leaf nodes (one for each newly-created trapezoid). Wire them into $D$ at the appropriate locations.

4. **Data Structures:**
   - The map $\mathcal{T}_i$ is stored as a collection of trapezoid records, each with pointers to its four bounding edges and four neighboring trapezoids.
   - The DAG $D$ has internal nodes of two types: $x$-nodes (containing an endpoint's $x$-coordinate) and $y$-nodes (containing a segment). Leaves are trapezoids.

5. **Role of the Search DAG:** $D$ is built *incrementally* alongside the map, so it costs almost nothing extra to maintain. It serves as the **point-location structure**: given a query point $q$, follow the root-to-leaf path by comparing $q.x$ with $x$-nodes and $q.y$ with the segments at $y$-nodes. The leaf reached is the trapezoid containing $q$. The expected path length is $O(\log n)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Prove that the expected preprocessing time of the algorithm in (a) is $O(n \log n)$, using *backwards analysis*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** Let $T_{\text{prep}}$ be the total preprocessing time. Decompose:
$$T_{\text{prep}} = T_{\text{sort}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{insert}}(i)$$

- $T_{\text{sort}} = O(n \log n)$ — sorting the $2n$ endpoints.
- $T_{\text{insert}}(i) = O(\log i) + O(k_i)$, where $k_i$ is the number of trapezoids crossed by the $i$-th segment, and the $O(\log i)$ term is the cost to locate the left endpoint.

**Goal:** Show $E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{insert}}(i)\right] = O(n \log n)$.

**Step 1 — Bound the location cost:**
$$E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log i)\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log i) = O(n \log n)$$

**Step 2 — Bound $E[k_i]$ using backwards analysis:**

$k_i$ counts the trapezoids in the map $\mathcal{T}_{i-1}$ that the new segment $s_i$ crosses. We rewrite:
$$E[k_i] = \sum_{\Delta \in \mathcal{T}_{i-1}} P[s_i \text{ crosses } \Delta]$$

A trapezoid $\Delta$ has $k(\Delta) \in \{1, 2\}$ defining segments on top and bottom. The segment $s_i$ can cross $\Delta$ only if $s_i$ is one of the segments that "close off" $\Delta$ from above or below. By random ordering, segment $s_i$ is equally likely to be any of the $i$ segments seen so far, so:
$$P[s_i \text{ crosses } \Delta] \le \frac{k(\Delta)}{i} \le \frac{2}{i}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:** Since $|\mathcal{T}_{i-1}| = O(i)$ (trapezoids only grow in number):
$$E[k_i] \le \frac{2 \cdot O(i)}{i} = O(1)$$

**Step 4 — Final bound:**
$$E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{insert}}(i)\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(\log i) + O(1) = O(n \log n)$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E[T_{\text{prep}}] = O(n \log n) + O(n \log n) = O(n \log n)} \quad \blacksquare \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the *search DAG* used for point location in a trapezoidal map. What are its node types, and how is a query answered? Also derive the expected query time $O(\log n)$ using backwards analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Search DAG Structure:**

The search DAG $D$ is a directed acyclic graph with the following node types:

- **$x$-node:** Stores the $x$-coordinate of a segment endpoint (say, $p$). Has two children: "left" and "right". A query point $q$ follows the left child if $q.x \le p.x$ and the right child otherwise.

- **$y$-node:** Stores a segment $s$. Has two children: "above" and "below". A query point $q$ follows the child corresponding to whether $q$ is above or below $s$ at the relevant $x$-range.

- **Leaf node:** Stores a trapezoid. A query reaching this leaf is *answered* — the trapezoid contains $q$.

**Query Algorithm:** Start at the root of $D$. At each internal node, make a constant-time decision (compare $x$ or compare $y$ with the segment line) and descend to the appropriate child. When a leaf is reached, output its trapezoid.

**Derivation of Expected Query Time $O(\log n)$:**

Let $q$ be the query point. The path traced by $q$ ends at some leaf trapezoid $\Delta_q$ in the final map $T_n$. We bound the *length* of this path.

For each insertion step $i = 1, \ldots, n$, define the event $A_i$ = "the $i$-th inserted segment was responsible for adding a node to the search path leading to $\Delta_q$." The number of internal nodes on the path is at most:
$$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}_{A_i}$$

By linearity of expectation:
$$E[L] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P[A_i]$$

A trapezoid $\Delta_q$ in the final map has $k(\Delta_q) \le 2$ defining segments. The $i$-th insertion can only contribute a node to the path to $\Delta_q$ if the $i$-th segment is one of these $k$ defining segments. By random ordering:
$$P[A_i] = \frac{k(\Delta_q)}{i} \le \frac{2}{i}$$

Therefore:
$$E[L] \le \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2}{i} = 2 H_n = O(\log n)$$

where $H_n$ is the $n$-th harmonic number. Hence:
$$\boxed{E[T_{\text{query}}] = O(\log n)} \quad \blacksquare \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

---

**(b)** Consider a set $S$ of $n$ non-crossing horizontal segments. Compute, using the trapezoidal map construction, the **expected number of trapezoids** in the final map $T(S)$. Verify your answer using Euler's formula for planar graphs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Counting Vertices:** Each segment has 2 endpoints → $2n$ finite vertices. Plus 2 vertices at $x = \pm \infty$ (topological closures). So $V = 2n + 2$.

**Step 2 — Counting Edges:**
- Horizontal edges: $n$ (the original segments, each contributing 1 edge per "side" of the trapezoid they bound — but conservatively, the segment is split at most once by vertical rays from other segments, giving at most $2n$ horizontal edges, but in the worst case, just $n$).
- Vertical edges: Each endpoint shoots 2 rays (up + down). Each ray travels until it hits another segment. Conservatively, $2n$ vertical rays, each contributing 1 edge. So $\le 2n$ vertical edges.

Total: $E \le n + 2n = 3n$. More precisely, $E \le 3n + 1$.

**Step 3 — Counting Faces via Euler's formula:**
$$V - E + F = 1 + C$$
where $C$ is the number of connected components. For $n$ non-crossing segments plus the unbounded face: $C = n + 1$.

$$F = 1 + C - V + E = 1 + (n+1) - (2n+2) + (3n+1) = n + 1$$

Subtracting the unbounded face: number of bounded trapezoids $\le n + 1 - 1 = n$, plus the unbounded cells. Conservatively: $F_{\text{trapezoids}} \le n + 1$.

**Step 4 — Expected Number via Backwards Analysis:**

Define the indicator $X_\Delta = 1$ if trapezoid $\Delta$ exists in $T_n$. A trapezoid is *created* the moment the last of its $k(\Delta)$ defining segments is inserted. The expected number of trapezoids created during the algorithm:

$$E[t] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{\Delta \text{ created at step } i} 1 \le \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2 \cdot |\mathcal{T}_{i-1}|}{i} \le \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2 \cdot c \cdot i}{i} = 2cn = O(n)$$

This matches the Euler-formula bound. Therefore:
$$\boxed{E[t] = O(n)} \quad \blacksquare \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

**Verification with a concrete case ($n = 3$ horizontal segments):** $V = 8$, $E = 13$, $F = 6$ (5 trapezoids + 1 unbounded). $V - E + F = 1$ ✓. Expected $t = 5 = O(3)$ ✓.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trapezoidal Map & RIC**

- ✅ **Trapezoidal map** $T(S)$: vertical rays shot from every endpoint (up **and** down) until hitting another segment.
- ✅ **Algorithm name:** *Randomized Incremental Construction* (RIC) by Mulmuley, Seidel, etc. (foundational result in computational geometry).
- ✅ **Randomness source:** segments inserted in **uniformly random order** $\sigma$.
- ✅ **Preprocessing cost:** $E[T_{\text{prep}}] = O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ **Query cost:** $E[T_{\text{query}}] = O(\log n)$ via the **search DAG**.
- ✅ **Space:** $E[M] = O(n)$.
- ✅ **#Trapezoids:** $E[t] = O(n)$ — verified by both backwards analysis and Euler's formula $V - E + F = 1 + C$.
- ✅ **Search DAG node types:** **$x$-node** (split by $x$-coordinate) and **$y$-node** (split by a segment's line); leaves are trapezoids.
- ✅ **Backwards analysis key probability:** $P[\text{segment } i \text{ triggers event}] = \frac{k(\Delta)}{i}$ where $k(\Delta) \le 2$.
- ✅ **Harmonic sum:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i} = H_n = O(\log n)$ — appears in both preprocessing and query bounds.
- ✅ **Segments must be non-crossing** for the trapezoidal map to be well-defined as a planar subdivision; otherwise, generalize to *arrangements*.
- ✅ **Euler formula is the integrity check** — always use it to verify counts in exam answers.
- ✅ **DAG is built *for free* during construction** — no separate preprocessing pass is needed.
- ✅ **The "infinite trapezoid" convention** is used by KTU for the unbounded face — remember to count it in $F$.
- ✅ **Cannot use $O(\log^2 n)$ for query time** — common confusion with segment trees; the trapezoidal map is strictly $O(\log n)$ expected.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kirkpatrick's Point Location Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kirkpatrick's Point Location Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Point Location Problem (Formal Definition)**
> Given a planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ with $n$ vertices and a query point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$, the *point location problem* asks for the face (triangle, polygon, or unbounded region) of $\mathcal{S}$ that contains $q$.

**Kirkpatrick's Point Location Algorithm** is a classical solution to this problem that builds a hierarchical search structure from a triangulated planar subdivision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A planar point location method that pre-processes a triangulated subdivision of $n$ vertices into a *hierarchy of $O(\log n)$ levels* of progressively coarser triangulations, achieving a query time of $O(\log n)$ and space $O(n)$.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Preprocessing Time | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Query Time | $O(\log n)$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(n)$ |
| Hierarchy Levels | $O(\log n)$ |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🗺️ "The Multi-Scale Map Book" Analogy**
> Imagine you are looking for a specific shop in a giant city using a *map book with progressive zoom levels*:
> 1. **Level 0 (World Atlas):** You see continents — pick the right country.
> 2. **Level 1 (Country Map):** You zoom into the right state.
> 3. **Level 2 (City Map):** You find the right district.
> 4. **Level 3 (Street Map):** You pinpoint the exact shop.
>
> You never search at full resolution; you *coarsen* the map, find the rough region, then *lift* back to the original detail. This is exactly how Kirkpatrick's algorithm works — it constructs a sequence of coarser triangulations $T_0, T_1, \ldots, T_k$ and answers queries by locating the point in the coarsest level first, then descending.

### 1.3 Why This Matters (Engineering Relevance)

- **GIS Systems:** Real-time map queries (Google Maps, OpenStreetMap)
- **CAD/CAM:** Locating regions in computer-aided design models
- **Computer Graphics:** Ray tracing, hit detection in 3D models
- **Robotics:** Path planning over known terrain maps
- **VLSI Design:** Region queries in circuit layouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Most real subdivisions are *static* (built once, queried millions of times). The $O(n \log n)$ preprocessing cost is amortized — Kirkpatrick's algorithm makes every single query extremely fast.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Concepts

#### (a) Planar Triangulation
A *planar triangulation* of a set of $n$ vertices is a planar subdivision where every face (including the outer face) is a triangle, with **no** extra vertices on the edges.

> **Properties of a Triangulation with $n \geq 3$ vertices:**
> - Number of edges: $e = 3n - 6$ (and $3n_h - 6$ for convex $n$-gon)
> - Sum of vertex degrees: $2e = 6n - 12$
> - Average vertex degree: $< 6$
> - Number of triangles: $2n - 5$ (including outer face)
> - Number of bounded triangles: $n - 2$

#### (b) Independent Set
> [!NOTE]
> **Independent Set Definition**
> An *independent set* $I$ of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a subset of vertices $I \subseteq V$ such that **no two vertices in $I$ are connected by an edge**.

For Kirkpatrick's algorithm, we need an *independent set* of **low-degree** vertices, where the maximum degree is bounded by a small constant $d$.

#### (c) Why Low-Degree Vertices?
When we remove a vertex of degree $d$ from a triangulation, we leave a *polygonal hole* of $d$ edges. This hole can always be re-triangulated by adding $d - 3$ new diagonals (if $d \geq 3$).

---

### 2.2 Algorithm Logic — The Two Phases

#### **Phase 1: Preprocessing (Building the Hierarchy)**

1. **Start** with input triangulation $T_0$ of $n$ vertices.
2. **Repeat** for $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:
   - Find a *bounded-degree independent set* $S_i$ in $T_i$ with $\vert S_i \vert \geq n_i / c$ for some constant $c$.
   - Remove all vertices in $S_i$ from $T_i$.
   - Re-triangulate the resulting polygonal holes to obtain $T_{i+1}$.
   - Build a *mapping* from each triangle of $T_{i+1}$ to the corresponding sub-region in $T_i$.
3. **Terminate** when the triangulation has $O(1)$ vertices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Lemma (Independent Set Existence):**
> In any planar triangulation with $n$ vertices, there exists an independent set $I$ of size at least $n / 6$ such that every vertex in $I$ has degree $\leq 8$. This is because the average degree is $< 6$, so at least $n/2$ vertices have degree $\leq 11$, and an independent set can be extracted from them.

#### **Phase 2: Query**

1. Given a query point $q$.
2. **Brute force** locate $q$ in the top-level triangulation $T_k$ (constant time since $\vert T_k \vert = O(1)$).
3. **For $i = k-1$ down to $0$:**
   - Use the precomputed mapping to find the *region* in $T_i$ that corresponds to the triangle in $T_{i+1}$.
   - Perform a *point-in-triangle test* in $T_i$ (constant time).
4. **Return** the triangle of $T_0$ containing $q$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **🧾 High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Value | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of vertices in input subdivision | Input size |
| $e$ | $3n - 6$ | Edges in a planar triangulation |
| $f$ | $2n - 5$ | Faces (triangles) including outer face |
| Avg degree $\bar{d}$ | $\frac{2e}{n} < 6$ | Average vertex degree in triangulation |
| Hierarchy size | $O(\log n)$ | Number of levels in the search structure |
| Vertex reduction ratio | $n_{i+1} \leq \alpha \cdot n_i$, with $\alpha = 5/6$ | Constant factor reduction per level |
| Independent set lower bound | $\vert S_i \vert \geq n_i / c$, $c \approx 6$ | Size of removed vertices per level |
| Query time per level | $O(1)$ | Constant work per hierarchy level |
| Total query time | $O(\log n)$ | Sum across all hierarchy levels |
| Preprocessing time | $O(n \log n)$ | Geometric sum over all levels |
| Space complexity | $O(n)$ | Total storage for all levels |

> **Note on constants:** The reduction constant $\alpha = (1 - 1/c)$ where $c$ is the independent-set constant. Common textbook choice: $c = 6$, giving $\alpha = 5/6$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Navigation Systems:** GPS apps use point location on road networks to identify which road segment a user is on.
- **Mesh Processing:** Finite element analysis requires locating which triangle of a mesh contains a point.
- **Augmented Reality:** Hit-testing for AR objects requires determining which 3D face is intersected by a ray.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Context:** Kirkpatrick's algorithm excels when:
> - The subdivision is **static** (preprocessing cost is justified).
> - **Many queries** are expected (amortization benefit).
> - **Compact storage** is needed (linear space).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Hierarchy Levels

**Claim:** The number of levels $k$ in the hierarchy satisfies $k = O(\log n)$.

**Setup:** Let $n_i$ denote the number of vertices in $T_i$, with $n_0 = n$. Suppose at each level, the independent set $S_i$ has size at least $n_i / c$ for some constant $c$.

**Step 1: Vertex count recursion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{i+1} &= n_i - \vert S_i \vert \\
&\leq n_i - \frac{n_i}{c} \\
&= n_i \left(1 - \frac{1}{c}\right) \\
&= \alpha \cdot n_i \quad \text{where } \alpha = \left(1 - \frac{1}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **vertex reduction equation** at each hierarchy level.

**Step 2: Unrolling the recursion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &\leq \alpha \cdot n_0 \\
n_2 &\leq \alpha \cdot n_1 \leq \alpha^2 \cdot n_0 \\
n_3 &\leq \alpha \cdot n_2 \leq \alpha^3 \cdot n_0 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
n_k &\leq \alpha^k \cdot n_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Termination condition**

The algorithm terminates when $n_k = O(1)$, i.e., $n_k \leq C$ for some small constant $C$. We need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha^k \cdot n_0 &\leq C \\
\alpha^k &\leq \frac{C}{n_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solving for $k$**

Taking logarithms (with $\alpha < 1$, so $\log \alpha < 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \log \alpha &\leq \log \left(\frac{C}{n_0}\right) = \log C - \log n_0 \\
k &\geq \frac{\log n_0 - \log C}{-\log \alpha} \\
k &\geq \frac{\log n_0}{-\log \alpha} - \frac{\log C}{-\log \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C$ is a constant, the second term is $O(1)$, and since $\log \alpha$ is a negative constant:

$$
\boxed{k = O(\log n)}
$$

**Verification with $c = 6$ ($\alpha = 5/6$):** $-\log(5/6) = \log(6/5) \approx 0.1823$. So $k \geq 5.49 \log n$, i.e., the hierarchy has roughly $5.5 \log_2 n$ levels. Confirms $O(\log n)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Preprocessing Time

**Claim:** Preprocessing takes $O(n \log n)$ time.

**Step 1: Work at level $i$**

The work at level $i$ consists of:
1. Finding the independent set $S_i$ — takes $O(n_i)$ time.
2. Removing the vertices and re-triangulating the holes — the re-triangulation time is bounded by the total degree of removed vertices, which is $O(n_i)$.
3. Building the cross-level mapping — $O(n_i)$ time.

Total work at level $i$: $W_i = O(n_i)$.

**Step 2: Total preprocessing time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{preprocess}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} W_i \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} O(n_i) \\
&= O\left(\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} n_i\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bounding the geometric sum**

Using $n_i \leq \alpha^i \cdot n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} n_i &\leq n \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha^i \\
&= n \cdot \frac{1 - \alpha^k}{1 - \alpha} \\
&\leq \frac{n}{1 - \alpha} \\
&= \frac{n}{1 - (1 - 1/c)} \\
&= c \cdot n
\end{aligned}
$$

The geometric series $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha^i = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}$ is bounded by a constant.

**Step 4: Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{preprocess}} = O(n)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** Some textbooks (and original Kirkpatrick 1983 paper) state $O(n \log n)$ as the preprocessing time, accounting for a less optimized construction. The tighter $O(n)$ bound is achievable with careful implementation. **For KTU 2024 examinations, accept both $O(n)$ and $O(n \log n)$ as correct answers**, but justify the bound using a geometric series argument.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Query Time Complexity

**Claim:** Each query takes $O(\log n)$ time.

**Step 1: Time at level $i$ (descending the hierarchy)**

At each level $i$ (from $k-1$ down to $0$), the query:
1. Looks up the appropriate region in $T_i$ using the cross-level pointer — $O(1)$ (array indexing).
2. Performs a *point-in-triangle* test — $O(1)$ (constant-time barycentric test).

Total per level: $O(1)$.

**Step 2: Total query time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{query}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} O(1) \\
&= k \cdot O(1) \\
&= O(k) \\
&= O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Brute force at the top level**

At the top level $T_k$, we perform a brute-force search over the (constant-sized) triangulation. With $O(1)$ triangles, this takes $O(1)$ time.

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{query}} = O(\log n)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Space Complexity

**Claim:** Total space is $O(n)$.

**Step 1: Space at level $i$**

A triangulation with $n_i$ vertices has $O(n_i)$ triangles. Each triangle stores:
- 3 vertex references
- 1 pointer to the corresponding region in the next-coarser level $T_{i+1}$

Total space at level $i$: $O(n_i)$.

**Step 2: Total space across all levels**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{k} O(n_i) \\
&\leq c \cdot n \quad \text{(from Section 3.2)} \\
&= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = O(n)}
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Constructing the Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Concrete Example:** Consider a convex hexagon (6 vertices) triangulated by diagonals from vertex $v_1$:
> - $T_0$: 6 vertices, 4 triangles
> - Triangles: $(v_1, v_2, v_3), (v_1, v_3, v_4), (v_1, v_4, v_5), (v_1, v_5, v_6)$

**Step 1: Find independent set $S_0$ in $T_0$**

Degrees in $T_0$: $\deg(v_1) = 5$, $\deg(v_2) = \deg(v_6) = 2$, $\deg(v_3) = \deg(v_4) = \deg(v_5) = 3$.

Low-degree independent set: $S_0 = \{v_2, v_4, v_6\}$ (none are adjacent to each other).

**Step 2: Remove $S_0$ and re-triangulate**

After removing $v_2, v_4, v_6$, the remaining vertices are $\{v_1, v_3, v_5\}$. These form a single triangle.

So $T_1$ has 3 vertices and 1 triangle.

**Step 3: Build mapping**

- Triangle $(v_1, v_3, v_5)$ in $T_1$ maps to the region in $T_0$ containing the triangles $(v_1, v_2, v_3), (v_1, v_3, v_4), (v_1, v_4, v_5), (v_1, v_5, v_6)$.

**Step 4: Hierarchy**

$$
T_0 \xrightarrow{\text{remove } v_2, v_4, v_6} T_1
$$

- $T_0$: 6 vertices (4 triangles)
- $T_1$: 3 vertices (1 triangle, terminates)

**Step 5: Query example**

Query point $q = (2.5, 1.5)$. Locate in $T_1$: it's inside $(v_1, v_3, v_5)$. Lift to $T_0$: use the mapping to find that $q$ is in triangle $(v_1, v_3, v_4)$ of $T_0$.

Total query time: $O(1)$ per level $\times$ 2 levels $= O(1)$ for this small example, but in general $O(\log n)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Preprocessing Phase Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start T0 with n vertices] --> B[Find bounded-degree independent set Si]
    B --> C[Remove all vertices in Si from Ti]
    C --> D[Re-triangulate polygonal holes]
    D --> E[Build cross-level pointer map to Ti]
    E --> F{New Ti plus 1 has O1 vertices}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Store hierarchy T0 to Tk as data structure]
    G --> H[End preprocessing]
```

### 4.2 Query Phase Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive query point q] --> B[Brute force search in top level Tk]
    B --> C[Initialize current level to k]
    C --> D{Level equals 0}
    D -->|No| E[Use cross-level pointer to region in Ti]
    E --> F[Perform point-in-triangle test in Ti]
    F --> G[Decrement level to i minus 1]
    G --> D
    D -->|Yes| H[Return triangle in T0 containing q]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy Block Diagram (Data Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL0[Level 0 Original T0]
        T0A[Triangle 1] --> T0B[Triangle 2]
        T0B --> T0C[Triangle 3]
        T0C --> T0D[Triangle 4]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL1[Level 1 T1]
        T1A[Triangle A]
        T1B[Triangle B]
    end
    subgraph LEVELK[Level k Tk top]
        TK1[Single Triangle Top]
    end
    T0A -.-> T1A
    T0B -.-> T1A
    T0C -.-> T1B
    T0D -.-> T1B
    T1A -.-> TK1
    T1B -.-> TK1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows within a level show triangle adjacency. Dashed arrows show the cross-level mapping — each triangle in the *coarser* level $T_{i+1}$ points to the sub-region in $T_i$ that it represents.

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Point Location Query Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Query Point q] --> S1[Stage 1: Brute Force Top Level Tk]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2: Pointer Lookup Cross Level Map]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Point in Triangle Test]
    S3 --> S4{Level 0 Reached}
    S4 -->|No| S2
    S4 -->|Yes| R[Return Triangle of T0]
    S1 -.->|Constant Time| S2
    S2 -.->|O1 per level| S3
    S3 -.->|O1 per level| S4
```

> **Key Reading:** The pipeline runs in *logarithmic stages*; each stage is $O(1)$, total $O(\log n)$.

### 4.5 Independent Set Removal Process (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Original Triangulation T0 with n vertices] --> P1[Mark low-degree vertices degree at most 8]
    P1 --> P2[Build maximal independent set from marks]
    P2 --> P3[Remove selected vertices]
    P3 --> P4[Triangulate resulting polygonal holes]
    P4 --> P5[New Triangulation T1 with fewer vertices]
    P5 --> P6{Size is constant}
    P6 -->|No| P1
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Terminate Build Hierarchy]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *Define the point location problem. State the query and space complexity of Kirkpatrick's point location algorithm.* `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Point Location Problem:** Given a planar subdivision $\mathcal{S}$ with $n$ vertices and a query point $q \in \mathbb{R}^2$, the point location problem asks for the face (triangle) of $\mathcal{S}$ that contains $q$.
>
> **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm Complexity:**
> - Query time: $O(\log n)$
> - Space: $O(n)$
> - Preprocessing time: $O(n \log n)$

---

> **Q2.** *What is an independent set in a graph? Why is the choice of an independent set important in Kirkpatrick's algorithm?* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Independent Set:** A subset $I$ of vertices of a graph $G$ such that no two vertices in $I$ are connected by an edge.
>
> **Importance in Kirkpatrick's Algorithm:**
> 1. Removing an independent set ensures no two removed vertices are adjacent, so the resulting holes are non-overlapping and can be re-triangulated independently.
> 2. It guarantees that a *constant fraction* of vertices is removed at each level, ensuring $O(\log n)$ hierarchy depth.
> 3. It bounds the complexity of the cross-level mapping.

---

### 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Q.** *Explain Kirkpatrick's point location algorithm in detail. Discuss:*
> *(a) The preprocessing phase — construction of the hierarchy with the role of independent set and re-triangulation. (7 marks)*
> *(b) The query phase — locate a point using the hierarchical structure with time complexity analysis. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Preprocessing Phase (7 marks):**

1. **Input:** A planar subdivision represented as a triangulation $T_0$ with $n$ vertices.

2. **Iterative Construction:** Build a sequence of triangulations $T_0, T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$ where each $T_{i+1}$ is coarser than $T_i$.

3. **Step 1 — Find Independent Set:** At each level $i$, identify a *bounded-degree independent set* $S_i$ in $T_i$. The set must satisfy:
   - Every vertex in $S_i$ has degree $\leq d$ (a small constant, typically 8).
   - $\vert S_i \vert \geq n_i / c$ for some constant $c$ (e.g., $c = 6$).
   - No two vertices in $S_i$ are adjacent.

4. **Step 2 — Remove and Re-triangulate:** Delete all vertices in $S_i$ from $T_i$. The removal creates polygonal holes. Re-triangulate each hole to obtain $T_{i+1}$.

5. **Step 3 — Cross-Level Mapping:** For each triangle in $T_{i+1}$, store a pointer to the corresponding sub-region (set of triangles) in $T_i$.

6. **Step 4 — Termination:** Stop when the triangulation has $O(1)$ vertices (typically 3 or 4).

7. **Data Structure:** The final structure is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of $k = O(\log n)$ levels.

**(b) Query Phase and Complexity (7 marks):**

1. **Input:** A query point $q$ and the precomputed hierarchy.

2. **Top-Level Search:** Brute-force locate $q$ in $T_k$. Since $T_k$ has $O(1)$ triangles, this takes $O(1)$ time.

3. **Descent:** For $i = k-1$ down to $0$:
   - Use the cross-level pointer from the current triangle in $T_{i+1}$ to find the corresponding region in $T_i$.
   - Perform a *point-in-triangle* test (barycentric coordinates) to locate $q$ in $T_i$. Takes $O(1)$ time.

4. **Output:** The triangle in $T_0$ containing $q$ — this is the final answer.

5. **Time Complexity Analysis:**
   - Work per level: $O(1)$.
   - Number of levels: $k = O(\log n)$ (derived from the geometric series).
   - Total query time: $O(1) \times O(\log n) = O(\log n)$.

6. **Space Complexity:** The total space is the sum of triangle storage across all levels, which is $O(n)$ (geometric sum of $n_i$ values).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

> **Q.** *(a) Define a planar triangulation and explain the role of bounded-degree independent set selection in Kirkpatrick's algorithm. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Compare Kirkpatrick's algorithm with the Slab Decomposition method for point location. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Planar Triangulation and Independent Set (7 marks):**

**Planar Triangulation Definition:**
A planar triangulation of a set $V$ of $n$ points in the plane is a maximal planar subdivision whose vertex set is exactly $V$. Every face, including the outer face, is a triangle. For $n \geq 3$, it has $3n - 6$ edges and $2n - 5$ faces (including the outer face).

**Properties:**
- $e = 3n - 6$
- Sum of degrees $= 6n - 12$
- Average degree $< 6$
- Bounded triangles $= n - 2$

**Role of Bounded-Degree Independent Set:**

1. **Constant Reduction:** A bounded-degree independent set $S_i$ of size $\geq n_i / c$ ensures that the *vertex count reduces by a constant factor* at each level. This is the foundation of the $O(\log n)$ hierarchy depth.

2. **Re-Triangulation Feasibility:** If a removed vertex has degree $d$, the resulting polygonal hole has $d$ sides, which can be re-triangulated with $d - 3$ new diagonals. By restricting to low-degree vertices, the re-triangulation work per hole is bounded by a constant.

3. **Independent Set Property:** Independence ensures that the polygonal holes do not share edges, so re-triangulation is local and independent for each hole.

4. **Existence Lemma:** In any planar triangulation, an independent set of size at least $n/6$ with max degree $\leq 8$ can be found in $O(n)$ time using a greedy approach.

**(b) Comparison with Slab Decomposition (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Kirkpatrick's Algorithm | Slab Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Triangulated subdivision | General planar subdivision |
| **Query Time** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Preprocessing** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Persistent Search** | No | Yes (uses persistent BST) |
| **Subdivision Modification** | Requires triangulation | Requires adding vertical slabs (modifies input) |
| **Query Mechanism** | Hierarchy descent | Binary search on slabs + tree search |
| **Implementation** | More complex (independent set) | Simpler (persistent trees) |
| **Best For** | Static triangulations, GIS | General subdivisions, dynamic updates |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Slab Decomposition** partitions the bounding box into vertical slabs and uses persistent search trees. It modifies the subdivision by adding $O(n)$ vertical line segments (slabs).

2. **Kirkpatrick's Algorithm** does not modify the input subdivision; instead, it builds a hierarchy of coarser versions. It is purely structural.

3. **Slab Decomposition** handles arbitrary planar subdivisions, while Kirkpatrick's requires a triangulation as input (any planar subdivision can be pre-triangulated).

4. **Kirkpatrick's** has a smaller constant in practice for highly-triangulated inputs.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the directions of the cross-level pointer:** The pointer goes from a *coarser* triangle in $T_{i+1}$ to a *finer* region in $T_i$ — **not** the other way around. Many students draw this backward.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the brute-force top-level search:** Students often jump straight from the top level to descent, forgetting that the top level is small enough for brute force. This is a 1-2 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Claiming $O(\log n)$ without deriving it:** The derivation using the geometric series $\alpha^k \cdot n = O(1) \implies k = O(\log n)$ is **essential**. Stating the bound without justification will cost 2-3 marks.
>
> 4. **Mixing up query time and preprocessing time:** Kirkpatrick's preprocessing is $O(n \log n)$, query is $O(\log n)$. Swapping these is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Incorrect independent set bound:** Stating the independent set size as "exactly $n/2$" or "at most $n/6$" — the correct statement is "*at least* $n/6$."
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the re-triangulation step:** The algorithm is incomplete without describing how polygonal holes are re-triangulated.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **🔑 Core Idea:** Build a hierarchy of progressively coarser triangulations; query by descending from the coarsest to the finest.
- **📊 Time Complexities:** Preprocessing = $O(n \log n)$, Query = $O(\log n)$, Space = $O(n)$.
- **🏗️ Hierarchy Depth:** $k = O(\log n)$ levels, with vertex count reducing by a constant factor $\alpha = (1 - 1/c)$ per level.
- **📐 Independent Set Property:** $I \subseteq V$ such that no two vertices in $I$ are adjacent; size $\geq n/6$ with max degree $\leq 8$.
- **🔄 Re-Triangulation:** Removing a degree-$d$ vertex creates a $d$-sided hole; re-triangulated with $d - 3$ new diagonals.
- **🔗 Cross-Level Mapping:** Each triangle in $T_{i+1}$ points to its corresponding sub-region in $T_i$.
- **🎯 Query Process:** Brute force at top (constant time) → descend level by level (constant time per level) → output.
- **⚖️ Comparison:** Slab method works on general subdivisions; Kirkpatrick's requires a triangulation.
- **📈 Vertex Count Formula:** $n_{i+1} \leq (1 - 1/c) \cdot n_i$, leading to $n_k \leq n(1 - 1/c)^k$.
- **🧮 Geometric Sum Trick:** $\sum_{i=0}^{k} n_i \leq cn$ — the key to $O(n)$ space and $O(n \log n)$ preprocessing.
- **✅ Year of Algorithm:** Published by David Kirkpatrick in 1983.
- **🏆 Advantages:** Optimal query time, linear space, no subdivision modification needed.
- **❌ Disadvantages:** Requires triangulation as input, complex preprocessing, large constants in practice.
- **🎨 Engineering Uses:** GIS, CAD, computer graphics, robotics, VLSI design.

> **🎓 Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain Kirkpatrick's algorithm," always structure your answer in **three parts**: (1) the preprocessing/hierarchy construction, (2) the query descent procedure, and (3) the complexity analysis with the geometric-series derivation. Examiners reward systematic, well-structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary Space Partitioning - Definition and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Space Partitioning - Definition and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)** is a recursive, hierarchical method of subdividing a $d$-dimensional Euclidean space into two half-spaces (or convex subspaces) by means of a **hyperplane** $\mathcal{H}$. The subdivision is applied recursively to each resulting half-space, generating a binary tree data structure known as a **BSP Tree**.

Formally, given a set of geometric objects $S$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$:

$$T(S, \mathcal{H}) = \begin{cases} \text{Leaf}(S) & \text{if } |S| \leq 1 \text{ or termination criterion met} \\ \langle \mathcal{H},\, T(S^-,\, \mathcal{H}),\, T(S^+,\, \mathcal{H}) \rangle & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{H}$ is the **splitting hyperplane** (a line in 2D, a plane in 3D).
- $S^- = \{ s \in S \mid s \text{ lies in the negative half-space of } \mathcal{H} \}$
- $S^+ = \{ s \in S \mid s \text{ lies in the positive half-space of } \mathcal{H} \}$
- $S^= = \{ s \in S \mid s \text{ intersects } \mathcal{H} \}$ are the **coplanar** or **spanning** objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST418 / Module 3):** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates the study of BSP under *Hierarchical Spatial Decomposition Structures*. Students must demonstrate understanding of (i) the recursive partitioning mechanism, (ii) splitting heuristics, and (iii) classic graphics applications such as **hidden surface removal** and **ray tracing acceleration**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a massive library with millions of books and you need to find one quickly:

- **Step 1:** You split the entire library into two halves by a giant wall running North-South. All books with titles starting with A–M go Left; N–Z go Right.
- **Step 2:** You pick the Left half and again split it with a wall running East-West. Top half → hardcovers, Bottom half → paperbacks.
- **Step 3:** You keep splitting each region with **straight flat walls** (planes) until each tiny room contains at most one book.

This is **exactly what BSP does in geometric space** — it uses flat hyperplanes instead of physical walls and recursively bisects space until each region contains at most one primitive (polygon, point, or object). The set of all splitting walls forms a **binary tree**, which lets you answer spatial queries (visibility, intersection, nearest neighbour) in logarithmic time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Every internal node of a BSP tree is a *decision surface* (hyperplane), and every leaf node is a *single object or empty region*. The traversal path from the root to any leaf describes exactly **which sequence of half-spaces** that leaf lives in.

### 1.3 Key Parameters and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Space dimensionality | $d$ | $\mathbf{2}$ or $\mathbf{3}$ |
| Splitting hyperplane | $\mathcal{H}$ | Defined by $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c = 0$ |
| Splitting heuristic | $h$ | Auto-partition / Minimal / Balanced |
| Tree height | $h_T$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ average, $\Theta(n)$ worst-case |
| Object count per leaf | $k$ | Typically $k \le 1$ for BSP |
| Front/back test cost | $C_f$ | $\mathbf{O(d)}$ per primitive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Structured Logic

The BSP construction algorithm operates through five rigorous phases:

- **Phase 1 — Primitive Selection:** Choose a subset of primitives (or an associated splitting plane) from the input set $S$ that will define the next partitioning hyperplane $\mathcal{H}$.

- **Phase 2 — Front/Back/Spanning Classification:** For every primitive $p_i \in S$, evaluate the signed distance of each of its defining vertices to $\mathcal{H}$. The sign of the dot product $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + c$ determines membership:
  * **Positive (front):** all vertices yield $+$ → primitive lies entirely in $S^+$.
  * **Negative (back):** all vertices yield $-$ → primitive lies entirely in $S^-$.
  * **Mixed (spanning):** sign changes occur → primitive crosses $\mathcal{H}$ and must be **split** into two new primitives at the intersection points.

- **Phase 3 — Spanning Splitting:** A spanning polygon is cut along the intersection line (in 2D) or plane (in 3D). The resulting fragments are redistributed into $S^-$ and $S^+$.

- **Phase 4 — Recursive Sub-Tree Construction:** Recursively invoke the BSP procedure on $S^-$ and $S^+$ independently, building the left and right subtrees.

- **Phase 5 — Termination:** The recursion stops when a region contains at most $k$ primitives (commonly $k = 1$) or when a pre-set depth limit is reached.

### 2.2 Splitting Heuristics — The Core Decision

The **quality** of a BSP tree is determined by the splitting heuristic $h$. KTU examiners frequently test these:

- **Auto-Partition (Polygon-Aligned):** Use one of the input polygon's planes as the splitter. Produces **zero spanning fragments** at the chosen node but may still split other polygons.
- **Balanced Partition:** Choose $\mathcal{H}$ that minimizes $\big| \vert S^+ \vert - \vert S^- \vert \big|$. Yields shortest trees on average.
- **Minimal Splitting (Axis-Aligned):** Pick the hyperplane that minimizes the number of **new** spanning primitives produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Auto-partitioning is the gold standard in real-time graphics (Doom/Quake engines) because it produces **convex subspaces** without producing new polygons at the splitting node — critical for fast rendering.

### 2.3 The Why and The How

| Aspect | Reason | Mechanism |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| Why use BSP? | $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ average-case spatial query | Hierarchical binary decomposition |
| Why auto-partition? | Avoids new polygon creation at internal nodes | Uses existing polygon planes as splitters |
| Why front/back test? | Determines which subtree receives each primitive | Signed-distance evaluation |
| Why spanning split? | Preserves geometric correctness across cells | Edge-intersection plane cuts |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Splitting Hyperplane | $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c = 0$ | $\mathbf{n}$: unit normal, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $c \in \mathbb{R}$ | Defines the bisector |
| 2 | Signed Distance Test | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c$ | $f(\mathbf{x}) > 0$: front; $f(\mathbf{x}) < 0$: back | Per vertex evaluation |
| 3 | Recurrence (Construction) | $T(n) = T(\alpha n) + T((1-\alpha)n) + \mathbf{O}(n)$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$, $n = \vert S \vert$ | Average $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$; worst $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$ |
| 4 | Tree Height | $h_T = \mathbf{O}(\log n)$ (avg), $\mathbf{O}(n)$ (worst) | Dimensionless | Depends on balance |
| 5 | Query Cost | $\mathbf{O}(\log n + k)$ | $k$: results reported | Point location, ray traversal |
| 6 | Storage | $\mathbf{O}(n)$ nodes | Includes split fragments | May exceed input size for naive splits |
| 7 | Intersection Test (2D) | $\lambda = \frac{-(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p}_0 + c)}{\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{p}_1 - \mathbf{p}_0)}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ for segment hit | Used in spanning split |
| 8 | BSP Visibility Order | DFS traversal where back-to-front = front subtree last | Back-to-front or front-to-back painter's algorithm | Hidden surface removal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention:** All lengths are in user-defined scene units; $\mathbf{n}$ is dimensionless when the hyperplane equation is normalized such that $\vert \mathbf{n} \vert_2 = 1$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Video Game Engines (Doom, Quake, Half-Life):** Static-level BSP for sub-frame visibility ordering and collision pre-filtering.
- **CAD/CAM Systems:** Boolean operations on solid models — BSP accelerates set-theoretic CSG evaluation.
- **Robotics & Motion Planning:** Rapid collision-free path queries in configuration space.
- **Virtual Reality:** Per-frame occlusion culling reducing GPU overdraw by up to **70–90%** in indoor scenes.
- **Ray Tracing Accelerators:** BSP/kd-tree hybrids are the de-facto acceleration structure since the 2000s.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Complexity of BSP Construction

We derive the asymptotic cost $T(n)$ of building a BSP tree on $n$ input primitives.

Let $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ be the fraction of primitives classified as **front** after splitting; the remaining $(1-\alpha)n$ are **back** (spanning primitives are split and counted in both halves).

**Step 1 — Cost of Classification:** Each primitive must be tested against the hyperplane $\mathcal{H}$. For a polygon with $v$ vertices, this is $\mathbf{O}(v)$, summed across all primitives gives $\mathbf{O}(n)$ per node (assuming average $v$ is bounded by a constant).

**Step 2 — Recursive Cost:** Two recursive sub-calls of sizes $\alpha n$ and $(1-\alpha)n$.

**Step 3 — Master Recurrence Assembly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(\alpha n) + T\big((1-\alpha)n\big) + \mathbf{O}(n) \\
T(0) &= T(1) = \mathbf{O}(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve Using Akra–Bazzi Method (Smooth Case):** The generalized Akra–Bazzi theorem states:

$$
T(n) = \mathbf{O}\!\left(n^{p} \left(1 + \int_{1}^{n} \frac{g(u)}{u^{p+1}} \, du \right)\right)
$$

where $p$ satisfies $\alpha^{p} + (1-\alpha)^{p} = 1$. For $\alpha = 1/2$ (perfectly balanced), $p = 1$. Substituting $g(u) = u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \mathbf{O}\!\left(n^{1}\left(1 + \int_{1}^{n} \frac{u}{u^{2}} \, du \right)\right) \\
