&= \mathbf{O}\!\left(n \left(1 + \int_{1}^{n} u^{-1} \, du \right)\right) \\
&= \mathbf{O}\!\left(n (1 + \ln n)\right) \\
&= \mathbf{O}(n \log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worst-Case Bound:** If $\alpha \to 0$ (highly unbalanced, all primitives on one side except a single splitter), the recurrence becomes $T(n) = T(1) + T(n-1) + \mathbf{O}(n)$, which solves to:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \mathbf{O}(k) = \mathbf{O}(n^2)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result Box:** For balanced BSP trees, construction cost is $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$; for pathological inputs, it degrades to $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$. Practical implementations use random or surface-area heuristics to maintain the balanced regime.

### 3.2 Derivation: Query Complexity for Point Location

A point $\mathbf{q}$ is located in a BSP tree of height $h_T$ by descending from the root. At each internal node, we evaluate $f(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{q} + c$ and recurse into the appropriate subtree.

**Step 1 — Per-Level Cost:** A single signed-distance evaluation is $\mathbf{O}(d)$ (where $d$ is dimension; $d=2$ or $d=3$).

**Step 2 — Number of Levels Traversed:** In a balanced tree of $n$ leaves, $h_T = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.

**Step 3 — Total Query Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(n) &= h_T \cdot \mathbf{O}(d) \\
&= \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \cdot \mathbf{O}(d) \\
&= \mathbf{O}(d \log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

For fixed $d$ (i.e., 2D or 3D), this reduces to $\mathbf{O}(\log n)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Back-to-Front Traversal for Painter's Algorithm

For hidden surface removal, a BSP tree provides a strict total ordering of polygons from the viewer's position $\mathbf{e}$.

**Step 1 — Sign Test at Viewpoint:** Evaluate $f(\mathbf{e}) = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e} + c$.

**Step 2 — Recursive Ordering Rule:**

$$
\text{Order}(\text{Node}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Order}(\text{NegSubtree}),\; \text{Polygons},\; \text{Order}(\text{PosSubtree}) & \text{if } f(\mathbf{e}) > 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Order}(\text{PosSubtree}),\; \text{Polygons},\; \text{Order}(\text{NegSubtree}) & \text{if } f(\mathbf{e}) < 0
\end{cases}
$$

This yields polygons in strictly **far-to-near** order, allowing back-to-front rendering without a $z$-buffer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works:** Every BSP split guarantees that all polygons in the **far subtree** are entirely on the far side of every polygon in the near subtree — so painting far-first never overdraws incorrect pixels.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — BSP Variants Comparison

| Variant | Splitting Strategy | Tree Balance | Split Fragments | Use Case |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|------------------|----------|
| Auto-Partition BSP | One polygon's plane | Moderate | Some | Real-time games |
| Balanced BSP | Minimize $\vert S^+ \vert - \vert S^- \vert \vert$ | Excellent | Many | CAD, queries |
| Minimal BSP | Minimize new fragments | Moderate | Minimal | Real-time games |
| kd-Tree (special BSP) | Axis-aligned, median-cut | Excellent | None | Ray tracing |
| Randomized BSP | Random primitive selected | Expected $\log n$ | Expected few | Robustness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BSP Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Primitive Set S] --> B[Select Splitting Hyperplane H]
    B --> C[Classify Primitives]
    C --> D{Front / Back / Spanning}
    D -->|Front| E[Add to Positive Set S+]
    D -->|Back| F[Add to Negative Set S-]
    D -->|Spanning| G[Split Primitive at Intersection]
    G --> E
    G --> F
    E --> H{Is S+ Empty or k le 1?}
    F --> I{Is S- Empty or k le 1?}
    H -->|Yes| J[Create Leaf Node L+]
    H -->|No| K[Recursive BSP Build on S+]
    K --> L[Left Subtree of Node N]
    I -->|Yes| M[Create Leaf Node L-]
    I -->|No| N2[Recursive BSP Build on S-]
    N2 --> O[Right Subtree of Node N]
    L --> P[Assemble BSP Node N]
    O --> P
    J --> P
    M --> P
    P --> Q[Return Root of BSP Tree]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Tree Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Root H1: n1 x + c1 = 0] --> node2[Left Subtree H2]
    node1 --> node3[Right Subtree H3]
    node2 --> node4[Leaf Polygon P_A]
    node2 --> node5[Leaf Polygon P_B]
    node3 --> node6[Leaf Polygon P_C]
    node3 --> node7[Leaf Polygon P_D]
    node4 --> leafEnd1[STOP]
    node5 --> leafEnd2[STOP]
    node6 --> leafEnd3[STOP]
    node7 --> leafEnd4[STOP]
```

### 4.3 Application-Level Flow — Hidden Surface Removal via Painter's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene with Polygons] --> B[Preprocess: Build BSP Tree]
    B --> C[Input Viewer Position e]
    C --> D[Evaluate f at e for Root H]
    D --> E{Sign of f at e}
    E -->|Positive| F[Traverse Negative Subtree First]
    E -->|Negative| G[Traverse Positive Subtree First]
    F --> H[Render Far Polygons]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Render Polygons at Current Node]
    I --> J[Render Near Polygons]
    J --> K[Final Framebuffer Image]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block in the flowchart corresponds to one phase of the BSP algorithm described in **Section 2.1**. The recursive descent blocks (`Recursive BSP Build on S+` and `Recursive BSP Build on S-`) mirror the mathematical recurrence of **Section 3.1**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)**. Mention any **two applications** of BSP in computer graphics.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) is a hierarchical spatial subdivision technique that recursively divides a $d$-dimensional space into two convex half-spaces using a hyperplane $\mathcal{H}$ defined by $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c = 0$. The resulting structure is a binary tree where each internal node stores the splitting hyperplane, and each leaf stores one or more primitives.

**Two Applications (1.5 Marks each):**

1. **Hidden Surface Removal** — BSP enables back-to-front ordering of polygons via the Painter's Algorithm, eliminating the need for a $z$-buffer in static scenes.
2. **Ray Tracing Acceleration** — BSP (and its derivative kd-tree) accelerates ray-primitive intersection tests by $\mathbf{O}(\log n)$ traversal, drastically reducing per-pixel cost.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

What is the role of a **splitting hyperplane** in BSP tree construction? How are spanning polygons handled?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of Splitting Hyperplane (1.5 Marks):**
The splitting hyperplane $\mathcal{H}$ acts as the decision boundary that partitions the current cell of space into two half-spaces — positive ($\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c > 0$) and negative ($\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c < 0$). It is the *criterion* that routes each primitive into the left (negative) or right (positive) subtree of the BSP node.

**Spanning Polygon Handling (1.5 Marks):**
A spanning polygon crosses $\mathcal{H}$, i.e., some of its vertices satisfy $f(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ while others satisfy $f(\mathbf{x}) < 0$. It is **cut along the line of intersection** with $\mathcal{H}$, producing two new polygons. Each fragment is assigned to the appropriate half-space subtree, preserving geometric fidelity at the cost of increasing the primitive count.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step algorithm for constructing a BSP tree** for a set of 2D line segments. Use a labelled diagram to illustrate the process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **time complexity** of BSP tree construction in both best-case and worst-case scenarios. Provide the recurrence relations and their closed-form solutions. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — BSP Construction Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Given a set $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ of 2D line segments in the plane, select a splitting line $\mathcal{L}$ as the first hyperplane. This line is either an extension of an input segment (auto-partition) or an axis-aligned separator.

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** For each segment $s_i$, perform the **front/back classification** by evaluating the signed distance of its two endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ with respect to $\mathcal{L}$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = a \cdot x + b \cdot y + c
$$

A segment is classified as:
- **Front** if both endpoints give $f > 0$,
- **Back** if both endpoints give $f < 0$,
- **Spanning** if the signs differ.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** For each spanning segment, compute the intersection point with $\mathcal{L}$ using:

$$
\lambda = \frac{-(a \cdot x_1 + b \cdot y_1 + c)}{a(x_2 - x_1) + b(y_2 - y_1)}
$$

The segment is cut into two sub-segments at the point $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}_1 + \lambda (\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1)$, each routed to the appropriate subtree.

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Recursively invoke the construction on the **front** and **back** sub-sets. The recursion terminates when a sub-set is empty or contains a single segment.

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Assemble the BSP tree with the splitting line at the root and the recursively built subtrees as children. **Illustrative Labelled Diagram:**

```
         Level 0: Root Splitter L1
                 |
        -----------------
        |               |
   Level 1: L2     Level 1: L3
     |   |           |   |
   P_A P_B         P_C P_D
```

Here $P_A, P_B, P_C, P_D$ are leaf segments and $L_1, L_2, L_3$ are splitting lines.

#### Part (b) — Time Complexity Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the recurrence:

$$
T(n) = T(\alpha n) + T\big((1-\alpha)n\big) + \mathbf{O}(n)
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** **Balanced case** ($\alpha = 1/2$):

$$
T(n) = 2\,T(n/2) + \mathbf{O}(n) \;\Longrightarrow\; T(n) = \mathbf{O}(n \log n)
$$

This is solved via the Master Theorem with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$, giving $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = f(n)$, hence Case 2 applies: $T(n) = \mathbf{O}(n \log n)$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** **Worst case** ($\alpha \to 0$):

$$
T(n) = T(1) + T(n-1) + \mathbf{O}(n)
$$

Telescoping the sum:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \mathbf{O}(n^2)
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Conclude: BSP construction is efficient ($\mathbf{O}(n \log n)$) for balanced splits but degrades to $\mathbf{O}(n^2)$ on pathological inputs. Practical mitigations include random selection of the splitter and surface-area heuristics (in kd-trees).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the use of **BSP trees for hidden surface removal** in 3D graphics. Explain the back-to-front ordering using the Painter's Algorithm with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **BSP trees with BVH (Bounding Volume Hierarchies)** in terms of construction cost, query efficiency, and update support. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — BSP for Hidden Surface Removal (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** In a static 3D scene, BSP pre-processing builds a tree where each polygon of the scene becomes (or contributes to) a splitting plane. After construction, the tree encodes a **complete spatial ordering** of polygons.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** At render time, the viewer position $\mathbf{e}$ is evaluated against the root splitter $\mathcal{H}$:

- If $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e} + c > 0$, the **negative half-space is farther** from the viewer; recurse into negative subtree first, render its polygons, then render root polygon, then recurse into positive subtree.
- If $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e} + c < 0$, the order is reversed.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** **Example:** Suppose a room has a wall in front and a pillar behind. The BSP root is the wall plane. From a viewpoint in front of the wall, $f(\mathbf{e}) > 0$, so the algorithm first renders the pillar (which lies in the negative half-space relative to the wall, i.e., behind it), then the wall. The pillar is overwritten correctly by the wall — producing a correct image **without a $z$-buffer**.

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** This back-to-front order is **strict and total** for any convex BSP subdivision, ensuring no overdraw artifacts when polygons are painted in the computed sequence.

#### Part (b) — BSP vs. BVH Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (3 Marks):** Tabulated comparison:

| Criterion | BSP Tree | BVH |
|-----------|----------|-----|
| Construction | $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ avg, $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$ worst | $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ via SAH heuristic |
| Query (ray/object) | $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ average | $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ with SAH |
| Static vs. Dynamic | **Static** (rebuild on edits) | Supports **incremental updates** |
| Splitting planes | Arbitrary (often polygon-aligned) | Axis-aligned bounding boxes |
| Memory | Higher (split fragments) | Lower |

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** **BSP Advantages:** (i) Provably correct back-to-front order; (ii) Supports Boolean CSG operations natively. **BSP Disadvantages:** (i) Slow rebuilds for dynamic scenes; (ii) Worst-case $\mathbf{O}(n^2)$ construction.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** **BVH Advantages:** (i) Fast incremental updates (refit or rebuild); (ii) Lower memory footprint. **BVH Disadvantages:** (i) Only an approximate ordering; (ii) Less effective for CSG operations.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting spanning-polygon handling (–2 marks):** Many students only describe front/back classification but skip the spanning case — examiners explicitly deduct marks.
> 2. **Confusing BSP with kd-Tree (–2 marks):** A kd-tree is a *restricted* BSP using axis-aligned splits; full BSP allows arbitrary splitting planes.
> 3. **Omitting complexity derivations (–3 marks):** For Part B (b)-style questions, the recurrence *and* its closed-form solution are both required; stating only the result loses half marks.
> 4. **Missing diagram labels (–1 mark):** Every BSP tree diagram in the answer script **must label both the splitter lines and the leaf polygons**. Unlabelled diagrams fetch only partial credit.
> 5. **Painter's Algorithm misuse (–2 marks):** BSP guarantees ordering **only for static, convex, planar subdivisions**; do not claim correctness for transparent or non-planar polygons.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** BSP recursively subdivides $\mathbb{R}^d$ using hyperplanes into a binary tree of convex cells.
- **Splitting Hyperplane:** $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c = 0$ where $\mathbf{n}$ is the unit normal.
- **Classification Rule:** Sign of $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} + c$ at each primitive vertex decides front/back/spanning.
- **Spanning Polygons:** Must be split along the intersection with $\mathcal{H}$ before redistribution.
- **Three Key Heuristics:** Auto-partition, Balanced, Minimal — choose based on application.
- **Construction Cost:** $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ balanced; $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$ worst case.
- **Query Cost:** $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ average for point location, ray traversal, and visibility ordering.
- **Back-to-Front Order:** Painter's Algorithm via BSP — guarantees no overdraw artifacts in static scenes.
- **Real-Time Usage:** Doom, Quake engines use auto-partition BSP for fast per-frame visibility.
- **Modern Variant:** kd-tree is a restricted BSP with axis-aligned splits — dominant in ray tracing.
- **Limitation:** BSP is **static** — dynamic scenes require rebuild, favouring BVH instead.
- **Engineering Impact:** BSP underpins collision detection in robotics, CSG operations in CAD, and GPU occlusion culling in VR.
- **KTU-Favourite Topics:** Always expect a question combining (i) BSP construction algorithm, (ii) spanning-polygon handling, and (iii) time-complexity recurrence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BSP Trees Construction and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BSP Trees Construction and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) Tree Definition**
> A **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) tree** is a hierarchical data structure in computational geometry that recursively subdivides a $d$-dimensional space (typically $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$) into convex subspaces (called **cells**) using $(d-1)$-dimensional **splitting hyperplanes** (lines in 2D, planes in 3D). Each internal node of the tree represents a splitting hyperplane, while each leaf node represents a single convex region (a cell) that contains no further splitting hyperplane.

**Formal Recursive Definition (Inductive Form):**
A BSP tree $\mathcal{T}$ for a finite set of line segments (or polygons) $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a binary tree defined as:

- **Base Case:** If $|S| = 0$ or $|S| = 1$, the tree is a single leaf node containing the empty set or the singleton.
- **Recursive Case:** Choose a splitting line $L$. Partition $S$ into $S^+$ (segments strictly on the positive side of $L$), $S^-$ (segments strictly on the negative side), and $S^L$ (segments lying on $L$). Create a node labeled with $L$, with left child = BSP($\text{co}(S^+)$) and right child = BSP($\text{co}(S^-)$). Segments in $S^L$ are stored as **co-planar** at this node.

where $\text{co}(\cdot)$ denotes the convex region on the respective side of $L$ (clipped to the region).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Cutting a Birthday Cake in 3D"**
> Imagine a 3D room with floating walls inside it. Each wall you place divides the room into two halves — a "left" half and a "right" half. Now in each of those halves, you place another wall that further divides just that half. You keep doing this recursively. The final structure of "walls within walls" forms a **BSP tree**:
> - Each wall = an **internal node** (splitting plane).
> - Each final "compartment" you cannot divide further = a **leaf node** (convex cell).
> - The whole hierarchy = the **tree structure**.

A simpler 2D analogy: take a sheet of paper, draw a line across it (this is the **root**), then in each half draw another line (these are the **children**), and so on. At every step, each line partitions the existing regions into exactly two convex sub-regions.

## 1.3 Historical & Practical Context

The BSP tree was independently conceived by **Fuchs, Kedem, and Naylor (1980)** at the University of Rochester for hidden-surface removal, and by **Saade (1980)** for similar purposes. Today, BSP trees are used in:

- **Computer graphics:** Hidden surface removal (Painter's Algorithm), ray tracing acceleration, visibility culling.
- **Video game engines:** Quake/Doom engines famously used 2D BSP trees for level rendering.
- **CAD / Solid modeling:** Boolean operations on polyhedra.
- **Robotics & motion planning:** Free-space decomposition.
- **Collision detection:** Narrow phase tests in physics engines.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSP Tree vs. Other Spatial Structures (Exam-Favorite Comparison)**
>
> | Feature | BSP Tree | k-d Tree | Quad/Octree | R-Tree |
> |---------|----------|----------|-------------|--------|
> | Splitting | Arbitrary hyperplanes | Axis-aligned, alternating | Axis-aligned, equal | Bounding-box groups |
> | Balanced? | Not necessarily | Yes (if built well) | Yes (fixed depth) | Yes (rebalancing) |
> | Data stored | Geometry (segments/polys) | Points | Points/regions | Bounding boxes |
> | Best for | Hidden surface removal | Point queries | Spatial indexing | Spatial indexing |
> | Preserves geometry? | **Yes** | No | No | No |

The key distinguishing property of BSP trees is that **they preserve the original geometric objects (segments/polygons) as part of the tree structure**, rather than discarding them after partitioning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A BSP tree is **not** a data structure for storing points; it is a data structure for **partitioning space using the actual geometric objects** present in the scene. The construction proceeds as follows:

### Step 1 — Choose a Splitting Hyperplane
Select a line (in 2D) or plane (in 3D) from the set of input segments/polygons. Each input segment defines a candidate splitting line (its supporting line). The choice of *which* line to pick governs the **shape** and **efficiency** of the resulting tree.

### Step 2 — Classify the Input Set
For every input segment $s_i$ relative to the chosen line $L$:
- If $s_i$ lies entirely on the **positive side** of $L$ → it goes into $S^+$.
- If $s_i$ lies entirely on the **negative side** of $L$ → it goes into $S^-$.
- If $s_i$ is **crossed** by $L$ → it is **split** at the intersection point into two new sub-segments, one for $S^+$ and one for $S^-$.
- If $s_i$ is **coincident** with $L$ (lies on $L$) → it is stored as a "coplanar" segment at the current node.

### Step 3 — Recurse on Each Subset
Apply Steps 1 and 2 recursively to $S^+$ and $S^-$ to build the left and right subtrees. Termination occurs when a subset is empty (or contains a single co-planar segment).

### Step 4 — The 'Why' Behind BSP
- **Why split?:** Because in graphics, to render a scene correctly, you need to know which objects are in front of which. A BSP tree encodes **depth ordering** by the order in which you traverse the tree from a viewpoint.
- **How is the order determined?:** Given a viewpoint $v$, at each node you first render the child on the **far side** of the splitting plane (relative to $v$), then the coplanar geometry, then the child on the **near side**. This is exactly the **Painter's Algorithm** (back-to-front rendering).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All critical BSP tree formulas, conditions, and complexity bounds**

### Table 2.1 — BSP Tree Complexity & Property Summary

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Value | Meaning |
|---------------|----------------|---------|
| Input size $n$ | $n = \mid S \mid$ | Number of input segments/polygons |
| Tree size $T(n)$ | $T(n) = 1 + T(n^+) + T(n^-) + c$ | Recursive size of BSP tree |
| Splitting cost | $s$ = number of new segments created by splitting | Splits increase node count |
| Total splits $S(n)$ | $S(n) = S(n^+) + S(n^-) + s$ | Recursive splitting count |
| Naive construction | $O(n^2)$ | Worst case without heuristic |
| Optimal construction (2D) | $O(n \log n)$ expected | With random hyperplane choice (Mulmuley) |
| Size bound (axis-aligned 2D) | $O(n \log n)$ | For axis-aligned segments |
| Size bound (general 2D) | $O(n^2)$ worst case | Adversarial input can cause this |
| Height (balanced) | $O(\log n)$ | With good splitting heuristic |
| Height (worst case) | $O(n)$ | Pathological input |
| Query time (point location) | $O(\log n)$ expected | In balanced BSP |
| Storage | $O(n + S)$ | Linear in total segments after splits |

### Table 2.2 — Splitting Heuristic Trade-offs

| Heuristic | Tree Balance | Split Count | Use Case |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|----------|
| Random line | Medium | Medium | General-purpose |
| Median split | Good balance | High | Point sets |
| Min-cut (minimize splits) | Poor balance | **Lowest** | Rendering, collision |
| Surface area heuristic (SAH) | Medium | Medium | Ray tracing |
| Auto-partition (Fuchs) | Good | Bounded | Hidden surface removal |

### Table 2.3 — Key Property Equations

| Property | Statement |
|----------|-----------|
| Convex cell property | Every leaf corresponds to a convex region of $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| Disjointness | Cells at the same level are pairwise interior-disjoint |
| Exhaustiveness | Union of all leaves covers the full subdivided space |
| Hierarchy | $V(\text{leaf}) = \bigcap_{\text{ancestors}} H_i^{\pm}$ (intersection of half-spaces) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Game Engines (id Tech / Quake):** 2D BSP trees for level geometry enabled fast rendering and visibility in 1990s-era shooters. Modern engines (Unreal, Unity) use a mix of BSP for CSG, PVS, and occlusion culling.
- **Ray Tracing Acceleration:** Surface Area Heuristic (SAH) is a modern generalization of BSP cost-minimization.
- **Solid Modeling (Boolean CSG):** BSP trees make union/intersection/difference of polyhedra fast — each boolean op is a tree merge.
- **Robotics:** Configuration-space BSP trees for motion planning through obstacles.
- **Geometric Software Libraries:** CGAL implements BSP trees for general polyhedron operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed BSP Tree Construction — Worked Example

Consider five line segments in $\mathbb{R}^2$ given by their endpoints:

$$S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\}$$

where:
- $s_1 = [(0,0), (10,0)]$ (horizontal, on x-axis)
- $s_2 = [(0,0), (0,10)]$ (vertical, on y-axis)
- $s_3 = [(10,0), (10,10)]$ (vertical, right side)
- $s_4 = [(2,2), (8,8)]$ (diagonal)
- $s_5 = [(2,8), (8,2)]$ (anti-diagonal)

We will construct a BSP tree step by step.

### Step A — Choose the Root Splitting Line

We select $s_1$'s supporting line as the root splitting line:
$$L_1 : y = 0$$

This is the x-axis. Classify the remaining segments relative to $L_1$:

For each segment, we test the sign of the line equation at the segment's endpoints. For a line $ax + by + c = 0$, a point $(x_0, y_0)$ has signed distance $\delta = ax_0 + by_0 + c$.

For $L_1: 0\cdot x + 1\cdot y - 0 = 0$, we have $\delta = y_0$.

- $s_2 = [(0,0),(0,10)]$: endpoints have $y=0$ and $y=10$. So $\delta_1 = 0$ and $\delta_2 = 10$. **One endpoint is ON the line, the other is positive** → $s_2$ is split at $(0,0)$.
- $s_3 = [(10,0),(10,10)]$: similarly $y=0$ and $y=10$ → split at $(10,0)$.
- $s_4 = [(2,2),(8,8)]$: both $y>0$ → $s_4 \in S^+$.
- $s_5 = [(2,8),(8,2)]$: both $y>0$ → $s_5 \in S^+$.

Therefore:

$$S^- = \emptyset, \quad S^L = \{s_1, s_2', s_3'\}, \quad S^+ = \{s_4, s_5\}$$

where $s_2' = [(0,0),(0,10)]$ (retained as coplanar) and $s_3' = [(10,0),(10,10)]$ (retained as coplanar). Since $s_2$ and $s_3$ had an endpoint ON the line, they were not split into two pieces (they had a zero-length piece on the negative side which we discard as degenerate). Note: We retain $s_2$ and $s_3$ as coplanar with the splitting line.

### Step B — Process the Right Subtree (Positive Half-Plane)

We now build the BSP for $S^+ = \{s_4, s_5\}$. Choose $s_4$'s supporting line:

$$L_2 : y - x = 0 \;\;(\text{i.e., } y = x)$$

Classify $s_5 = [(2,8),(8,2)]$ relative to $L_2$. Endpoints:
- $(2,8)$: $\delta = 8 - 2 = +6 > 0$ → positive side.
- $(8,2)$: $\delta = 2 - 8 = -6 < 0$ → negative side.

So $s_5$ is **crossed** by $L_2$. Compute the intersection of $L_2$ with $s_5$.

$s_5$ has the parametric form $(2 + 6t, 8 - 6t)$ for $t \in [0,1]$. On $L_2$ we need $y = x$:
$$8 - 6t = 2 + 6t \;\Longrightarrow\; 6 = 12t \;\Longrightarrow\; t = 0.5$$

Intersection point: $(2 + 3, 8 - 3) = (5, 5)$. So $s_5$ is split into:
- $s_5^+ = [(5,5), (2,8)]$ (positive part)
- $s_5^- = [(5,5), (8,2)]$ (negative part)

Thus:
$$S^-_2 = \{s_5^-\}, \quad S^L_2 = \{s_4\}, \quad S^+_2 = \{s_5^+\}$$

### Step C — Terminate the Subtrees

Both $S^-_2$ and $S^+_2$ each contain only one segment, so we terminate — they become **leaf nodes**.

### Step D — Summary of the Final Tree

The constructed BSP tree has the structure:

```
Root: L1 (y = 0)
├── Coplanar: s1, s2, s3
├── Left child:  Empty leaf (S⁻ = ∅)
└── Right child: Node L2 (y = x)
    ├── Coplanar: s4
    ├── Left child:  Leaf containing s5⁻
    └── Right child: Leaf containing s5⁺
```

Total segments after construction: $1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7$ (the original 5 plus the 2 new pieces from splitting $s_5$). The total split count is $S = 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Worst-Case Size Bound

**Claim:** For $n$ line segments in general position in $\mathbb{R}^2$, the size of the BSP tree can be as large as $\Theta(n^2)$.

**Proof Sketch (Adversarial Construction):**
Arrange $n/2$ segments as horizontal lines $y = 1, 2, \dots, n/2$ and $n/2$ segments as vertical lines $x = 1, 2, \dots, n/2$. Each vertical segment is crossed by every horizontal segment, and vice versa.

If the root splitting line is horizontal (say $y = 1$), it crosses all $n/2$ vertical segments, creating $n/2$ new segment endpoints. Recursively choosing vertical lines, then horizontal, then vertical, etc., produces a tree where every recursive call splits nearly every remaining segment.

Let $T(n)$ denote the number of nodes in the BSP tree. The recurrence is:

$$T(n) \le T(n - k) + T(k) + c \cdot s$$

where $s$ is the number of splits and $k$ is the number of segments sent to one side. In the worst case, $k = 1$ and $s = n-1$ at every level, giving:

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + c(n-1) = T(n-1) + O(n)$$

Solving this recurrence by unrolling:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(i) = O\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} i\right) = O\!\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right) = O(n^2)$$

Hence the worst-case size is $\Theta(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Random Construction Bound (Mulmuley's Result)

**Claim:** For a random set of $n$ non-intersecting line segments in $\mathbb{R}^2$, the expected size of the BSP tree is $O(n \log n)$ if splitting lines are chosen uniformly at random.

**Expected Splits per Recursion:**
Consider a vertical splitting line $L$ chosen uniformly at random. For a given segment $s$ of length $\ell_s$, the probability that $L$ crosses $s$ is proportional to $\ell_s / W$ where $W$ is the width of the bounding box. The expected number of splits at the root is:

$$\mathbb{E}[s] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\ell_{s_i}}{W} \le \frac{n \cdot L_{\max}}{W}$$

where $L_{\max}$ is the maximum segment length.

By a careful choice of vertical/horizontal alternation and bounding each segment's length by the side length of the cell it lies in, one can show:

$$\mathbb{E}[T(n)] \le 2n + \sum_{\text{children}} \mathbb{E}[T(\text{child})]$$

This leads to the solution $\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Painter's Algorithm Rendering Order Derivation

Given a viewpoint $v$ and a BSP tree, the back-to-front rendering order is derived as follows. At each node with splitting plane $H$:

- The signed distance from $v$ to $H$ determines which child is "far" and which is "near".
- The **recursive rendering function** is:

$$\text{Render}(N, v) = \begin{cases} \text{do nothing} & \text{if } N \text{ is null} \\ \text{co-planar}(N, v) & \text{if } N \text{ is leaf} \\ \text{Render}(\text{FarChild}(N, v), v) \;\to\; \text{DrawCoplanar}(N) \;\to\; \text{Render}(\text{NearChild}(N, v), v) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The correctness follows by induction: at every node, the far child is entirely behind the near child with respect to $v$, because the splitting plane $H$ separates them, and $v$ is on a known side.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BSP Tree Architecture Block Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the block-level functional architecture of the BSP tree construction pipeline, from input geometry to traversable tree structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Geometry Set S] --> procB[Choose Splitting Hyperplane H]
    procB --> classC[Classify Segments]
    classC --> posD[Positive Side S positive]
    classC --> negE[Negative Side S negative]
    classC --> copF[Co-planar S on H]
    posD --> recG[Recursive Build BSP of S positive]
    negE --> recH[Recursive Build BSP of S negative]
    copF --> storeI[Store Co-planar at Node]
    recG --> nodeJ[Node N with Children L and R]
    recH --> nodeJ
    storeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> checkK{Empty Subsets?}
    checkK -->|Yes| leafL[Create Leaf Node]
    checkK -->|No| procB
    leafL --> outputM[Final BSP Tree]
    nodeJ --> outputM
```

## 4.2 BSP Tree Structure for the Worked Example

The following diagram shows the specific BSP tree structure constructed in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["Root L1 y equals 0"]
    copRoot["Coplanar: s1, s2, s3"]
    leftLeaf["Empty Leaf S negative empty"]
    rightInternal["Node L2 y equals x"]
    copL2["Coplanar: s4"]
    leftLeafL2["Leaf: s5 negative piece"]
    rightLeafL2["Leaf: s5 positive piece"]
    rootNode --- copRoot
    rootNode --- leftLeaf
    rootNode --- rightInternal
    rightInternal --- copL2
    rightInternal --- leftLeafL2
    rightInternal --- rightLeafL2
```

## 4.3 Hidden Surface Removal (Painter's Algorithm) Flow

The following diagram illustrates the rendering pipeline that uses a BSP tree to draw geometry back-to-front.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Render Viewpoint v] --> traverseB[Traverse BSP from Root]
    traverseB --> checkC{v Null?}
    checkC -->|Yes| returnD[Return]
    checkC -->|No| distE[Compute Signed Distance of v to Hyperplane H]
    distE --> decideF{v on Positive Side?}
    decideG{v on Negative Side?}
    distE --> decideF
    distE --> decideG
    decideF -->|Far is Right| recurseFar1[Recurse on Right Child First]
    decideF -->|Near is Left| recurseNear1[Recurse on Left Child Second]
    decideG -->|Far is Left| recurseFar2[Recurse on Left Child First]
    decideG -->|Near is Right| recurseNear2[Recurse on Right Child Second]
    recurseFar1 --> drawCop1[Draw Coplanar Geometry]
    recurseNear1 --> drawCop1
    recurseFar2 --> drawCop1
    recurseNear2 --> drawCop1
    drawCop1 --> nextNode[Move to Next Node]
    nextNode --> traverseB
```

## 4.4 Construction Complexity Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputN[Input Size n] --> naiveAlg[Naive Recursive Algorithm]
    inputN --> heurAlg[Heuristic Guided Algorithm]
    naiveAlg --> oN2[Worst Case O n squared]
    heurAlg --> oNlogN[Expected O n log n]
    oN2 --> useCase1[Worst Case Adversarial]
    oNlogN --> useCase2[Real World Game Graphics]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: BSP Tree Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) tree. How does it differ from a k-d tree?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **BSP tree** is a hierarchical data structure that recursively subdivides a $d$-dimensional space using $(d-1)$-dimensional **splitting hyperplanes**, where each hyperplane is drawn from the input geometry itself. Each internal node stores a splitting hyperplane, and each leaf represents a convex region (cell) of the final subdivision.

**Key differences from a k-d tree** (each worth marks):
1. **Splitting plane source:** A BSP tree picks splitting hyperplanes from the **input geometry's supporting lines/planes**, whereas a k-d tree always uses axis-aligned splitting planes.
2. **Data preservation:** BSP trees retain the original geometry as part of the tree (co-planar storage); k-d trees typically only store points.
3. **Balance guarantee:** A k-d tree is typically rebalanced to $O(\log n)$ height; a BSP tree's height depends on the splitting heuristic and can be $O(n)$ in the worst case.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark per correct distinguishing point.)*

### Question 2: BSP Tree Co-planar Storage `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by "co-planar" segments in a BSP tree? Why is co-planar storage necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Co-planar segments** are input segments that lie **exactly on** the splitting hyperplane chosen at a given node (i.e., they are collinear with the splitting line in 2D or coplanar with the splitting plane in 3D). They do not belong to either the positive or negative side; instead, they are stored as a list at the current node itself.

**Why co-planar storage is necessary** (1.5 marks each):
1. **Preserves geometry fidelity:** Discarding co-planar segments would mean losing parts of the input geometry, which is unacceptable in rendering and CSG operations.
2. **Enables correct depth ordering:** During back-to-front rendering, co-planar geometry must be drawn at the precise moment the splitting plane is reached — they cannot be deferred to either child subtree.
3. **Maintains tree correctness:** Segments lying on a splitting plane cannot be classified into $S^+$ or $S^-$; without a third "co-planar" slot, the BSP construction would either lose them or incorrectly assign them.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in BSP tree questions (resulting in 2–4 mark deductions):
> 1. **Confusing BSP with B-trees or binary search trees** — BSP trees operate on *spatial regions*, not sorted keys.
> 2. **Forgetting the co-planar list** — many students build only two children, losing geometry that lies on the splitting hyperplane.
> 3. **Omitting the recursion termination condition** — the base case $|S| \le 1$ must be stated explicitly.
> 4. **Failing to split crossed segments** — when a splitting line crosses a segment, the segment MUST be subdivided into two new pieces, one per side.
> 5. **Confusing Painter's Algorithm order** — the FAR child is rendered FIRST (back-to-front), not the near child.
> 6. **Hand-waving complexity** — students often write "$O(n \log n)$" without specifying the conditions (random splitting, no adversarial input).

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Given the following set of six line segments in $\mathbb{R}^2$, construct a BSP tree using $s_1$ as the root splitting line. Clearly show the classification, splitting, and recursion steps.**

Segments:
- $s_1 = [(1,1), (9,1)]$ (horizontal at $y=1$)
- $s_2 = [(1,1), (1,9)]$ (vertical at $x=1$)
- $s_3 = [(9,1), (9,9)]$ (vertical at $x=9$)
- $s_4 = [(1,9), (9,9)]$ (horizontal at $y=9$)
- $s_5 = [(3,3), (7,3)]$ (horizontal at $y=3$, inside)
- $s_6 = [(3,7), (7,7)]$ (horizontal at $y=7$, inside)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Root splitting line is $L_1: y = 1$, the supporting line of $s_1$.**

**Step 2 (3 marks): Classify all segments relative to $L_1$.** Using the signed distance function $\delta(x,y) = y - 1$:

- $s_1$: $y = 1$ everywhere → $\delta = 0$ → **Co-planar (stored at root)**.
- $s_2$: endpoints $(1,1)$ and $(1,9)$ have $\delta = 0$ and $\delta = 8$. One endpoint is on the line, one is above. **The portion above $L_1$ is $s_2^+ = [(1,1),(1,9)]$; the portion below is degenerate (zero length) and is discarded.** So $s_2$ is essentially fully co-planar/above; we retain it as a co-planar-above segment, but to be precise, since it has an endpoint on $L_1$, we keep it in co-planar (the non-degenerate piece above is preserved).
- $s_3$: similarly, endpoints $(9,1)$ and $(9,9)$ → co-planar.
- $s_4$: $y = 9 > 1$ → $s_4 \in S^+$.
- $s_5$: $y = 3 > 1$ → $s_5 \in S^+$.
- $s_6$: $y = 7 > 1$ → $s_6 \in S^+$.

Result: $S^- = \emptyset$, $S^L = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $S^+ = \{s_4, s_5, s_6\}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark): The left subtree of the root is an empty leaf (no segments below $y=1$).**

**Step 4 (2 marks): Recurse on $S^+ = \{s_4, s_5, s_6\}$.** Choose $s_4$'s supporting line $L_2: y = 9$. Classify:

- $s_4$: $y = 9$ → co-planar at this node.
- $s_5$: $y = 3 < 9$ → $S^-$.
- $s_6$: $y = 7 < 9$ → $S^-$.

Recurse on $S^- = \{s_5, s_6\}$. Choose $s_5$'s line $L_3: y = 3$. Classify $s_6$: $y = 7 > 3$ → $S^+$. Recurse on $S^+ = \{s_6\}$: one element → leaf.

**Final tree:**

```
Root: L1 (y = 1)
├── Coplanar: s1, s2, s3
├── Left:  Empty leaf
└── Right: Node L2 (y = 9)
    ├── Coplanar: s4
    ├── Left:  Node L3 (y = 3)
    │   ├── Coplanar: s5
    │   ├── Left:  Empty leaf
    │   └── Right: Leaf (s6)
    └── Right: Empty leaf
```

**Total splits:** 0 (no segment was crossed). **Total nodes:** 3 internal + 4 leaves = 7.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: (i) State and explain two key properties of a BSP tree. (ii) Derive the worst-case size bound of $\Theta(n^2)$ for a BSP tree on $n$ line segments.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Two key properties (2 marks each = 4 marks total):**

1. **Convex Cell Property:** Every leaf node of a BSP tree represents a **convex** region of $\mathbb{R}^d$. This is because the leaf region is the intersection of half-spaces defined by the splitting hyperplanes along the root-to-leaf path, and the intersection of convex sets (half-spaces) is convex. **Engineering utility:** Convex cells enable constant-time point-in-cell tests and simple intersection computations.

2. **Hierarchical Subdivision Property:** The cells at any level of the tree are **pairwise interior-disjoint** and their **union equals the parent cell**. This is a direct consequence of the recursive splitting: a hyperplane divides a region into two disjoint interiors whose union recovers the original region. **Engineering utility:** This guarantees a partition of unity, so no geometric object is double-counted during rendering.

**(ii) Worst-case size bound derivation (3 marks):**

Arrange the $n$ segments as $\frac{n}{2}$ horizontal and $\frac{n}{2}$ vertical line segments forming a near-complete grid. If the BSP algorithm picks a horizontal segment as the root line, it splits all $\frac{n}{2}$ vertical segments into $\frac{n}{2}$ pieces, increasing the total to $n + \frac{n}{2}$. If the next line chosen is vertical, it splits the remaining horizontal pieces, increasing the count further. The recurrence for the number of segments $T(n)$ satisfies:

$$T(n) \le T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) = 2 T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n)$$

By the Master Theorem (Case 2), this solves to $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ in this specific configuration. However, an **adversarial configuration** can force a highly unbalanced tree. Consider segments arranged so that the chosen splitting line always crosses all $n-1$ other segments, sending 1 to one side and $n-1$ to the other. Then:

$$T(n) = T(1) + T(n-1) + O(n)$$

Unrolling:

$$T(n) = O(1) + \sum_{k=2}^{n} O(k) = O(n^2)$$

This proves the **worst-case** size bound is $\Theta(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the Painter's Algorithm for hidden-surface removal using a BSP tree. How does the recursion order guarantee back-to-front rendering?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The Painter's Algorithm (2 marks introduction):**
The Painter's Algorithm renders a 3D scene by drawing objects in **back-to-front** order so that nearer objects overwrite farther ones on the framebuffer, mimicking the way a painter layers oil paints. A BSP tree encodes this order implicitly in its structure.

**Recursive rendering function (3 marks):**
Given a viewpoint $v$ and a BSP tree node $N$ with splitting plane $H_N$:

$$\text{Render}(N, v) = \begin{cases}
\text{draw all coplanar geometry of } N & \text{if } N \text{ is a leaf} \\
\text{Render}(\text{FarChild}(N,v), v) & \text{if } N \text{ is internal} \\
\quad\to\;\text{DrawCoplanar}(N) & \\
\quad\to\;\text{Render}(\text{NearChild}(N,v), v) &
\end{cases}$$

where:
- $\text{FarChild}(N, v)$ is the child on the side of $H_N$ **opposite** to $v$ (farther from $v$).
- $\text{NearChild}(N, v)$ is the child on the side of $H_N$ **closer to** $v$.

**Why this is correct (2 marks — induction proof):**
By induction on tree depth:
- **Base case:** A leaf has only coplanar geometry; drawing it once is trivially correct.
- **Inductive step:** Assume correct ordering within both subtrees. The splitting plane $H_N$ separates the two subtrees. Every object in the far subtree is strictly behind every object in the near subtree (relative to $v$), because $H_N$ is a separating plane and $v$ is on the near side. Thus, drawing the far subtree first, then the coplanar geometry, then the near subtree produces a globally correct back-to-front order.

**Practical note:** This is why 1990s game engines (id Software's Quake) used precomputed BSP trees for static level geometry — the back-to-front order was computed once, then reused for every frame.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the auto-partitioning property of BSP trees as introduced by Fuchs, Kedem, and Naylor. Why is auto-partitioning essential for hidden-surface removal in polygonal scenes?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The Problem with Standard BSP (1 mark):**
A naive BSP construction **splits** polygon edges that are crossed by the splitting plane, creating new edges. This means the resulting BSP tree has more polygons than the input scene — sometimes exponentially more. For hidden-surface removal, this is problematic because the rendering cost grows with the number of polygons.

**Auto-Partitioning (Fuchs–Kedem–Naylor 1980) (3 marks):**
The auto-partitioning BSP method **avoids splitting** by extending each split polygon outward to infinity (or to the bounding box). Specifically, given a polygon $P$ that is crossed by the splitting plane $H$:
- $P$ is **not split** into two pieces.
- Instead, $P$ is classified entirely to one side of $H$ (the side containing $P$'s interior, or a chosen side).
- The "tail" of $P$ that crossed $H$ is extended as a new polygon to "fill the gap" on the other side.

Formally, if a polygon's normal and centroid are used to classify it, and a "new polygon" is generated to cover the area on the opposite side, the **total number of polygons in the BSP tree remains $O(n)$** (linear in the input), provided the input polygons are in "general position" (no two polygons share an edge).

**Why Auto-Partitioning is Essential (3 marks):**

1. **Linear-size tree guarantee:** For polygonal scenes with no shared edges, the auto-partitioning BSP has size $O(n)$. This is a sharp bound: it does not blow up like the splitting variant.

2. **Order-independent correctness:** The FKN auto-partitioning construction produces a BSP that, regardless of the order in which polygons were input, yields a **correct** back-to-front rendering for any viewpoint. This is a profound property: input order does not affect correctness, only efficiency.

3. **Enables real-time rendering of static scenes:** Once built, the auto-partitioning BSP can be traversed in $O(n)$ time per frame (for any viewpoint) to produce the rendering order. This is what made 1990s game engines like Quake practical.

**Limitation (bonus point):**
Auto-partitioning requires polygons to be in **general position** (no shared edges). For scenes with shared edges (e.g., two triangles meeting at an edge of a mesh), T-junctions can cause cracks during rendering. This is mitigated in practice by preprocessing to handle coplanar faces.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **BSP Trees — Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** BSP tree = hierarchical subdivision of $\mathbb{R}^d$ by $(d-1)$-dimensional splitting hyperplanes chosen from the input geometry.
> - **Node types:** Internal node = splitting hyperplane + coplanar list; leaf node = empty or single co-planar polygon.
> - **Three subsets at every split:** $S^+$ (positive side), $S^-$ (negative side), $S^L$ (co-planar).
> - **Crossed segments must be split** at the intersection point into two new segments, one per side.
> - **Co-planar storage is mandatory** — discarding coplanar geometry breaks the structure.
> - **Worst-case size:** $O(n^2)$ for adversarial input.
> - **Random-input expected size:** $O(n \log n)$ in 2D.
> - **Tree height:** $O(\log n)$ for balanced heuristics, $O(n)$ worst case.
> - **Construction complexity:** $O(n^2)$ naive, $O(n \log n)$ with random splitting heuristic.
> - **Query complexity (point location):** $O(\log n)$ expected in balanced BSP.
> - **Auto-partitioning (FKN 1980):** Linear-size BSP, $O(n)$ per frame for rendering.
> - **Painter's Algorithm order:** Recurse FAR → draw coplanar → recurse NEAR.
> - **Applications:** Hidden-surface removal, ray tracing, CSG booleans, visibility culling, motion planning.
> - **Common confusions:** BSP ≠ B-tree, BSP ≠ k-d tree, BSP ≠ Quad/Octree. The key distinguishing feature is that BSP trees **store the actual geometry** (segments/polygons) as part of the tree.
> - **Splitting heuristics:** Random, median-balance, min-cut, surface-area-heuristic (SAH). Trade-off between tree balance and split count.
> - **Key reference:** Fuchs, Kedem, Naylor, *"On Visible Surface Generation by A Priori Tree Structures,"* SIGGRAPH 1980.
> - **Key exam trap:** Always specify whether the input is in general position, whether coplanar geometry is stored, and what splitting heuristic is used when stating complexity bounds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use in Computer Graphics and Collision Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Use in Computer Graphics and Collision Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computational Geometry**, the sub-domain concerning **Computer Graphics (CG) and Collision Detection (CD)** refers to the design, analysis, and implementation of geometric algorithms and data structures that enable efficient rendering, visibility computation, geometric querying, and physical interaction simulation within digital virtual environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:**
> *Computer Graphics in Computational Geometry* is the study of algorithms for representing, manipulating, and rendering 2D/3D geometric primitives. *Collision Detection* is the computational problem of detecting the intersection, contact, or penetration between two or more geometric objects described by spatial boundaries.

The two fields are deeply intertwined because:
- **Computer Graphics** requires fast geometric queries (e.g., ray-object intersection for pixel shading).
- **Collision Detection** is the geometric "backbone" of any physical simulation, robotics navigation, or game engine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are playing a **3D video game** where a ball is thrown against a wall.

- The **wall** is represented not as one giant object, but as a mesh of **thousands of tiny triangles** (a fundamental geometric primitive in CG).
- The game engine must answer two questions every **16 milliseconds** (60 FPS):
  1. *"Which triangle on the wall does the ball hit first?"* — This is a **Ray Tracing / Ray Casting** problem (CG).
  2. *"Is the ball currently inside the wall or outside?"* — This is a **Collision / Penetration Detection** problem (CD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of *Computer Graphics* as the **art of drawing 3D scenes on a 2D screen** using geometry, and *Collision Detection* as the **art of asking "do these two virtual objects overlap?"** before letting them physically interact. Computational Geometry provides the **mathematical hammer and chisel** for both.

## 1.3 Core Geometric Primitives Used in CG & CD

| Primitive | Mathematical Form | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Point** | $P = (x, y, z)$ | Vertices of meshes, particle systems |
| **Line Segment** | $L = P_1 + t(P_2 - P_1)$ | Edges, ray segments, animation paths |
| **Plane** | $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{x} = d$ | Half-space tests, polygon faces |
| **Triangle** | $T = (P_0, P_1, P_2)$ | Mesh faces (de-facto rendering primitive) |
| **Sphere** | $\vert P - C \vert = r$ | Bounding volumes, particle physics |
| **AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box)** | $[x_{min}, x_{max}] \times [y_{min}, y_{max}] \times [z_{min}, z_{max}]$ | Broad-phase collision culling |
| **OBB (Oriented Bounding Box)** | Arbitrary-orientation cuboid | Tight-fitting bounding volume |
| **Convex Hull** | Smallest convex polygon/polyhedron containing a set of points | Collision proxies, mesh simplification |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The triangle is the *de-facto* rendering primitive in modern GPUs because every polygon can be decomposed (triangulated) into triangles, and modern hardware is highly optimized for triangle rasterization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Pipeline of Collision Detection

Collision detection is universally decomposed into a **Broad Phase** and a **Narrow Phase**, both of which are pure Computational Geometry problems.

### Phase 1: Broad Phase (Coarse Culling)
The goal is to **quickly reject** pairs of objects that are obviously not colliding. This is dominated by **Bounding Volume Hierarchies (BVH)**.

- Use **inexpensive bounding primitives** like AABBs, spheres, or OBBs.
- Standard data structures: **AABB Trees**, **Sphere Trees**, **BVH Trees**, **Uniform Spatial Grids**, **Octrees** (3D), and **BSP Trees** (Binary Space Partitioning).
- The asymptotic complexity of broad-phase culling is reduced to near $O(n \log n)$ using sweep-and-prune or hierarchical bounding volumes, instead of the naive $O(n^2)$ pair test.

### Phase 2: Narrow Phase (Exact Test)
The goal is to **precisely determine** the intersection state between the surviving candidate pairs.

- **Convex vs. Convex:** Separating Axis Theorem (SAT), Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi (GJK) distance algorithm.
- **Convex vs. Non-Convex:** Triangle-mesh tests (e.g., AABB-Triangle, OBB-Triangle, Triangle-Triangle).
- **Point vs. Geometry:** Minkowski Portal Refinement (MPR).

## 2.2 Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) — The CG Backbone

A BVH is a tree where:
- **Leaf nodes** contain one or a few primitives (e.g., triangles).
- **Internal nodes** contain a *bounding volume* that tightly encloses all primitives of their children.
- The **root** encloses the entire object/scene.

**Build heuristic (Surface Area Heuristic - SAH):** A node is split along the axis and position that minimizes expected query cost. The SAH cost is given by:

$$C_{SAH} = C_{traversal} + \frac{SA(L)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_{L} + \frac{SA(R)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_{R}$$

where $SA(\cdot)$ is the surface area of the bounding volume, $L$ and $R$ are the left/right children, and $C_{traversal}, C_L, C_R$ are traversal costs.

## 2.3 Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAT Theorem:** *Two convex polytopes are disjoint if and only if there exists an axis (a "separating axis") on which the projections of the two polytopes do not overlap.*

For two convex objects $A$ and $B$, the test axes are:
- The face normals of $A$,
- The face normals of $B$,
- All $\mathbf{n}_A^i \times \mathbf{n}_B^j$ cross products of edges from $A$ and $B$.

If a gap exists on **any** candidate axis, the objects are **separated**; otherwise, they **overlap**.

## 2.4 Ray-Object Intersection (The Heart of Ray Tracing)

A **ray** is defined parametrically as:

$$R(t) = O + t \mathbf{d}, \quad t \geq 0$$

where $O$ is the ray origin, $\mathbf{d}$ is the unit direction vector, and $t$ is the distance parameter.

**Rendering pipeline (per pixel):**

1. Cast a primary ray from the camera through the pixel center.
2. Test intersection against the scene's BVH (accelerated traversal).
3. For the closest hit point, evaluate the **Phong/Blinn-Phong shading equation**:

$$I = k_a I_a + \sum_{m} I_m \left[ k_d (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L}_m) + k_s (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{H}_m)^{n_s} \right]$$

where $k_a, k_d, k_s$ are ambient/diffuse/specular coefficients, $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal, $\mathbf{L}_m$ is the light direction, and $\mathbf{H}_m$ is the half-vector.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R(t) = O + t\mathbf{d}$ | Parametric ray equation | Ray tracing, picking |
| $\mathbf{n} \cdot (P - P_0) = 0$ | Plane equation (point-normal form) | Polygon clipping, half-space tests |
| $t = \dfrac{\mathbf{n} \cdot (P_0 - O)}{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{d}}$ | Ray-plane intersection distance | Rendering, intersection tests |
| $\vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert = 1$ | Ray direction normalization (precondition) | Required for $t$ to equal Euclidean distance |
| $\vert P - C \vert^2 = r^2$ | Sphere implicit equation | Bounding volume, collision |
| $t = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}$ (over 2a) | Quadratic solution for ray-sphere | Sphere intersection |
| $C_{SAH} = C_{trav} + \frac{SA(L)}{SA(P)}C_L + \frac{SA(R)}{SA(P)}C_R$ | Surface Area Heuristic | Optimal BVH construction |
| $\text{Overlap}(A, B) = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} [a_i, b_i] \neq \emptyset$ | AABB overlap test on $n$ axes | Broad-phase culling |
| $\mathbf{s}_k = \mathbf{v}_k^B - \mathbf{v}_k^A$ | Minkowski difference support point | GJK algorithm |
| $A_{\text{visible}} \iff (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{v} < 0)$ | Back-face culling test | Hidden surface removal |
| $f_{clip}(x, y) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < x_{min} \\ x_{min}, & x < x_{min} \\ x, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Cohen-Sutherland outcode | Line clipping |

> [!NOTE]
> In the AABB overlap test, each axis is tested **independently**; only if all axes overlap do we conclude true 3D intersection. This is a direct consequence of the **Separating Axis Theorem** for axis-aligned boxes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Computational Geometry Technique Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR ray casting, voxel grids, polygon clipping for drivable area |
| **Robotic Surgery (da Vinci)** | Triangle-mesh collision for haptic feedback, GPU BVH traversal |
| **3D Animation & VFX** | BVH acceleration for ray tracing, polygon clipping for compositing |
| **Video Game Engines (Unity, Unreal)** | PhysX / Bullet engines: AABB broad phase, GJK narrow phase |
| **CAD / CAM** | Boolean operations (CSG), polygon offsetting, meshing |
| **AR / VR (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro)** | Real-time hand tracking, frustum culling, collision with virtual objects |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ray-Plane Intersection Distance

> **Given:** A plane defined by a point $P_0$ and a normal $\mathbf{n}$ (where $\mathbf{n}$ is a unit vector), and a ray $R(t) = O + t\mathbf{d}$. **Find:** the value of $t$ at which the ray intersects the plane, if any.

**Step 1 — Express the plane equation.** Any point $P$ on the plane satisfies:

$$(P - P_0) \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ray into the plane equation.** Since the intersection point must lie on the ray, we set $P = R(t) = O + t\mathbf{d}$:

$$(O + t\mathbf{d} - P_0) \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand using distributivity of the dot product.**

$$(O - P_0) \cdot \mathbf{n} + t (\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n}) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the scalar $t$.**

$$t (\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n}) = -(O - P_0) \cdot \mathbf{n}$$

$$t (\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n}) = (P_0 - O) \cdot \mathbf{n}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t$, with the assumption that $\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n} \neq 0$ (ray not parallel to plane).**

$$t = \frac{(P_0 - O) \cdot \mathbf{n}}{\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Geometric interpretation.** The denominator $\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert \mathbf{n} \vert \cos\theta = \cos\theta$ (when both are unit vectors) is the cosine of the angle between the ray and the plane normal. If this is near zero, the ray is grazing or parallel and the intersection is at infinity (numerically unstable).

**Step 7 — Final intersection point.** Plug $t$ back into the ray equation:

$$P_{hit} = O + t \cdot \mathbf{d}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ray-Sphere Intersection

> **Given:** A sphere centered at $C$ with radius $r$, and a ray $R(t) = O + t\mathbf{d}$. **Find:** the values of $t$ at which the ray enters and exits the sphere.

**Step 1 — Write the sphere's implicit equation.**

$$\vert R(t) - C \vert^2 = r^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ray equation into the implicit equation.**

$$\vert (O + t\mathbf{d}) - C \vert^2 = r^2$$

Let $\mathbf{L} = O - C$ (the vector from the sphere center to the ray origin).

$$(\mathbf{L} + t\mathbf{d}) \cdot (\mathbf{L} + t\mathbf{d}) = r^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand the dot product.**

$$\mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{L} + 2t(\mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{d}) + t^2 (\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{d}) = r^2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard quadratic form $at^2 + bt + c = 0$.**

$$(\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{d}) t^2 + 2(\mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{d}) t + (\mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{L} - r^2) = 0$$

Identifying the coefficients:

$$a = \mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{d}, \quad b = 2(\mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{d}), \quad c = \mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{L} - r^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the discriminant $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac$$

If $\Delta < 0$, the ray misses the sphere (no real solutions). If $\Delta \geq 0$, the ray hits.

**Step 6 — Solve using the quadratic formula.**

$$t = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{\Delta}}{2a}$$

The two solutions $t_1$ and $t_2$ (with $t_1 < t_2$) represent the **near intersection** (entry point) and the **far intersection** (exit point), respectively.

**Step 7 — Optimization using half-b (to avoid two multiplications by 2).** Many CG engines use the alternative form $b' = \mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{d}$ to halve the arithmetic cost:

$$t = \frac{-b' \pm \sqrt{b'^2 - ac}}{a}$$

This is the foundational algorithm for the **glass-and-sphere shaders** in any ray tracer, and it is also used by **PhysX** for particle-sphere collision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Separating Axis Theorem for Two AABBs

> **Given:** Two Axis-Aligned Bounding Boxes, $A = [a_{x,min}, a_{x,max}] \times [a_{y,min}, a_{y,max}] \times [a_{z,min}, a_{z,max}]$ and $B$ defined analogously. **Prove:** they overlap if and only if they overlap on all three coordinate axes.

**Step 1 — Recall SAT.** If two convex sets are disjoint, there exists a separating axis. For AABBs, the only candidate separating axes are the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes (because face normals are axis-aligned).

**Step 2 — Test on the $x$-axis.** The projections of $A$ and $B$ onto the $x$-axis are intervals $[a_{x,min}, a_{x,max}]$ and $[b_{x,min}, b_{x,max}]$.

**Step 3 — Overlap condition for the $x$-axis.** They overlap on the $x$-axis if and only if:

$$a_{x,min} \leq b_{x,max} \quad \text{AND} \quad b_{x,min} \leq a_{x,max}$$

Equivalently, in "no-gap" form:

$$a_{x,max} \geq b_{x,min} \quad \text{AND} \quad b_{x,max} \geq a_{x,min}$$

**Step 4 — Compose all three axes.** By the SAT, the boxes overlap if and only if **all three** 1D tests succeed:

$$\text{Overlap}(A, B) = \bigwedge_{k \in \{x, y, z\}} \left( a_{k,max} \geq b_{k,min} \;\wedge\; b_{k,max} \geq a_{k,min} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Disjointness shortcut.** Equivalently, they are disjoint if and only if **at least one** axis test fails:

$$\text{Disjoint}(A, B) = \bigvee_{k \in \{x, y, z\}} \left( a_{k,max} < b_{k,min} \;\vee\; b_{k,max} < a_{k,min} \right)$$

This early-exit formulation is the **most widely used broad-phase test in real-time game engines**, requiring at most **6 floating-point comparisons** in the worst case.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Surface Area Heuristic (SAH) for BVH Construction

> **Goal:** Find the splitting plane along an axis that minimizes the expected cost of a ray traversing the BVH node.

**Step 1 — Define variables.** Let $SA(P)$ be the surface area of the parent bounding box $P$, and $SA(L), SA(R)$ the surface areas of the left and right children after splitting at some plane $x = x_{cut}$.

**Step 2 — Probability of a ray hitting a child.** Because rays are uniformly distributed with respect to surface area, the probability of a ray entering the child bounding box is proportional to its surface area relative to the parent:

$$P(L) = \frac{SA(L)}{SA(P)}, \quad P(R) = \frac{SA(R)}{SA(P)}$$

**Step 3 — Total expected cost.** If the cost of traversing a node is $C_{trav}$, and the cost of intersecting all geometry in a child is $C_L$ (or $C_R$), then:

$$C_{total} = C_{trav} + P(L) \cdot C_L + P(R) \cdot C_R$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the probabilities yields the SAH cost:**

$$C_{SAH} = C_{trav} + \frac{SA(L)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_L + \frac{SA(R)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_R$$

**Step 5 — Application.** For each candidate split, the BVH builder evaluates $C_{SAH}$ and picks the split that minimizes it. This is the **gold standard** for offline BVH construction in film-quality ray tracing (e.g., used in **Embree**, **OptiX**, and **DXR**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Two-Phase Collision Detection Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["World with N Dynamic Objects"] --> B["PHASE 1 BROAD PHASE"]
    B --> B1["Assign Bounding Volumes: AABBs Spheres OBBs"]
    B1 --> B2["BVH Tree or Sweep and Prune"]
    B2 --> B3{"Test All Pairs O n log n"}
    B3 -->|No Overlap| C1["Discard Pair Quickly Rejected"]
    B3 -->|Possible Overlap| D["Candidate Pair List"]
    D --> E["PHASE 2 NARROW PHASE"]
    E --> E1["Convex vs Convex: Use SAT or GJK"]
    E1 --> E2{"Are SAT Axes Separated?"}
    E2 -->|Yes| F1["Disjoint Confirmed"]
    E2 -->|No| F2["Convex Overlap Confirmed"]
    E2 --> F3{"Triangle Mesh vs Triangle Mesh?"}
    F3 -->|Yes| F4["Triangle Triangle Exact Test"]
    F3 -->|No| F5["Generic Mesh: Spatial Hashing"]
    F2 --> G["Contact Manifold Generation"]
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H["Physics Solver: Apply Impulse"]
    H --> I["Update Object Poses and Velocities"]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Broad Phase prunes the $O(n^2)$ pair test down to a near-linear search using BVHs. The Narrow Phase then mathematically certifies (or refutes) the remaining candidate pairs using SAT, GJK, or triangle tests.

## 4.2 Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) — Tree Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["Root: World AABB Encloses All Objects"]
    L1["Left Child: AABB Region L"]
    R1["Right Child: AABB Region R"]
    LL["Leaf: Object Triangle 1"]
    LR["Leaf: Object Triangle 2"]
    RL["Leaf: Object Triangle 3"]
    RR["Leaf: Object Triangle 4"]
    Root --> L1
    Root --> R1
    L1 --> LL
    L1 --> LR
    R1 --> RL
    R1 --> RR
```

**Why it matters:** A ray entering the root only descends into children whose AABB is hit. The number of intersection tests drops from $O(N)$ (all triangles) to $O(\log N)$ on average, enabling **real-time ray tracing** at 60 FPS even with millions of triangles.

## 4.3 Computer Graphics Rendering Pipeline (Geometry Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Application Stage Game Logic"] --> B["Geometry Stage"]
    B --> B1["Model and View Transform"]
    B1 --> B2["Back Face Culling: n dot v less than 0"]
    B2 --> B3["Frustum Culling: Outside View Frustum?"]
    B3 --> B4["Clipping: Cohen Sutherland or Sutherland Hodgman"]
    B4 --> B5["Screen Mapping: NDC to Pixels"]
    B5 --> C["Rasterization Stage"]
    C --> D["Per Pixel Shading: Phong PBR BRDF"]
    D --> E["Frame Buffer: Final Image Output"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** *Culling* and *Clipping* are pure **Computational Geometry** stages. Back-face culling uses the dot product $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{v}$, and clipping uses half-space intersection against the view frustum's six planes. Together they remove up to 70 percent of geometry before rasterization.

## 4.4 Separating Axis Theorem — 2D Illustration (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Separated_Case
        A1["Convex Polygon A"] --- S1["Separating Axis L Vertical"]
        B1["Convex Polygon B"] --- S1
    end
    subgraph Overlapping_Case
        A2["Convex Polygon A"] --- B2["Convex Polygon B"]
        A2 -.->|No axis separates| Result["SAT Fails: Overlap Detected"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left pair has a vertical line $L$ between them such that the projection of $A$ and $B$ onto $L$ do not overlap. The right pair has no such axis; therefore, they overlap.

## 4.5 Polygon Clipping (Sutherland-Hodgman) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Input Polygon Vertices List"] --> Clip1["Clip Against Left Edge: x equal to xmin"]
    Clip1 --> Clip2["Clip Against Right Edge: x equal to xmax"]
    Clip2 --> Clip3["Clip Against Bottom Edge: y equal to ymin"]
    Clip3 --> Clip4["Clip Against Top Edge: y equal to ymax"]
    Clip4 --> Output["Clipped Polygon Output to Rasterizer"]
```

**Sutherland-Hodgman logic per edge:**
1. For each edge $S \to E$ of the polygon:
   - If both endpoints are **inside** the clipping half-plane: emit $E$.
   - If $S$ is inside, $E$ is outside: emit the intersection point.
   - If $S$ is outside, $E$ is inside: emit intersection point, then $E$.
   - If both outside: emit nothing.

This is a classic **Output-Sensitive Algorithm** running in $O(n \cdot m)$ time for an $n$-vertex polygon and an $m$-edged convex clip region.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between broad-phase and narrow-phase collision detection. Why is this two-phase strategy used in real-time systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Broad Phase | Narrow Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Quickly reject non-colliding pairs | Precisely certify collision |
| **Primitives** | AABBs, spheres, OBBs | Convex polytopes, triangle meshes |
| **Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ typically | $O(1)$ to $O(\log n)$ per pair |
| **Output** | Candidate list of pairs | Boolean (and contact data) |

The two-phase strategy is used because the **narrow phase** (e.g., triangle-triangle) is computationally expensive ($O(n^2)$ per pair), while the **broad phase** is extremely cheap ($O(1)$ per pair). By filtering out the vast majority of non-colliding pairs cheaply first, the system avoids paying the high cost of the narrow phase on pairs that obviously do not collide. *(1 mark for broad-phase explanation, 1 mark for narrow-phase explanation, 1 mark for justification of two-phase strategy.)*

---

### **Q2. State the Separating Axis Theorem (SAT) and list the candidate axes to test for two convex polygons in 2D.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Theorem:** *Two convex polygons in 2D are disjoint if and only if there exists a line (axis) such that the orthogonal projections of the two polygons onto that line do not overlap.* **(1 mark)**

**Candidate Axes in 2D:** **(2 marks)**
1. All **edge normals** of polygon A.
2. All **edge normals** of polygon B.
   *(No cross products are needed in 2D; cross products become relevant in 3D.)*

**Practical test:** If a separating axis is found on **any** candidate axis, the polygons are **disjoint**; otherwise, they **overlap**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style: Module 3, Full 14-Mark Question]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): BVH and Ray-Triangle Intersection**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) in computer graphics. Describe the AABB, OBB, and sphere bounding volumes with neat sketches and discuss the Surface Area Heuristic (SAH) for BVH construction.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the ray-triangle intersection algorithm using the Möller-Trumbore method. Show that the algorithm can be expressed as a $2 \times 2$ system of linear equations and find the values of $t, u, v$ in terms of the triangle's edge vectors.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A BVH is a tree structure in which:
- The **root** node bounds the entire scene or object.
- Each **internal node** contains a bounding volume that encloses all geometry in its subtree.
- Each **leaf node** contains one or a few geometric primitives (typically triangles).

**Comparison of Bounding Volumes:**

| Volume Type | Tightness | Transform Cost | Best Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AABB** | Loose (suffers for rotated objects) | None (axis-aligned) | Static or kinematic objects |
| **Sphere** | Loosest (but rotation-invariant) | Trivial (just translate center) | Particle systems, dynamic objects |
| **OBB** | Tightest of the three | $O(n)$ per frame to update | Rotating rigid bodies, articulated models |

**Surface Area Heuristic (SAH):** For a node split into left and right children $L$ and $R$, the SAH cost is:

$$C_{SAH} = C_{trav} + \frac{SA(L)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_L + \frac{SA(R)}{SA(P)} \cdot C_R$$

The builder evaluates this cost for every candidate split plane and picks the minimum. This produces **near-optimal trees** for ray tracing. **(7 marks: 2 for BVH explanation, 2 for volume comparison table, 3 for SAH cost formula and explanation.)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the ray and the triangle.**

$$R(t) = O + t\mathbf{d}$$

A point inside the triangle with vertices $P_0, P_1, P_2$ is parameterized by **barycentric coordinates** $(u, v)$ as:

$$T(u, v) = (1 - u - v)P_0 + u P_1 + v P_2$$

**Step 2 — Set the ray equal to the triangle point.**

$$O + t\mathbf{d} = (1 - u - v)P_0 + u P_1 + v P_2$$

**Step 3 — Define edge vectors.**

$$\mathbf{e}_1 = P_1 - P_0, \quad \mathbf{e}_2 = P_2 - P_0, \quad \mathbf{s} = O - P_0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Cramer's-rule form.**

$$t \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{s} + u \mathbf{e}_1 + v \mathbf{e}_2$$

Rearranging for Cramer's rule:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -\mathbf{d} & \mathbf{e}_1 & \mathbf{e}_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} t \\ u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{s}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Cramer's rule.** Let $p = \mathbf{d} \times \mathbf{e}_2$, $q = \mathbf{s} \times \mathbf{e}_1$. The determinant of the full $3 \times 3$ system is $\mathbf{e}_1 \cdot p$, and the Möller-Trumbore closed form solution is:

$$t = \frac{\mathbf{e}_2 \cdot q}{\mathbf{e}_1 \cdot p}, \quad u = \frac{\mathbf{s} \cdot p}{\mathbf{e}_1 \cdot p}, \quad v = \frac{\mathbf{d} \cdot q}{\mathbf{e}_1 \cdot p}$$

**Step 6 — Validity test.** The intersection is valid if and only if $t \geq 0$ and $0 \leq u \leq 1$, $0 \leq v \leq 1$, $u + v \leq 1$. **(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for edge vectors and Cramer's setup, 3 for closed-form solution and validity test.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): SAT and GJK Collision Detection**

**(a)** *Explain the Separating Axis Theorem (SAT) in detail. Using SAT, derive the overlap test for two OBBs in 3D, listing all 15 candidate separating axes that must be tested.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi (GJK) algorithm. Explain how the Minkowski difference and support functions are used to detect the collision of two convex polytopes. State its time complexity.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — State SAT.** Two convex polytopes $A$ and $B$ are disjoint **iff** there exists a vector $\mathbf{L}$ (a separating axis) such that the projection intervals of $A$ and $B$ onto $\mathbf{L}$ do not overlap. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Project a convex polytope onto an axis.** The projection of polytope $A$ onto axis $\mathbf{L}$ is the interval $[a_{min}, a_{max}]$, where $a_{min} = \min_{P \in A}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P)$ and $a_{max} = \max_{P \in A}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Candidate axes for two OBBs in 3D.** For two OBBs $A$ and $B$ with edge directions $\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{a}_2, \mathbf{a}_3$ and $\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2, \mathbf{b}_3$, the 15 candidate separating axes are:
- The 3 face normals of $A$: $\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{a}_2, \mathbf{a}_3$.
- The 3 face normals of $B$: $\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2, \mathbf{b}_3$.
- The 9 edge-edge cross products: $\mathbf{a}_i \times \mathbf{b}_j$ for $i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Projection interval test.** For each candidate axis $\mathbf{L}$, the two OBBs overlap on $\mathbf{L}$ iff the intervals satisfy:

$$\max_{i}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P_i^A) \geq \min_{i}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P_i^B) \quad \text{AND} \quad \max_{i}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P_i^B) \geq \min_{i}(\mathbf{L} \cdot P_i^A)$$

If a single axis fails this test (i.e., produces a gap), the OBBs are **disjoint**. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Minkowski Difference.** The **Minkowski difference** of two convex sets $A$ and $B$ is defined as:

$$A \ominus B = \{a - b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$$

A crucial property: $A$ and $B$ **overlap** if and only if the **origin $O$** lies within the Minkowski difference $A \ominus B$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Support Function.** The support function $S_{C}(\mathbf{d})$ of a convex set $C$ in direction $\mathbf{d}$ returns the extreme point:

$$S_{C}(\mathbf{d}) = \arg\max_{p \in C} (\mathbf{d} \cdot p)$$

For the Minkowski difference, the support function has the elegant decomposition:

$$S_{A \ominus B}(\mathbf{d}) = S_{A}(\mathbf{d}) - S_{B}(-\mathbf{d})$$

This is the **central insight** of GJK: we never need to explicitly construct the Minkowski difference (which is exponentially complex); we just evaluate the support functions of $A$ and $B$ independently. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — GJK Iterative Loop.** GJK maintains a simplex (a set of 1, 2, 3, or 4 points from $A \ominus B$) and iterates:
1. Find the **closest point on the simplex to the origin**.
2. Compute the direction $\mathbf{d}$ from this closest point to the origin.
3. Compute the new support point $S_{A \ominus B}(\mathbf{d})$.
4. If the new support point does not extend past the closest point (within $\epsilon$ tolerance), the algorithm terminates — the origin is **not** in $A \ominus B$, so $A$ and $B$ are **disjoint**.
5. Otherwise, add the new support point to the simplex and repeat (with simplex reduction via Johnson's sub-algorithm). **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Time Complexity.** GJK runs in **$O(n)$** time per query, where $n$ is the number of vertices of the smaller polytope, and in practice it converges in a small constant number of iterations (typically 5-10) for most convex shapes. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Bounding Volume" with "Shape":** A bounding volume is **not** the object itself; it is a **simpler primitive** that encloses the object. Writing "the AABB is the object" will cost you 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $t \geq 0$ constraint:** In ray equations, students often forget that $t$ must be non-negative (the ray is **half-line**, not a full line). Solutions with $t < 0$ correspond to intersections **behind** the camera, which must be rejected.
>
> 3. **Ray-Sphere discriminant sign:** Students frequently write "if $\Delta < 0$, the ray hits" — this is **wrong**. $\Delta < 0$ means the quadratic has **no real solutions**, i.e., **no intersection**.
>
> 4. **Mishandling the unit normal:** When $\mathbf{n}$ is not a unit vector, the ray-plane formula $t = \frac{(P_0 - O) \cdot \mathbf{n}}{\mathbf{d} \cdot \mathbf{n}}$ is **incorrect** unless $\mathbf{d}$ is also a unit vector, or you divide by $\vert \mathbf{n} \vert^2$ appropriately. Always **normalize** $\mathbf{n}$ first.
>
> 5. **SAT axes in 3D:** Many students list only the 6 face normals and forget the 9 edge-edge cross products. In KTU exams, the question specifically asks for **15 axes** for two OBBs in 3D; missing the cross-product axes typically costs 2 marks.
>
