```mermaid
    L --> K
```

**Reading guide:** Duality and configuration space are the **two fundamental transformation tools** of computational geometry. Both convert hard geometric problems into easier ones — duality converts *static* problems (hull, arrangement), C-space converts *dynamic* problems (planning, optimization).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define point–line duality transform. State any four properties it preserves.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *point–line duality* is a bijective mapping $\mathcal{D}$ between points and non-vertical lines in the plane defined by $\mathcal{D}(a, b): y = ax - b$ and $\mathcal{D}(y = mx + c) = (m, -c)$. The four key properties preserved (or reversed) are: **(1) Incidence** — $p \in \ell \iff \ell^* \in p^*$; **(2) Order reversal** — above/below in the primal becomes below/above in the dual; **(3) Collinearity ↔ Concurrency** — collinear points correspond to concurrent lines and vice-versa; **(4) Intersection ↔ Line construction** — intersection of two primal lines maps to the line joining their dual points. (1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for four properties)

### Q2. What is configuration space? Why is it used in motion planning?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible placements (configurations) of a robot, parameterized by its degrees of freedom. For a translating rigid body in 2-D, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$. **(Why used — 2 marks):** It **(1)** reduces a *shape-shifting* planning problem to a *point* navigation problem by enlarging obstacles via the Minkowski difference $\mathcal{C O}_i = \mathcal{O}_i \ominus \mathcal{R}$, **(2)** allows classical graph-search algorithms (Dijkstra, A*) to be applied to find collision-free paths in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{C O}_i$, and **(3)** provides a clean mathematical abstraction unifying geometry, kinematics, and obstacles. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 marks] Apply the standard point–line duality to the points $p_1 = (2, 3)$, $p_2 = (4, 7)$, $p_3 = (1, 5)$ and the line $\ell: y = 3x + 2$. Verify the incidence property and the order-reversal property.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the dual lines of the three points.**

For $p_1 = (2, 3)$: substitute $a=2$, $b=3$ into $y = ax - b$:
$$p_1^{*}: y = 2x - 3$$

For $p_2 = (4, 7)$: substitute $a=4$, $b=7$:
$$p_2^{*}: y = 4x - 7$$

For $p_3 = (1, 5)$: substitute $a=1$, $b=5$:
$$p_3^{*}: y = x - 5$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dual point of $\ell$.**

For $\ell: y = 3x + 2$ (so $m=3$, $c=2$):
$$\ell^{*} = (m, -c) = (3, -2)$$

**Step 3 — Verify incidence for $p_2 \in \ell$.**

Check: $b_2 = 7$ and $m a_2 + c = 3 \cdot 4 + 2 = 14$. Since $7 \ne 14$, $p_2 \notin \ell$. Hence $\ell^{*} \notin p_2^{*}$. Verify: at $x = 3$, $p_2^{*}$ gives $y = 4(3) - 7 = 5$. The point $\ell^* = (3, -2)$ has $y = -2 \ne 5$. ✓ **Incidence preserved.**

**Step 4 — Verify order reversal for $p_3$ above $\ell$.**

Check primal: $b_3 = 5$, $m a_3 + c = 3(1) + 2 = 5$. They are equal — $p_3$ is **on** $\ell$ (not strictly above). So the test is degenerate; pick another.

**Step 4 (revised) — Test $p_1$ vs $\ell$.**

Primal: $b_1 = 3$, $m a_1 + c = 3(2) + 2 = 8$. So $3 < 8$, meaning $p_1$ is **below** $\ell$ in the primal.

**Dual check:** At $x = 3$, the line $p_1^{*}: y = 2x - 3$ has $y = 2(3) - 3 = 3$. The dual point $\ell^* = (3, -2)$ has $y = -2$. So $3 > -2$, meaning $p_1^*$ passes **above** $\ell^*$.

**Conclusion:** Below in primal → Above in dual. ✓ **Order reversed.** (7 marks distributed: 2 for dual computation, 2 for incidence check, 3 for order-reversal verification with clear comparison.)

#### (b) [7 marks] Explain the configuration space approach to motion planning for a translating convex polygonal robot among convex polygonal obstacles. Show the construction of the configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C O}_i$ using the Minkowski difference. What is the complexity of $\mathcal{C O}_i$?

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1.5 marks):** In the configuration space (C-space) approach, introduced by Lozano-Pérez (1983), the robot is abstracted to a single point and the obstacles are enlarged by the shape of the robot using the **Minkowski difference**. This converts the problem of moving a shape through space into the simpler problem of moving a point through enlarged space.

**Mathematical construction (3 marks):** Let the robot be a convex polygon $\mathcal{R}$ with reference point $r$ (e.g., centroid), and let the $i$-th obstacle be a convex polygon $\mathcal{O}_i$. The robot at configuration (translation vector) $q$ occupies the set $\mathcal{R} \oplus \{q\} = \{p + q : p \in \mathcal{R}\}$. A collision with $\mathcal{O}_i$ occurs iff:
$$(\mathcal{R} \oplus \{q\}) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \ne \varnothing \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; q \in \mathcal{O}_i \ominus \mathcal{R}$$

The **configuration-space obstacle** is defined as:
$$\mathcal{C O}_i = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 : (\mathcal{R} \oplus \{q\}) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \ne \varnothing\} = \mathcal{O}_i \ominus \mathcal{R} = \{o - p : o \in \mathcal{O}_i, \; p \in \mathcal{R}\}$$

Geometrically, $\mathcal{C O}_i$ is obtained by sweeping $\mathcal{R}$ (or its reflection $-\mathcal{R}$) along the boundary of $\mathcal{O}_i$. The free configuration space is:
$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \mathcal{C O}_i$$

A collision-free path from $q_s$ to $q_g$ is a continuous curve $\tau \subset \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with $\tau(0) = q_s$ and $\tau(1) = q_g$. (1.5 marks for free space and path statement.)

**Complexity (2 marks):** For a convex robot with $m$ vertices and a convex obstacle with $n_i$ vertices, the resulting $\mathcal{C O}_i$ is a convex polygon with $m + n_i$ vertices. The total C-space complexity is $N = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(m + n_i) = km + \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$. The construction time per obstacle is $O(m + n_i)$ using a standard convex-polygon Minkowski-sum algorithm (e.g., the rotating-calipers merge).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The C-space approach is the cornerstone of algorithmic motion planning; once $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is built, classical shortest-path algorithms (Dijkstra, A*) on a roadmap can find the optimal collision-free path.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 marks] State and prove the key incidence property of standard point–line duality. Illustrate with an example.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** For the standard point–line duality $\mathcal{D}$ defined by $\mathcal{D}(a, b): y = ax - b$ and $\mathcal{D}(y = mx + c) = (m, -c)$:
$$p = (a, b) \text{ lies on line } \ell: y = mx + c \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \ell^* = (m, -c) \text{ lies on line } p^*: y = ax - b$$

**Proof (4 marks):** ($$\Rightarrow$$) Suppose $p \in \ell$, i.e., $b = ma + c$. We must show $\ell^* \in p^*$. Substituting $\ell^* = (m, -c)$ into $p^*: y = ax - b$ at $x = m$ gives:
$$y = a \cdot m - b = am - b$$
From the incidence $b = ma + c$, we get $am - b = am - (ma + c) = -c$. So the $y$-value on $p^*$ at $x = m$ is exactly $-c$, which matches the $y$-coordinate of $\ell^*$. Thus $\ell^* = (m, -c)$ lies on $p^*$.

($\Leftarrow$) Conversely, if $\ell^* = (m, -c)$ lies on $p^*: y = ax - b$, then at $x = m$: $-c = a \cdot m - b$, which gives $b = am + c$, i.e., $p = (a, b)$ lies on $\ell: y = mx + c$. $\blacksquare$

**Example (1.5 marks):** Let $p = (3, 8)$ and $\ell: y = 2x + 2$. Check: $3 \cdot 2 + 2 = 8$ ✓, so $p \in \ell$. Dual point: $\ell^* = (2, -2)$. Dual line: $p^*: y = 3x - 8$. Check $\ell^* \in p^*$: at $x = 2$, $p^*$ gives $y = 3 \cdot 2 - 8 = -2$. So $(2, -2) \in p^*$. ✓

#### (b) [7 marks] Discuss the visibility graph approach to motion planning. State its construction, complexity, and one limitation.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **visibility graph** $G = (V, E)$ of a polygonal environment is the graph whose vertices $V$ are the polygon vertices plus the start $q_s$ and goal $q_g$, with an edge between two vertices iff the straight segment between them is fully contained in the free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ (i.e., neither endpoint's segment intersects the interior of any obstacle).

**Construction (2.5 marks):** For each vertex $u \in V$, perform a **rotational sweep** about $u$ and test line-of-sight to all other vertices. Two vertices are connected by an edge iff the line segment $uv$ does not cross any obstacle edge (i.e., for every obstacle edge $e$, the segments $uv$ and $e$ do not properly intersect). Naïve: $O(n^3)$. With the Lee (1978) algorithm: $O(n^2)$.

**Shortest-path query (1.5 marks):** Once the graph is built, run **Dijkstra's algorithm** (or A*) with Euclidean edge weights $|uv|$ to find the shortest collision-free path from $q_s$ to $q_g$. Total query time: $O(n^2 + n \log n) = O(n^2)$.

**Limitation (1.5 marks):** The visibility graph produces the **shortest Euclidean path** in the limit (because the optimal path "touches" obstacle vertices), but **(1)** it scales poorly to **higher dimensions** (3-D and 6+ DoF arms give super-quadratic graphs), **(2)** it offers **no clearance** from obstacles — the robot may graze walls, which is unsafe in real-world applications with sensor/control uncertainty, and **(3)** the shortest path is **not smooth** — it has sharp corners at vertices, which a real robot with bounded turning radius cannot follow without further post-processing (e.g., smoothing or shortcutting).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in the dual line intercept:** The dual of $(a, b)$ is $y = ax - b$, **not** $y = ax + b$. Mixing this up makes every subsequent computation wrong and is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.
> 2. **Forgetting to handle vertical lines:** The standard duality cannot directly map a vertical line $x = k$. The projective extension is required, or one should explicitly state "we assume general position with no vertical lines."
> 3. **Confusing dual and inverse:** A common error is to write the dual of $\ell: y = 3x + 5$ as $(3, 5)$ instead of $(3, -5)$. The negative sign is essential.
> 4. **C-space dimensionality confusion:** For a free-flying rigid body in 3-D, the configuration space is $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$, i.e., **6-dimensional**, not 3-D. Students often write the workspace dimension instead.
> 5. **Forgetting the Minkowski difference in $\mathcal{C O}_i$:** Many students write $\mathcal{C O}_i = \mathcal{O}_i$ (no transformation), losing the central insight of the C-space approach.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Duality mapping rules (memorize):** Point $(a, b) \to$ Line $y = ax - b$; Line $y = mx + c \to$ Point $(m, -c)$. The transform is **involutory** (self-inverse).
- **Key preserved properties:** Incidence, collinearity ↔ concurrency, intersection ↔ line construction. **Key reversed property:** vertical order (above/below swapped).
- **Convex hull ↔ Half-plane intersection** is the most exploited duality in $O(n \log n)$ algorithms.
- **Configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$ is the parameter space of all robot placements; $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{C O}_i$ is the set of safe configurations.
- **Minkowski difference** $\mathcal{C O}_i = \mathcal{O}_i \ominus \mathcal{R}$ converts a shape-moving problem into a point-moving problem.
- **C-space complexity** for convex polygon robot ($m$ vertices) and convex obstacle ($n_i$ vertices): $\mathcal{C O}_i$ has $m + n_i$ vertices.
- **Motion-planning algorithms:** Visibility Graph ($O(n^2)$, optimal in 2-D, no clearance), Voronoi Diagram ($O(n \log n)$, max clearance, not shortest), Cell Decomposition ($O(n \log n)$, simple), RRT/PRM (probabilistic, high-dimensional).
- **Geometric optimization** problems (shortest path, MST, smallest enclosing circle, TSP) are the natural next stage once the free space and roadmap are built.
- **Engineering applications:** Robotics, autonomous vehicles, VLSI routing, CAD/CAM, surgical robotics, computational biology, computer graphics, GPS/GNSS route planning.
- **Default KTU marks allocation:** Part A (3 marks) — definition + 1 property; Part B (14 marks) — split as (a) 7 marks derivation + (b) 7 marks application.
- **Examiner's favourite topic for 14-mark questions:** *Apply duality to a given point set and verify two properties*, or *Derive the C-space obstacle and compute its vertex count*.
- **Watch out for:** vertical-line edge cases, sign of intercept, C-space dimension vs workspace dimension, and the difference between *shortest* path and *clearance-maximizing* path.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visibility Graphs and Shortest Path Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Visibility Graphs and Shortest Path Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visibility Graph — Formal Definition

A **Visibility Graph** $G = (V, E)$ of a polygonal environment $P$ is a planar graph in which:

- The **vertex set** $V$ consists of the vertices of the polygon (and, optionally, the source $s$ and target $t$).
- An edge $(u, v) \in E$ exists if and only if the open line segment $\overline{uv}$ lies entirely in the interior of $P$ (or on its boundary) — that is, $u$ and $v$ are **mutually visible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECST418 Module 4):**
> A visibility graph is the *discrete skeleton* of all possible "line-of-sight" connections inside a polygon. It transforms a continuous geometric problem into a combinatorial graph problem that classical shortest-path algorithms (Dijkstra, A*) can solve.

## 1.2 Shortest Path Problem — Formal Definition

Given a polygonal domain $P$ with $n$ vertices, a source point $s$ and a target point $t$, the **Euclidean shortest path problem** asks for the path from $s$ to $t$ contained in $P$ that minimizes the total Euclidean length. The optimal path is guaranteed to be a polygonal chain whose **internal vertices are reflex vertices of $P$**, and which belongs to the visibility graph of $P \cup \{s, t\}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Laser Pointer" Picture**
>
> Imagine you are inside a dark, irregularly shaped room. You carry a laser pointer. For every pair of wall-corners, ask: *"Can I shine a laser from corner A to corner B without it being blocked by any wall?"* Every such pair that answers "yes" gets a string tied between A and B. After checking all pairs, the resulting string-mesh is your **visibility graph**.
>
> Now suppose a robot must travel from the door to a charging station. The shortest collision-free route will always be a sequence of corners it touches — in other words, a path on this string-mesh. The shortest such path is the **Euclidean shortest path**.

## 1.4 Physical / Geometric Constants Referenced

- **Euclidean distance** between two points $p_i = (x_i, y_i)$ and $p_j = (x_j, y_j)$:
$$d(p_i, p_j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$$

- **Reflex vertex** standard metric: interior angle $\theta_i > 180^\circ$.
- **Convex vertex** standard metric: interior angle $\theta_i < 180^\circ$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Visibility Graphs Solve Shortest Paths in Polygons

Classical Dijkstra works on a graph with $V$ vertices and $E$ edges in $O(E + V \log V)$ time. To use it for shortest geometric paths inside a polygon, we need a *discrete graph* whose edges represent legal motion. The visibility graph is exactly that discretisation.

**Key theoretical facts (high-yield for KTU):**

1. The shortest $s$–$t$ path in a simple polygon lies on the visibility graph of $P$ extended by $\{s, t\}$.
2. Internal vertices of any optimal path must be **reflex vertices of $P$** whose visibility to the previous/next waypoint is unobstructed.
3. Number of edges in the visibility graph of a simple polygon with $n$ vertices is $O(n^2)$ in the worst case (achieved by "comb" or "spiral" polygons) and $\Omega(n)$ in the best case (convex polygon $\Rightarrow n$ edges).

## 2.2 Construction Strategies (Operational Logic)

### Strategy A — Rotational Sweep (per vertex)
For each vertex $v_i$ of the polygon:
1. Sort all other vertices $v_j$ by polar angle around $v_i$.
2. Sweep a ray from $v_i$ in increasing angle.
3. Maintain the *nearest* polygon edge intersecting the ray; only the closest visible vertex emits an edge.

**Time complexity:** $O(n^2 \log n)$ naïve, optimisable to $O(n^2)$.

### Strategy B — Lee's Algorithm (1984) — Output-Sensitive
A celebrated algorithm that computes the visibility graph of a simple polygon with $n$ vertices in:
$$O(E_{\text{vis}} + n \log n)$$
where $E_{\text{vis}}$ is the number of visibility edges actually produced. This is **optimal** in the worst case because the output size is $\Theta(E_{\text{vis}})$.

### Strategy C — Visibility-Complex Approach (for polygon *with holes*)
For polygons with $h$ holes, the visibility graph is no longer polynomial in $n$ — it can have $O(n^2 \cdot 2^{h})$ edges, making exact solutions impractical. Approximate / shortest-path approaches such as the **Continuous Dijkstra (Mitchell's algorithm)** or **funnel algorithms** are then preferred.

## 2.3 Shortest Path Algorithms Used on the Visibility Graph

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Suitable For | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | $O(E + V \log V)$ | Non-negative weights | Default on vis. graph |
| A* Search | $O(E + V \log V)$ worst | Heuristic-guided | Speeds up via Euclidean heuristic |
| Funnel Algorithm | $O(n + k \log n)$ | Triangulated polygon | Optimal once triangulation given |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **must-memorise** formula set for Module 4 problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean edge weight | $w(u,v) = \sqrt{(x_u - x_v)^2 + (y_u - y_v)^2}$ | Length units (e.g., m) |
| 2 | Reflex vertex | $\theta_i > \pi \;\;(180^\circ)$ | Internal angle |
| 3 | Convex vertex | $\theta_i < \pi$ | Internal angle |
| 4 | Visibility graph edge bound (simple polygon) | $\vert E \vert \le 2 \binom{n}{2} = n(n-1)$ | Worst case $O(n^2)$ |
| 5 | Visibility graph construction (Lee) | $T(n) = O(\vert E \vert + n \log n)$ | Output-sensitive |
| 6 | Dijkstra on vis. graph | $O(n^2 \log n)$ or $O(n^2)$ with heap | After graph built |
| 7 | Funnel algorithm on triang. polygon | $O(n + k \log n)$ | $k$ = # query paths |
| 8 | Polygon with $h$ holes — vis. edges | $O\!\left(n^2 \cdot 2^{h}\right)$ | Exponential in $h$ |
| 9 | Shortest path property | Optimal path vertices $\subseteq$ reflex vertices of $P$ | Fundamental theorem |
| 10 | Length of polygonal path | $L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2+(y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$ | Sum of Euclidean edges |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles:** Motion planning in cluttered indoor spaces uses visibility-graph-based A*.
- **VLSI CAD:** Wire routing on a single layer reduces to a shortest-path-in-polygon problem with rectangular obstacles.
- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Routing through polygonal land parcels.
- **Video Game AI:** NPC navigation through polygonal maps.
- **Urban Planning:** Pedestrian walkway design respecting building footprints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Upper Bound on Edges of a Simple Visibility Graph

We derive $\vert E \vert \le n(n-1)$ for a simple polygon with $n$ vertices.

**Step 1 — Total possible edges in an undirected simple graph:**
The maximum number of undirected edges between $n$ distinct vertices, with no self-loops, is the binomial coefficient:
$$
E_{\max} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Visibility is a strict subset of "all pairs":**
Every visibility edge is by definition an edge between two polygon vertices. Hence:
$$
E_{\text{vis}} \subseteq E_{\max} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E_{\text{vis}} \le \binom{n}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Loosening to big-O:**
Multiplying by 2 (to absorb the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ into the constant) and writing asymptotically:
$$
E_{\text{vis}} = O(n^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Show tightness (comb polygon):**
Consider a "comb" polygon formed by a horizontal base with $n/2$ vertical teeth of increasing length. Each tooth tip is visible to every other tooth tip, yielding:
$$
E_{\text{vis}} = \Theta(n^2)
$$
Therefore the $O(n^2)$ bound is **tight**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Path Length via Successive Euclidean Sums

Given a polygonal path $\pi = (s, v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k, t)$ in the visibility graph, the total length is:

**Step 1 — Identify consecutive pairs:**
The path consists of $k+1$ straight segments, between $(s, v_1), (v_1, v_2), \dots, (v_k, t)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Euclidean distance to each segment:**

$$
L(\pi) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{k} \sqrt{\bigl(x_{\pi(i+1)} - x_{\pi(i)}\bigr)^2 + \bigl(y_{\pi(i+1)} - y_{\pi(i)}\bigr)^2}
$$

where $\pi(0) = s$ and $\pi(k+1) = t$.

**Step 3 — Expand an example for two segments** (source $s=(0,0)$, waypoint $w=(3,4)$, target $t=(7,1)$):

$$
L \;=\; \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} \;+\; \sqrt{(7-3)^2 + (1-4)^2}
$$

$$
L \;=\; \sqrt{25} \;+\; \sqrt{16+9}
$$

$$
L \;=\; 5 \;+\; 5 \;=\; 10
$$

**Step 4 — Verify monotonicity:** If a path $\pi'$ differs from $\pi$ by replacing an internal vertex $v_i$ with a vertex $v_i'$ that lies on the segment $\overline{v_{i-1} v_{i+1}}$ (collinear case), then by the triangle inequality in Euclidean form:
$$
d(v_{i-1}, v_i) + d(v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\ge\; d(v_{i-1}, v_{i+1})
$$
with equality iff $v_i \in \overline{v_{i-1} v_{i+1}}$. This proves that "redundant" intermediate vertices only increase path length.

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Internal Vertices of the Shortest Path are Reflex

Let $P$ be a simple polygon and $\pi^*$ an optimal $s$–$t$ path in $P$.

**Step 1 — Suppose for contradiction** that $\pi^*$ contains a convex vertex $v_c$ as an internal point.

**Step 2 — By convexity of $v_c$:** the local wedge of free space around $v_c$ is convex, and the two edges of $\pi^*$ meeting at $v_c$ form an angle of at most $180^\circ$.

**Step 3 — Replace the kink at $v_c$** by a straight segment from its predecessor $p$ to successor $q$. By the triangle inequality:
$$
d(p, v_c) + d(v_c, q) \;\ge\; d(p, q)
$$
and the new segment lies in $P$ (because $v_c$ is convex ⇒ the wedge at $v_c$ is fully in $P$).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** $v_c$ can always be shortcut, contradicting the optimality of $\pi^*$ (which was assumed to have *no* shortcuttable vertex). Hence:
$$
\text{Internal vertices of } \pi^* \subseteq \{\text{reflex vertices of } P\}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Algorithm

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Simple polygon, one source-target query | Visibility graph + Dijkstra | Exact, $O(n^2)$ post-construction |
| Simple polygon, multiple queries | Triangulate + Funnel algorithm | $O(n)$ preproc., $O(\log n)$ per query |
| Polygon with $h$ holes, $h$ small | Visibility graph with continuous Dijkstra | Handles holes |
| Polygon with many holes | Sampling-based (PRM, RRT) | Approximate, practical |
| Real-time game / large map | NavMesh + A* heuristic | Trade accuracy for speed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: From Polygon to Shortest Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Polygon P and points s, t] --> B[Enumerate all vertices V of P]
    B --> C[For each vertex vi in V]
    C --> D[Compute visibility: check all polygon edges]
    D --> E{Is segment vi-vj blocked?}
    E -- No --> F[Add edge vi-vj to visibility graph]
    E -- Yes --> G[Discard edge]
    F --> H{All pairs processed?}
    G --> H
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Insert source s and target t into graph]
    I --> J[Apply Dijkstra or A search from s]
    J --> K[Output shortest polygonal path s -> t]
```

## 4.2 Visibility-Graph Construction Flow (Rotational Sweep View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerVertexLoop
        P0[Select current vertex vi] --> P1[Sort other vertices by polar angle]
        P1 --> P2[Sweep ray counterclockwise]
        P2 --> P3[Track nearest blocking edge]
        P3 --> P4{Visible vertex reached?}
        P4 -- Yes --> P5[Emit edge vi to vj]
        P4 -- No --> P6[Skip vj]
        P5 --> P7{All angles swept?}
        P6 --> P7
        P7 -- No --> P2
        P7 -- Yes --> P8[Next vertex]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Diagram: Which Shortest-Path Algorithm?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given: polygon with n vertices and source s, target t] --> Q2{Is the polygon simple?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Number of queries?}
    Q3 -- Single query --> Q4[Build full visibility graph + Dijkstra]
    Q3 -- Multiple queries --> Q5[Triangulate polygon once + Funnel algorithm]
    Q2 -- No, has holes --> Q7{Number of holes h small?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Continuous Dijkstra / Mitchell]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9[Sampling-based planners PRM or RRT]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Visibility-Graph-Based Motion Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputStage [Input Stage]
        I1[Polygon P]
        I2[Source s]
        I3[Target t]
    end

    subgraph BuildStage [Visibility Graph Builder]
        B1[Vertex Extractor]
        B2[Edge Tester: segment vs polygon]
        B3[Edge Weight Assigner: Euclidean distance]
    end

    subgraph SearchStage [Path Search Engine]
        S1[Dijkstra or A search module]
        S2[Open set priority queue]
        S3[Closed set and predecessor map]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [Output Stage]
        O1[Polygonal path s to t]
        O2[Total length L]
    end

    I1 --> B1
    I2 --> B1
    I3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S1
    S3 --> O1
    S1 --> O2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE pattern for PECST418):**
> Part A: 3-mark short answers (no choice) — recall / understand level.
> Part B: 14-mark questions with **internal choice** (answer ONE out of two).
> Bloom's tags used: *R = Remember, U = Understand, Ap = Apply, An = Analyze, E = Evaluate*.

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU Model Paper 2024]`
**Define a visibility graph. Mention its vertex and edge sets for a simple polygon.** `[Bloom: R, CO4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A visibility graph $G = (V, E)$ of a simple polygon $P$ with $n$ vertices is defined as:

- **Vertex set $V$:** the set of all vertices of polygon $P$, optionally augmented with the source $s$ and target $t$ points.
- **Edge set $E$:** an unordered pair $(u, v) \in E$ exists iff the open line segment $\overline{uv}$ lies entirely in the closed interior of $P$ (i.e., $u$ and $v$ are mutually visible to each other through $P$).

Each edge $(u,v)$ is assigned a weight equal to the Euclidean distance $d(u,v) = \sqrt{(x_u - x_v)^2 + (y_u - y_v)^2}$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU Model Paper 2024]`
**State the fundamental property that any internal vertex of an optimal Euclidean shortest path inside a simple polygon must satisfy.** `[Bloom: U, CO4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Any internal vertex of an optimal (shortest) Euclidean $s$–$t$ path inside a simple polygon $P$ must be a **reflex vertex of $P$** (a vertex whose interior angle exceeds $180^\circ$).

**Justification:** A convex vertex can always be shortcut by a straight line (triangle inequality) lying inside the locally convex wedge, producing a strictly shorter path — contradicting optimality. Reflex vertices, however, are necessary waypoints because a straight shortcut would cross the polygon boundary.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct the visibility graph of the polygon with vertices
$A=(0,0),\; B=(6,0),\; C=(6,4),\; D=(3,4),\; E=(3,2),\; F=(0,2)$
and source $S=(1,1)$, target $T=(5,3)$. Identify which edges are present, and explain your reasoning for at least three non-trivial visibility checks. **[7 marks]** `[Bloom: Ap, CO4]`

**(b)** Using the visibility graph from part (a), apply Dijkstra's algorithm starting from $S$ to determine the shortest path to $T$. Show the priority-queue state at each extraction step and report the total path length. **[7 marks]** `[Bloom: An, CO4]`

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify reflex vertices.

Compute interior angles by walking the polygon $A\to B\to C\to D\to E\to F\to A$:
- $A$: $90^\circ$ (convex)
- $B$: $90^\circ$ (convex)
- $C$: $90^\circ$ (convex)
- $D$: interior angle formed by edges $C\to D$ (going right→left) and $D\to E$ (going down) ⇒ $270^\circ$ ⇒ **reflex**
- $E$: $270^\circ$ ⇒ **reflex**
- $F$: $90^\circ$ (convex)

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Test visibility of every candidate pair. Sample three non-trivial checks:

- **$\overline{D\to B}$:** segment from $(3,4)$ to $(6,0)$. Parameterise and check intersection with edge $C\to D$ (the segment $y=4,\; 3 \le x \le 6$). The two segments share only the endpoint $D$, so the interior of $\overline{DB}$ does not cross $C\to D$. The segment lies outside the polygon in the upper-right region. Hence $\overline{DB}$ is **not visible** (exits polygon at $D$).
- **$\overline{E\to B}$:** from $(3,2)$ to $(6,0)$. The interior of this segment is outside the polygon. **Not visible.**
- **$\overline{A\to E}$:** from $(0,0)$ to $(3,2)$. It crosses the edge $F\to A$ at $A$ only (endpoint) and does not enter the interior of any other edge. **Visible.**
- **$\overline{S\to T}$:** from $(1,1)$ to $(5,3)$. The line is $y - 1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(x - 1)$. The edge $E\to D$ is $x=3, \; 2 \le y \le 4$. At $x=3$, $y=2$, which is the endpoint $E$. No other edge is crossed. Hence $\overline{ST}$ is **visible** and is itself the shortest path of length
$$
L(S,T) = \sqrt{16+4} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** List the full edge set (selected):

$$
V' = \{S, A, B, C, D, E, F, T\}
$$
$$
E' = \{SA, SF, AB, BC, CD, DE, EF, FA, ST, SD, SE, FT, AE, AT\}
$$
(Marks for completeness; full enumeration expected.)

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Highlight the *trick* of testing collinear intersections and endpoint-touching intersections, which do not block visibility.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Initialise Dijkstra.

| Vertex | dist | prev |
|---|---|---|
| S | 0 | — |
| A | $\infty$ | — |
| B | $\infty$ | — |
| C | $\infty$ | — |
| D | $\infty$ | — |
| E | $\infty$ | — |
| F | $\infty$ | — |
| T | $\infty$ | — |

Edge weights: $SA = \sqrt{2}$, $SF = \sqrt{2}$, $SD = \sqrt{(3-1)^2+(4-1)^2} = \sqrt{13}$, $SE = \sqrt{5}$, $ST = 2\sqrt{5}$, $AT = \sqrt{26}$, $FT = \sqrt{25+1} = \sqrt{26}$, $AE = \sqrt{9+4}=\sqrt{13}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Extract $S$. Update neighbours: $A\leftarrow\sqrt{2}$, $F\leftarrow\sqrt{2}$, $D\leftarrow\sqrt{13}$, $E\leftarrow\sqrt{5}$, $T\leftarrow 2\sqrt{5}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Extract $A$ (dist $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$). Update $T$: candidate via $A$ is $\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{26} \approx 1.414 + 5.099 = 6.513 > 2\sqrt{5}$. No update. Update $E$: $\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{13} \approx 1.414 + 3.606 = 5.020 > \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$. No update.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Extract $F$ (dist $\sqrt{2}$). Update $T$: $\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{26} \approx 6.513$. No update.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Extract $E$ (dist $\sqrt{5}$). Update $D$ via $E$: $\sqrt{5} + d(E,D) = \sqrt{5}+2 \approx 4.236 < \sqrt{13}\approx 3.606$? No, $4.236 > 3.606$. No update. Update $T$ via $E$: $\sqrt{5} + d(E,T) = \sqrt{5} + \sqrt{5} = 2\sqrt{5}$. No change.

**Final result:** Shortest path is the **direct segment $S\to T$** of length
$$
L = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the rotational-sweep method for constructing the visibility graph of a simple polygon. State its time complexity and justify the bound. **[7 marks]** `[Bloom: U, CO4]`

**(b)** For a polygon with $n=10$ vertices in the worst case (a "comb" configuration), how many visibility edges can the visibility graph contain? Justify using the bound on edges. Compare this with the lower bound for a convex polygon. **[7 marks]** `[Bloom: An, E, CO4]`

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the algorithm.

For each vertex $v_i$ of the polygon $P$ (where $i = 1, \dots, n$):
1. Compute polar angles of all other vertices $v_j$ ($j \ne i$) with respect to $v_i$.
2. Sort these angles in counterclockwise order, producing a sequence $\theta_1 < \theta_2 < \dots < \theta_{n-1}$.
3. Sweep a ray from $v_i$ counterclockwise, maintaining the *nearest* polygon edge that intersects the current ray direction.
4. The first vertex reached by the ray at any direction is visible; emit the edge $(v_i, v_j)$. Subsequent vertices at the same angle (collinear cases) are handled with a tie-break rule.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Justify the inner loop.
At any sweep angle, only the **nearest** polygon edge matters. Therefore, between two consecutive angle events, no visibility status changes. The number of "events" per vertex is $O(n)$, and processing each event is $O(1)$ using a balanced BST that stores current intersecting edges by radial distance.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** State and derive the overall complexity.
- Per vertex: $O(n \log n)$ for sorting + $O(n)$ for sweep.
- Total: $n \cdot O(n \log n) = O(n^2 \log n)$.
- Using an angular sort based on a global reference (advanced techniques), this reduces to **$O(n^2)$** overall.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention that Lee's algorithm improves this to $O(\vert E \vert + n \log n)$, which is *output-sensitive*.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the upper bound.

For any simple polygon with $n$ vertices, the number of edges in the visibility graph is at most:
$$
\vert E \vert \le \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$
For $n = 10$:
$$
\vert E \vert \le \frac{10 \cdot 9}{2} = 45
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Construct a comb polygon with $n=10$.

Take a horizontal base of length 5, with 4 vertical "teeth" pointing up, each at a distinct $x$-coordinate. Plus 4 short "teeth" caps, plus 2 base endpoints. The two endpoints of each tooth (one on the base, one at the tip) are mutually visible to those of every other tooth. The number of tip-to-tip visibilities is $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ and similarly for the base anchors. Including the polygon's own $n$ boundary edges, total visibility edges is close to the maximum.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compare with convex case.

In a convex polygon, every pair of vertices is visible (no interior edges to block), so:
$$
\vert E_{\text{convex}} \vert = \binom{n}{2} = 45
$$
In a *strictly convex* polygon this is also the maximum. For *non-convex* polygons, the bound still holds but actual $\vert E \vert$ can be much smaller (e.g., star-shaped or spiral polygons with $\Theta(n)$ visibilities).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude:
- **Upper bound (any simple polygon):** $O(n^2)$.
- **Lower bound (convex / comb):** $\Omega(n^2)$.
- **Minimum (spiral polygons):** $\Omega(n)$.

Hence the visibility graph size spans a wide range depending on polygon shape.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
>
> 1. **Forgetting endpoint-tangent intersections are NOT blockers.** Students often wrongly mark an edge as blocked when the candidate segment merely touches a polygon vertex. Visibility requires the *interior* of the segment to avoid crossing the *interior* of polygon edges.
> 2. **Stating "all pairs are visible" for a non-convex polygon.** The visibility graph is *not* complete for reflex-heavy polygons. Always verify with a ray-segment intersection test.
> 3. **Using Dijkstra without the visibility graph.** Dijkstra on the polygon's *boundary* graph (just the $n$ perimeter edges) gives the shortest *boundary* walk, **not** the shortest *interior* Euclidean path. Both are different problems.
> 4. **Confusing $\binom{n}{2}$ with $n^2$.** The factor of 2 in the denominator matters for the exact edge count; use it to be precise in derivations.
> 5. **Ignoring the reflex-vertex theorem.** Examiners award marks for justifying *why* the optimal path is built from reflex vertices; missing this justification is a 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4, Visibility Graphs & Shortest Paths**

- **Visibility graph $G = (V, E)$**: vertices = polygon vertices $\cup \{s, t\}$; edges = mutually visible pairs.
- **Edge weight** = Euclidean distance $d(u, v) = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$.
- **Maximum number of edges** = $\binom{n}{2} = \tfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (achieved by convex or comb polygons).
- **Reflex vertex** (interior angle $> 180^\circ$) — *mandatory* waypoint in any optimal path.
- **Convex vertex** (interior angle $< 180^\circ$) — *never* an internal vertex of the optimal path.
- **Construction algorithms:**
  - Rotational sweep: $O(n^2 \log n)$ or $O(n^2)$.
  - Lee's algorithm: $O(\vert E \vert + n \log n)$ — output-sensitive, optimal for simple polygons.
- **Shortest path algorithms on visibility graph:**
  - Dijkstra: $O(n^2)$ with a binary heap for sparse graphs; $O(n^2 \log n)$ generally.
  - A* search: same worst-case bound, faster in practice with Euclidean heuristic.
  - Funnel algorithm (after triangulation): $O(\log n)$ per query.
- **Polygon with holes** $h$ can have up to $O(n^2 \cdot 2^h)$ visibility edges — exponential in $h$.
- **Triangle inequality** is the proof engine for: (a) convex-vertex shortcut, (b) collinearity redundancy, (c) path-length derivation.
- **Real-world domains:** robot motion planning, VLSI routing, GIS navigation, video game AI.
- **Examiner tip:** Always pair each step with a brief justification — KTU 2024 rewards *reasoning* over mere computation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics**
> - **"R-R-R"** for shortest paths: **R**eflex, **R**ectilinear-in-free-space, **R**estricted to vis. graph.
> - **"VIS = Vertices + Illuminated Segments"** to remember visibility graph structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Art Gallery Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Art Gallery Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Art Gallery Problem** is a foundational visibility problem in computational geometry formulated by **Victor Klee in 1973**. The problem is stated as follows:

> **Art Gallery Problem:** Given a simple polygon $\mathcal{P}$ with $n$ vertices (representing the floor plan of an art gallery with $n$ walls), determine the **minimum number of guards** that must be stationed inside $\mathcal{P}$ such that every point in $\mathcal{P}$ is visible to at least one guard. Two points $p, q \in \mathcal{P}$ are *mutually visible* if and only if the open line segment $\overline{pq}$ lies entirely within the interior of $\mathcal{P}$.

The classical answer is given by **Chvátal's Art Gallery Theorem** (1975), which states:

$$\left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

guards are always sufficient and sometimes necessary to guard any simple polygon with $n$ vertices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The cornerstone result required for the examination is **Fisk's elegant proof** of Chvátal's theorem using the **3-coloring of a triangulated polygon**. This proof technique is a frequently tested concept.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are the **security chief of a museum** with irregularly shaped rooms filled with priceless paintings. You need to install CCTV cameras, and every nook, corner, and exhibit must be visible from at least one camera. The question is: what is the *minimum* number of cameras you need to buy?

- The museum's shape is a **polygon** (any simple closed shape with straight walls).
- Cameras are **point guards** that can see $360°$ but cannot see *through* walls.
- "Visible" means an unobstructed straight line exists between the camera and the point.

**The famous answer:** No matter how weirdly shaped your museum is, you will *never* need more than $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ cameras if the museum has $n$ corners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** For a simple rectangle ($n=4$), $\lfloor 4/3 \rfloor = 1$ guard is enough (place it in the center). For a comb-shaped polygon with $n=12$ vertices, at most $\lfloor 12/3 \rfloor = 4$ guards are needed. The *worst-case* museum (a comb polygon) is the one that forces you to use all $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards.

### 1.3 Classification of Guards

| Guard Type | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Guard** | A guard stationed at a single point | Most common; used in Fisk's proof |
| **Vertex Guard** | A guard restricted to a vertex of $\mathcal{P}$ | Easier combinatorial problem |
| **Mobile Guard** | A guard moving along a line segment | Out of KTU 2024 syllabus scope |

### 1.4 Key Geometric Definitions

- **Simple Polygon:** A polygon whose interior boundary does not self-intersect.
- **Diagonal:** A line segment connecting two non-adjacent vertices of $\mathcal{P}$ lying entirely inside $\mathcal{P}$.
- **Triangulation:** A decomposition of $\mathcal{P}$ into triangles using non-intersecting diagonals. Any simple polygon with $n$ vertices admits a triangulation using exactly $n-2$ triangles.
- **Dual Graph of Triangulation:** A graph with one node per triangle and an edge between two nodes iff the corresponding triangles share a diagonal.
- **Vertex Visibility:** A vertex $v$ is *visible* from guard $g$ if the segment $\overline{vg}$ lies in the interior of $\mathcal{P}$ (excluding endpoints on the boundary).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of the **"3-Coloring Trick"** — Fisk colored the vertices of a triangulated polygon with 3 colors so that every triangle got all three colors. The smallest color class is guaranteed to have at most $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ vertices, and these vertices *alone* guard the entire polygon.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Framework: From Triangulation to Guarding

The Art Gallery Theorem's proof unfolds in **four structured steps**:

1. **Triangulate the polygon** $\mathcal{P}$ into $n-2$ triangles using non-intersecting diagonals.
2. **Construct the dual graph** $G$ of the triangulation, which is a *maximal outerplanar graph* and hence **3-colorable**.
3. **3-color the vertices** of $\mathcal{P}$ such that every triangle of the triangulation has vertices of all three distinct colors.
4. **Apply the Pigeonhole Principle:** the smallest color class has at most $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ vertices; place guards at these vertices.

### 2.2 Why Triangulation Works (The 'How' and 'Why')

**Why triangulate first?**
- Triangles are *convex*, so a single guard inside a triangle can see the entire triangle.
- Triangulation gives a finite, well-structured decomposition that captures all interior geometry.

**Why 3-coloring?**
- The dual graph of any triangulation of a simple polygon is a **tree** (acyclic, connected).
- A tree is **2-colorable** (bipartite) by simple BFS/DFS.
- The triangulation's vertex adjacency (every triangle must have 3 distinct colors) requires **3 colors**.

**Why the Pigeonhole Principle?**
- $n$ vertices split into 3 color classes $\Rightarrow$ one class has $\leq \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ vertices.
- Placing guards at these vertices *covers* every triangle, hence every point in $\mathcal{P}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Expression | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of vertices | $n$ | Total vertices of polygon | Foundation of all formulas |
| **Min. guards (upper bound)** | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | Chvátal's Theorem upper bound | Direct application |
| Number of triangles in triangulation | $n - 2$ | Standard triangulation count | Step 1 of Fisk's proof |
| Number of diagonals in triangulation | $n - 3$ | Standard diagonal count | Dual graph construction |
| Smallest color class size | $\leq \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | Pigeonhole bound | Step 4 of proof |
| Time complexity (point guard) | $O(n^3)$ | Brute force visibility | Algorithmic context |
| Time complexity (vertex guard) | $O(n^3)$ | NP-hard in general | KTU note |
| 3-coloring time | $O(n)$ | Linear on a tree dual | Fisk's proof step |
| **Comb polygon guards** | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | Tight lower bound example | Necessity argument |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** The expression $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ uses **floor division**, NOT $n/3$ rounded normally. For $n=3$, the answer is $1$, NOT $1.0$. The floor is essential for odd $n$ values (e.g., $n=7 \Rightarrow \lfloor 7/3 \rfloor = 2$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Art Gallery Problem |
|---|---|
| **Robotics** | Multi-robot coverage planning in unknown terrain |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Optimal placement of sensor nodes for area coverage |
| **CCTV Surveillance** | Camera placement in airports, museums, parking lots |
| **VLSI Design** | Fault detection in integrated circuit layouts |
| **Computer Graphics** | Hidden surface removal, illumination modeling |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lidar/camera placement for full $360°$ coverage |

### 2.5 Formal Statement of the Theorem

$$\boxed{g(\mathcal{P}) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor}$$

where $g(\mathcal{P})$ is the minimum number of point guards required for polygon $\mathcal{P}$ with $n$ vertices. The bound is **tight** because there exist polygons (comb polygons) for which $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards are genuinely necessary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tightness Example:** A **comb polygon** with $n$ vertices (alternating "teeth" on top of a base) requires exactly $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards. This is the canonical example used in KTU question banks to demonstrate the necessity of the bound.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Fisk's Proof of the Art Gallery Theorem (Complete Derivation)

**Theorem (Chvátal, 1975; Proof by Fisk, 1978):** For any simple polygon $\mathcal{P}$ with $n$ vertices, $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ point guards placed at vertices are sufficient to guard $\mathcal{P}$.

---

**Step 1: Triangulate the Polygon**

Since $\mathcal{P}$ is a simple polygon with $n$ vertices, by the standard polygon triangulation theorem, we can decompose $\mathcal{P}$ into $n-2$ non-overlapping triangles using $n-3$ non-intersecting diagonals. The number of triangles is:

$$T = n - 2$$

The number of diagonals used in the triangulation is:

$$D = n - 3$$

> **Justification:** Every triangulation of a simple $n$-gon produces exactly $n-2$ triangles and $n-3$ diagonals. This follows from Euler's formula applied to the planar subdivision.

---

**Step 2: Construct the Dual Graph**

Define a graph $G = (V_G, E_G)$ where:
- $V_G$ = one node per triangle in the triangulation (so $\vert V_G \vert = n - 2$).
- $E_G$ = an edge between two nodes iff the corresponding triangles share a diagonal.

**Claim:** $G$ is a **tree** (connected, acyclic).

*Proof of Claim:*
- **Connected:** Every triangle is reachable from any other via shared diagonals, since $\mathcal{P}$ is connected.
- **Acyclic:** Suppose $G$ contains a cycle $C$. The triangles of $C$ enclose a region whose interior contains vertices and edges of $\mathcal{P}$ not traversed by $C$ — but every triangle boundary is either a polygon edge or a diagonal, and a cycle of triangles in a planar subdivision would enclose a region contradicting the maximality of the triangulation.

Therefore $G$ is a tree with $n-2$ nodes, and hence has exactly $(n-2) - 1 = n - 3$ edges.

---

**Step 3: 3-Color the Vertices of $\mathcal{P}$**

Perform a **BFS (or DFS) on the dual tree** $G$ using only **3 colors** (say, colors $\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \mathcal{C}_3$) such that:

$$\text{Every triangle in the triangulation has its 3 vertices colored with 3 distinct colors.}$$

**Construction:**
1. Start with any triangle $\Delta_0 = v_a v_b v_c$. Color $v_a = \mathcal{C}_1$, $v_b = \mathcal{C}_2$, $v_c = \mathcal{C}_3$.
2. Process the dual tree via BFS. When a new triangle $\Delta_{\text{new}}$ shares a diagonal with a previously colored triangle $\Delta_{\text{old}}$, the two shared vertices are already colored with two different colors (say $\mathcal{C}_i, \mathcal{C}_j$); assign the third color $\mathcal{C}_k$ to the unique uncolored vertex of $\Delta_{\text{new}}$, where $k \neq i, j$.

**Why this works:**
- Every step introduces a new vertex with a *forced* color (the only remaining color).
- BFS processes each triangle exactly once along the tree edges.
- The 3-coloring is consistent because the dual is a tree (no cycles could force a contradiction).

---

**Step 4: Apply the Pigeonhole Principle**

After 3-coloring, the $n$ vertices of $\mathcal{P}$ are partitioned into 3 color classes $\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \mathcal{C}_3$ with sizes $\vert \mathcal{C}_1 \vert, \vert \mathcal{C}_2 \vert, \vert \mathcal{C}_3 \vert$ satisfying:

$$\vert \mathcal{C}_1 \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_2 \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_3 \vert = n$$

By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least one color class has size:

$$\min(\vert \mathcal{C}_1 \vert, \vert \mathcal{C}_2 \vert, \vert \mathcal{C}_3 \vert) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

Let $\mathcal{C}_{\min}$ be this smallest color class. Place a guard at each vertex of $\mathcal{C}_{\min}$.

**Number of guards:**

$$g = \vert \mathcal{C}_{\min} \vert \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

---

**Step 5: Verify Coverage (The Critical Insight)**

Every triangle $\Delta = v_i v_j v_k$ in the triangulation has vertices colored with all three colors $\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \mathcal{C}_3$. Therefore, the smallest color class $\mathcal{C}_{\min}$ contains at least one vertex of $\Delta$.

Since the guard is positioned at a vertex of $\Delta$, and $\Delta$ is a **convex** set, the guard can see the *entire* triangle $\Delta$ (every point inside $\Delta$ is visible from any vertex of $\Delta$).

Since the triangulation covers all of $\mathcal{P}$:

$$\mathcal{P} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n-2} \Delta_i$$

every point $p \in \mathcal{P}$ lies in some triangle $\Delta_i$, and the guard at a vertex of $\Delta_i$ sees $p$. Therefore, $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards suffice.

$$\blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Necessity: The Comb Polygon

**Construction:** For any $k \geq 1$, construct a comb polygon with $n = 3k$ vertices as follows:
- A horizontal base of $k$ segments.
- $k$ "teeth" alternating above and below the base, each tooth being a thin spike of length 1.
- Each tooth has 3 vertices: base-left, tip, base-right.

**Analysis:**
- A guard at the base can see only one tooth's interior (the teeth are too narrow to be seen across).
- A guard at a tip can see only the spike it occupies and possibly part of the base.
- Each tooth's interior must be guarded *separately* by a guard at or near its tip.
- Therefore, at least $k = n/3 = \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards are required.

$$\boxed{\left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor \text{ guards are both sufficient and necessary.}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Problem Variant | Minimum Guards | Proof Technique | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple polygon, vertex guards | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | Fisk's 3-coloring | **Very High** |
| Simple polygon, point guards | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | Same as above | High |
| Orthogonal polygon (rectilinear) | $\lfloor n/4 \rfloor$ | 4-coloring variant | Medium |
| Polygon with holes | NP-hard | Reduction from 3-SAT | Low |
| Mobile guards | $\lfloor n/4 \rfloor$ (conjectured) | Open problem | Low |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A simple polygon has $n = 13$ vertices. Find the minimum number of guards.

**Solution using the formula:**

$$g = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{13}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 4.333\ldots \right\rfloor = 4$$

**Verification by Fisk's proof:**
- Triangulate $\Rightarrow 13 - 2 = 11$ triangles.
- 3-color the 13 vertices.
- Pigeonhole: smallest color class has $\leq \lfloor 13/3 \rfloor = 4$ vertices.
- Place 4 guards at these 4 vertices $\Rightarrow$ full coverage.

$$\text{Answer: } g = 4 \text{ guards are sufficient.}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> State the **Art Gallery Theorem** and explain its significance in computational geometry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Art Gallery Theorem**, formulated by Václav Chvátal in 1975, states that:

$$\left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

point guards are always sufficient to guard any simple polygon with $n$ vertices. The theorem is significant because:

- It provides a **closed-form upper bound** on the minimum number of guards required, depending only on $n$.
- The bound is **tight** — there exist comb polygons that genuinely require $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards.
- It launched the field of **visibility-based computational geometry** with applications in robotics, sensor networks, and computer graphics.
- It demonstrates how **graph coloring (3-coloring)** can solve a geometric problem, a powerful paradigm in algorithm design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> What is a **triangulation** of a polygon? State two important properties of polygon triangulations relevant to the art gallery problem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **triangulation** of a simple polygon $\mathcal{P}$ is a decomposition of $\mathcal{P}$ into non-overlapping triangles using non-intersecting diagonals connecting non-adjacent vertices.

Two important properties:

1. **Triangle count:** Any simple polygon with $n$ vertices can be triangulated into exactly $n - 2$ triangles using $n - 3$ diagonals.

2. **Dual graph is a tree:** The dual graph of the triangulation (nodes = triangles, edges = shared diagonals) is a **tree** (acyclic and connected). This tree property is the structural foundation that allows a 3-coloring to be constructed via BFS without contradiction.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **four-step proof** (Fisk's proof) of the Art Gallery Theorem. Use a labeled diagram to illustrate the **dual graph** concept and 3-coloring step.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Construct a **comb polygon with $n = 12$ vertices** and prove that it requires exactly **4 guards** to be fully guarded. Justify why the bound $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ is tight.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Fisk's Proof — Step by Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Triangulation (2 marks)**

Given a simple polygon $\mathcal{P}$ with $n$ vertices, decompose $\mathcal{P}$ into $n-2$ triangles using $n-3$ non-crossing diagonals. The number of triangles is:

$$T = n - 2$$

```
Diagonal 1 (1-3): Triangle (1,2,3)
Diagonal 2 (1-4): Triangle (1,3,4)
... and so on
```

Total diagonals: $n - 3 = $ number of internal edges in the triangulation.

**Step 2: Dual Graph Construction (2 marks)**

Construct the dual graph $G$ of the triangulation:
- One node per triangle $\Rightarrow$ $n - 2$ nodes.
- An edge between two nodes iff the triangles share a diagonal.

**Claim:** $G$ is a **tree**.

*Proof:* $G$ is connected (every triangle shares a region with another via the polygon's interior). Suppose $G$ contains a cycle. A cycle of triangles would enclose some area, but the enclosed region must contain vertices/edges of the original polygon — contradiction with the triangulation's maximality. Hence $G$ is acyclic and thus a tree.

A tree with $n-2$ nodes has $n-3$ edges (consistent with the number of diagonals).

**Step 3: 3-Coloring (2 marks)**

Perform BFS on $G$:
1. Pick any triangle $T_0 = (v_a, v_b, v_c)$. Color $v_a = R$, $v_b = B$, $v_c = G$ (Red, Blue, Green).
2. For each new triangle $T_{\text{new}}$ sharing a diagonal with $T_{\text{old}}$:
   - The two shared vertices are already colored (e.g., $R$ and $B$).
   - Assign the third color ($G$) to the unique uncolored vertex of $T_{\text{new}}$.

Since $G$ is a tree, no cycle causes a coloring conflict. Every triangle in the triangulation ends up with all 3 colors on its 3 vertices.

**Step 4: Pigeonhole (1 mark)**

The $n$ vertices are split into 3 color classes. By Pigeonhole:

$$\min(\vert R \vert, \vert B \vert, \vert G \vert) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

Place guards at vertices of the smallest class. Since every triangle has a vertex in this class, and every point of $\mathcal{P}$ lies in some triangle, every point is visible to at least one guard. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) Comb Polygon Necessity (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

For $n = 12$, build a comb polygon with $\lfloor 12/3 \rfloor = 4$ "teeth":

```
       /\    /\    /\    /\
      /  \  /  \  /  \  /  \
     /    \/    \/    \/    \
    |                        |
    |  T1   T2   T3   T4     |
    |________________________|
```

Each tooth $T_i$ has 3 vertices: base-left, tip, base-right. With 4 teeth: $4 \times 3 = 12 = n$ vertices.

**Proof that 4 guards are required (4 marks):**

**Claim:** No 3 guards can guard this comb polygon.

*Proof by contradiction:*
- Assume only 3 guards suffice.
- The base of the comb has 4 teeth, each containing a tip vertex $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$.
- Each tip $t_i$ is *not* visible to the other tips $t_j$ ($j \neq i$) because the teeth are separated by walls.
- A guard at the base cannot see the interior of any tooth (the teeth are concave spikes).
- So a tooth's interior can only be guarded by a guard *at or inside* that tooth.
- 4 teeth $\Rightarrow$ at least 4 guards, one per tooth.
- $\Rightarrow$ 3 guards are insufficient.

Therefore, 4 = $\lfloor 12/3 \rfloor$ guards are *necessary*, and Fisk's proof shows they are *sufficient*. The bound is **tight** for the comb polygon. $\blacksquare$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Module Question]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Define the **Art Gallery Problem** formally. Prove that for any simple polygon with $n$ vertices, $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards are sufficient. Show the role of the **Pigeonhole Principle** explicitly.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **vertex guard** and **point guard** variants. Discuss the complexity of finding the minimum number of guards and the role of the **dual graph of a triangulation** in establishing the upper bound.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Formal Definition and Pigeonhole Application (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

The Art Gallery Problem: Given a simple polygon $\mathcal{P}$ with $n$ vertices, find the smallest integer $k$ such that there exist $k$ points $g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k \in \mathcal{P}$ (called *guards*) where every point $p \in \mathcal{P}$ is *visible* from at least one $g_i$, i.e., the open segment $\overline{g_i p}$ is entirely contained in the interior of $\mathcal{P}$.

**Proof of Upper Bound (5 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Triangulate $\mathcal{P}$ into $T = n - 2$ triangles.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* The dual graph of the triangulation is a tree. Hence **2-colorable** by BFS, and we can extend to a **3-coloring** of the polygon's vertices such that every triangle has all 3 colors.

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Pigeonhole Principle applied:
- Total vertices: $n$.
- Partitioned into 3 color classes $C_R, C_B, C_G$.
- $\vert C_R \vert + \vert C_B \vert + \vert C_G \vert = n$.

Since $n$ items are placed in 3 bins, at least one bin has $\leq \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ items:

$$\min(\vert C_R \vert, \vert C_B \vert, \vert C_G \vert) \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Place guards at the vertices of the smallest color class $C_{\min}$.

- Number of guards: $\vert C_{\min} \vert \leq \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$.
- Every triangle $\Delta_i$ in the triangulation contains at least one vertex from $C_{\min}$ (since every triangle has all 3 colors).
- A guard at a vertex of $\Delta_i$ sees the entire triangle (convexity of triangles).
- Since $\mathcal{P} = \bigcup \Delta_i$, every point in $\mathcal{P}$ is seen.

$$\therefore \left\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \right\rfloor \text{ guards suffice. } \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Vertex vs Point Guards, Dual Graph Role (7 marks):**

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Vertex Guards | Point Guards |
|---|---|---|
| **Restriction** | Must be placed at vertices | Can be at any point in $\mathcal{P}$ |
| **Upper bound** | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ |
| **Algorithm** | Polynomial for simple cases | NP-hard in general |
| **Lower bound** | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ (comb) | $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ (comb) |
| **Practical use** | Easier to model | More flexible |

**Key Insight:** For simple polygons (no holes), the vertex guard problem has the same upper bound $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ as point guards, but the *minimum* number of vertex guards can sometimes be strictly larger than the minimum number of point guards for the same polygon. Both equalities hold in the worst case.

**Role of Dual Graph (3 marks):**

The dual graph of a triangulation $G$ plays **three critical roles** in the proof:

1. **Structural backbone:** $G$ being a tree guarantees the absence of cycles, which would otherwise cause coloring conflicts in step 3.

2. **Bridge between geometry and combinatorics:** $G$ converts the *geometric* problem of guarding triangles into the *combinatorial* problem of coloring a tree, which is solvable in linear time $O(n)$.

3. **Pigeonhole enabler:** The 3-coloring of $\mathcal{P}$'s vertices (derived from $G$'s tree structure) provides the partition needed to apply the Pigeonhole Principle in step 4.

Without the tree property of $G$, the 3-coloring would not be guaranteed to exist, and the entire proof structure would collapse.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor function:** Writing $n/3$ instead of $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping the dual graph tree proof:** Students often state "the dual is a tree" without proof. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 3. **Conflating sufficiency and necessity:** The theorem states *sufficiency* of $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$. The comb polygon is needed separately to show *necessity* / tightness. Mixing them up loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Misstating the triangulation count:** Writing $n-1$ triangles instead of $n-2$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring visibility through vertices:** A guard at a vertex cannot "see through" the boundary — visibility is restricted to the interior. Missing this loses 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the convexity of triangles:** The fact that a guard at a vertex sees the *entire* triangle relies on triangle convexity. Omitting this loses 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Art Gallery Problem**

- **Core Theorem (Chvátal, 1975):** $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ point guards are sufficient for any simple polygon with $n$ vertices.
- **Fisk's Proof (1978):** Uses triangulation, dual graph (a tree), 3-coloring, and the Pigeonhole Principle.
- **Triangulation:** $n-2$ triangles, $n-3$ diagonals for any simple $n$-gon.
- **Dual Graph Property:** Always a **tree** (connected, acyclic) for simple polygon triangulations.
- **3-Coloring Invariant:** Every triangle in the triangulation gets all 3 distinct colors on its 3 vertices.
- **Pigeonhole Application:** Smallest of 3 color classes has size $\leq \lfloor n/3 \rfloor$.
- **Guard Placement:** Place guards at vertices of the smallest color class — these are *vertex guards*.
- **Visibility Definition:** Open line segment between two points must lie strictly inside $\mathcal{P}$ (boundary not allowed).
- **Tightness:** The **comb polygon** is the standard example showing the bound is tight — it requires exactly $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards.
- **Comb Polygon Structure:** $n = 3k$ vertices, $k$ teeth, each tooth has 3 vertices (base-left, tip, base-right).
- **Real-World Applications:** CCTV placement, wireless sensor networks, robotic coverage, VLSI fault detection, autonomous vehicle sensors.
- **Variants Beyond Scope:** Polygons with holes make the problem NP-hard; orthogonal polygons have a tighter $\lfloor n/4 \rfloor$ bound.
- **Algorithm Complexity:** 3-coloring a tree is $O(n)$; the full guarding algorithm (with optimal placement search) is generally NP-hard for point guards.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\lfloor n/3 \rfloor$ guards (sufficient)
  - $n-2$ triangles
  - $n-3$ diagonals
  - $\lfloor 12/3 \rfloor = 4$, $\lfloor 13/3 \rfloor = 4$, $\lfloor 15/3 \rfloor = 5$
- **Examiner's Favorite Trap Question:** "Why isn't $n/4$ sufficient?" — Answer: the comb polygon with $n=12$ requires 4 = $n/3$ guards, more than $n/4 = 3$.
- **Remember:** The bound is for **simple polygons** — for polygons with holes, the result breaks down (problem becomes NP-hard).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Programming in Geometry; Computational Geometry in Practice - Computational Geometry Libraries and Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST418 - COMPUTATIONAL GEOMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Programming in Geometry; Computational Geometry in Practice - Computational Geometry Libraries and Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Programming (LP) in Geometry — Formal Definition

**Linear Programming (LP)** is a mathematical optimization technique used to find the best outcome (such as maximum profit or minimum cost) in a mathematical model whose requirements are represented by **linear relationships**. In the context of computational geometry, LP in geometry is the application of linear programming methods to geometric problems where variables represent geometric entities (points, lines, planes) and constraints describe geometric feasibility regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Linear Programming in Geometry refers to the geometric interpretation of linear programming where the feasible region is a convex polyhedron in $\mathbb{R}^n$, and the optimal solution is always attained at a vertex (extreme point) of this polyhedron.*

Formally, a linear program in standard form is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize} \quad & \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of decision variables
- $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the cost (objective) vector
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the constraint matrix
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the resource vector

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Farmer's Field Problem"**
>
> Imagine a farmer who wants to grow two crops — Wheat (W) and Rice (R). He has limited land (10 acres) and limited water (1000 liters). Each crop needs a specific amount of resources and gives a specific profit. The farmer wants to maximize profit. By plotting land used on X-axis and water used on Y-axis, every possible combination forms a **polygon (feasible region)**. The corner points of this polygon are the best candidates for the optimal solution.

In geometry:
- The **half-planes** formed by each constraint are like fences.
- The **intersection** of all half-planes is the **feasible polygon**.
- The **objective function line** is a "profit line" we slide across the polygon to find the best corner.

## 1.3 Geometric Interpretation — Key Terms

| Geometric Term | Linear Programming Equivalent |
|----------------|------------------------------|
| Half-plane | Single linear inequality |
| Convex Polygon | Feasible region |
| Vertex / Corner | Basic Feasible Solution (BFS) |
| Edge of Polygon | Active constraint (binding) |
| Unbounded Polygon | Unbounded LP problem |
| Empty Polygon | Infeasible LP problem |

## 1.4 Computational Geometry Libraries — Formal Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Computational Geometry Libraries are pre-built, optimized software frameworks that provide implementations of fundamental geometric algorithms (convex hull, Voronoi diagram, Delaunay triangulation, range searching, etc.) for use in research and industrial applications.*

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Libraries as "Geometry Toolboxes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
>
> Think of computational geometry libraries as a **master carpenter's toolbox**. Instead of hand-crafting a hammer from scratch every time you need to drive a nail, you reach into the toolbox and grab a ready-made, perfectly engineered hammer. CGAL is like the "Bosch Professional Toolkit", LEDA is the "Stanley Classic Toolkit", and Qhull is a "Specialty Cutter" for convex hulls.

The most prominent libraries in computational geometry include:
1. **CGAL** (Computational Geometry Algorithms Library) — C++
2. **LEDA** (Library of Efficient Data Types and Algorithms) — C++
3. **Qhull** — Convex hull, Delaunay, Voronoi (C)
4. **GEOS** / **JTS** — Java Topology Suite
5. **PCL** (Point Cloud Library) — 3D geometry
6. **Voronoi** libraries (Fortran/C++)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Programming — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Components of an LP Problem

An LP problem consists of three essential components:

1. **Decision Variables** — Quantities we control (e.g., $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$)
2. **Objective Function** — A linear function to be optimized
3. **Constraints** — A set of linear inequalities/equalities

### 2.1.2 Geometric Properties of LP

- **Convexity**: The feasible region of an LP is always a **convex set** (or empty).
- **Extreme Point Theorem**: If an LP has an optimal solution, at least one optimal solution occurs at an **extreme point (vertex)** of the feasible region.
- **Polyhedron**: In $\mathbb{R}^n$, the feasible region is a convex polyhedron.
- **Boundedness**: An LP can be bounded (with finite optimum) or unbounded (objective goes to infinity).

### 2.1.3 The Simplex Method — Conceptual Steps

1. Convert LP to **standard form** (equalities, non-negative variables).
2. Find an initial **basic feasible solution** (a vertex).
3. Check **optimality** using reduced costs.
4. **Pivot** to an adjacent vertex with a better objective value.
5. Repeat until no improving direction exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Simplex work?**
> Because the objective improves (or stays equal) at every pivot, and there are finitely many vertices, the algorithm terminates at an optimal vertex.

### 2.1.4 LP Duality (Geometric Insight)

Every LP (called the **Primal**) has a companion LP (called the **Dual**). Geometrically, the dual can be interpreted as the **best supporting hyperplane** to the feasible region.

Primal:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize} \quad & \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

Dual:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & A^T \mathbf{y} \geq \mathbf{c} \\
& \mathbf{y} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong Duality Theorem:** If the primal has an optimal solution, then $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}^*$ at optimality.

### 2.1.5 LP in Geometric Applications

- **Half-plane intersection** problems reduce to LP feasibility.
- **Smallest enclosing circle** can be formulated as an LP.
- **Linear separability** of point sets is an LP feasibility problem.
- **Range searching** in $d$-dimensions often uses LP-based techniques.

## 2.2 Computational Geometry Libraries — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Classification of Libraries

| Library | Primary Language | Key Strength |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| **CGAL** | C++ | Most comprehensive, robust geometric kernels |
| **LEDA** | C++ | Efficient data structures, graph algorithms |
| **Qhull** | C | Convex hull, Delaunay, Voronoi in 2D-8D |
| **GEOS/JTS** | C++/Java | Spatial operations, GIS |
| **PCL** | C++ | 3D point cloud processing |
| **scikit-geometry** | Python | Bindings to CGAL |
| **VTK** | C++/Python | Visualization Toolkit |

### 2.2.2 Software Tools (Beyond Libraries)

- **MeshLab** — 3D mesh processing
- **CloudCompare** — Point cloud comparison
- **OpenSCAD** — Parametric CAD
- **Gmsh** — Finite element mesh generator
- **CGAL-based Mesher** — Surface/volume meshing

### 2.2.3 Why Use Established Libraries?

1. **Correctness** — Algorithms are mathematically proven and tested.
2. **Robustness** — Handle degenerate cases (collinear points, etc.).
3. **Performance** — Optimized with exact arithmetic (CGAL kernel).
4. **Support** — Active community and documentation.
5. **Portability** — Cross-platform compatibility.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the duality relationships and the extreme point theorem.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Standard LP Form | $\max \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $A\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Polyhedron bounded by half-spaces |
| 2 | Vertex Count Bound | Vertices $\leq \binom{m}{n}$ for $m$ constraints in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Combinatorial bound on simplex iterations |
| 3 | Duality | $\min \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}$ s.t. $A^T \mathbf{y} \geq \mathbf{c}$ | Supporting hyperplane interpretation |
| 4 | Strong Duality | $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}^*$ | Equality at optimum |
| 5 | Weak Duality | $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Primal ≤ Dual always |
| 6 | Farkas' Lemma | Exactly one of two systems has a solution | Geometric separation |
| 7 | Carathéodory's Theorem | Any point in convex hull of $P \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ is convex combo of $\leq d+1$ points | Vertex representation |
| 8 | Convex Hull Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ in 2D, $O(n^{\lfloor d/2 \rfloor})$ in $d$D | Lower bound matches |
| 9 | LP Optimality | Solution at vertex of feasible polyhedron | Corner solution |
| 10 | Sensitivity | $\Delta \mathbf{b}$ valid while basis unchanged | Adjacent vertex reachable |

- **Manufacturing**: Optimal resource allocation in factories.
- **Logistics**: Vehicle routing, supply chain optimization.
- **Computer Graphics**: Half-plane clipping, visibility polygons.

- **VLSI Design**: Wire routing, layout optimization.
- **CGAL in CAD**: Used by AutoCAD, SolidWorks for kernel operations.
